# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These mirror the KTU 2024 Part A question pattern — concise, direct, and definition-based.

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any three industrial applications each of DC series motor and squirrel-cage induction motor."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**DC Series Motor applications** (High starting torque applications):
1. **Traction drives** — Electric locomotives, trams, trolleybuses.
2. **Cranes and hoists** — Overhead gantry cranes, winches.
3. **Elevators (old systems)** — High starting torque to lift heavy loads from rest.

**Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor applications** (General purpose, robust):
1. **Pumps and compressors** — Water pumps, air compressors, refrigeration compressors.
2. **Fans and blowers** — HVAC fans, industrial exhaust systems.
3. **Conveyors** — Bottling plants, packaging lines, food processing.

**Justification principle:** Series motor torque $T \propto I_a^2$ — provides huge start-up pull. Squirrel-cage is cheap, rugged, maintenance-free — ideal for continuous-duty applications.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1]**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State and explain Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction as applied to a single-phase transformer."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's Law (in KTU language):**

> *"The magnitude of EMF induced in a coil is equal to the rate of change of flux linkages with respect to time."*

**Mathematical form:**

$$e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Application to transformer:**

1. When the **primary winding** of a transformer is connected to an **AC supply**, an **alternating current** flows through it.
2. This current produces an **alternating magnetic flux** $\Phi = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ in the core.
3. The flux **links the secondary winding** (mutual coupling through the core).
4. The rate of change of this flux **induces an EMF** in the secondary winding, given by $E_2 = -N_2 \, d\Phi/dt$.
5. The negative sign (Lenz's Law) indicates the **induced EMF opposes the cause** that produces it.

**Final RMS value:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$

**[Marks Distribution: 1 (Statement) + 1 (Math) + 1 (Application)]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **construction of a single-phase core-type transformer**. List the materials used and the function of each major part. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A single-phase core-type transformer consists of a laminated magnetic core, primary and secondary windings, an oil-filled tank, and various accessories for protection and cooling.

**Construction Details (5 marks):**

The core-type transformer has **two vertical limbs** carrying the windings, connected by **top and bottom yokes**. The core is built up from **thin laminations (0.23–0.35 mm)** of **CRGO silicon steel**, insulated from each other by varnish or paper to reduce eddy current losses.

**Windings:** Both primary and secondary are **cylindrical**. The **LV winding** is placed **closest to the core** (for easier insulation from the grounded core), and the **HV winding** is wound **over the LV** with proper insulation in between.

**Tank and Oil:** The core-coil assembly is placed inside a **sealed steel tank** filled with **transformer oil**, which serves three functions: **(i) Insulation**, **(ii) Cooling**, **(iii) Protection from moisture**.

**Major Parts and Their Functions:**

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Core** | Provides low-reluctance magnetic path for flux |
| **Laminations** | Reduce eddy current losses |
| **HV Winding** | Connected to high-voltage side |
| **LV Winding** | Connected to low-voltage side |
| **Transformer Oil** | Insulation, cooling, and protection |
| **Conservator** | Accommodates oil expansion due to heating |
| **Breather (Silica Gel)** | Absorbs moisture from incoming air; gel turns pink when saturated |
| **Bushings** | Insulated terminals for HV/LV connections exiting the tank |
| **Radiator Tubes** | Increase surface area for heat dissipation |
| **Tap Changer** | Adjusts turns ratio to maintain secondary voltage |
| **Buchholz Relay** | Detects internal faults via gas accumulation or oil surge |
| **Explosion Vent / PRV** | Releases overpressure to prevent tank rupture |

**Diagram (1 mark):** A neatly labelled cross-section of core-type transformer showing core, windings, tank, conservator, breather, bushings, and radiator must be drawn.

**Conclusion:** The core-type construction is **preferred for high-voltage applications** due to easier cooling and simpler HV insulation.

---

**(b)** **Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer** and hence find the **number of turns on the primary** for a **230/115 V, 50 Hz transformer** with a **core cross-section of 40 cm²**. Assume **maximum flux density $B_m = 1.2$ T**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $\Phi_m$ = maximum flux in core (Wb), $f$ = supply frequency, $N$ = number of turns, $\omega = 2\pi f$.

The flux varies sinusoidally:
$$\Phi = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

By Faraday's Law:
$$e = N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = N \, \Phi_m \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t)$$

Peak EMF:
$$E_m = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$$

Average EMF (over quarter cycle):
$$E_{avg} = 4 f N \Phi_m$$

RMS EMF (using form factor $K_f = 1.11$ for sinusoidal wave):
$$E_{rms} = 1.11 \times 4 f N \Phi_m$$

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $V_1 = 230$ V, $V_2 = 115$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $A_c = 40$ cm² $= 40 \times 10^{-4}$ m² $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m², $B_m = 1.2$ T.

**Step 1 — Maximum flux:**
$$\Phi_m = B_m \times A_c = 1.2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 4.8 \text{ mWb}$$

**Step 2 — Primary turns:**
$$N_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m} = \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Compute denominator:
$$4.44 \times 50 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-3} = 4.44 \times 0.24 = 1.0656$$

Therefore:
$$N_1 = \dfrac{230}{1.0656} \approx 216 \text{ turns}$$

**Step 3 — Secondary turns (bonus):**
$$N_2 = \dfrac{V_2}{4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m} = \dfrac{115}{1.0656} \approx 108 \text{ turns}$$

**Verification:** $N_1 / N_2 = 216/108 = 2$ and $V_1/V_2 = 230/115 = 2$ ✓ (Turns ratio matches voltage ratio.)

---

### Question B-B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the **selection criteria for industrial motor drives** with reference to **load characteristics, duty cycle, and supply**. List suitable motors for: (i) **Cement mill**, (ii) **Domestic ceiling fan**, (iii) **CNC machine tool axis drive**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Selection Criteria Framework (4 marks):**

A motor is selected based on the following engineering parameters:

**1. Load Characteristics:**
- **Constant Torque Loads** (conveyors, hoists, compressors) → Require motors with high starting torque → **Slip-Ring IM / DC Series / DC Compound**.
- **Variable Torque Loads** (centrifugal pumps, fans — torque ∝ speed²) → Require high-speed motors with soft starting → **Squirrel-Cage IM / DC Shunt**.
- **Constant Power Loads** (machine tools, lathes) → Require wide speed range → **DC Shunt with field control / Synchronous**.
- **Intermittent / High-Inertia Loads** (punch presses, elevators) → Require high starting torque & robust construction → **Slip-Ring IM / DC Compound**.

**2. Duty Cycle (per IS 325 / IEC 60034-1):**
- **S1 — Continuous duty:** Standard TEFC motors
- **S2 — Short-time duty:** Crane motors (15, 30, 60 min ratings)
- **S3 — Intermittent periodic duty:** Welding sets, hoist motors

**3. Supply Availability:**
- **3-Phase AC** → 3-phase IM (cheapest, most robust)
- **1-Phase AC** → 1-phase IM (split-phase, capacitor-start, capacitor-run)
- **DC supply (battery)** → DC motor / BLDC motor

**4. Speed Control Requirement:**
- **No speed control needed** → Squirrel-cage IM (single fixed speed near synchronous)
- **Stepped speed control** → Pole-changing IM
- **Continuous variable speed** → DC Shunt with armature/field control, or Squirrel-cage IM with VFD
- **Precise position control** → Servo / Stepper motor

**5. Environmental Conditions:**
- **Dusty / Hazardous:** TEFC, flameproof, or IP55/IP65 enclosures
- **Wet locations:** IP67, marine-grade enclosures
- **Submerged:** Submersible pump motors

**Application Mapping (3 marks):**

| **Application** | **Recommended Motor** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Cement Mill** | **Slip-Ring Induction Motor (Wound Rotor)** | High-inertia load needs high starting torque AND low starting current; rotor resistance can be varied to control starting current and acceleration smoothly. |
| **(ii) Domestic Ceiling Fan** | **Single-Phase Capacitor-Run Induction Motor** | Single-phase supply only; low starting torque sufficient; capacitor-run gives near-sinusoidal field and quiet operation; cheap and reliable. |
| **(iii) CNC Machine Tool Axis Drive** | **AC Servo Motor (with encoder + VFD drive)** | Requires sub-micron positioning accuracy, fast dynamic response, closed-loop feedback; servo system provides precise position, velocity, and torque control. |

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction of a single-phase shell-type transformer** and **state four differences between core-type and shell-type** transformers. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

In a shell-type transformer, the **core surrounds the windings**. The core has **three limbs** — a **central limb** and **two outer limbs**. Both primary and secondary windings are wound on the **central limb**.

The windings are usually **sandwiched (interleaved)** — half of the LV, then half of the HV, then remaining LV, then remaining HV — to **minimize leakage reactance**.

The core is built from **CRGO silicon steel laminations** (0.23–0.35 mm thick), insulated and stacked. The complete core-coil assembly is immersed in **transformer oil** inside a steel tank, fitted with **conservator, breather, bushings, and radiator** as in core-type.

**Working Principle:** AC supply to primary → produces alternating flux in the central limb → flux divides into two parallel paths through the outer limbs → completes the magnetic circuit back to the central limb → flux links the secondary → induces EMF.

**Four Differences (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Core-Type** | **Shell-Type** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Core vs. Winding Position** | Windings surround the core limbs | Core surrounds the windings |
| **2. Number of Limbs** | Two limbs (both carry windings) | Three limbs (only central carries windings) |
| **3. Magnetic Path Length** | Longer | Shorter (less core material needed) |
| **4. Typical Application** | High-voltage power transformers (in substations) | Low-voltage, high-current transformers (welding, distribution) |
| **5. Cooling** | Easier (more surface exposed) | More difficult (windings enclosed) |
| **6. Mechanical Protection** | Less robust | Better (core shields windings) |
| **7. Leakage Reactance** | Higher | Lower (due to sandwich winding) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS-LOSING PITFALLS IN THIS TOPIC (FROM KTU VALUATION KEYS)**
>
> **1. Forgetting the form factor 1.11:**
> Many students write $E = 4 f N \Phi_m$ (the AVERAGE EMF equation) instead of the **RMS EMF equation** $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_m$. KTU strictly requires **RMS** since voltmeters read RMS. **Loss of 2 marks** is common.
>
> **2. Confusing "Core Type" with "Shell Type" Definitions:**
> When asked for core-type, students often draw a shell-type diagram (or vice versa). The key mnemonic: **"Core Type = Core has Windings around it"** (i.e., windings OUTSIDE the core). **"Shell Type = Windings have Core around it"** (i.e., core OUTSIDE the windings).
>
> **3. Wrong Application Justification:**
> Simply naming the motor (e.g., "DC series motor") without explaining WHY earns only **partial credit**. You must always state the **engineering reason** (e.g., "DC series motor is used for traction because its torque is proportional to I²ₐ, providing very high starting torque required to pull a heavy train from rest").
>
> **4. Skipping the Diagram in 7-Mark Construction Questions:**
> KTU mandates a **neat labelled diagram** for full marks in construction questions. A text-only answer (even if technically correct) loses **at least 1 to 2 marks**.
>
> **5. Mixing Up Motors in Industrial Drive Questions:**
> "Squirrel cage" and "Slip ring" are **two different induction motor types** — students often confuse them. Remember: **Squirrel Cage = simple, robust, low cost**; **Slip Ring = external resistor, high starting torque, high cost**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Memorize it the night before the exam.**

### Industrial Motor Drives

- [ ] An **industrial drive** = Motor + Controller + Transmission + Load (NOT just the motor).
- [ ] **DC Series motor** = Traction (T ∝ I²ₐ, very high starting torque, NEVER no-load).
- [ ] **DC Shunt motor** = Constant speed (Lathes, Fans, Blowers).
- [ ] **DC Compound motor** = High starting torque + good regulation (Elevators, Rolling Mills).
- [ ] **Squirrel-cage IM** = Most common industrial motor (Pumps, Compressors, Conveyors, Fans).
- [ ] **Slip-ring IM** = Heavy load starting (Cement Mills, Hoists, Cranes).
- [ ] **Synchronous motor** = Constant speed + Power Factor correction (Large compressors, Power plants).
- [ ] **Stepper motor** = Open-loop digital positioning (CNC, 3D Printers, Robotics).
- [ ] **Servo motor** = Closed-loop precise control (CNC axes, Robotic arms).
- [ ] **BLDC motor** = High efficiency, no brushes (EVs, Drones, Computer fans).

### Single-Phase Transformer

- [ ] Transformer is a **static device** (no moving parts) operating on **mutual induction**.
- [ ] **Faraday's Law:** $e = -N \, d\Phi/dt$.
- [ ] **EMF Equation (THE formula):** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ — **RMS value**.
- [ ] **Form Factor** $= 1.11$ for sine wave (used to convert average → RMS).
- [ ] **Transformation Ratio:** $k = N_1/N_2 = V_1/V_2 = I_2/I_1$.
- [ ] **Core Type** = Windings surround core (HV applications).
- [ ] **Shell Type** = Core surrounds windings (LV, high-current applications).
- [ ] **Core material** = CRGO silicon steel laminations (0.23–0.35 mm).
- [ ] **Transformer oil** does 3 jobs: Insulation, Cooling, Protection.
- [ ] **Buchholz relay** detects internal faults (gas accumulation or oil surge).
- [ ] **Silica gel breather** absorbs moisture; turns pink when saturated.
- [ ] **Conservator** allows oil expansion; keeps main tank always full.
- [ ] **Bushings** are insulated terminals for HV/LV connections.
- [ ] Standard supply in India: **50 Hz, 415 V (3-phase), 240 V (1-phase)**.
- [ ] Transformer efficiency: **98–99.5%** for large units.

### Cross-Cutting Engineering Insight

- [ ] A motor is selected for an industrial drive based on **Load type + Duty cycle + Supply + Speed control + Environment**.
- [ ] A transformer's core cross-section, number of turns, and voltage ratio are **all inter-locked** by the single equation $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_m$.
- [ ] **Safety is integrated:** Buchholz relay, breather, PRV, conservator — all ensure the transformer does not catch fire or explode under fault.

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — PCSFT403 — ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY**

topics.title: EMF Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EMF Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EMF Equation (Electromotive Force Equation):** The fundamental mathematical relationship that expresses the magnitude of voltage induced in the windings of an electrical machine (DC generator, transformer, or alternator) in terms of its constructional parameters (number of poles, flux per pole, speed, number of conductors) and operational parameters (frequency, winding factor).

In any electromagnetic energy conversion device, whenever a conductor **cuts magnetic flux** or experiences a **change in flux linkage**, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced. The EMF equation quantifies this induced voltage.

### 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Flux Density (B)** is measured in **Tesla (Wb/m²)**
- **Magnetic Flux (Φ)** is measured in **Weber (Wb)**
- **Frequency (f)** is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**
- **Angular Velocity (ω)** is measured in **rad/s**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Wheel of Electricity":**
> Imagine a water wheel placed under a flowing river. The faster the river flows (analogy to **magnetic flux**), and the more paddles the wheel has (**number of conductors**), and the faster the wheel rotates (**speed**), the more water it scoops and turns into energy per second.
>
> In the same way, an electrical machine's induced EMF depends on:
> 1. **How strong is the magnetic field?** → Flux per pole (Φ)
> 2. **How many conductors are cutting the field?** → Number of conductors (Z)
> 3. **How fast are they cutting?** → Speed (N) or Frequency (f)
> 4. **How are they connected?** → Winding arrangement (A, T_p, K_w)

---

### 1.4 Three Key EMF Equations (Module 1 Focus)

The EMF equation differs based on the type of machine:

| Machine Type | EMF Equation |
|--------------|--------------|
| DC Generator / Motor | $E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ |
| Single-Phase Transformer | $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ |
| Three-Phase Alternator | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 EMF Equation of a DC Generator — Step-by-Step Logic

**Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction (KTU Foundation Block):**

The EMF induced in a single conductor when it cuts magnetic flux at a uniform rate is:

$$e = B \times l \times v$$

where:
- **B** = Flux density (Wb/m²)
- **l** = Active length of conductor (m)
- **v** = Velocity of conductor (m/s)

---

**Derivation Logic — From Faraday's Law to DC Generator EMF:**

**Step 1:** Flux cut per revolution by one conductor.

A conductor in a DC generator sweeps past **P poles** in one revolution, and the flux cut per pole is **Φ**.

$$\text{Flux cut per revolution} = P \Phi$$

**Step 2:** Number of revolutions per second.

If the armature rotates at **N rpm**, then revolutions per second = **N / 60**.

**Step 3:** Flux cut per second by one conductor.

$$\text{Flux cut per second} = P \Phi \times \dfrac{N}{60}$$

**Step 4:** Average EMF induced in one conductor.

By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced per conductor equals the rate of change of flux:

$$e_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

**Step 5:** Total EMF for **Z** total conductors in the armature.

If there are **Z** armature conductors, the total EMF before parallel paths:

$$E_{\text{total}} = Z \times \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60}$$

**Step 6:** Account for parallel paths (**A**).

Conductors are connected in parallel paths. For a **wave-wound** DC machine, **A = 2**. For a **lap-wound** DC machine, **A = P**. The EMF between brushes equals the EMF per parallel path:

$$\boxed{E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \text{ volts}}$$

---

### 2.2 EMF Equation of a Transformer

A transformer has a stationary magnetic circuit. The flux in the core varies sinusoidally:

$$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The induced EMF in a winding of **N** turns is:

$$e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -N \omega \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

The **RMS value** of this sinusoidal EMF is:

$$E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{N \omega \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{N \times 2\pi f \times \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$E_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{2} \, \pi \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$$

Using the KTU-preferred constant $\sqrt{2}\pi \approx 4.44$:

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N \text{ volts}}$$

---

### 2.3 EMF Equation of a Three-Phase Alternator

For an alternator, the induced EMF per phase accounts for winding distribution and coil span:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, T_{ph} \, K_w$$

where **K_w** = **K_d × K_c** (winding factor = distribution factor × coil span factor)

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Cheat Sheet — Save This for Last-Minute Revision**

| # | Machine | EMF Formula | Key Symbols & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **DC Generator** | $E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ | P = poles, Φ = Wb, N = rpm, Z = conductors, A = parallel paths |
| 2 | **DC Motor (Back EMF)** | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ | Same as DC generator (Lenz's Law opposition) |
| 3 | **Single-Phase Transformer** | $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$ | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_2$ |
| 4 | **Three-Phase Alternator (per phase)** | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | $T_{ph}$ = turns/phase, $K_w$ = winding factor |
| 5 | **Alternator (Line Voltage, Star)** | $E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph}$ | $E_L = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, T_{ph} \, K_w \times \sqrt{3}$ |
| 6 | **Flux from EMF (DC Machine)** | $\Phi = \dfrac{60 \, A \, E_g}{P \, N \, Z}$ | Rearranged formula |
| 7 | **Speed from EMF (DC Machine)** | $N = \dfrac{60 \, A \, E_g}{P \, \Phi \, Z}$ | Rearranged formula |
| 8 | **Emf Equation Constant** | $K_a = \dfrac{P \, Z}{60 \, A}$ | So $E_g = K_a \, \Phi \, N$ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Engineers Study the EMF Equation:**
>
> - **DC Generators:** Used in automobile alternators (charging systems), DC welding machines, and excitation systems of alternators in power stations.
> - **Transformers:** The heart of every power grid — stepping voltage up at generation (11 kV → 400 kV) and stepping down for distribution (400 V for homes).
> - **Alternators:** The primary source of bulk electrical energy in thermal, hydro, and nuclear power plants worldwide.
> - **Safety Application:** The EMF equation directly governs **electrical isolation distances**, **insulation voltage ratings**, and **touch-safe design limits** in accordance with IS/IEC standards — making it foundational to **electrical safety engineering**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — DC Generator EMF Equation

Let the following constructional data be assumed for a DC generator:
- Number of poles = **P**
- Flux per pole = **Φ** Weber
- Total armature conductors = **Z**
- Speed of armature = **N** rpm
- Number of parallel paths = **A**

---

**Step 1: Flux cut per pole by one conductor in one revolution.**

In one complete revolution, a conductor moves past **P** poles. The flux cut per pole is **Φ**.

$$\text{Flux cut per pole} = \Phi \text{ Weber}$$

$$\therefore \text{Flux cut per revolution} = P \times \Phi \text{ Weber}$$

---

**Step 2: Number of revolutions per second.**

The armature rotates at **N** revolutions per minute.

$$\text{Revolutions per second} = \dfrac{N}{60}$$

---

**Step 3: Flux cut per second by ONE conductor.**

This equals the flux cut per revolution multiplied by revolutions per second.

$$\text{Flux cut per second} = P \Phi \times \dfrac{N}{60} \text{ Weber/sec}$$

By Faraday's First Law, the average EMF induced per conductor equals the rate of flux cutting:

$$e_{\text{avg per conductor}} = \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

---

**Step 4: Total EMF generated in the armature (before parallel paths).**

If there are **Z** conductors in total, each cutting flux at the same average rate:

$$E_{\text{total}} = Z \times \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

---

**Step 5: Net EMF available at the brushes (after parallel paths).**

In a DC machine, the **Z** conductors are divided among **A** parallel paths. Conductors in the same parallel path add in series, while the A paths are in parallel. Hence, the EMF across the brushes equals the EMF of ONE parallel path:

$$\text{Conductors per parallel path} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = \dfrac{Z}{A} \times \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \text{ Volts}}$$

This is the **EMF Equation of a DC Generator**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

> **Problem:** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 600 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.05 Wb. The armature is driven at 1000 rpm. Calculate the EMF generated. If the machine is rewound as lap-wound keeping all other parameters same, find the new EMF.

---

**Given Data:**

$$P = 4, \quad \Phi = 0.05 \text{ Wb}, \quad N = 1000 \text{ rpm}, \quad Z = 600$$

For **wave winding**: $A = 2$

For **lap winding**: $A = P = 4$

---

**Solution (a) — Wave Wound:**

Using the EMF Equation:

$$E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

Substituting values:

$$E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 600}{60 \times 2}$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 600}{120}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 0.05 \times 600000}{120}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 0.05 \times 5000}{1}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{1000}{1} = 1000 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_g \text{(wave)} = 1000 \text{ V}}$$

---

**Solution (b) — Lap Wound:**

$$E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 600}{60 \times 4}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 600}{240}$$

$$E_g = \dfrac{120000}{240} = 500 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_g \text{(lap)} = 500 \text{ V}}$$

---

**Key Insight:** Doubling the number of parallel paths **halves** the generated EMF for the same flux and speed.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Transformer EMF Equation

Consider a transformer with **N** turns on a winding. The core flux varies sinusoidally:

$$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate flux to get rate of change.

$$\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = \Phi_m \times 2\pi f \times \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2:** By Faraday's Law, the instantaneous EMF in N turns:

$$e(t) = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -N \times 2\pi f \times \Phi_m \times \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3:** Identify the peak value of this sinusoidal EMF.

$$E_{\max} = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$$

**Step 4:** Convert peak value to RMS value by dividing by $\sqrt{2}$.

$$E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{E_{\max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the KTU constant $2\pi/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}\pi \approx 4.44$.

$$\boxed{E_{\text{rms}} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N \text{ Volts}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Transformer EMF

> **Problem:** A 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has a maximum core flux of 0.01 Wb. Find the number of turns on the primary winding if it is to be operated at 230 V. Also calculate the peak value of the flux density if the core cross-sectional area is 200 cm².

---

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $\Phi_m = 0.01$ Wb, $E_1 = 230$ V, $A_c = 200 \text{ cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.02 \text{ m}^2$

---

**Solution (a) — Number of turns on primary:**

Using the transformer EMF equation:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$$

Rearranging:

$$N_1 = \dfrac{E_1}{4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m}$$

Substituting:

$$N_1 = \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 0.01}$$

$$N_1 = \dfrac{230}{2.22}$$

$$\boxed{N_1 \approx 103.6 \approx 104 \text{ turns}}$$

---

**Solution (b) — Peak flux density:**

$$B_m = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{A_c} = \dfrac{0.01}{0.02} = 0.5 \text{ T}$$

$$\boxed{B_m = 0.5 \text{ Tesla}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Conceptual Cross-Comparison

| Aspect | DC Machine | Transformer | Alternator |
|--------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Energy Conversion** | Mechanical ↔ Electrical | AC ↔ AC (Voltage level change) | Mechanical → AC Electrical |
| **Moving Part** | Armature rotates | Stationary (both windings) | Rotor rotates |
| **Nature of Flux** | Constant (DC) | Pulsating (AC, sinusoidal) | Rotating (sinusoidally varying) |
| **Faraday's Law Application** | Conductor cuts static flux | Time-varying flux links stationary coil | Rotating flux links stationary coil |
| **EMF Type Induced** | DC (commutated) | AC (no commutation needed) | AC (sinusoidal) |
| **Equation Form** | $E = k \, \Phi \, N$ | $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ | $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, T \, K_w$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.4 Physical Concept Schematic (Adapted to Block Diagram)

Since the EMF equation derives from a physical rotating machine, the following block diagram captures the energy conversion chain:


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

State the EMF equation of a DC generator. Explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF equation of a DC generator is:

$$E_g = \dfrac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} \text{ volts}$$

- **P** = Number of poles
- **Φ** = Flux per pole in Weber
- **N** = Speed of armature in rpm
- **Z** = Total number of armature conductors
- **A** = Number of parallel paths (A = 2 for wave, A = P for lap winding)

**Significance:** It gives the voltage generated at the armature terminals and shows that EMF is directly proportional to flux, speed, and inversely proportional to the number of parallel paths.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Derive the constant 4.44 used in the EMF equation of a transformer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The constant 4.44 arises from:

$$E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Since $2\pi / \sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2} \pi = 1.414 \times 3.1416 \approx 4.44$

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

This constant converts peak sinusoidal flux to RMS voltage accounting for the sinusoidal nature of the time-varying flux linkage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator with neat steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, lap-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.04 Wb. Calculate the speed at which it must be driven to generate an EMF of 250 V. If the load current is 50 A and the armature resistance is 0.2 Ω, find the terminal voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation.

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU valuation key):**
- Step 1 (Flux per revolution): 1 mark
- Step 2 (Revolutions per second): 1 mark
- Step 3 (Flux cut per second per conductor): 1.5 marks
- Step 4 (Total EMF for Z conductors): 1.5 marks
- Step 5 (Final EMF with parallel paths): 2 marks

---

**Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P = 4$, Lap-wound → $A = P = 4$, $Z = 600$, $\Phi = 0.04$ Wb, $E_g = 250$ V, $I_L = 50$ A, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω

**Step 1:** Rearrange EMF equation to find N:

$$N = \dfrac{60 \, A \, E_g}{P \, \Phi \, Z}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$N = \dfrac{60 \times 4 \times 250}{4 \times 0.04 \times 600}$$

$$N = \dfrac{60000}{96}$$

$$\boxed{N = 625 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 3:** Find armature current. In a shunt generator, assuming field current is negligible:

$$I_a \approx I_L = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Armature voltage drop:

$$I_a R_a = 50 \times 0.2 = 10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5:** Terminal voltage:

$$V_t = E_g - I_a R_a = 250 - 10$$

$$\boxed{V_t = 240 \text{ V}}$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Rearranged equation: 1 mark
- N calculation: 2 marks
- Armature current identification: 1 mark
- Voltage drop calculation: 1.5 marks
- Terminal voltage final answer: 1.5 marks

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer and explain why the constant 4.44 is used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250/2500 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has 100 turns on the primary side. Calculate: (i) the number of turns on the secondary, (ii) the maximum flux in the core, and (iii) the cross-sectional area of the core if the maximum flux density is 1.2 T. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.3** above for the complete derivation.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Flux equation setup: 1 mark
- Differentiation step: 1.5 marks
- Peak EMF identification: 1.5 marks
- RMS conversion with 4.44: 2 marks
- Final boxed equation: 1 mark

---

**Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_1 = 250$ V, $V_2 = 2500$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $N_1 = 100$ turns, $B_m = 1.2$ T

**Part (i) — Number of turns on secondary:**

Using turns ratio:

$$\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$$

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = 100 \times \dfrac{2500}{250}$$

$$\boxed{N_2 = 1000 \text{ turns}}$$

---

**Part (ii) — Maximum flux in core:**

Using primary EMF equation:

$$V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$$

$$\Phi_m = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \dfrac{250}{4.44 \times 50 \times 100}$$

$$\Phi_m = \dfrac{250}{22200}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_m = 0.01126 \text{ Wb} \approx 11.26 \text{ mWb}}$$

---

**Part (iii) — Cross-sectional area of core:**

$$B_m = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{A_c}$$

$$A_c = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{B_m} = \dfrac{0.01126}{1.2}$$

$$\boxed{A_c = 0.00938 \text{ m}^2 = 93.8 \text{ cm}^2}$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Turns ratio with correct formula: 2 marks
- Maximum flux using 4.44: 2 marks
- Area using flux density: 2 marks
- Units and final boxed answers: 1 mark

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "60" denominator:** Always remember the EMF equation of DC machines has **60** in the denominator because N is in **rpm** (revolutions per minute), but flux cut per second requires revolutions per second.
>
> 2. **Confusing Wave and Lap Winding:** A = 2 for **wave winding** (always, irrespective of poles). A = P for **lap winding**. Mixing these up gives wrong EMF and costs full marks.
>
> 3. **Using 4.44 vs 4.44 f Φm N:** The 4.44 factor already includes frequency. Students sometimes double-multiply by frequency.
>
> 4. **Units of Flux:** Flux must be in **Weber (Wb)**. If given in **milliWeber (mWb)**, divide by 1000 first before substituting. Examiners explicitly test this conversion skill.
>
> 5. **Neglecting Winding Factor (K_w) in Alternator EMF:** For distributed windings, K_w < 1 (typically 0.95–0.98). Omitting it gives higher EMF than actual.
>
> 6. **Showing only final answer:** KTU examiners **mandate** stepwise derivation. Even if the answer is correct, full marks require showing every substitution and simplification step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EMF Equation**
>
> - The **DC generator EMF equation** is: $E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$
> - The **transformer EMF equation** is: $E = 4.44 f \Phi_m N$
> - The **alternator EMF equation** is: $E_{ph} = 4.44 f \Phi_m T_{ph} K_w$
> - **A = 2** for wave winding; **A = P** for lap winding (DC machines)
> - The constant **4.44** comes from $\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}\pi$
> - EMF is **directly proportional** to flux, speed, and number of turns/conductors
> - EMF is **inversely proportional** to the number of parallel paths
> - For **back EMF in a DC motor**, the same equation applies (by Lenz's Law)
> - **Practical Use:** The equation determines insulation levels, voltage ratings, and safe working clearances — directly linking this theory to **electrical safety standards (IS/IEC 60950, IEC 61558)**
> - **Star-connected alternator line voltage** $E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph}$
> - Always carry out **unit conversions** before substitution (Wb, rpm, m², cm²)
> - **KTU Pattern:** A 14-mark question usually combines a 7-mark derivation with a 7-mark numerical — practice both rigorously
> - Memorize the **rearranged forms** (Φ from E, N from E, N₁/N₂ from V₁/V₂)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phasor Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phasor Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Phasor** is a complex-number representation of a sinusoidal function of time, used in **Alternating Current (AC) circuit analysis** to convert time-domain differential equations into algebraic equations. A phasor captures two intrinsic properties of a sinusoid: its **magnitude (amplitude or RMS value)** and its **phase angle (initial angular displacement)** with respect to a chosen reference axis.

Mathematically, a sinusoid
$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$
is represented as the phasor
$$\boxed{\mathbf{V} = V_{\text{rms}} \angle \phi = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\,\angle\,\phi}$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency** (in **radians/second**), and $f$ is the **supply frequency** (**50 Hz** in India, **60 Hz** in the US).

A **Phasor Diagram** is the geometric visualization of these phasors on the complex plane, where the horizontal axis represents the **real (in-phase) component** and the vertical axis represents the **imaginary (quadrature) component**. The diagram is a *snapshot* of all rotating phasors at $\omega t = 0$, drawn relative to a common reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – A phasor is NOT a vector in physical space.** It is a mathematical construct that rotates counter-clockwise at angular velocity $\omega$. When we "freeze" time at $t = 0$, we get the static phasor diagram used for analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **second hand of an analog clock**:
- The **length** of the hand represents the **magnitude** ($V_m$ or $I_m$).
- The **angle** it makes with the 12-o'clock position (vertical) represents the **phase angle** $\phi$.
- All hands on different "voltage clocks" and "current clocks" rotate at the same speed ($\omega$), but each may start from a *different angle* (different $\phi$).

When we want to compare how two AC quantities relate, we cannot simply subtract their instantaneous values because they are constantly changing. Instead, we **stop the clock** at $t = 0$ and measure the angles between the hands. The angle between the voltage hand and the current hand is precisely the **phase difference**, which tells us whether the circuit is resistive, inductive, or capacitive.

**Real-world example**: A ceiling fan (mostly inductive) draws current that **lags** the supply voltage. A pure capacitor bank (used for power-factor correction) draws current that **leads** the voltage. The phasor diagram is the engineer's tool to visualize this "chase" between $V$ and $I$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Phasors Matter in Engineering**
> - They convert differential equations (hard) into algebraic equations (easy).
> - They enable **Ohm's Law** to be applied in AC: $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z}$, where $\mathbf{Z}$ is the **complex impedance**.
> - They are the foundation of **power triangles**, **impedance triangles**, and **power-factor correction** in industrial systems.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Value / Unit (KTU 2024) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Supply frequency in India | **$f = 50\text{ Hz}$** |
| Supply frequency in US | **$f = 60\text{ Hz}$** |
| Angular frequency | **$\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s** (314 rad/s for 50 Hz) |
| RMS-to-Peak factor | $V_{\text{rms}} = V_m / \sqrt{2} \approx 0.707\,V_m$ |
| Average-to-Peak factor | $V_{\text{avg}} = 2V_m / \pi \approx 0.637\,V_m$ |
| Form factor | $K_f = V_{\text{rms}} / V_{\text{avg}} = 1.11$ (for pure sine) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation: From Sinusoid to Phasor

The transition from a time-domain sinusoid to a frequency-domain phasor relies on **Euler's identity**:

$$e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi$$

A general sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ can be rewritten using the imaginary part of a complex exponential:

$$v(t) = \text{Im}\bigl\{V_m e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}\bigr\} = \text{Im}\bigl\{\underbrace{V_m e^{j\phi}}_{\text{Phasor}} \cdot e^{j\omega t}\bigr\}$$

We define the **phasor** $\mathbf{V}$ as the time-independent part:

$$\mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} = V_m \angle \phi \quad \text{(Peak-value phasor)}$$

For power calculations, the **RMS phasor** is preferred:

$$\mathbf{V}_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \angle \phi$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: Always specify whether your phasor is in *peak* or *RMS* form. In KTU 2024 board exams, the **RMS phasor** is the default unless otherwise stated.

### 2.2 Phasor Relationships for Basic Circuit Elements

The three passive elements respond to AC in fundamentally different ways. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logic:

**1. Pure Resistor (R)**
- Ohm's law holds instantaneously: $v_R(t) = R \cdot i_R(t)$.
- If $i_R(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, then $v_R(t) = R I_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- **Both quantities have the same phase angle** $\phi$.
- Phasor diagram: $\mathbf{V}_R$ and $\mathbf{I}_R$ are **collinear** (overlapping arrows).

**2. Pure Inductor (L)**
- Voltage–current relation: $v_L(t) = L\,\dfrac{di_L(t)}{dt}$.
- If $i_L(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, then
$$v_L(t) = L \cdot I_m \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t) = \omega L I_m \sin(\omega t + 90^{\circ})$$
- The voltage **leads** the current by **$90^{\circ}$** (or $\pi/2$ rad).
- Inductive reactance: $X_L = \omega L$ (in **ohms**, $\Omega$).
- Phasor form: $\mathbf{V}_L = j \omega L \mathbf{I}_L = j X_L \mathbf{I}_L$.

**3. Pure Capacitor (C)**
- Voltage–current relation: $i_C(t) = C\,\dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$.
- If $v_C(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, then
$$i_C(t) = \omega C V_m \cos(\omega t) = \omega C V_m \sin(\omega t + 90^{\circ})$$
- The current **leads** the voltage by **$90^{\circ}$** (equivalently, voltage **lags** current by $90^{\circ}$).
- Capacitive reactance: $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ (in **ohms**, $\Omega$).
- Phasor form: $\mathbf{V}_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\mathbf{I}_C = -j X_C \mathbf{I}_C$.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the $j$ Operator

The symbol $j$ (engineers use $j$ instead of $i$ to avoid confusion with current) represents a **$90^{\circ}$ counter-clockwise rotation** in the complex plane. Multiplying any phasor by $j$ rotates it by $+90^{\circ}$; multiplying by $-j$ rotates it by $-90^{\circ}$. This is the algebraic reason why an inductive voltage leads its current.

### 2.4 Impedance and the Impedance Triangle

For any two-terminal network driven by a single frequency $\omega$, the **complex impedance** is defined as:

$$\mathbf{Z} = \frac{\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{I}} = R + jX \quad \text{[ohms, } \Omega\text{]}$$

The **impedance triangle** decomposes $\mathbf{Z}$ into:
- **Real axis (horizontal)**: Resistance $R$.
- **Imaginary axis (vertical)**: Net reactance $X = X_L - X_C$.
- **Hypotenuse**: Magnitude $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$.
- **Phase angle**: $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X}{R}\right)$.

The **power triangle** decomposes apparent power $S$ into:
- **Real axis**: Active (true) power $P = VI\cos\theta$ (in **watts, W**).
- **Imaginary axis**: Reactive power $Q = VI\sin\theta$ (in **volt-amperes reactive, VAR**).
- **Hypotenuse**: Apparent power $S = VI$ (in **volt-amperes, VA**).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | RMS Phasor | $\mathbf{V}_{\text{rms}} = V_m / \sqrt{2} \,\angle\,\phi$ | V (volts) | Pure sinusoid only |
| 2 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | $f$ in Hz |
| 3 | Inductive reactance | $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ | $\Omega$ | — |
| 4 | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = 1/(\omega C) = 1/(2\pi f C)$ | $\Omega$ | — |
| 5 | Resistor impedance | $\mathbf{Z}_R = R$ | $\Omega$ | In phase with $I$ |
| 6 | Inductor impedance | $\mathbf{Z}_L = jX_L$ | $\Omega$ | $V$ leads $I$ by $90^{\circ}$ |
| 7 | Capacitor impedance | $\mathbf{Z}_C = -jX_C$ | $\Omega$ | $V$ lags $I$ by $90^{\circ}$ |
| 8 | Magnitude of $\mathbf{Z}$ | $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ | $\Omega$ | Series RLC |
| 9 | Phase angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\bigl[(X_L - X_C)/R\bigr]$ | degrees or rad | — |
| 10 | Power factor | $\cos\theta = R / \vert \mathbf{Z} \vert$ | dimensionless | Leading or lagging |
| 11 | Active power | $P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta$ | W (watts) | — |
| 12 | Reactive power | $Q = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \sin\theta$ | VAR | — |
| 13 | Apparent power | $S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | VA | — |
| 14 | Resonant frequency | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ | Hz | $X_L = X_C$, $\theta = 0$ |
| 15 | Quality factor (series RLC) | $Q_f = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ | dimensionless | At resonance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative Sign for $X_C$**: In KTU problems, ALWAYS treat $X_C$ as negative when summing reactances. A common board-exam error is to write $X = X_L + X_C$ instead of the correct $X = X_L - X_C$. The correct expression is $X = X_L - X_C$, and this **negative sign** is what determines whether the circuit is inductive ($X > 0$) or capacitive ($X < 0$).

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Phasor Diagrams

1. **Power-Factor Correction (PFC)**: Industrial plants install capacitor banks to shift the current phasor towards the voltage phasor, reducing the phase angle $\theta$ and the line current. Phasor diagrams visualize the "before" and "after" states.
2. **Transformer & Motor Design**: Engineers use phasors to compute the no-load and short-circuit phasor diagrams of transformers, which dictate voltage regulation and efficiency.
3. **Transmission-Line Analysis**: The receiving-end voltage, sending-end voltage, and line-drop are represented as phasors to evaluate the voltage profile along a feeder.
4. **Harmonic Analysis**: While basic phasor analysis assumes a single frequency, the technique generalizes to **Fourier-domain** analysis where multiple frequency components are superposed.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Sinusoid to Phasor (Euler's Form)

**Problem**: Convert $v(t) = 311 \sin(314 t + 30^{\circ})\text{ V}$ into its RMS phasor form.

**Step 1** — Identify the sinusoidal parameters.
- Peak voltage: $V_m = 311\text{ V}$.
- Angular frequency: $\omega = 314\text{ rad/s} \Rightarrow f = 50\text{ Hz}$.
- Phase angle: $\phi = 30^{\circ}$.

**Step 2** — Apply Euler's identity to express the sinusoid as the imaginary part of a rotating complex exponential.
$$v(t) = \text{Im}\bigl\{V_m\,e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}\bigr\}$$

**Step 3** — Factor out the time-dependent part $e^{j\omega t}$ to isolate the time-independent part.
$$v(t) = \text{Im}\bigl\{\underbrace{V_m e^{j\phi}}_{\text{Phasor}} \cdot e^{j\omega t}\bigr\}$$

**Step 4** — Identify the phasor (peak form).
$$\mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} = 311\,e^{j 30^{\circ}}\text{ V}$$

**Step 5** — Convert to polar form.
$$\mathbf{V} = 311\,\angle 30^{\circ}\text{ V}$$

**Step 6** — Convert to RMS form by dividing by $\sqrt{2}$.
$$\mathbf{V}_{\text{rms}} = \frac{311}{\sqrt{2}}\,\angle 30^{\circ} \approx 220\,\angle 30^{\circ}\text{ V}$$

**Conclusion**: The given sinusoid is equivalent to the RMS phasor $\mathbf{V} = 220\angle 30^{\circ}\text{ V}$ rotating at $314\text{ rad/s}$ in the counter-clockwise direction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Magnitude and Phase of Series RL Impedance

**Problem**: A series circuit has $R = 30\;\Omega$ and $X_L = 40\;\Omega$ at $f = 50\text{ Hz}$. Find the magnitude of impedance $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert$, the phase angle $\theta$, and the power factor.

**Step 1** — Write the complex impedance.
$$\mathbf{Z} = R + jX_L = 30 + j40\;\Omega$$

**Step 2** — Compute the magnitude using the Pythagorean theorem.
$$\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\;\Omega$$

**Step 3** — Compute the phase angle.
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{40}{30}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \approx 53.13^{\circ}$$

**Step 4** — Express $\mathbf{Z}$ in polar form.
$$\mathbf{Z} = 50\,\angle 53.13^{\circ}\;\Omega$$

**Step 5** — Compute the power factor.
$$\cos\theta = \frac{R}{\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6 \;\;(\text{lagging, since } X_L > 0)$$

**Step 6** — Verify using the sine component.
$$\sin\theta = \frac{X_L}{\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.8$$

**Step 7** — Sanity check (Pythagorean identity).
$$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 0.6^2 + 0.8^2 = 0.36 + 0.64 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion**: $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = 50\;\Omega$, $\theta = 53.13^{\circ}$, **$\cos\phi = 0.6$ lagging**. The "3-4-5" right triangle is a classic KTU problem archetype.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Series RLC with Net Capacitive Behavior

**Problem**: A series circuit has $R = 12\;\Omega$, $X_L = 30\;\Omega$, and $X_C = 50\;\Omega$ at $\omega = 1000\text{ rad/s}$. Find $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert$, $\theta$, and the power factor. Also compute the inductive and capacitive reactances if $L = 30\text{ mH}$ and $C = 20\;\mu\text{F}$.

**Step 1** — Compute the inductive reactance.
$$X_L = \omega L = 1000 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} = 30\;\Omega \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2** — Compute the capacitive reactance.
$$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{1000 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50\;\Omega \;\checkmark$$

**Step 3** — Compute the net reactance.
$$X = X_L - X_C = 30 - 50 = -20\;\Omega$$

The negative sign indicates the circuit is **net capacitive** at this frequency.

**Step 4** — Write the complex impedance.
$$\mathbf{Z} = R + jX = 12 - j20\;\Omega$$

**Step 5** — Compute the magnitude.
$$\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{12^2 + (-20)^2} = \sqrt{144 + 400} = \sqrt{544} \approx 23.32\;\Omega$$

**Step 6** — Compute the phase angle.
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-20}{12}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right) \approx -59.04^{\circ}$$

The negative angle indicates that the **current leads the voltage** (capacitive behaviour).

**Step 7** — Compute the power factor.
$$\cos\theta = \frac{R}{\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert} = \frac{12}{23.32} \approx 0.5146 \;\;(\text{leading})$$

**Step 8** — Express in polar form.
$$\mathbf{Z} = 23.32\,\angle -59.04^{\circ}\;\Omega$$

**Conclusion**: The circuit behaves as a **net capacitive** load. Even though $R$, $L$, and $C$ are all positive physical values, the **reactance sign** determines the leading/lagging nature. This is a frequent KTU board question: students are expected to recognize that *capacitive reactance dominates* here.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Series vs Parallel RLC

| Property | Series RLC | Parallel RLC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Same quantity in all branches | Current $I$ | Voltage $V$ |
| Total impedance / admittance | $\mathbf{Z}_{\text{eq}} = \sum \mathbf{Z}_k$ (vector sum) | $\mathbf{Y}_{\text{eq}} = \sum \mathbf{Y}_k$ (vector sum) |
| Resonance condition | $X_L = X_C$ | $B_L = B_C$ |
| Impedance at resonance | $Z_{\min} = R$ | $Z_{\max} = R$ (if losses present) |
| Current at resonance | $I_{\max} = V/R$ | $I_{\min}$ (line current minimum) |
| Phase angle at resonance | $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ | $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ |
| Practical application | Tuning circuits, filters | Power-factor correction, tank circuits |
| Series-to-parallel conversion | $\mathbf{Y} = 1/\mathbf{Z}$ | $\mathbf{Z} = 1/\mathbf{Y}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall**: In a **series** circuit, the *current* is the common reference (drawn horizontally). In a **parallel** circuit, the *voltage* is the common reference. Students often confuse the two, leading to inverted phasor diagrams.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Phasor Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete engineering workflow** for drawing and interpreting a phasor diagram for any two-terminal AC network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given AC Circuit] --> B[Identify Circuit Topology]
    B --> B1{Series or Parallel?}
    B1 -- Series --> C[Choose Current I as Reference]
    B1 -- Parallel --> D[Choose Voltage V as Reference]
    C --> E[Compute Reactances X_L and X_C]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Net Reactance X = X_L minus X_C]
    F --> G[Compute Magnitude of Z]
    G --> H[Compute Phase Angle theta]
    H --> I[Draw Reference Phasor on +X axis]
    I --> J[Draw V_R in phase with I]
    J --> K[Draw V_L leading I by 90 deg]
    K --> L[Draw V_C lagging I by 90 deg]
    L --> M[Vector Sum of V_R V_L V_C]
    M --> N[Resultant V at angle theta]
    N --> O[Compute P, Q, S, and Power Factor]
    O --> P[End: Phasor Diagram Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Flowchart, not a Geometric Drawing?** Mermaid cannot natively draw geometric phasor arrows on a polar grid. The flowchart above captures the *engineering reasoning* behind a phasor diagram, which is what KTU examiners actually test in the 14-mark problem.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Series RL Phasor Relationships

This Mermaid graph shows how voltage phasors in a series RL circuit relate to the current reference.

```mermaid
graph LR
    RefI[Reference: Current I] --> V_R[V_R = IR along +X axis]
    RefI --> V_L[V_L = I times X_L along +Y axis leading by 90 deg]
    V_R --> V_S[V_S = V_R + jV_L hypotenuse]
    V_L --> V_S
    V_S --> Theta[Phase angle theta = tan inverse V_L over V_R]
    V_S --> Mag[Z magnitude = sqrt of V_R squared + V_L squared over I]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Power Triangle and Impedance Triangle Correspondence

The two triangles are *similar* (same angle $\theta$). The power triangle is essentially a scaled version of the impedance triangle, multiplied by $I^2$ on each side.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Imp[Impedance Triangle] --> Z_mag[Hypotenuse Z magnitude ohms]
    Imp --> Z_R[Adjacent R ohms]
    Imp --> Z_X[Opposite X ohms]
    Pwr[Power Triangle] --> S[Hypotenuse S in VA]
    Pwr --> P[Adjacent P in W]
    Pwr --> Q[Opposite Q in VAR]
    Z_mag --> S
    Z_R --> P
    Z_X --> Q
    Imp -- Multiplied by I squared --> Pwr
    Pwr -- Divided by I squared --> Imp
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Leading vs Lagging Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute X = X_L minus X_C] --> B{X greater than 0?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Inductive Net: Current LAGS Voltage]
    B -- No --> D[Capacitive Net: Current LEADS Voltage]
    B -- Equal to 0 --> E[Resonance: Unity Power Factor]
    C --> F[theta positive, cos theta lagging]
    D --> G[theta negative, cos theta leading]
    E --> H[theta equals 0 deg, Z equals R]
```

### 4.5 Phasor Diagram for a Series RLC Circuit (Schematic Description)

For a series RLC circuit where $X_L > X_C$ (inductive dominance), the phasor diagram is constructed as follows:

1. Draw the **reference current $\mathbf{I}$** along the positive real axis.
2. Draw $\mathbf{V}_R = \mathbf{I} R$ collinear with $\mathbf{I}$ (in phase).
3. Draw $\mathbf{V}_L = \mathbf{I} X_L$ along the **positive imaginary axis** (leads $\mathbf{I}$ by $90^{\circ}$).
4. Draw $\mathbf{V}_C = \mathbf{I} X_C$ along the **negative imaginary axis** (lags $\mathbf{I}$ by $90^{\circ}$).
5. The net reactive drop is $\mathbf{V}_X = \mathbf{V}_L - \mathbf{V}_C$ along the positive imaginary axis.
6. The total voltage $\mathbf{V}_S$ is the vector sum: $\mathbf{V}_S = \mathbf{V}_R + j\mathbf{V}_X$, forming the hypotenuse of the voltage triangle.
7. The phase angle $\theta$ between $\mathbf{V}_S$ and $\mathbf{I}$ is the angle whose tangent is $V_X / V_R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textbook Phasor Diagram (Reference)**: $\mathbf{I} \rightarrow$ horizontal right; $\mathbf{V}_R \rightarrow$ horizontal right (same line as $\mathbf{I}$); $\mathbf{V}_L \rightarrow$ vertical up; $\mathbf{V}_C \rightarrow$ vertical down; $\mathbf{V}_S \rightarrow$ diagonal in the first quadrant (lagging power factor).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define a *phasor* and state **two** advantages of representing sinusoidal AC quantities as phasors.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A phasor is a complex-number representation of a sinusoidal waveform, capturing its magnitude and phase angle relative to a reference. (1 mark)
The phasor form of $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ is $\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi$ (or $V_{\text{rms}} \angle \phi$). (1 mark)
**Advantages:** (a) Converts time-domain differential equations into algebraic equations, simplifying analysis. (b) Provides a visual diagram (phasor diagram) to compute resultant quantities via vector addition. (Any two advantages — 1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
A pure capacitor of $C = 31.83\;\mu\text{F}$ is connected across a $230\text{ V}$, $50\text{ Hz}$ supply. Calculate the capacitive reactance and the RMS current.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 31.83 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$X_C = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100\;\Omega \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$
$$I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}}{X_C} = \frac{230}{100} = 2.3\text{ A} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice. Sub-part (a) is typically worth **7 marks** and sub-part (b) **7 marks**. You may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 marks]** For the **series RL circuit** shown (with $R = 60\;\Omega$, $L = 0.2\text{ H}$, $V_{\text{rms}} = 200\text{ V}$, $f = 50\text{ Hz}$):
1. Compute $X_L$ and the magnitude of impedance $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert$.
2. Compute the RMS current $I_{\text{rms}}$ and the phase angle $\theta$.
3. Calculate the active power $P$, reactive power $Q$, and apparent power $S$.
4. Draw the phasor diagram showing $\mathbf{V}_R$, $\mathbf{V}_L$, and $\mathbf{V}_S$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (1) — Reactance and Impedance (2 marks)**
$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 0.2 = 62.83\;\Omega$$
$$\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 62.83^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 3947.5} = \sqrt{7547.5} \approx 86.88\;\Omega$$

**Part (2) — Current and Phase Angle (2 marks)**
$$I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}}{\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert} = \frac{200}{86.88} \approx 2.302\text{ A}$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{62.83}{60}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.0472) \approx 46.32^{\circ}$$

**Part (3) — Power Quantities (1.5 marks)**
$$P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\theta = 200 \times 2.302 \times \cos(46.32^{\circ}) = 200 \times 2.302 \times 0.6907 \approx 318.1\text{ W}$$
$$Q = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \sin\theta = 200 \times 2.302 \times \sin(46.32^{\circ}) = 200 \times 2.302 \times 0.7231 \approx 332.9\text{ VAR}$$
$$S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} = 200 \times 2.302 = 460.4\text{ VA} \quad \text{(or verify: } \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{318.1^2 + 332.9^2} = 460.4\text{ VA}\text{)}$$

**Part (4) — Phasor Diagram (1.5 marks)**
- Draw $\mathbf{I}$ along the positive real axis.
- $\mathbf{V}_R = I R = 2.302 \times 60 = 138.1\text{ V}$ along $\mathbf{I}$.
- $\mathbf{V}_L = I X_L = 2.302 \times 62.83 = 144.6\text{ V}$ along positive imaginary axis.
- $\mathbf{V}_S = \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_L^2} = \sqrt{138.1^2 + 144.6^2} = \sqrt{19072 + 20909} = \sqrt{39981} \approx 200\text{ V}$ (matches supply — sanity check).
- $\theta$ between $\mathbf{V}_S$ and $\mathbf{I}$ is $46.32^{\circ}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the construction of a **phasor diagram for a series RLC circuit** where $X_L > X_C$. State the condition for resonance and the effect on current and impedance at resonance.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction Steps (4 marks):**
1. Choose $\mathbf{I}$ as the reference (horizontal right).
2. Draw $\mathbf{V}_R = \mathbf{I} R$ collinear with $\mathbf{I}$.
3. Draw $\mathbf{V}_L = \mathbf{I} X_L$ along positive imaginary axis ($+90^{\circ}$ from $\mathbf{I}$).
4. Draw $\mathbf{V}_C = \mathbf{I} X_C$ along negative imaginary axis ($-90^{\circ}$ from $\mathbf{I}$).
5. Combine: $\mathbf{V}_L$ and $\mathbf{V}_C$ are antiparallel. The net reactive drop is $\mathbf{V}_X = \mathbf{V}_L - \mathbf{V}_C$ (positive in this case since $X_L > X_C$).
6. Vector sum: $\mathbf{V}_S = \mathbf{V}_R + j\mathbf{V}_X$. This is the hypotenuse at angle $\theta$ above $\mathbf{I}$.
7. The phasor diagram looks like a right triangle with $V_R$ on the base, $V_X$ on the vertical, and $V_S$ as the hypotenuse.

**Resonance Condition (1.5 marks):**
At resonance, $X_L = X_C$, so the net reactance $X = 0$, and $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = R$ (minimum).

**Effect at Resonance (1.5 marks):**
- Impedance is **minimum** ($= R$) and purely resistive.
- Current is **maximum** ($I = V/R$).
- The phase angle $\theta = 0^{\circ}$, so the **power factor is unity**.
- The applied frequency is the **resonant frequency** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- This is the principle behind **tuning circuits** in radio receivers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** A series circuit has $R = 10\;\Omega$, $L = 0.05\text{ H}$, and $C = 100\;\mu\text{F}$, connected to a $230\text{ V}$, $50\text{ Hz}$ supply. Calculate:
1. The inductive and capacitive reactances.
2. The magnitude and phase of the impedance.
3. The RMS current, active power, and reactive power.
4. The resonant frequency of the circuit.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (1) — Reactances (2 marks)**
$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 0.05 = 15.71\;\Omega$$
$$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{0.031416} = 31.83\;\Omega$$

**Part (2) — Impedance (2 marks)**
$$X = X_L - X_C = 15.71 - 31.83 = -16.12\;\Omega \;\;(\text{net capacitive})$$
$$\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + (-16.12)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 259.9} = \sqrt{359.9} \approx 18.97\;\Omega$$
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-16.12}{10}\right) = -58.18^{\circ}\;\;(\text{current leads voltage})$$

**Part (3) — Current and Powers (2 marks)**
$$I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{230}{18.97} \approx 12.13\text{ A}$$
$$P = V I \cos\theta = 230 \times 12.13 \times \cos(-58.18^{\circ}) = 230 \times 12.13 \times 0.5271 \approx 1470.4\text{ W}$$
$$Q = V I \sin\theta = 230 \times 12.13 \times \sin(-58.18^{\circ}) = 230 \times 12.13 \times (-0.8499) \approx -2370.7\text{ VAR}$$
The negative $Q$ confirms **capacitive** behaviour.

**Part (4) — Resonant Frequency (1 mark)**
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times \sqrt{0.05 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}}$$
$$= \frac{1}{6.2832 \times \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 2.2361 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.01405} \approx 71.18\text{ Hz}$$

Since the supply frequency ($50\text{ Hz}$) is **less than** the resonant frequency ($71.18\text{ Hz}$), the circuit is **capacitive** at the operating point — consistent with our negative $\theta$.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the aid of a phasor diagram, derive the relationship between **line voltage and phase voltage in a three-phase star-connected system**, and state the relevant numerical factors for the magnitude ratio and the phase difference.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Let $\mathbf{V}_{RN}$, $\mathbf{V}_{YN}$, $\mathbf{V}_{BN}$ be the three phase voltages (R, Y, B) of equal magnitude $V_{\text{ph}}$, displaced by $120^{\circ}$ in sequence.
- The line voltages are differences: $\mathbf{V}_{RY} = \mathbf{V}_{RN} - \mathbf{V}_{YN}$, $\mathbf{V}_{YB} = \mathbf{V}_{YN} - \mathbf{V}_{BN}$, $\mathbf{V}_{BR} = \mathbf{V}_{BN} - \mathbf{V}_{RN}$.
- Drawing the phasor diagram: the three phase phasors form an equilateral triangle when summed to zero. Each pair-difference phasor forms the *side* of a larger equilateral triangle.

**Magnitude Relationship (2 marks):**
By geometry (or by the parallelogram law of phasor subtraction):
$$V_{\text{line}} = 2 V_{\text{ph}} \cos(30^{\circ}) = 2 V_{\text{ph}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{ph}}$$

**Phase Relationship (2 marks):**
The line voltage $\mathbf{V}_{RY}$ **leads** the phase voltage $\mathbf{V}_{RN}$ by **$30^{\circ}$** (in the RYB positive sequence). Similarly for the other two pairs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to RMS**: KTU questions asking for the "supply current" expect the **RMS** value, not the peak. Dividing by $\sqrt{2}$ after computing the peak loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign error in net reactance**: Writing $X = X_L + X_C$ instead of $X = X_L - X_C$. Always treat $X_C$ as **negative** when computing net reactance.
> 3. **Mixing series and parallel conventions**: In series, *current is common*; in parallel, *voltage is common*. Drawing a parallel-circuit phasor with current as reference will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Wrong reference angle**: If a problem says "voltage is the reference", draw $\mathbf{V}$ on the positive real axis and the current $\mathbf{I}$ at the appropriate lag/lead angle. KTU examiners explicitly check the reference.
> 5. **Missing units**: A phasor of $220\angle 30^{\circ}$ without the unit **V** loses a mark. Always state the units (V, A, $\Omega$, W, VAR, VA).
> 6. **Confusing leading/lagging with the angle sign**: A **positive** $\theta$ (e.g., $+46^{\circ}$) means the **current lags** the voltage (inductive). A **negative** $\theta$ (e.g., $-58^{\circ}$) means the **current leads** the voltage (capacitive). Many students invert this.
> 7. **Skipping the phasor diagram in 14-mark answers**: Even if the numerical answer is correct, KTU awards **1.5 to 2 marks** specifically for a *neat, labeled phasor diagram*. A diagram with missing arrows, missing angles, or missing labels will lose these marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phasor definition**: A complex number $\mathbf{V} = V_{\text{rms}} \angle \phi$ that represents a sinusoid $V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. It rotates counter-clockwise at $\omega$ rad/s.
- **Standard reference**: In a **series** circuit, **current is the reference** (horizontal right). In a **parallel** circuit, **voltage is the reference** (horizontal right).
- **Memory aid for $V/I$ phase in passive elements**: **"ELI the ICE man"** — In a capacitor **I** leads **E** (voltage); In an inductor **E** (voltage) leads **I**.
- **Resistor**: $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{I}$ are **in phase** (angle $0^{\circ}$).
- **Inductor**: $\mathbf{V}$ leads $\mathbf{I}$ by **$90^{\circ}$**; $\mathbf{Z}_L = jX_L$.
- **Capacitor**: $\mathbf{V}$ lags $\mathbf{I}$ by **$90^{\circ}$**; $\mathbf{Z}_C = -jX_C$.
- **Net reactance**: $X = X_L - X_C$. Positive = inductive; negative = capacitive; zero = resonance.
- **Impedance magnitude**: $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$.
- **Phase angle**: $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\bigl[(X_L - X_C)/R\bigr]$.
- **Power factor**: $\cos\theta = R / \vert \mathbf{Z} \vert$. **Lagging** if inductive, **leading** if capacitive, **unity** at resonance.
- **Power triangle**: $P$ (active, W) on real axis, $Q$ (reactive, VAR) on imaginary axis, $S$ (apparent, VA) as hypotenuse.
- **Resonant frequency** (series RLC): $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- **KTU must-show items in 14-mark answers**: (1) Phasor diagram with clear reference, (2) labeled $\theta$, (3) all units, (4) RMS values, (5) sign of $\theta$ explicitly stated (lagging/leading).
- **Indian supply standard**: $f = 50\text{ Hz}$, $V = 230\text{ V}$ (single-phase) / $415\text{ V}$ (three-phase line).
- **Peak to RMS conversion**: divide by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
- **Series vs parallel RLC**: impedance minimum at series resonance; impedance maximum (ideal) at parallel resonance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1: Transformers and DC Machines**, the **Equivalent Circuit** of a single-phase two-winding transformer is a lumped-parameter electrical network consisting of passive elements (resistors and inductors) that electrically replicates the behavior of the actual electromagnetic device at a specific operating frequency (typically **50 Hz** in India).

It mathematically models the copper losses (ohmic heating) of the windings, the leakage fluxes (which do not link both windings), and the core losses (hysteresis + eddy current) that occur in the magnetic core. The circuit allows the entire transformer to be analyzed using simple **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The equivalent circuit is the *fundamental prerequisite* for deriving Voltage Regulation, Efficiency, and for solving problems involving the **Open-Circuit (OC) and Short-Circuit (SC) tests** on transformers. Mastering this topic is compulsory to score in Part B questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Plumbed Water Pipe Model"

Imagine a real transformer as a complex water distribution system:
- The **primary winding** is the inlet pipe. When water (current) flows through it, friction (resistance) reduces the pressure (voltage) and causes a swirling, sluggish flow (leakage reactance).
- The **magnetic core** is the central pumping chamber. Even when no water is drawn from the outlet, a small amount leaks through the chamber's seals (hysteresis + eddy losses → core loss resistance $R_c$) and the pump needs a "kick-start" to build up pressure (magnetizing reactance $X_m$).
- The **secondary winding** is the outlet pipe, again with its own friction and swirl.

The **equivalent circuit** is just a *plumbing schematic on paper* — a simplified blueprint of how much pressure is lost at each pipe and chamber, so we can predict the outlet pressure (secondary voltage) for any inlet pressure and water demand (load current), without building the actual physical system.

> [!NOTE]
> The **physical constants** used throughout this topic are:
> - Frequency: $f = 50$ **Hz** (Indian standard)
> - Angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ **rad/s**
> - Transformation ratio: $K = N_2 / N_1$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need an Equivalent Circuit?

A real transformer has distributed parameters — the leakage flux varies along the winding length, the core loss is non-linear with flux density, and the windings have frequency-dependent resistance (skin effect). Direct mathematical analysis of the magnetic field equations is impractical for engineering design. The equivalent circuit reduces this distributed system to a **lumped, linear, bilateral** network.

### 2.2 Building Blocks of the Equivalent Circuit

A real transformer is decomposed into the following ideal + lossy elements:

1. **Primary Winding Resistance $R_1$ (Ω):** Represents ohmic (copper) loss in the primary winding.
2. **Primary Leakage Reactance $X_1$ (Ω):** Represents the primary flux that links only the primary winding and not the secondary.
3. **No-Load / Shunt Branch ($R_c \parallel jX_m$):** Represents the magnetizing current and the core loss.
   - $R_c$ (Ω) → Core loss resistance: models hysteresis + eddy current loss. Draws an active power component $I_w$.
   - $X_m$ (Ω) → Magnetizing reactance: produces the mutual flux $\phi_m$ in the core. Draws a reactive (wattless) component $I_\mu$.
4. **Secondary Leakage Reactance $X_2$ (Ω)** and **Secondary Resistance $R_2$ (Ω):** Analogous to primary, but on the secondary side.
5. **Ideal Transformer:** A perfect coupling element with turns ratio $K = N_2/N_1$.

### 2.3 Two Variants of the Equivalent Circuit

| Variant | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Exact Equivalent Circuit** | Shunt branch placed at the primary terminals, between $V_1$ and the primary impedance. | Theoretical analysis, all calculations. |
| **Approximate Equivalent Circuit** | Shunt branch moved to either primary or secondary terminals, combining series impedances into a single equivalent $R_{01}, X_{01}$. | Simplified hand calculation of voltage regulation, when load current $I_2 \gg I_0$. |
| **Referred Equivalent Circuit (Referred to Primary or Secondary)** | All secondary quantities multiplied by $K$ or $K^2$ so that the ideal transformer is eliminated. | Universal practice — makes KVL and KCL straightforward. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$ (ideal) | Transformation ratio (Step-down) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_2' = K^2 \, R_2$ | Secondary resistance referred to primary | Ω |
| 3 | $X_2' = K^2 \, X_2$ | Secondary leakage reactance referred to primary | Ω |
| 4 | $V_2' = K \, V_2$ | Secondary voltage referred to primary | V |
| 5 | $I_2' = I_2 / K$ | Secondary current referred to primary | A |
| 6 | $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m \, \text{(max)}$ | EMF equation per turn | V |
| 7 | $R_{01} = R_1 + R_2' = R_1 + K^2 R_2$ | Total equivalent resistance referred to primary | Ω |
| 8 | $X_{01} = X_1 + X_2' = X_1 + K^2 X_2$ | Total equivalent reactance referred to primary | Ω |
| 9 | $\text{VR}\% = \dfrac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi \pm X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$ | Voltage Regulation (approximate) | % |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 \, I_2 \, \cos\phi}{V_2 \, I_2 \, \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}}$ | Efficiency of transformer | % |
| 11 | $P_{cu} = I_2^2 R_{02} = I_1^2 R_{01}$ | Full-load copper loss | W |
| 12 | $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_\mu^2} = \sqrt{(P_i/V_1)^2 + (I_\mu)^2}$ | No-load current | A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention in VR formula:** Use "$+$" for **lagging** power factor (inductive load) and "$-$" for **leading** power factor (capacitive load). For unity power factor, the $\sin\phi$ term vanishes.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer Design Validation:** Manufacturers test prototypes using the equivalent circuit to predict regulation and efficiency before mass production.
- **Power System Analysis:** In load-flow studies of electrical grids, every transformer in the network is represented by its $\pi$-model or T-model equivalent circuit.
- **Protection Coordination:** Short-circuit currents used to set relay ratings are calculated from the equivalent impedance derived from this circuit.
- **Condition Monitoring:** Aging of insulation or winding deformation changes the leakage reactance $X_{01}$ — utilities measure $X_{01}$ using Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) to detect mechanical faults.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Transferring Secondary Quantities to Primary Side

Let us consider a transformer with transformation ratio $K = N_2/N_1$.

**Step 1: Write the secondary EMF equation.**
The secondary winding has $N_2$ turns. The mutual flux $\phi_m$ cutting the secondary induces:

$$
E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m
$$

**Step 2: Write the primary EMF equation.**
Similarly, for the primary winding with $N_1$ turns:

$$
E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m
$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio $E_2/E_1$.**
Dividing Step 1 by Step 2:

$$
\frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m}{4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = K
$$

**Step 4: Express the load current relation.**
For an ideal transformer, the MMF balance requires $N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2$. Therefore:

$$
I_1 = K \, I_2 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I_2' = \frac{I_2}{K}
$$

where $I_2' = I_1$ is the secondary current *referred* to the primary.

**Step 5: Derive the referred secondary voltage.**
The power delivered to the secondary must equal the power transferred through the ideal transformer (lossless assumption):

$$
V_1 \, I_1 \, (\text{ideal}) = V_2 \, I_2
$$

Substituting $I_1 = K I_2$:

$$
V_1 \, (K I_2) = V_2 \, I_2
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{V_2}{K} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2' = K \, V_2
$$

**Step 6: Derive the referred secondary impedance.**
The secondary terminal equation is $V_2 = I_2 Z_2$, where $Z_2 = R_2 + jX_2$. The referred values must satisfy $V_2' = I_2' Z_2'$:

$$
V_2' = I_2' \, Z_2' \quad \Rightarrow \quad K V_2 = \frac{I_2}{K} \, Z_2'
$$

Substituting $V_2 = I_2 Z_2$:

$$
K \, I_2 \, Z_2 = \frac{I_2}{K} \, Z_2' \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_2' = K^2 \, Z_2
$$

Since $Z_2 = R_2 + jX_2$, we get **$R_2' = K^2 R_2$** and **$X_2' = K^2 X_2$**.

> [!NOTE]
> This $K^2$ factor for impedance is a **KTU-favorite** result. Memorize it — it appears in nearly every Part B problem involving equivalent circuit calculations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Regulation (Approximate Method)

For a transformer delivering load current $I_2$ at power factor angle $\phi$:

**Step 1: Write the phasor secondary equation.**

$$
V_2 = V_1' - I_2(R_2 + jX_2)
$$

(where $V_1'$ is the primary voltage referred to the secondary side).

**Step 2: Construct the phasor diagram with $V_2$ as reference.**

Let $V_2 = V_2 \angle 0$, $I_2 = I_2 \angle -\phi$ (lagging pf). The voltage drop across the secondary impedance is $I_2 Z_2$, which is decomposed into:

- $I_2 R_2$ along $I_2$ (in-phase component)
- $I_2 X_2$ leading $I_2$ by 90°

**Step 3: Geometric projection onto the horizontal axis.**
The magnitude of $V_1'$ along the reference axis (approx.) is:

$$
\vert V_1' \vert \approx V_2 + I_2 R_2 \cos\phi + I_2 X_2 \sin\phi
$$

(The $\sin\phi$ component for lagging pf adds because $I_2 X_2$ has a horizontal projection in the same direction as $V_2$.)

**Step 4: Substitute into the regulation formula.**

$$
\text{VR} = \frac{\vert V_1' \vert - V_2}{V_2} = \frac{I_2 (R_2 \cos\phi + X_2 \sin\phi)}{V_2}
$$

In percentage:

$$
\text{VR}\% = \frac{I_2 (R_2 \cos\phi + X_2 \sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation assumes $I_2 R_2$ and $I_2 X_2$ are small compared to $V_2$ (typically less than 5%). For greater accuracy, the **exact VR** formula (using the full square root expression) must be used — but KTU typically tests the approximate form.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Open-Circuit vs. Short-Circuit Test

| Parameter | Open-Circuit (OC) Test | Short-Circuit (SC) Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Where performed** | LV side open, instrumented on HV (or whichever is convenient) | LV side shorted, instrumented on HV |
| **Voltage applied** | Rated voltage $V_1$ | Reduced voltage $V_{sc}$ (≈ 5–10% of rated) |
| **Measured quantities** | No-load current $I_0$, core loss $P_i = W_{oc}$ | Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$, copper loss $P_{cu} = W_{sc}$ |
| **What it yields** | $R_c$, $X_m$ (from shunt branch) | $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$ (from equivalent series impedance) |
| **Why low voltage for SC** | To avoid excessive current that would burn the windings | Rated current must flow with minimal applied voltage (Z is small) |
| **Power factor at test** | Very low, lagging (≈ 0.2 to 0.3) | Closer to 0 (almost pure leakage) |
| **Losses determined** | Core (iron) loss | Full-load copper loss |
