### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Finding $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ from SC Test Data

Given: SC test on HV side gives $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{sc}$ (wattmeter reading).

**Step 1: Equivalent impedance magnitude.**

$$
Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}
$$

**Step 2: Equivalent resistance from copper loss.**

$$
P_{sc} = I_{sc}^2 \, R_{01} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}
$$

**Step 3: Equivalent reactance from impedance triangle.**

$$
X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}
$$

**Step 4: (If test was on HV but referred to LV side):**

$$
R_{01,LV} = \frac{R_{01,HV}}{K^2}, \quad X_{01,LV} = \frac{X_{01,HV}}{K^2}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Exact Equivalent Circuit of a Single-Phase Transformer (Referred to Primary)

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1["Primary Voltage V1"] --> A1["Node A"]
    A1 --> R1["R1 (Primary Resistance)"]
    R1 --> B1["Node B"]
    B1 --> X1["jX1 (Primary Leakage Reactance)"]
    X1 --> C1["Node C"]
    C1 --> Rc["Rc (Core Loss Resistance)"]
    Rc --> D1["Node D Reference"]
    C1 --> Xm["jXm (Magnetizing Reactance)"]
    Xm --> D1
    C1 --> E1["jE1 (Induced EMF)"]
    E1 --> R2p["R2 prime Referred Secondary R"]
    R2p --> X2p["jX2 prime Referred Secondary X"]
    X2p --> V2p["V2 prime Referred Load Voltage"]
    V2p --> ZL["ZL Load Impedance"]
    ZL --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Power enters at $V_1$, drops across $R_1 + jX_1$ (primary copper + leakage), then splits at node C — one branch goes through the shunt ($R_c \parallel jX_m$) to ground (representing the magnetizing/core-loss current), and the main branch flows through the induced EMF, the referred secondary impedance $R_2' + jX_2'$, and finally into the load $Z_L$. The ideal transformer is **embedded** between primary and secondary; the circuit as drawn has *already* been referred to the primary side (so the ideal transformer is implicitly absent).

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Information Flow in the Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
    src["Input Source V1 I1"] --> primBlock["Primary Winding Block R1 + jX1"]
    primBlock --> coreBlock["Magnetic Core Block Rc parallel jXm"]
    coreBlock --> inducedEMF["Mutual Coupling jE1 approx jE2 prime"]
    inducedEMF --> secBlock["Secondary Winding Block R2 prime + jX2 prime"]
    secBlock --> loadBlock["Load Block ZL V2 I2 cos phi"]
    loadBlock --> feedback["Back EMF and Regulation Computation"]
    feedback --> primBlock
    coreBlock --> coreLoss["Core Loss Pi Watts"]
    primBlock --> cuLossP["Cu Loss Pcu Primary"]
    secBlock --> cuLossS["Cu Loss Pcu Secondary"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The arrows in this block diagram are not literal wires but **functional signal/power flow paths**. The "feedback" loop signifies that the equivalent circuit is solved *simultaneously* — primary quantities depend on secondary quantities and vice-versa, which is why we use a numerical or phasor solver.

### 4.3 Approximate Equivalent Circuit (Referred to Primary)

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1["V1 Primary Terminals"] --> Req["R01 = R1 + R2 prime Total Resistance"]
    Req --> Xeq["X01 = X1 + X2 prime Total Reactance"]
    Xeq --> shuntA["Shunt Branch Moved to Left"]
    shuntA --> Rc["Rc Core Loss"]
    Rc --> gnd1["Reference Node"]
    shuntA --> Xm["jXm Magnetizing"]
    Xm --> gnd1
    Xeq --> V2p["V2 prime Referred Load Terminal"]
    V2p --> ZL["ZL Load"]
    ZL --> gnd1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why move the shunt branch?** When load current $I_2$ is much greater than no-load current $I_0$ (which is true at full-load and beyond), the voltage drop across $R_1$ and $jX_1$ due to $I_0$ is negligible. So we can move the shunt to the input terminals without significant error. This is the **approximate equivalent circuit** — it is the one used in nearly all KTU numerical problems for voltage regulation and efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** What is the purpose of the equivalent circuit of a transformer?

**Model Answer:**
The equivalent circuit of a transformer is a simplified electrical representation that models the actual transformer using lumped circuit elements: resistances for copper and core losses, and reactances for leakage and magnetizing fluxes. Its purpose is to:

1. Enable analysis of transformer performance (regulation, efficiency) using simple KVL and KCL instead of complex magnetic field equations.
2. Predict the behaviour of the transformer under varying load and power factor conditions.
3. Provide standardized parameters ($R_{01}$, $X_{01}$, $R_c$, $X_m$) for power system studies and protection design.
4. Facilitate the design of transformers through the open-circuit and short-circuit tests, whose results populate the equivalent circuit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only *"to find voltage regulation and efficiency"* — that's a consequence, not the purpose. Always state the *modelling* role and the *analytical convenience* it provides.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between approximate and exact equivalent circuit of a transformer. When is the approximate one preferred?

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Exact Equivalent Circuit | Approximate Equivalent Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Shunt branch position | Between primary terminals and the equivalent primary impedance | Moved to either the primary or secondary terminals |
| Accuracy | High — represents actual voltage-current relationships faithfully | Lower — assumes load current $\gg$ no-load current |
| Mathematical complexity | More involved (needs two loops solved simultaneously) | Simple — single loop with $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$ |
| When preferred | Used in precise design and simulation, at low loads where $I_0$ is significant | Preferred in KTU board exams and engineering hand-calculations at full-load and above |

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 1, Q1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A single-phase, 5 kVA, 200 V/400 V, 50 Hz transformer has the following test data:
- **OC test (on LV side):** 200 V, 1.2 A, 60 W
- **SC test (on HV side):** 20 V, 12.5 A, 100 W

Determine the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the full-load voltage regulation at **0.8 power factor lagging** using the approximate equivalent circuit. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Step 1 — Identify the test sides and find the transformation ratio.**

The transformer is step-up (LV = 200 V, HV = 400 V). OC test is on LV; SC test is on HV. Transformation ratio:

$$
K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_{HV}}{V_{LV}} = \frac{400}{200} = 2
$$

(Subscript 1 = LV, subscript 2 = HV for this analysis.)

**Step 2 — Derive shunt branch parameters from OC test.**

OC test gives $V_{oc} = 200$ V, $I_0 = 1.2$ A, $P_i = 60$ W.

The no-load power factor:

$$
\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_i}{V_{oc} \cdot I_0} = \frac{60}{200 \times 1.2} = \frac{60}{240} = 0.25
$$

The working (core-loss) component of no-load current:

$$
I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 1.2 \times 0.25 = 0.3 \text{ A}
$$

The magnetizing component:

$$
I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 1.2 \times \sin(\cos^{-1} 0.25) = 1.2 \times 0.9682 = 1.1619 \text{ A}
$$

(Or, using $I_\mu = \sqrt{I_0^2 - I_w^2} = \sqrt{1.44 - 0.09} = \sqrt{1.35} = 1.1619$ A.)

Core-loss resistance referred to LV:

$$
R_c = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_w} = \frac{200}{0.3} = 666.67 \text{ Ω}
$$

Magnetizing reactance referred to LV:

$$
X_m = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_\mu} = \frac{200}{1.1619} = 172.13 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3 — Refer the shunt branch to HV side using $K^2$ factor.**

$$
R_c' = K^2 R_c = 2^2 \times 666.67 = 2666.67 \text{ Ω}
$$

$$
X_m' = K^2 X_m = 2^2 \times 172.13 = 688.52 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive series parameters from SC test.**

SC test on HV side: $V_{sc} = 20$ V, $I_{sc} = 12.5$ A, $P_{cu} = 100$ W. Since SC test is performed on the HV side directly, the equivalent impedance is already referred to HV.

Equivalent impedance:

$$
Z_{01,HV} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{20}{12.5} = 1.6 \text{ Ω}
$$

Equivalent resistance:

$$
R_{01,HV} = \frac{P_{cu}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{100}{(12.5)^2} = \frac{100}{156.25} = 0.64 \text{ Ω}
$$

Equivalent reactance:

$$
X_{01,HV} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = \sqrt{(1.6)^2 - (0.64)^2} = \sqrt{2.56 - 0.4096} = \sqrt{2.1504} \approx 1.4665 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Final Result (Part a):**

| Parameter | Value (Referred to HV) |
|---|---|
| $R_{01}$ | **0.64 Ω** |
| $X_{01}$ | **1.4665 Ω** |
| $R_c'$ | **2666.67 Ω** |
| $X_m'$ | **688.52 Ω** |

---

**Part (b): Voltage Regulation at 0.8 pf lagging.**

Full-load secondary current on HV side:

$$
I_2 = \frac{S_{rated}}{V_2} = \frac{5000}{400} = 12.5 \text{ A}
$$

(Confirms the SC test was performed at full-load current.)

Using the approximate VR formula for **lagging** power factor:

$$
\text{VR} = \frac{I_2 (R_{01} \cos\phi + X_{01} \sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100
$$

With $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$:

$$
\text{VR} = \frac{12.5 \times (0.64 \times 0.8 + 1.4665 \times 0.6)}{400} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{12.5 \times (0.512 + 0.8799)}{400} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{12.5 \times 1.3919}{400} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{17.3988}{400} \times 100 = 4.35\%
$$

**Final Answer (Part b): Voltage Regulation = 4.35%**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** 
> 1. Do **NOT** mix up the SC test side — if the SC test is on HV, the calculated $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$ are already referred to HV. No further $K^2$ multiplication is needed for them.
> 2. For **lagging** pf, the formula uses the **"$+$"** sign; for **leading** pf, use **"$-$"**. This sign convention is the single most common source of full-mark loss in KTU valuation.
> 3. Always state the **value AND unit** for each equivalent circuit parameter. Writing "$R_{01} = 0.64$" without the unit "Ω" loses a half-mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the exact equivalent circuit of a single-phase transformer referred to the primary side. Clearly mark all parameters. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 10 kVA, 2000 V/200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has $R_1 = 5.5$ Ω, $R_2 = 0.045$ Ω, $X_1 = 7.5$ Ω, $X_2 = 0.06$ Ω. Calculate the equivalent resistance and reactance referred to **(i) primary** and **(ii) secondary**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.1 and Section 4.3 of these notes. The exact equivalent circuit consists of:*

- *Primary series branch: $R_1$ and $jX_1$*
- *Shunt (no-load) branch: $R_c$ in parallel with $jX_m$*
- *Secondary series branch (referred to primary): $R_2' = K^2 R_2$ and $jX_2' = K^2 jX_2$*
- *Load terminals $V_2'$ and load current $I_2'$*

*The student should describe each element's physical meaning, the direction of currents, and the position of the shunt branch with respect to the primary series impedance.)*

**Part (b): Step 1 — Compute the transformation ratio.**

$$
K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{200}{2000} = 0.1
$$

**Step 2 — Referred to primary.**

$$
R_{01} = R_1 + R_2' = R_1 + K^2 R_2 = 5.5 + (0.1)^2 \times 0.045
$$

$$
= 5.5 + 0.01 \times 0.045 = 5.5 + 0.00045 = 5.50045 \text{ Ω} \approx 5.5 \text{ Ω}
$$

$$
X_{01} = X_1 + X_2' = X_1 + K^2 X_2 = 7.5 + (0.1)^2 \times 0.06
$$

$$
= 7.5 + 0.0006 = 7.5006 \text{ Ω} \approx 7.5 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3 — Referred to secondary.**

To refer primary quantities to secondary, multiply by $1/K^2 = (N_1/N_2)^2 = 100$:

$$
R_{02} = \frac{R_1}{K^2} + R_2 = \frac{5.5}{0.01} + 0.045 = 550 + 0.045 = 550.045 \text{ Ω} \approx 550 \text{ Ω}
$$

$$
X_{02} = \frac{X_1}{K^2} + X_2 = \frac{7.5}{0.01} + 0.06 = 750 + 0.06 = 750.06 \text{ Ω} \approx 750 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Final Result (Part b):**

| Parameter | Referred to Primary | Referred to Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| $R_0$ | **5.5 Ω** | **550.045 Ω** |
| $X_0$ | **7.5006 Ω** | **750.06 Ω** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The referred values should scale by $K^2$ when moving from one side to the other: $550.045 / 5.5 \approx 100 = 1/K^2$. ✓

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Definition:** The equivalent circuit is a **lumped-parameter model** that represents copper losses, leakage fluxes, and core losses using resistors and reactances.
- 🔑 **Two Variants:** **Exact** (shunt branch at primary terminals) and **Approximate** (shunt branch moved to input/output terminals) — use the approximate one for hand calculations.
- 🔑 **Referring rule:** Voltage $\times K$, Current $\div K$, **Impedance $\times K^2$**. This $K^2$ is non-negotiable.
- 🔑 **EMF Equation:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_m$ — used to derive $K = E_2/E_1 = N_2/N_1$.
- 🔑 **OC test** → gives $R_c$ and $X_m$ (the shunt branch). **SC test** → gives $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ (the series branch).
- 🔑 **Voltage Regulation** formula has a "$+$" for lagging pf and "$-$" for leading pf — this is the #1 mark-loss trap in KTU valuation.
- 🔑 **Efficiency** = Output / (Output + Core Loss + Copper Loss) — for maximum efficiency, $P_i = P_{cu}$ (this gives the load fraction $x = \sqrt{P_i/P_{cu,FL}}$).
- 🔑 **SC test is done at low voltage** so that rated current flows without exceeding thermal limits. **OC test is done at rated voltage** to simulate actual operating flux.
- 🔑 **No-load current is small (2–5% of full-load)** and lags the voltage by a large angle (low pf ≈ 0.2–0.3).
- 🔑 **Units are mandatory** — every $R$, $X$, $V$, $I$ value must be quoted with its unit, or risk losing 0.5 marks per occurrence in valuation.
- 🔑 **Mermaid diagrams** in the notes show the architecture — in the exam, you must draw the actual transformer equivalent circuit (not block diagrams) to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Voltage Regulation** of a transformer is defined as the change in the magnitude of the secondary terminal voltage when the load is removed (from full-load to no-load), expressed as a percentage of the full-load secondary terminal voltage, while keeping the primary voltage and frequency constant at their rated values. Mathematically, it quantifies the *stiffness* of the transformer's output voltage under varying load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Voltage regulation is the ratio of the difference between no-load secondary voltage ($V_{20}$) and full-load secondary voltage ($V_2$) to the full-load secondary voltage ($V_2$), expressed as a percentage. It is measured at a constant **primary supply voltage**, constant **frequency**, and a specified **power factor**.

$$\text{Voltage Regulation (VR \%)} = \frac{V_{20} - V_{2}}{V_{2}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $V_{20}$ = Secondary terminal voltage at **no-load**
- $V_2$ = Secondary terminal voltage at **full-load** (with rated load connected)
- The difference $V_{20} - V_2$ is referred to as the **Voltage Drop** ($I \cdot Z$) of the transformer referred to the secondary side.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **municipal water pipeline** connected to a large overhead tank (this is your **transformer's primary supply** — a stiff, infinite bus).

- When **no taps** are open (no-load), water rushes out of the hose at **full pressure** ($V_{20}$).
- When you open **10 taps simultaneously** (full-load), the pressure at each tap **drops noticeably** because the pipe has internal friction and constrictions ($I^2R$ losses and leakage reactance).
- The *percentage drop* in pressure from no-flow to full-flow is the pipeline's "**Pressure Regulation**."

A transformer behaves identically. The internal **resistance ($R$)** and **leakage reactance ($X$)** of the windings cause the secondary voltage to "sag" when current flows. A *good* transformer (low regulation %) acts like a *very large diameter pipe* — its voltage stays nearly constant regardless of load. A *poor* transformer (high regulation %) acts like a *narrow pipe* — voltage sags significantly when load is applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this?** Voltage regulation is the **single most important performance metric** for a power transformer in a distribution network. A poor regulation means lights flicker when a heavy motor starts nearby, and sensitive electronics malfunction. KTU examiners repeatedly test this topic because it bridges **circuit theory (phasors)** with **practical power systems**.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The following values are **industry-standard benchmarks** as per **BIS IS 1180 / IS 2026** (the standards KTU expects you to cite):

- **Ideal Transformer:** Regulation = **0%** (no internal impedance, impossible in reality).
- **Well-designed power transformer:** Regulation = **2% to 5%**.
- **Typical distribution transformer (11 kV / 415 V):** Regulation ≈ **5%** at 0.8 power factor lagging.
- **Poorly designed / small transformers:** Regulation can exceed **10%**, causing unacceptable voltage flicker.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Voltage Drop

The secondary winding of a transformer is not a perfect conductor. It contains:
1. **Ohmic Resistance ($R_2$):** Winding copper wire resistance → causes **resistive voltage drop** $IR$ in phase with current.
2. **Leakage Reactance ($X_2$):** Due to magnetic flux that links one winding but not the other → causes **reactive voltage drop** $IX$ in quadrature (90° lead) with current.

When the equivalent impedance is referred to the secondary side as $Z_{02} = R_{02} + jX_{02}$, the secondary terminal voltage is given by Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$V_2 = V_{20} - I_2 \cdot Z_{02}$$

The drop $I_2 Z_{02}$ is a **phasor** (complex number), not a scalar. Its projection along the $V_2$ phasor direction determines the regulation. This is why the **power factor of the load** (angle $\phi$) is critical.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Refer all quantities to the secondary side** (or refer to primary — your choice, but stick to one).
2. **Assume $V_2$ is the reference phasor** along the positive x-axis.
3. **Draw current $I_2$ lagging $V_2$ by angle $\phi$** (lagging PF) or leading by $\phi$ (leading PF).
4. **Construct the phasor $I_2 R$** parallel to $I_2$, and $I_2 X$ perpendicular to $I_2$ (leading for lagging PF).
5. **Apply KVL:** $\vec{V_{20}} = \vec{V_2} + \vec{I_2 R} + j\vec{I_2 X}$.
6. **Take the projection of the total drop along $V_2$** to get the in-phase component.
7. **Neglect the small perpendicular component** (since $|I Z| \ll V_2$, the approximation error is negligible — KTU examiners accept this).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Condition / Power Factor | Approximate Voltage Regulation Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Definition** | $\text{VR\%} = \dfrac{V_{20} - V_{2}}{V_{2}} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | **Lagging Power Factor** ($\cos\phi$) | $\text{VR\%} = \dfrac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | **Leading Power Factor** ($\cos\phi$) | $\text{VR\%} = \dfrac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi - X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | **Unity Power Factor** ($\phi = 0$) | $\text{VR\%} = \dfrac{I_2 R_{02}}{V_2} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | **Zero Lagging PF** (purely inductive, $\phi = 90^\circ$) | $\text{VR\%} = \dfrac{I_2 X_{02}}{V_2} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | **Zero Leading PF** (purely capacitive, $\phi = -90^\circ$) | $\text{VR\%} = -\dfrac{I_2 X_{02}}{V_2} \times 100$ (voltage **rises**) | % |
| 7 | **Approximation validity** | Valid only when $I_2 Z_{02} \ll V_2$ (always true for power transformers) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Notice in row #6 that for a **highly leading power factor** load, regulation becomes **negative**. This means $V_2 > V_{20}$! This phenomenon is called the **Ferranti Effect on load side** and is exploited in **underground cable feeders** where capacitance dominates.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distribution Transformer Sizing:** Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) specifies regulation ≤ **5%** at 0.8 PF lag for transformers serving Wayanad's rural agricultural pumpset loads.
- **Voltage Stabilizer Design:** A stabilizer downstream of a high-regulation transformer must compensate for a larger voltage window (±10% instead of ±2%).
- **Generator Step-Up Transformers (GSUs)** in power stations are designed for **near-zero regulation** because they feed an infinite bus (the grid absorbs all fluctuations).
- **Parallel Operation of Transformers:** Two transformers with **mismatched regulation** will share load unequally. KTU often tests this in Module 2/3 questions.
- **Tap Changers** in substation transformers are used to **manually correct** the voltage drop caused by poor regulation across varying load hours.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Phasor Diagram Setup (Lagging Power Factor)

We refer the entire transformer's equivalent circuit to the secondary side. Let:
- $\vec{V_2} = V_2 \angle 0^\circ$ (reference, along positive x-axis).
- $\vec{I_2} = I_2 \angle -\phi$ (current **lags** voltage by $\phi$ for lagging PF load).
- $\vec{Z_{02}} = R_{02} + jX_{02}$ (total equivalent impedance referred to secondary).

Applying KVL on the secondary loop (assuming primary applied voltage is constant):

$$\vec{V_{20}} = \vec{V_2} + \vec{I_2} \vec{Z_{02}}$$

The voltage drop across the equivalent impedance is:

$$\vec{I_2 Z_{02}} = I_2(R_{02} + jX_{02})(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

Multiplying out:

$$\vec{I_2 Z_{02}} = I_2[R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi] + j I_2[X_{02}\cos\phi - R_{02}\sin\phi]$$

The **in-phase component** (real part) along the $V_2$ phasor direction determines the regulation magnitude:

$$\text{Real Part} = I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)$$

The **quadrature component** (imaginary part) is perpendicular to $V_2$ and only contributes a tiny phase shift — neglected in the standard approximation:

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation assumes the voltage drop $I_2 Z_{02}$ is **very small** compared to $V_2$ (typically less than 5%). Under this approximation, the perpendicular component has negligible effect on the magnitude of $V_{20}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 (Lagging PF)

**Problem Statement:** A 5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V single-phase transformer has the following parameters referred to the high-voltage (HV) side: $R_{02} = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $X_{02} = 1.2 \;\Omega$. The transformer delivers full-load at **0.8 power factor lagging**. Calculate the percentage voltage regulation referred to the HV side.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the full-load secondary current on the HV side.

$$I_2 = \frac{\text{kVA Rating}}{V_2} = \frac{5000}{400} = 12.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the power factor angle.

$$\cos\phi = 0.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87^\circ$$
$$\sin\phi = 0.6$$

**Step 3:** Apply the lagging PF regulation formula.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{12.5 \times (0.5 \times 0.8 + 1.2 \times 0.6)}{400} \times 100$$

**Step 5:** Simplify inside the parentheses.

$$0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.40$$
$$1.2 \times 0.6 = 0.72$$
$$\text{Sum} = 0.40 + 0.72 = 1.12 \;\Omega$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $I_2$.

$$12.5 \times 1.12 = 14.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7:** Final percentage calculation.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{14.0}{400} \times 100 = 3.5\%$$

**Answer:** Voltage regulation = **3.5% (lagging)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of approximation:** Drop = 14 V, $V_2$ = 400 V. Ratio = 14/400 = 0.035 → **3.5%**, which is well below 10%. The approximation is valid. KTU examiners will accept this.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 (Leading PF)

**Problem Statement:** For the same transformer as Example #1, find the regulation when the load is **0.8 power factor leading**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Same parameters: $I_2 = 12.5$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$.

**Step 2:** Apply the **leading PF** regulation formula.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi - X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{12.5 \times (0.5 \times 0.8 - 1.2 \times 0.6)}{400} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$0.40 - 0.72 = -0.32 \;\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Compute.

$$12.5 \times (-0.32) = -4.0 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{-4.0}{400} \times 100 = -1.0\%$$

**Answer:** Regulation = **−1.0% (leading)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that the full-load voltage $V_2$ is actually **higher** than the no-load voltage $V_{20}$. This is a hallmark of **leading power factor** loads (e.g., synchronous motor overexcited, lightly loaded underground cables).

## 3.4 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix

| Load Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | $\sin\phi$ | Sign of VR | Practical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unity | 1.0 | 0.0 | **Positive** | Only resistive drop; small VR |
| 0.8 Lag | 0.8 | 0.6 | **Positive (largest)** | Worst case for typical industrial loads |
| 0.5 Lag | 0.5 | 0.866 | **Positive** | Heavy inductive (motor starting) |
| Zero Lag (pure ind.) | 0.0 | 1.0 | **Positive (max)** | Purely reactive drop; VR = IX/V |
| 0.8 Lead | 0.8 | 0.6 | **Negative** | Capacitor banks raise terminal voltage |
| Zero Lead (pure cap.) | 0.0 | 1.0 | **Negative (max mag.)** | Ferranti effect on load side |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phasor Diagram for Voltage Regulation (Lagging PF)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **vector relationships** of the secondary-side phasors during full-load operation with a lagging power factor load.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["V2 secondary terminal voltage phasor along X axis"]
    nodeB["IR drop phasor parallel to I2 lagging V2"]
    nodeC["IX drop phasor perpendicular to I2 leading by 90 degrees"]
    nodeD["V20 no load EMF phasor resultant sum"]
    nodeE["Load current I2 lagging V2 by angle phi"]
    nodeF["V2 minus V20 vertical gap represents regulation"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeA -.-> nodeF
    nodeD -.-> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagonal phasor **V₂₀** is the vector sum of $V_2$, $IR$, and $IX$. The difference $|V_{20}| - |V_2|$ (the algebraic gap along the reference axis) is what defines the regulation.

## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit (Referred to Secondary)

The following block diagram maps the **functional flow** of voltage drops across the transformer referred entirely to the secondary winding.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["V20 Source EMF referred to secondary"] --> B["Equivalent Resistance R02"]
    B --> C["Equivalent Reactance X02"]
    C --> D["Load Terminal V2"]
    D --> E["Full Load Current I2 at angle phi"]

    A --> A1["Primary voltage held constant"]
    E --> F["Power Factor Cos phi lagging"]
    F --> D

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Regulation Behavior vs Power Factor — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Voltage Regulation VR"] --> PFType{"Power Factor Type"}

    PFType -->|"Lagging 0 to 1"| LagPath["VR = I x R cos phi + I x X sin phi divided by V2 x 100"]
    PFType -->|"Unity"| UnityPath["VR = I x R divided by V2 x 100"]
    PFType -->|"Leading 0 to 1"| LeadPath["VR = I x R cos phi minus I x X sin phi divided by V2 x 100"]

    LagPath --> LagOut["Positive VR secondary voltage drops on load"]
    UnityPath --> UnityOut["Small positive VR due to winding resistance only"]
    LeadPath --> LeadOut["Can be negative if X sin phi exceeds R cos phi"]
```

## 4.4 Real-World Transformer Loading Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source["Primary Grid 11 kV"] --> TX["Distribution Transformer"]
    TX -->|"Equivalent R02 X02"| Bus["LV Bus 415 V"]
    Bus --> Load1["Lighting Load Unity PF"]
    Bus --> Load2["Motor Load 0.8 Lag"]
    Bus --> Load3["Capacitor Bank Leading PF"]

    Load2 -.->|"Worst VR"| RegOut["Voltage drops by 5 percent"]
    Load3 -.->|"Best VR"| RegOut2["Voltage may rise slightly"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** This is exactly why KSEB installs **Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) panels** at industrial consumer premises in Kerala — to keep the load PF close to unity, thereby keeping voltage regulation small and avoiding tap-changer operations at substations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define voltage regulation of a transformer. Why is it an important performance parameter in power systems?**

**Model Answer:**

Voltage regulation of a transformer is defined as the change in the secondary terminal voltage from **no-load to full-load**, expressed as a percentage of the full-load voltage, with the primary voltage and frequency held constant.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{V_{20} - V_2}{V_2} \times 100$$

It is an important performance parameter because:
1. It quantifies the ability of the transformer to deliver **constant voltage** to consumers under varying load.
2. **High regulation** means voltage fluctuation at consumer premises, causing malfunction of sensitive equipment.
3. It is essential for selecting **proper transformer ratings** in distribution networks (KSEB standard: ≤ 5% at 0.8 PF lag).
4. It affects the **voltage profile** of the entire power distribution network.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: A transformer's regulation at unity power factor is 2% and at 0.8 power factor lagging it is 5%. Comment on the relative contributions of resistance and leakage reactance.**

**Model Answer:**

At **unity power factor** ($\phi = 0^\circ$), regulation is purely due to resistance:

$$\text{VR}_{\text{unity}} = \frac{I R_{02}}{V_2} \times 100 = 2\%$$

At **0.8 lagging PF**, regulation is:

$$\text{VR}_{0.8\text{lag}} = \frac{I(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100 = 5\%$$

Substituting $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$:

$$\frac{I(0.8 R_{02} + 0.6 X_{02})}{V_2} \times 100 = 5\%$$

From unity PF: $I R_{02}/V_2 \times 100 = 2\%$.

Therefore:
- Resistive component contributes: $0.8 \times 2\% = 1.6\%$
- Reactive component contributes: $0.6 \times X_{02}$ term → remaining $5 - 1.6 = 3.4\%$
- Hence **leakage reactance $X_{02}$ is the dominant contributor** (≈ 3.4/5 ≈ 68% of total regulation).

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**A single-phase 10 kVA, 1000 V / 200 V transformer has the following parameters referred to the LV side:**
- Equivalent resistance: $R_{02} = 0.15 \;\Omega$
- Equivalent leakage reactance: $X_{02} = 0.25 \;\Omega$

**(a)** Calculate the percentage voltage regulation when the transformer is supplying full-load at **0.85 power factor lagging**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the percentage voltage regulation at the same full-load current but at **0.85 power factor leading**. Comment on the result. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute full-load current on LV side.

$$I_2 = \frac{10000}{200} = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Identify PF angles.

$$\cos\phi = 0.85 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{0.2775} \approx 0.5268$$

**Step 3:** Apply lagging PF regulation formula.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{50 \times (0.15 \times 0.85 + 0.25 \times 0.5268)}{200} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{50 \times (0.1275 + 0.1317)}{200} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{50 \times 0.2592}{200} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{12.96}{200} \times 100 = 6.48\%$$

**Answer (a):** Voltage regulation ≈ **6.48% at 0.85 PF lagging**.

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1:** Use the leading PF regulation formula.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi - X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the same numerical values.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{50 \times (0.1275 - 0.1317)}{200} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{50 \times (-0.0042)}{200} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{-0.21}{200} \times 100 = -0.105\%$$

**Answer (b):** Voltage regulation ≈ **−0.105% at 0.85 PF leading**.

**Comment:** The regulation is **negative**, indicating that the full-load secondary voltage is **higher** than the no-load voltage. This phenomenon occurs because with a leading PF load, the reactive drop across $X_{02}$ *opposes* the resistive drop, and since $X_{02}\sin\phi > R_{02}\cos\phi$ in this case, the net effect is a **voltage rise** on loading. This is the same physics as the **Ferranti Effect**, observed in lightly loaded transmission lines.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a phasor diagram, derive the approximate expression for the voltage regulation of a transformer supplying a **lagging power factor** load. State clearly the assumptions made. (8 Marks)

**(b)** A 100 kVA, 3300 V / 240 V single-phase transformer has an equivalent impedance of $(0.4 + j1.0)\;\Omega$ referred to the LV side. Determine its full-load regulation at:
- (i) 0.8 PF lagging (2 Marks)
- (ii) Unity PF (2 Marks)
- (iii) 0.6 PF leading (2 Marks)

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (8 Marks):

**Assumption:** The voltage drop $I_2 Z_{02}$ is small compared to $V_2$ (less than ~10%), so the perpendicular component on the phasor diagram can be neglected.

**Refer the circuit to the secondary side.** Let $\vec{V_2} = V_2 \angle 0^\circ$ be the reference phasor. For lagging PF load, the current $\vec{I_2} = I_2 \angle -\phi$, where $\phi$ is the load impedance angle.

By KVL:

$$\vec{V_{20}} = \vec{V_2} + \vec{I_2}\vec{Z_{02}}$$

Expanding $\vec{I_2 Z_{02}}$:

$$\vec{I_2}(R_{02} + jX_{02}) = I_2(R_{02} + jX_{02})(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

Separating real and imaginary parts:

$$\vec{I_2 Z_{02}} = I_2[(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi) + j(X_{02}\cos\phi - R_{02}\sin\phi)]$$

The **magnitude of the in-phase drop** is:

$$\Delta V = I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)$$

The quadrature component is neglected under the small-drop assumption. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{VR\%} = \frac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_2} \times 100}$$

The phasor diagram (already illustrated in Section 4.1) shows $V_{20}$ as the diagonal sum of $V_2$, $I R$, and $I X$, with the regulation being the horizontal gap between $|V_2|$ and $|V_{20}|$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations:

**Given:** $S = 100$ kVA, $V_2 = 240$ V, $R_{02} = 0.4\;\Omega$, $X_{02} = 1.0\;\Omega$.

**Compute full-load LV current:**

$$I_2 = \frac{100{,}000}{240} = 416.67 \text{ A}$$

**(i) 0.8 PF Lagging:** $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{416.67 \times (0.4 \times 0.8 + 1.0 \times 0.6)}{240} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{416.67 \times (0.32 + 0.60)}{240} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{416.67 \times 0.92}{240} \times 100 = \frac{383.33}{240} \times 100 \approx 159.7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** This answer of **~160%** is unrealistically high because the approximation breaks down — the drop is comparable to the voltage itself! However, the formula *mechanically* gives this result. In practice, such a transformer is poorly designed. **KTU expects the student to comment on this** if the drop exceeds 10%. For the exam, present the calculated value, then note that the assumption $|IZ| \ll V$ is violated.

**(ii) Unity PF:** $\cos\phi = 1$, $\sin\phi = 0$.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{416.67 \times 0.4}{240} \times 100 = \frac{166.67}{240} \times 100 \approx 69.4\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** Notice how a "unity PF" regulation of 69.4% is still absurd. This means $R_{02}$ itself is too large (likely the values are hypothetical for testing arithmetic). In a real exam, **state the numerical answer first, then add the qualitative comment**.

**(iii) 0.6 PF Leading:** $\cos\phi = 0.6$, $\sin\phi = 0.8$.

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{416.67 \times (0.4 \times 0.6 - 1.0 \times 0.8)}{240} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{416.67 \times (0.24 - 0.80)}{240} \times 100 = \frac{416.67 \times (-0.56)}{240} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR\%} = \frac{-233.33}{240} \times 100 \approx -97.2\%$$

**Negative regulation** again confirms the leading PF phenomenon.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON THIS TOPIC:**
>
> 1. **Confusing lagging and leading formulas** — students write $+X\sin\phi$ for leading PF. Remember: leading PF uses **minus sign**.
> 2. **Forgetting to refer impedance to the correct side** — if $R$ and $X$ are given on the HV side, use HV current and HV voltage. Mismatch = **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption** that $IZ \ll V$ — KTU deducts 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Mixing up $V_{20}$ and $V_2$** in the numerator — regulation is $(V_{20} - V_2)$, not the reverse.
> 5. **Ignoring negative regulation** — if your answer is negative, **explain** what it physically means (voltage rise on load). Silent negative answers lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Voltage regulation = $(V_{20} - V_2)/V_2 \times 100\%$, always evaluated at **constant primary voltage** and **rated frequency**.
- ✅ A **good transformer** has regulation between **2% and 5%** at rated load and 0.8 PF lagging.
- ✅ The **lagging PF** regulation formula has a **plus sign** between $R\cos\phi$ and $X\sin\phi$ terms.
- ✅ The **leading PF** regulation formula has a **minus sign**.
- ✅ **Unity PF** → only resistive drop contributes ($IR$ term).
- ✅ **Purely inductive load** → only reactive drop contributes ($IX$ term) — this is the maximum regulation case for lagging PF.
- ✅ **Purely capacitive load** → negative regulation (voltage rises on loading).
- ✅ The approximate formula is valid only when $|I \cdot Z_{02}| \ll V_2$ (drop < 10% of rated).
- ✅ Regulation is a **performance parameter** distinct from **efficiency** — a transformer can be highly efficient (> 98%) yet still have poor regulation (e.g., 8%).
- ✅ In KTU problems, **always state units** (V, A, %, Ω) and **show phasor reasoning** for full marks.
- ✅ The **quadrature component** $(X\cos\phi - R\sin\phi)$ is **neglected** in the standard KTU-approved approximation.
- ✅ For **parallel operation** of transformers (Module 2 topic), **equal regulation** is a desirable but not strictly necessary condition — proper connection requires equal voltage ratio and same polarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Losses and Efficiency. Instrument Transformers (CT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Losses and Efficiency. Instrument Transformers (CT) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Losses in DC Machines — Formal Definition In any electromechanical energy conversion device (DC generator or motor), the input electrical/mechanical power is **never fully converted** to the desired output. The difference between input and output power manifests as **losses** — energy dissipated as heat, sound, or stray magnetic fields. For a KTU 2024 Scheme examination, you must classify every loss under one of three families: **electrical (copper) losses**, **magnetic (iron/core) losses**, and **mechanical losses**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"Losses in a DC machine are the unavoidable conversion of useful energy into undesired forms (heat, sound, vibration) occurring in the armature, field, core, and rotating parts. Efficiency is the ratio of useful output power to total input power, expressed as a percentage."* ### 1.2 Efficiency — Intuitive Overview **Conceptual Analogy (Water Bucket View):** Imagine pouring **10 litres of water** into a leaky bucket. Only **8 litres** reach the destination. The 2 litres lost through cracks and evaporation are analogous to machine losses. **Efficiency = 8/10 = 80 %**. A well-engineered machine is simply a *less leaky bucket* — losses are inevitable (Second Law of Thermodynamics), but engineering minimises them. ### 1.3 Instrument Transformers (CT) — Formal Definition An **Instrument Transformer** is a precision electrical device used to isolate, step down, and safely measure high voltages and currents in power systems. There are two families: | Family | Purpose | Output Quantity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Current Transformer (CT)** | Steps down **high primary current** | Standard **1 A / 5 A** secondary | | **Potential Transformer (PT / VT)** | Steps down **high primary voltage** | Standard **110 V / 120 V** secondary | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A CT is fundamentally a *step-down transformer* whose **secondary is ALWAYS short-circuited through a low-impedance burden** (ammeter or relay coil). It is never operated with an open secondary — this is a critical safety rule. **Conceptual Analogy (The Clamp Meter View):** A CT behaves like a *non-intrusive current clamp*. Instead of physically breaking the conductor to insert an ammeter, the conductor itself acts as the **single-turn primary**. The CT "listens" to the magnetic field created by the primary current and reproduces a safe, scaled-down replica on the secondary side for measurement. > [!WARNING] > **NEVER open-circuit a CT secondary.** The primary current remains unchanged (it is determined by the power system load, not the CT). With an open secondary, the entire primary MMF acts as core excitation, producing dangerously high voltages (kV range) and saturating/damaging the core. *Always short the secondary before disconnecting the burden.* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Losses in a DC Machine #### A. Copper Losses (Variable / I²R Losses) Copper losses are **load-dependent** — they vary with the square of the current. 1. **Armature Copper Loss:** $P_{ac} = I_a^2 \, R_a$ 2. **Field Copper Loss (Shunt):** $P_{f} = V \, I_f = I_f^2 \, R_f$ (constant in a shunt machine, since $V$ and $I_f$ are constant) 3. **Brush Contact Loss:** $P_{brush} = V_{brush} \, I_a$ (usually lumped with armature copper loss; $V_{brush} \approx 2\,\text{V}$ for carbon brushes) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a problem says *"a 250 V DC shunt machine"*, the field loss is **constant** (since $I_f$ is fixed) and the armature copper loss is **variable** (depends on load $I_a$). #### B. Iron / Core Losses (Constant Losses) Iron losses occur in the **armature core** due to the rotating magnetic field. They are considered **constant** (speed and flux being roughly fixed). 1. **Hysteresis Loss:** $P_h = \eta \, B_{max}^{1.6} \, f \, V$ (Steinmetz equation) 2. **Eddy Current Loss:** $P_e = k_e \, B_{max}^{2} \, f^2 \, t^2$ Where $B_{max}$ = maximum flux density, $f$ = frequency, $V$ = volume of core, $t$ = lamination thickness. #### C. Mechanical Losses (Constant Losses) 1. **Friction Loss** — bearing + brush friction 2. **Windage Loss** — air resistance against rotating armature > These two are often lumped together as **"Friction and Windage Loss"** ($P_{fw}$). #### D. Stray Load Loss A small additional loss ($\approx 1\%$ of output) caused by **flux distortion** due to armature reaction. In KTU problems, it is often lumped with iron losses or explicitly given. --- ### 2.2 Power Flow in a DC Machine (Cascade Stages) **For a DC Generator:** $$P_{mech,\,in} \rightarrow \underbrace{P_{mech,\,in} - P_{fw}}_{P_{dev}} \rightarrow \underbrace{P_{dev} - P_{iron} - P_{stray}}_{P_{em} = E_b I_a} \rightarrow \underbrace{P_{em} - P_{ac}}_{P_{out} = V I_L}$$ **For a DC Motor (reverse the arrows):** $$P_{elec,\,in} = V I_L \rightarrow \underbrace{V I_L - P_{f}}_{E_b I_a} \rightarrow \underbrace{E_b I_a - P_{ac}}_{P_{dev}} \rightarrow \underbrace{P_{dev} - P_{iron} - P_{mech}}_{P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega}$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Remarks | | :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Efficiency (General) | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Output / Input | | 2 | Efficiency (DC Motor) | $\eta = \dfrac{V I_L - (P_{cu} + P_{iron} + P_{fw})}{V I_L}$ | % | | | 3 | Efficiency (DC Gen.) | $\eta = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + (P_{cu} + P_{iron} + P_{fw})}$ | % | | | 4 | Armature Cu loss | $P_{ac} = I_a^2 R_a$ | W | Variable | | 5 | Field Cu loss | $P_{f} = V I_f$ | W | Constant in shunt | | 6 | Total Constant Loss | $P_c = P_{iron} + P_{fw} + P_{f}$ (shunt) | W | Independent of $I_L$ | | 7 | Max Efficiency Condition | Variable Loss $=$ Constant Loss | W | **Most Important KTU Formula** | | 8 | Load for Max $\eta$ | $I_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_c}{R_a}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K}$ | A | $K$ is current scaling factor | | 9 | CT Turns Ratio (Nominal) | $K_n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$ | – | Step-down device | | 10 | CT Ratio Error | $\varepsilon_R = \dfrac{K_n I_2 - I_1}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Negative for most CTs | | 11 | Secondary EMF (CT) | $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m$ | V | | | 12 | CT Phasor Equation | $\bar{I}_2 = \dfrac{N_1 \bar{I}_1}{N_2} - \dfrac{\bar{E}_2}{Z_2}$ | A | Actual secondary current | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Table 12:** This is the **most-tested CT formula in KTU**. The actual secondary current equals the *ideal reflected primary current* MINUS a small error current driven by the secondary induced EMF. --- ### 2.4 Current Transformer — Deep Theory #### Construction - **Core:** High-permeability silicon steel or **mumetal / permalloy** (nickel-iron alloy) for measurement-grade accuracy at low currents. - **Primary winding:** Often a **single straight bar** (especially for high-current switchgear CTs) — $N_1 = 1$. - **Secondary winding:** Many turns of fine wire wound uniformly around the core — $N_2 \gg N_1$. - **Burden:** The external impedance connected across the secondary (ammeter, relay coil, or shunt). #### Working Principle (Phasor Analysis) The primary current $I_1$ produces an MMF $N_1 I_1$. To maintain core flux, the secondary must produce an opposing MMF $N_2 I_2$. $$N_1 \bar{I}_1 + N_2 \bar{I}_2 = N_1 \bar{I}_0$$ The excitation component $N_1 I_0$ is responsible for the magnetising flux $\Phi$ and the **errors** (ratio error and phase error). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** In an *ideal* CT, $I_0 = 0$ and $N_1 I_1 = -N_2 I_2$ exactly. In a *real* CT, $I_0 \neq 0$, leading to two error types: **ratio error** (magnitude deviation) and **phase error** (angular deviation). #### Why CT vs. Shunt Resistor? | Feature | Shunt Resistor | CT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Isolation | ❌ Direct contact | ✅ Galvanically isolated | | High current (>1000 A) | ❌ Heat dissipation issues | ✅ Ideal | | Accuracy at low current | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Core saturates | | Cost & size at high I | ❌ Bulky | ✅ Compact | **Real-World Utility:** CTs are the *backbone* of every protection relay, energy meter, and SCADA system in substations. Without CTs, measuring 10 kA currents safely would be impossible. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency Let the constant losses be $P_c$ and the variable (armature copper) loss be $P_v = I_a^2 R_a$. Total losses: $P_L = P_c + P_v$ Input power: $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_L$ $$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_c + I_a^2 R_a$$ $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{in} - P_L}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{P_L}{P_{in}}$$ To find the maximum, we differentiate $\eta$ w.r.t. $P_{out}$ and set $\dfrac{d\eta}{dP_{out}} = 0$. $$\eta = 1 - \frac{P_c + I_a^2 R_a}{P_{out} + P_c + I_a^2 R_a}$$ Let $x = P_{out}$. Then $I_a^2 R_a$ is a function of $x$. For a *linear* approximation around operating point, we treat $P_v$ as the variable. $$\eta = 1 - \frac{P_c + P_v}{x + P_c + P_v}$$ Differentiating: $$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = - \frac{-(P_c + P_v)}{(x + P_c + P_v)^2} \cdot \frac{dP_v}{dx} + \frac{1}{x + P_c + P_v} \cdot \frac{dP_v}{dx} \cdot (\text{...})$$ A more elegant route: maximum efficiency occurs when **each watt lost as variable loss equals each watt lost as constant loss** — this is obtained by direct differentiation: $$\frac{d}{dP_v} \left( \frac{P_c + P_v}{x + P_c + P_v} \right) = 0$$ Applying the quotient rule and setting to zero yields: $$\boxed{P_v = P_c \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad I_a^2 R_a = P_c}$$ **Interpretation:** At maximum efficiency, the **variable copper loss equals the constant (iron + mechanical + field) loss**. The machine is then operating at its *most economical load point*. --- ### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example 1 — DC Shunt Motor Efficiency > **Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern):** > A **250 V DC shunt motor** has the following nameplate data: > Armature resistance $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$ > Field resistance $R_f = 250 \, \Omega$ > Iron losses $= 1000 \, \text{W}$ > Friction and windage $= 500 \, \text{W}$ > No-load armature current $I_{a0} = 5 \, \text{A}$ > > **Find:** (a) Efficiency at full-load armature current of **50 A**. > (b) Load current at which efficiency is **maximum**. #### Part (a) — Efficiency at $I_a = 50$ A **Step 1 — Field Current** $$I_f = \frac{V}{R_f} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 2 — Line Current** $$I_L = I_a + I_f = 50 + 1 = 51 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 3 — Input Power** $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 51 = 12750 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 4 — Armature Copper Loss** $$P_{ac} = I_a^2 R_a = 50^2 \times 0.1 = 2500 \times 0.1 = 250 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 5 — Field Copper Loss** $$P_f = V I_f = 250 \times 1 = 250 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 6 — Total Losses** $$P_L = P_{ac} + P_f + P_{iron} + P_{fw} = 250 + 250 + 1000 + 500 = 2000 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 7 — Output Power** $$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_L = 12750 - 2000 = 10750 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 8 — Efficiency** $$\eta = \frac{10750}{12750} \times 100 = 84.31 \, \%$$ #### Part (b) — Load for Maximum Efficiency **Step 9 — Identify Constant Losses** $$P_c = P_f + P_{iron} + P_{fw} = 250 + 1000 + 500 = 1750 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 10 — Apply Maximum Efficiency Condition** $$I_a^2 R_a = P_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$ $$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{1750}{0.1}} = \sqrt{17500} = 132.29 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 11 — Corresponding Line Current** $$I_L = I_a + I_f = 132.29 + 1 = 133.29 \, \text{A}$$ > [!NOTE] > Since $I_L = 133.29$ A is greater than the full-load current of 50 A, the machine is *over-designed*. Maximum efficiency occurs **beyond rated load** — this means at full load, we are still on the *rising portion* of the efficiency curve (more load gives more efficiency, up to the optimum point). --- ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example 2 — Current Transformer > **Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern):** > A CT has primary turns $N_1 = 1$, secondary turns $N_2 = 200$. The nominal ratio is **400 A : 5 A**. The secondary burden is a purely resistive impedance of **1.2 Ω**. The core requires a magnetising MMF of **30 A-turns** at the operating flux. The primary carries a current of **400 A**. > > **Find:** (a) The ideal secondary current. > (b) The actual secondary current. > (c) The ratio error in percent. #### Part (a) — Ideal Secondary Current $$I_2^{ideal} = \frac{N_1 \cdot I_1}{N_2} = \frac{1 \times 400}{200} = 2.0 \, \text{A}$$ *(Note: 2.0 A is the *reflected* ideal current, but the problem states the ratio is 400:5. With $N_1 = 1$ and $N_2 = 200$, the natural ratio is 1:200, giving 2.0 A. We proceed with this.)* #### Part (b) — Actual Secondary Current By the MMF balance (phasor equation): $$N_1 \bar{I}_1 = N_2 \bar{I}_2 + N_1 \bar{I}_0$$ The excitation (magnetising) component $N_1 I_0 = 30$ A-turns. $$N_2 I_2 = N_1 I_1 - N_1 I_0 = 400 - 30 = 370 \, \text{A-turns}$$ $$I_2^{actual} = \frac{370}{N_2} = \frac{370}{200} = 1.85 \, \text{A}$$ #### Part (c) — Ratio Error $$\varepsilon_R = \frac{K_n I_2^{actual} - I_1}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$$ Where $K_n = N_2 / N_1 = 200$. $$\varepsilon_R = \frac{200 \times 1.85 - 400}{400} \times 100\,\% = \frac{370 - 400}{400} \times 100\,\% = \frac{-30}{400} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon_R = -7.5\,\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that the *actual reflected secondary current* is **less than** the primary current — this is the typical behaviour of a CT (the core always consumes some MMF for magnetisation). In practice, ratio errors are kept within **±1 %** for metering CTs (Class 0.5, 1) and **±5 to ±10 %** for protection CTs (Class 5P, 10P). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Loss Behaviour vs. Load | Load Condition | Constant Loss $P_c$ | Variable Loss $P_v = I_a^2 R_a$ | Total Loss | Efficiency Trend | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **No Load ($I_a \to 0$)** | Full ($1750$ W) | 0 | $1750$ W | $\eta \to 0\,\%$ | | **Light Load ($I_a = 25$ A)** | $1750$ W | $62.5$ W | $1812.5$ W | Rising | | **Optimum ($I_a = 132.3$ A)** | $1750$ W | $1750$ W | $3500$ W | **Maximum** | | **Heavy Load ($I_a = 200$ A)** | $1750$ W | $4000$ W | $5750$ W | Falling | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Power Flow Block Diagram of a DC Motor (Cascade Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR src1[Input Electrical Power
V x IL] sub1[Subtract Field Cu Loss
Vf x If] eb1[Subtract Armature Cu Loss
Ia squared x Ra] dev1[Mechanical Power Developed
Eb x Ia] sub2[Subtract Iron Loss
Hysteresis plus Eddy Current] sub3[Subtract Friction and Windage
F plus W] out1[Output Shaft Power
T sh x omega] src1 --> sub1 --> eb1 --> dev1 --> sub2 --> sub3 --> out1 ``` ### 4.2 Power Flow Block Diagram of a DC Generator ```mermaid flowchart LR inp[Input Mechanical Power
Shaft] fw1[Subtract Friction and Windage] dev2[Power Developed
Eg x Ia] irn[Subtract Iron and Stray Load Loss] cu1[Subtract Armature Cu Loss
Ia squared x Ra] fd1[Subtract Field Cu Loss] gen1[Output Electrical Power
V x IL] inp --> fw1 --> dev2 --> irn --> cu1 --> fd1 --> gen1 ``` ### 4.3 Current Transformer — Equivalent Circuit (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD pPri[Primary Current I1] --> pMMF[Magnetising MMF
N1 I0] pMMF --> pFlux[Core Flux Phi] pFlux --> pEMF[Secondary Induced EMF E2] pEMF --> pBur[Burden Zb] pBur --> pSec[Actual Secondary Current I2] pPri --> pRef[Reflected MMF
N1 I1 minus N1 I0] pRef --> pSec ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Solving Efficiency Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Read Machine Data: V, IL, Ra, Rf, Iron, Friction] --> B[Compute Field Current If] B --> C[Compute Armature Current Ia] C --> D[Compute Each Loss Component] D --> E[Sum Total Losses PL] E --> F[Compute Pin and Pout] F --> G{Question Type?} G -->|Find Efficiency| H[Eta equals Pout by Pin] G -->|Find Max Eta Load| I[Set Ia squared Ra equals Pc] G -->|Find Cu Loss at Given Load| J[Use Ia squared Ra directly] H --> K[Express Answer as Percentage] I --> K J --> K ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** *List any **six** types of losses occurring in a DC machine and classify each as constant or variable.* **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):** | # | Loss | Type | Brief Reason | | :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Armature copper loss | **Variable** | $\propto I_a^2$ | | 2 | Field copper loss (shunt) | **Constant** | $V$ and $I_f$ are fixed | | 3 | Hysteresis loss | **Constant** | Flux and speed are constant | | 4 | Eddy current loss | **Constant** | Flux and speed are constant | | 5 | Friction loss | **Constant** | Speed is constant | | 6 | Windage loss | **Constant** | Speed is constant | | 7 | Stray load loss | **Variable** | Depends on load current | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Why is the secondary of a Current Transformer (CT) never left open-circuited during operation? What happens if it is accidentally opened?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The primary current $I_1$ is determined by the external power system load and is **independent** of the CT secondary condition. (1 mark) - Under normal operation, the secondary MMF $N_2 I_2$ nearly cancels the primary MMF $N_1 I_1$, leaving only a tiny excitation component $N_1 I_0$. (1 mark) - If the secondary is **open-circuited**, $I_2 = 0$. The entire primary MMF $N_1 I_1$ acts as excitation, driving the core into **deep saturation**. This produces a dangerously high voltage across the secondary terminals (kV range), causes severe overheating, and may permanently damage the core insulation — a serious safety hazard. (1 mark) --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]` > A **250 V, 50 A DC shunt generator** delivers rated current at rated voltage. The armature resistance is **0.2 Ω** and the shunt field resistance is **125 Ω**. The iron losses are **800 W**, friction and windage losses are **400 W**, and stray load loss is **1 % of the output power**. > > **(a)** Draw the power flow diagram and compute the efficiency at full load. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Determine the armature current at which efficiency becomes maximum, and find the maximum efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Field and Armature Current (½ mark)** $$I_f = \frac{V}{R_f} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \, \text{A}$$ $$I_a = I_L + I_f = 50 + 2 = 52 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 2 — Armature Copper Loss (1 mark)** $$P_{ac} = I_a^2 R_a = 52^2 \times 0.2 = 2704 \times 0.2 = 540.8 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 3 — Field Copper Loss (1 mark)** $$P_f = V I_f = 250 \times 2 = 500 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 4 — Stray Load Loss (1 mark)** $$P_{stray} = 0.01 \times P_{out}$$ We need $P_{out}$. Input = $250 \times 50 = 12500$ W. $$P_{out} = 12500 - P_L \Rightarrow P_{out} = 12500 - (P_{ac} + P_f + P_{iron} + P_{fw} + P_{stray})$$ $$P_{out} = 12500 - 540.8 - 500 - 800 - 400 - 0.01 P_{out}$$ $$1.01 \, P_{out} = 12500 - 2240.8 = 10259.2$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{10259.2}{1.01} = 10157.6 \, \text{W}$$ $$P_{stray} = 0.01 \times 10157.6 = 101.6 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 5 — Total Losses (½ mark)** $$P_L = 540.8 + 500 + 800 + 400 + 101.6 = 2342.4 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 6 — Efficiency (1 mark)** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{10157.6}{12500} \times 100 = 81.26 \,\%$$ **Step 7 — Power Flow Diagram (2 marks)** ```mermaid flowchart LR a1[Input Mechanical
Pm in] --> a2[minus Friction
minus Windage] --> a3[Power Developed
Eg x Ia] --> a4[minus Iron
minus Stray] --> a5[Eg x Ia equals V Ia plus Ia squared Ra] --> a6[minus Armature Cu Loss] --> a7[minus Field Cu Loss] --> a8[Output Electrical
V x IL equals 12500 W] ``` --- ##### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Constant Losses (1 mark)** $$P_c = P_f + P_{iron} + P_{fw} = 500 + 800 + 400 = 1700 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Max Efficiency Condition (2 marks)** $$I_a^2 R_a = P_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$ $$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{1700}{0.2}} = \sqrt{8500} = 92.2 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 3 — Variable Loss at Max Efficiency (1 mark)** $$P_{v,\,max} = I_a^2 R_a = 92.2^2 \times 0.2 = 8500 \times 0.2 = 1700 \, \text{W} \quad \text{(equals } P_c\text{ ✓)}$$ **Step 4 — Total Losses at Max Efficiency (½ mark)** $$P_{L,\,max} = 2 P_c = 2 \times 1700 = 3400 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 5 — Output Power (1 mark)** Load current: $I_L = I_a - I_f = 92.2 - 2 = 90.2$ A $$P_{in} = V I_L = 250 \times 90.2 = 22550 \, \text{W}$$ $$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_L = 22550 - 3400 = 19150 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 6 — Maximum Efficiency (1 mark)** $$\eta_{max} = \frac{19150}{22550} \times 100 = 84.92 \,\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):** > 1. **Stray load loss trap:** Many students forget the $0.01 P_{out}$ term in Part (a). Since $P_{out}$ appears on *both* sides, it must be solved as a simultaneous equation — *do not assume* $P_{out} = V I_L$. > 2. **Direction of $I_a$ in generator vs. motor:** In a **generator**, $I_a = I_L + I_f$ (field current is supplied internally). In a **motor**, $I_a = I_L - I_f$. Reversing this gives a wrong answer. > 3. **Forgetting field copper loss:** In shunt machines, $P_f$ is **constant** and must be added to the constant losses — students often omit it, losing 1 mark. --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]` > **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Current Transformer (CT)**. Define **(i) ratio error** and **(ii) phase angle error**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A CT has **200 primary turns** and **1000 secondary turns**. The secondary is connected to an ammeter of resistance **0.5 Ω** and a relay coil of reactance **0.3 Ω** in series. The iron loss in the core is **10 W** and the magnetising current is **0.5 A** when the primary carries **100 A**. Calculate the **(i) nominal ratio**, **(ii) actual ratio**, **(iii) ratio error**, and **(iv) phase angle error**. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Core:** Laminated silicon steel (or mumetal for high accuracy) shaped as a *ring* or *rectangular window-type*. - **Primary winding:** Either a *single bar* ($N_1 = 1$, for high current) or a few turns of heavy conductor passing *through* the central window. - **Secondary winding:** Many turns ($N_2 = 100$ to $1000+$) of fine insulated copper wire, uniformly distributed around the core to minimise leakage reactance. - **Terminal box:** Houses the secondary terminals, earthing, and a short-circuiting link (for safety during maintenance). - **Burden:** External load — ammeter, wattmeter current coil, or relay coil — connected in series across the secondary. **Working (2 marks):** The primary current $I_1$ flowing through $N_1$ turns sets up an alternating MMF $N_1 I_1$. This MMF drives an alternating flux $\Phi$ in the core. The flux links the secondary winding of $N_2$ turns, inducing an EMF $E_2 = 4.44 f N_2 \Phi_m$. This EMF drives a current $I_2$ through the burden. The secondary MMF $N_2 I_2$ opposes the primary MMF, and the small difference $N_1 I_0$ magnetises the core. **Errors (2 marks):** - **(i) Ratio Error:** The deviation of the actual transformation ratio from the nominal (marked) ratio, expressed as a percentage of the primary current. $$\varepsilon_R = \frac{K_n I_2 - I_1}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$$ - **(ii) Phase Angle Error:** The angular difference (in minutes) between the *primary current phasor* and the *reversed secondary current phasor* ($\bar{I}_1$ and $-\bar{I}_2$). It is caused by the magnetising component of excitation current. --- ##### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $N_1 = 200$, $N_2 = 1000$, $I_1 = 100$ A - $R_{ammeter} = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_{relay} = 0.3 \, \Omega$ - Iron loss $P_i = 10$ W, magnetising current $I_0 = 0.5$ A **Step 1 — Nominal Ratio (1 mark)** $$K_n = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1000}{200} = 5$$ Nominal ratio: $I_1 : I_2 = 5 : 1$, i.e., **100 A : 20 A**. **Step 2 — Secondary Induced EMF (1 mark)** $$E_2 = \frac{P_i}{I_0} = \frac{10}{0.5} = 20 \, \text{V}$$ *(This comes from $E_2 I_0 \cos\phi_0 \approx P_i$ assuming $\cos\phi_0 \approx 1$ for the loss component.)* **Step 3 — Burden Impedance (½ mark)** $$Z_b = R + jX = 0.5 + j0.3 = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.3^2} \, \angle \tan^{-1}(0.3/0.5)$$ $$Z_b = 0.5831 \, \angle 30.96^\circ \, \Omega$$ **Step 4 — Actual Secondary Current (1 mark)** $$I_2^{actual} = \frac{E_2}{Z_b} = \frac{20}{0.5831} = 34.30 \, \text{A}$$ **Step 5 — Actual Transformation Ratio (½ mark)** $$K_a = \frac{I_1}{I_2^{actual}} = \frac{100}{34.30} = 2.915$$ **Step 6 — Ratio Error (1 mark)** $$\varepsilon_R = \frac{K_n - K_a}{K_a} \times 100\,\% = \frac{5 - 2.915}{2.915} \times 100\,\% = 71.6\,\%$$ **Step 7 — Phase Angle Error (1 mark)** For a highly inductive burden and small $I_0$, the phase angle error is approximated by: $$\theta \approx \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{I_w}{I_1 / K_n}\right)$$ where $I_w$ is the wattful (iron-loss) component of $I_0$. Assuming $I_0$ is mostly reactive: $$\theta \approx \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{P_i / E_2}{I_1 / K_n}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.5}{20}\right) = 1.43^\circ = 85.8 \text{ arc-minutes}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Phase Angle Units:** > KTU examiners expect phase angle in **minutes of arc** (not degrees), because CT standards (IS 2705, IEC 61869) use arc-minutes. 1 degree = 60 arc-minutes. Converting correctly can be the difference between full marks and partial credit. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Three loss families:** Copper (variable, $I^2R$), Iron (constant, hysteresis + eddy), Mechanical (constant, friction + windage). - [x] **Stray load loss** $\approx 1\%$ of output — often ignored unless specifically given. - [x] **In a DC shunt machine:** Field loss is constant (since $I_f = V/R_f$ is fixed). - [x] **Power flow rule of thumb:** Trace the input → output chain, subtracting losses at each stage. - [x] **Maximum efficiency** occurs when $P_{variable} = P_{constant}$ — i.e., $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$. - [x] **Efficiency equation:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}}$. - [x] **CT is a step-down transformer** for current, NOT for voltage. - [x] **CT secondary must NEVER be open-circuited** — risk of lethal over-voltage and core damage. - [x] **CT nominal ratio** $K_n = N_2 / N_1 = I_1 / I_2$ (marked nameplate ratio). - [x] **CT ratio error** $\varepsilon_R = \dfrac{K_n I_2 - I_1}{I_1} \times 100\%$ (negative for most CTs). - [x] **CT phase error** measured in **arc-minutes**, caused by magnetising component $I_0$. - [x] **CT burden** = the total external impedance on the secondary (ammeter + relay + leads). - [x] **For KTU numericals:** Always solve $P_{out}$ as a self-referential equation if stray loss is given as a percentage of $P_{out}$. - [x] **Direction sign convention:** Motor: $I_a = I_L - I_f$. Generator: $I_a = I_L + I_f$.

topics.title: Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **three-phase induction motor** is a self-starting, rugged, brushless, singly-excited alternating current (AC) motor in which the rotor receives its power exclusively by electromagnetic induction from the stator winding, without requiring any physical electrical connection between the stator and the rotor. The rotor never runs at synchronous speed; the necessary relative motion between the rotating stator field and the stationary rotor conductors is quantified by a dimensionless quantity called **slip ($s$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (PCSFT403 / Module 2):** The student must be able to explain the construction, principle of operation, rotating magnetic field (RMF), concept of slip, torque–slip characteristic, and starting methods of the three-phase induction motor. Constructional differences between **squirrel cage** and **wound (slip-ring) rotor** must be clearly understood.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Magnetic Carousel"

Imagine a large rotating circular platform (the **rotating magnetic field** generated by the stator) at a fair-ground, and a small lightweight metal horse (the **rotor**) placed on it. Because of friction between the horse's hooves and the platform, the horse is dragged along in the same direction as the platform. However, the horse's hooves always slip a tiny bit on the platform surface — it never quite catches up. That permanent slip is exactly what engineers call **slip ($s$)**. If the horse magically froze to the platform, there would be no slip and therefore no induced current in the rotor, and hence no torque — the system would stall.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Indian supply frequency | **50 Hz** |
| US / most of world supply | **60 Hz** |
| Synchronous speed ($N_s$) | 750, 1000, 1500, 3000 RPM (for 50 Hz) |
| Slip at full load (typical) | **3 % to 5 %** |
| Rotor slots skew | Yes (in squirrel-cage to prevent cogging) |

> [!NOTE]
> The induction motor is the **"workhorse of industry"** — over **90 %** of all industrial AC motors in the world are induction motors, owing to their rugged construction, low cost, and minimal maintenance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy

The induction motor has two main parts:

**A. Stator (Stationary Part)**
- Three-phase distributed winding placed in slots of a laminated silicon-steel core.
- The three phases are spatially displaced by **120° electrical** from each other.
- When energized by a balanced 3-φ supply, a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** is produced.

**B. Rotor (Rotating Part) — Two Designs**

| Feature | Squirrel Cage Rotor | Wound (Slip-Ring) Rotor |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Solid bars shorted by end rings | Insulated windings connected via slip rings |
| External resistance | Not possible | Possible via rheostat |
| Maintenance | Almost zero | Requires brush maintenance |
| Starting torque | Moderate (1.5 – 2 × FL) | High (controlled by external R) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Application | Fans, pumps, compressors | Cranes, hoists, elevators |

### 2.2 The Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) — Step by Step

When a balanced 3-phase supply is fed to the stator winding:

1. Each phase produces a pulsating magnetic flux along its axis.
2. Because the three currents are phase-shifted by **120° in time**, their resultant flux vector has a **constant magnitude** but rotates in space at the synchronous speed.
3. The mechanical rotation of the magnetic field is achieved *without any moving mechanical part* — purely by temporal phase displacement.

The synchronous speed is given by:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} \text{ revolutions per minute (RPM)}$$

where $f$ = supply frequency (Hz) and $P$ = total number of stator poles.

### 2.3 Why the Rotor Must Slip

According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in the rotor only when there is a *rate of change of flux linkage*. If the rotor were to rotate at exactly $N_s$, the flux linkage through rotor conductors would be constant in time — hence **no induced EMF, no rotor current, no torque**. Therefore the rotor must rotate at $N_r < N_s$. This difference defines slip.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — every numerical in the KTU University Exam is solved from one or more of these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM |
| 2 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | **Slip** | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per-unit (or %) |
| 4 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | RPM |
| 5 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz |
| 6 | Rotor induced EMF (running) | $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$ | Volts/phase |
| 7 | Rotor reactance (running) | $X_{2r} = s \cdot X_2$ | Ω/phase |
| 8 | Rotor current | $I_2 = \dfrac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$ | Amp |
| 9 | Rotor power factor | $\cos \phi_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Air-gap (rotor input) power | $P_g = \dfrac{3 I_2^2 R_2}{s}$ | Watts |
| 11 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = (1 - s) P_g = 3 I_2^2 R_2 \dfrac{(1-s)}{s}$ | Watts |
| 12 | Gross torque developed | $T_g = \dfrac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ | N·m |
| 13 | Slip at maximum torque | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | **Maximum (pull-out) torque** | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{X_2}$ | N·m (independent of $R_2$) |
| 15 | Starting torque ($s = 1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m |
| 16 | Approx. full-load slip (linear region) | $\dfrac{s}{s_m} = \dfrac{T}{T_{max}}$ | for $s < s_m$ |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The induction motor is the **default prime mover** in modern industry — it drives centrifugal pumps, conveyors, compressors, mixers, mills, fans, blowers, machine-tool spindles, HVAC blowers, and even electric vehicles (Tesla Model S/X/3 use squirrel-cage induction motors). The formulas above allow practicing engineers to: (i) select proper motor rating, (ii) design soft-starters and VFDs, (iii) estimate starting current impact on the grid, and (iv) optimize rotor resistance for high-starting-torque applications such as elevators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed from Pole-Pitch Geometry

A 2-pole winding produces one North and one South pole — covering **360° electrical = 360° mechanical**. A $P$-pole winding completes one electrical cycle every $\frac{360°}{P/2}$ mechanical degrees.

$$\text{Mechanical degrees per electrical cycle} = \frac{360°}{P/2} = \frac{720°}{P}$$

Supply frequency $f$ means $f$ electrical cycles per second. Therefore:

$$\text{Mechanical revolutions per second} = \frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_s = \frac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{RPM}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rotor Frequency and Rotor EMF

At standstill ($N_r = 0$), rotor conductors cut the RMF at synchronous speed $N_s$ and the induced frequency equals supply frequency $f$.

At running condition, relative speed between RMF and rotor = $N_s - N_r$. Induced rotor frequency:

$$f_r = \frac{P(N_s - N_r)}{120} = \frac{P \cdot N_s}{120} \cdot \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = f \cdot s$$

Since induced EMF is directly proportional to the rate of flux cutting (proportional to relative speed), the running rotor EMF is:

$$E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$$

where $E_2$ = rotor EMF at standstill (rated value).

Similarly rotor reactance (proportional to frequency):

$$X_{2r} = 2 \pi f_r L = 2 \pi (s f) L = s \cdot X_2$$

> Only rotor resistance $R_2$ remains constant; rotor reactance and EMF scale linearly with slip.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mechanical Power Developed (Power Flow Stages)

The complete power-flow chain in an induction motor is:

$$\boxed{\text{Stator Input }(P_{in}) \to \text{Stator Cu + Iron Loss} \to \text{Air-gap Power }(P_g) \to \text{Rotor Cu Loss} \to P_m \to \text{Friction + Windage} \to \text{Shaft Power }(P_{sh})}$$

Mathematical derivation:

The rotor copper loss (per phase) = $I_2^2 R_2$. Total rotor copper loss (3 phases):

$$P_{cu2} = 3 I_2^2 R_2$$

Total air-gap power transferred to rotor:

$$P_g = 3 \cdot \frac{I_2^2 R_2}{s}$$

Mechanical power developed in rotor:

$$P_m = P_g - P_{cu2} = 3 \frac{I_2^2 R_2}{s} - 3 I_2^2 R_2$$

Factorising:

$$P_m = 3 I_2^2 R_2 \left( \frac{1}{s} - 1 \right) = 3 I_2^2 R_2 \cdot \frac{1-s}{s} = (1 - s) P_g$$

Therefore the **ratio $P_m : P_{cu2} : P_g = (1-s) : s : 1$** — a critical result for the KTU exam.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gross Torque Equation

Gross torque developed (internal, before rotational losses):

$$T_g = \frac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \frac{P_g (1-s)}{\omega_s (1-s)} = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s}$$

Substituting $P_g = \dfrac{3 I_2^2 R_2}{s}$ and $I_2 = \dfrac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_g &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{I_2^2 R_2}{s} \\
&= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} \cdot R_2 \\
T_g &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Condition for Maximum Torque and Slip at Max Torque

Differentiate $T_g$ with respect to slip $s$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{d T_g}{d s} = 0$$

Using quotient rule on $T_g = \dfrac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$, and equating numerator derivative to zero:

$$\left[R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2\right] \cdot 1 - s \cdot \left[2(s X_2) X_2\right] = 0$$

$$R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$\boxed{s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

Substituting back into the torque equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2/X_2}{R_2^2 + (R_2/X_2)^2 X_2^2} \\
&= \frac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2/X_2}{2 R_2^2} \\
T_{max} &= \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{X_2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Conclusion:** $T_{max}$ depends *only* on $E_2$ and $X_2$, **not** on rotor resistance $R_2$. However, the *slip* at which $T_{max}$ occurs *is* directly proportional to $R_2$. This is why adding external resistance to a slip-ring motor shifts the maximum-torque point toward higher slip without changing $T_{max}$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Torque vs Slip Behaviour (Qualitative)

| Region | Slip range | Rotor behaviour | Torque trend |
|---|---|---|---|
| Near $s = 0$ (running) | $0 < s < s_m$ | Low $I_2$, low losses, high pf | Torque rises linearly with $s$ |
| At $s = s_m$ | $s_m = R_2 / X_2$ | Maximum torque (pull-out) | $T_{max}$ (peak) |
| Near $s = 1$ (starting) | $s_m < s < 1$ | High $I_2$, low pf | Torque falls as $s$ increases |
| At $s = 1$ (standstill) | $s = 1$ | Full voltage across rotor | Starting torque (depends on design) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Power-Flow Stages of Induction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Three-Phase AC Supply 50 Hz] --> B[Stator Winding - Laminated Core]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field RMF]
    C --> D[Relative Motion with Rotor]
    D --> E[Rotor EMF Induced E2r = sE2]
    E --> F[Rotor Current I2]
    F --> G[Gross Torque Tg Developed]
    G --> H[Mechanical Power Pm at Rotor Shaft]
    H --> I[Friction and Windage Losses]
    I --> J[Shaft Output Psh - Load Work]
    
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

> The standard *approximate* equivalent circuit (KTU-examinable form) replaces the magnetising branch by a constant-current source at the stator terminals and shifts the rotor branch to the stator side, yielding a simple series circuit of $R_1, X_1, R_2/s, X_2$.

---
