<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define slip of an induction motor. A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor runs at 1440 RPM. Calculate the slip and rotor frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

Slip is defined as the difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed, expressed as a per-unit (or percentage) of synchronous speed:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

Step 1 — Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

Step 2 — Slip:

$$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \;\; (\text{or } 4\%)$$

Step 3 — Rotor frequency:

$$f_r = s f = 0.04 \times 50 = \mathbf{2 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1), Synchronous speed (1), Final slip + rotor frequency (1).

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sessional II]`
**Why can an induction motor never run at synchronous speed? What is the rotor copper loss if the air-gap power is 20 kW and slip is 4 %?**

**Model Answer:**

If the rotor were to rotate at synchronous speed ($N_r = N_s$), there would be no relative motion between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors. Therefore the rate of change of flux linkage through rotor conductors would be zero. By Faraday's law, no EMF would be induced, no rotor current would flow, and hence **no torque would be produced** — the motor would decelerate immediately. Hence slip is a fundamental necessity for torque production.

Rotor copper loss is related to air-gap power by:

$$P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g = 0.04 \times 20 \text{ kW} = \mathbf{0.8 \text{ kW} = 800 \text{ W}}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Reason (2), Computation (1).

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### CHOICE A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2 Full Question]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. Derive the expression for synchronous speed.

(b) **[7 Marks]** The power input to a 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor is 40 kW. The stator losses total 1.5 kW, the rotor copper losses are 1.2 kW, and the friction & windage losses are 0.8 kW. Calculate: (i) slip, (ii) mechanical power developed, (iii) shaft power, (iv) shaft torque, and (v) efficiency.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working:**

A 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor consists of:

- **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel core (to reduce eddy-current loss) carrying a 3-phase distributed winding. The three phases are physically displaced by 120° electrical in space. When supplied with a balanced 3-phase AC, a Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) is produced that rotates at synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / P$.

- **Rotor:** A laminated cylindrical core mounted on the shaft, carrying copper or aluminium bars placed parallel to the shaft axis in semi-enclosed slots. All bars are short-circuited at both ends by conducting **end-rings**, forming a cage-like structure (hence "squirrel cage"). No external connection, no slip rings, no brushes.

**Working Principle:** The RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors at synchronous speed, inducing an EMF (Faraday's law). Since the rotor circuit is closed, this EMF drives a large rotor current. By Lenz's law, the rotor conductors experience a force in the magnetic field ($F = BIl$), producing torque that rotates the rotor in the *same* direction as the RMF. Once the rotor accelerates, the relative speed reduces, and the rotor settles at a steady speed slightly below $N_s$ where motor torque equals load torque.

**Synchronous speed derivation** (as in Section 3.1):

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

**(b) Numerical:**

Given: $P = 40$ kW, Stator losses $= 1.5$ kW, $P_{cu2} = 1.2$ kW, Mechanical losses $= 0.8$ kW, $f = 50$ Hz, $P_{poles} = 6$.

Step 1 — Air-gap power:

$$P_g = P_{in} - \text{Stator Losses} = 40 - 1.5 = 38.5 \text{ kW}$$

Step 2 — Slip from rotor copper loss:

$$s = \frac{P_{cu2}}{P_g} = \frac{1.2}{38.5} = 0.0312 \;\; (3.12\%)$$

Step 3 — Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}; \;\; \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 4 — Rotor actual speed:

$$N_r = N_s (1-s) = 1000 (1 - 0.0312) = 968.8 \text{ RPM}; \;\; \omega_r = 101.45 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 5 — Mechanical power developed:

$$P_m = P_g - P_{cu2} = 38.5 - 1.2 = 37.3 \text{ kW}$$

Step 6 — Shaft power (after mechanical losses):

$$P_{sh} = P_m - P_{mech} = 37.3 - 0.8 = \mathbf{36.5 \text{ kW}}$$

Step 7 — Shaft torque:

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{sh}}{\omega_r} = \frac{36500}{101.45} = \mathbf{359.8 \text{ N·m}}$$

Step 8 — Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{sh}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{36.5}{40} \times 100\% = \mathbf{91.25\%}$$

---

#### CHOICE B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Alternate Full Question]`

**Question B (14 Marks):**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the torque-slip characteristic of a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the condition for maximum torque and prove that the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.04$ Ω/phase and standstill reactance $X_2 = 0.2$ Ω/phase. The ratio of stator to rotor turns is 2:1. If the stator is fed at 400 V (line), find: (i) slip at maximum torque, (ii) maximum torque in N·m, (iii) starting torque in N·m, (iv) speed at maximum torque.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Torque-Slip Characteristic & Maximum Torque Derivation:**

As slip $s$ increases from 0 to 1:

- At $s = 0$ (synchronous): $T = 0$ (no relative motion, no rotor EMF, no current).
- As $s$ rises slightly, rotor EMF $sE_2$ and current $I_2$ rise, producing a torque that rises **linearly**.
- Torque reaches a **maximum** at slip $s_m = R_2 / X_2$.
- Beyond $s_m$, current increase slows (rotor reactance dominates), power factor falls rapidly, and torque decreases.
- At $s = 1$ (standstill): torque is the **starting torque**.

The derivation of $T_{max}$ and $s_m$ is shown in **Section 3.5** of these notes. Key results:

$$\boxed{s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} \quad ; \quad T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{X_2}}$$

Crucially, $T_{max}$ contains no $R_2$ term — hence **maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance**. However, the *slip* at which maximum torque occurs is *directly proportional* to $R_2$. Therefore, in slip-ring (wound-rotor) motors, external rotor resistance can be added to shift $s_m$ to higher values, increasing starting torque without changing $T_{max}$.

**(b) Numerical:**

Given: $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_2 = 0.04$ Ω/ph, $X_2 = 0.2$ Ω/ph, turns ratio $N_1/N_2 = 2/1$, $V_L = 400$ V.

Step 1 — Synchronous speed and angular speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}; \;\; \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Stator phase voltage (star-connected, assumed):

$$V_{ph} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$

Step 3 — Rotor standstill EMF per phase (referred to rotor side):

$$E_2 = \frac{V_{ph}}{N_1/N_2} = \frac{230.94}{2} = 115.47 \text{ V/phase}$$

Step 4 — Slip at maximum torque:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.04}{0.2} = \mathbf{0.2 \; (20\%)}$$

Step 5 — Maximum torque:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{X_2} \\
&= \frac{3}{2 \times 157.08} \cdot \frac{(115.47)^2}{0.2} \\
&= \frac{3}{314.16} \cdot \frac{13333.3}{0.2} \\
&= 0.009549 \times 66666.7 \\
&\approx \mathbf{636.6 \text{ N·m}}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 6 — Starting torque (at $s = 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{(115.47)^2 \times 0.04}{(0.04)^2 + (0.2)^2} \\
&= 0.01910 \times \frac{533.33}{0.0416} \\
&= 0.01910 \times 12817 \\
&\approx \mathbf{244.8 \text{ N·m}}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 7 — Speed at maximum torque:

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s_m) = 1500 (1 - 0.2) = \mathbf{1200 \text{ RPM}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_2$ (standstill rotor EMF) with $E_{2r} = s E_2$ (running rotor EMF).** The formula for $T_{max}$ uses $E_2$, *not* $sE_2$. Many students wrongly substitute $sE_2$ and lose 3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the turns ratio** when computing $E_2$ from stator voltage. If stator is star-connected, divide line voltage by $\sqrt{3}$ first, then divide by turns ratio.
> 3. **Using $N_r$ instead of $N_s$ in $\omega_s$** in the torque denominator. The gross torque formula uses *synchronous* angular speed.
> 4. **Forgetting factor 3** (for three phases) in the torque equation. This single error wipes out 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up $P_g$, $P_m$, $P_{cu2}$, $P_{sh}$.** Remember the sequence: $P_g \to P_{cu2}$ (subtract) $\to P_m$ $\to$ mechanical losses $\to P_{sh}$.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [ ] **Definition:** 3-phase induction motor is a self-starting, singly-excited, brushless AC motor where rotor power is transferred purely by electromagnetic induction.
- [ ] **Construction:** Stator (laminated core + 3-φ distributed winding) and Rotor (squirrel cage *or* wound/slip-ring type).
- [ ] **RMF Speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$. For 50 Hz supply: 3000 RPM (2-pole), 1500 RPM (4-pole), 1000 RPM (6-pole), 750 RPM (8-pole).
- [ ] **Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. Always positive for motor, zero for synchronous, negative for generator.
- [ ] **Rotor frequency:** $f_r = s f$. Rotor EMF: $E_{2r} = s E_2$. Rotor reactance: $X_{2r} = s X_2$.
- [ ] **Power stages:** $P_{in} \to P_g$ (air gap) $\to P_m$ (mechanical developed) $\to P_{sh}$ (shaft). Ratios: $P_g : P_{cu2} : P_m = 1 : s : (1-s)$.
- [ ] **Torque equation:** $T_g = (3/\omega_s) \cdot s E_2^2 R_2 / [R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2]$.
- [ ] **Max torque:** $T_{max} = (3 / 2\omega_s) \cdot E_2^2 / X_2$ — **independent of $R_2$**, occurs at $s_m = R_2 / X_2$.
- [ ] **Starting torque:** Put $s = 1$ in the torque equation.
- [ ] **Starting methods:** DOL (Direct-On-Line), Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer, Rotor Resistance starting (for slip-ring only), Soft-starters, VFDs.
- [ ] **Speed control methods:** (i) V/f control (most common via VFD), (ii) Changing number of poles, (iii) Rotor resistance control (slip-ring only).
- [ ] **Applications:** Fans, pumps, compressors, conveyors (cage); elevators, hoists, cranes (wound rotor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Numericals:** Always begin by computing $N_s$, then $s$, then $f_r$, then any power stage using the relation $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$. Draw the power-flow diagram in your answer script — KTU examiners award extra credit for clean, labelled diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction and Principle of Operation, Torque Slip Characteristics, Method of Starting, Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Construction and Principle of Operation, Torque Slip Characteristics, Method of Starting, Applications ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Three-Phase Induction Motor (Asynchronous Motor):** A self-starting, brushless, rugged AC machine in which a three-phase supply fed to the stator produces a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)**, which induces an EMF in the short-circuited rotor by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, and the resulting induced current interacting with the stator's magnetic field develops a mechanical torque (via **Lorentz Force**) that drags the rotor along the direction of the RMF. The rotor *never* catches up with the rotating magnetic field — the very *induction* mechanism requires a **relative speed difference (slip)** between the two. This is why it is called an "asynchronous" machine. **Key Synchronous Speed Equation:** $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$ Where: - $N_s$ = synchronous speed in **rpm** - $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz** (standard Indian value: **50 Hz**) - $P$ = total number of stator poles (must be an even integer: 2, 4, 6, 8…) ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) > [!IMPORTANT] > **"The Magnet on a Stick" Analogy:** Imagine you are sitting in the centre of a circular pond with a long stick. At the end of the stick, a bar magnet is tied. You rotate the stick at a constant speed — the magnet moves in a circle around a small metal ball floating on the water surface. The ball is initially stationary, so the magnet's field sweeps past it, **inducing eddy currents** in the ball. These currents turn the ball into a temporary magnet, which is *attracted* by the moving bar magnet and starts chasing it. The ball spins in the same direction as the stick, but **slightly slower** — because if it caught up, the magnet would be stationary *relative* to the ball, no currents would be induced, and there would be no force. - **You rotating the stick** = the **stator's 3-phase winding** producing a Rotating Magnetic Field - **The bar magnet** = the **stator's RMF** - **The metal ball** = the **short-circuited rotor** - **The ball always lagging behind** = the **slip** ($s$) ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Indian / KTU Value | |---|---| | Supply Frequency ($f$) | **50 Hz** | | Common Pole Counts ($P$) | 2, 4, 6, 8 | | Industrial Voltages | **415 V (3-phase, line)** | | Standard Slip Range (Full Load) | **2 % to 5 %** | | Typical Efficiency | **85 % to 95 %** | --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Construction — The Two Main Parts #### A. Stator (The Stationary Part) - A **hollow cylindrical core** built from **thin silicon-steel laminations** (each lamination typically 0.35 mm – 0.5 mm thick) to minimise **eddy-current losses**. - The inner periphery has evenly-spaced **slots** (semi-closed type is most common). - A **three-phase winding** (each phase physically displaced by **120° electrical**) is placed in these slots. - The three phases are named **R, Y, B** (or A, B, C) and are connected either in **Star (Y)** or **Delta (Δ)**. - The stator frame is made of cast iron / cast aluminium for mechanical strength and ventilation. > [!NOTE] > **Why laminations?** A solid iron core would allow circulating eddy currents when subjected to alternating flux, wasting energy as heat. Laminations insulated from each other break the path of these currents, dramatically reducing losses. #### B. Rotor (The Rotating Part) — Two Designs | Feature | Squirrel-Cage Rotor | Wound / Slip-Ring Rotor | |---|---|---| | Construction | Conducting bars (aluminium / copper) embedded in rotor slots, **all permanently shorted by two end-rings** | Insulated windings on rotor slots, **similar to stator winding**, connected in Star internally | | External Access | **None** — fully self-contained | Brought out via **slip rings and carbon brushes** | | Starting Methods | Limited (DOL, Star-Delta, Autotransformer) | External rotor resistance can be added | | Cost & Maintenance | **Low** — most common, highly rugged | **High** — needs brush-gear maintenance | | Typical Use | Fans, pumps, compressors | Cranes, hoists, elevators (high inertia) | ### 2.2 Principle of Operation (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Three-phase AC supply** is given to the stator windings. The three currents are displaced in time by **120°**. 2. These three time-displaced currents create a magnetic field whose **axis physically rotates** in space at synchronous speed $N_s$. This is the **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)**. 3. At the moment of switching on, the rotor is **at rest** ($N_r = 0$), so the RMF sweeps past stationary rotor conductors at the **maximum relative speed**. 4. By **Faraday's Law**, this relative motion **induces an EMF** in the rotor bars. By **Lenz's Law**, the induced current flows in a direction that *opposes* the relative motion. 5. The rotor conductors (now carrying current) lie within the stator's magnetic field. By the **Lorentz Force** ($\vec{F} = I \vec{\ell} \times \vec{B}$), a force acts on every conductor. 6. The collective tangential forces produce a **mechanical torque** that accelerates the rotor in the **same direction** as the RMF. 7. As the rotor accelerates, the **relative speed** $(N_s - N_r)$ decreases → less induced EMF → less rotor current → less torque. The rotor settles at a steady speed where the developed torque exactly balances the load torque. 8. The rotor can **never reach** $N_s$ — if it did, slip would be zero, no EMF would be induced, no current, no torque. This self-regulating equilibrium is the *fundamental reason* induction motors are called **asynchronous**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Significance:** Induction motors are the **workhorse of industry** — they consume roughly **60–70 % of all industrial electrical energy worldwide** because they are cheap, rugged, brushless, and explosion-proof (no sparking). ### 2.3 Slip — The Heart of Induction Motor Theory $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{\omega_s - \omega_r}{\omega_s}$$ > [!NOTE] > - $s = 1$ → standstill (just switched on) > - $s = 0$ → hypothetical synchronous speed (theoretical, never reached) > - $s = s_{fl}$ → full-load slip (typically **0.02 to 0.05**) > - Rotor actual speed: $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ > - Rotor induced frequency: $f_r = s \cdot f$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the table below. Every KTU ESE question in this module reduces to one or more of these equations.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | **rpm** | | 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless (per unit or %) | | 3 | Rotor actual speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | rpm | | 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | | 5 | Rotor EMF (running) | $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$ | $E_2$ = standstill rotor EMF/phase | | 6 | Rotor reactance (running) | $X_{2r} = s \cdot X_2$ | $X_2$ = standstill rotor reactance/phase | | 7 | Rotor impedance | $Z_{2r} = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ | $\Omega$ per phase | | 8 | Rotor current (per phase) | $I_2 = \dfrac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$ | Ampere | | 9 | Power factor of rotor | $\cos \phi_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$ | lagging | | 10 | Torque equation | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ | **N·m** | | 11 | Slip at maximum torque | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Maximum (breakdown) torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \, \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{X_2}$ | **independent of $R_2$!** | | 13 | Starting torque ($s = 1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{R_2 \, E_2^2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | | 14 | Ratio of torques | $\dfrac{T}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s}}$ | **Useful for finding slip at any torque** | | 15 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P/2}$ | rad/s | | 16 | Ratio of max to full-load torque | $\dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \dfrac{1}{2 s_m} \left( \dfrac{s_{fl}}{s_m} + \dfrac{s_m}{s_{fl}} \right)$ | often used to find $R_2$ added | | 17 | Gross mech. power developed | $P_m = \dfrac{3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2 \, (1 - s)}{s}$ | Watts (air-gap power × (1 − s)) | | 18 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{cu,rotor} = \dfrac{3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2}{s}$ | Watts | | 19 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = P_{cu,rotor} + P_m = \dfrac{3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2}{s}$ | Watts | | 20 | Air-gap → Mech. Power | $P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_{ag}$ | **fundamental energy flow** | --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Production of Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) Consider a balanced 3-phase supply: $$i_R = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$i_Y = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ The flux produced by each phase coil (whose axis is 120° apart in space) can be written as: $$\phi_R = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$\phi_Y = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$\phi_B = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ Resolving along the **X-axis** (horizontal) and **Y-axis** (vertical) — the **R-phase coil axis** is along Y, Y-phase is 120° from Y, B-phase is 240° from Y. **Resultant X-component:** $$\begin{aligned} \phi_X &= \phi_R \cos(90°) + \phi_Y \cos(90° + 120°) + \phi_B \cos(90° + 240°) \\ &= 0 + \phi_Y \cos(210°) + \phi_B \cos(330°) \\ &= -\phi_Y \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \phi_B \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned}$$ Substituting the flux expressions and simplifying (using standard trigonometric identities), we get: $$\phi_X = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, \phi_m \, \cos(\omega t)$$ **Resultant Y-component:** $$\begin{aligned} \phi_Y' &= \phi_R \sin(90°) + \phi_Y \sin(90° + 120°) + \phi_B \sin(90° + 240°) \\ &= \phi_R \cdot 1 + \phi_Y \cdot \sin(210°) + \phi_B \cdot \sin(330°) \\ &= \phi_R - \frac{1}{2}\phi_Y - \frac{1}{2}\phi_B \end{aligned}$$ After substitution and simplification: $$\phi_Y' = \frac{3}{2} \, \phi_m \, \sin(\omega t)$$ **Resultant flux magnitude:** $$\phi_{res} = \sqrt{\phi_X^2 + (\phi_Y')^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\phi_m\right)^2 \cos^2(\omega t) + \left(\frac{3}{2}\phi_m\right)^2 \sin^2(\omega t)} = \frac{3}{2} \phi_m$$ **Resultant flux direction (angle θ from X-axis):** $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\phi_Y'}{\phi_X}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{3}{2}\phi_m \sin(\omega t)}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\phi_m \cos(\omega t)}\right) = \omega t$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The resultant flux is **constant in magnitude** (equal to $\frac{3}{2} \phi_m$) and **rotates in space at synchronous angular speed** $\omega$ rad/s. This is the RMF. The "120° time-displaced" three currents in "120° space-displaced" coils jointly produce a constant-amplitude, uniformly-rotating magnetic field. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Rotor EMF, Current and Impedance When the rotor is stationary, let $E_2$ be the EMF induced per phase and $X_2 = 2\pi f L_2$ be the standstill reactance. When the rotor runs at slip $s$: - The relative speed between RMF and rotor is $(N_s - N_r) = s \, N_s$ - The frequency of rotor flux variation becomes: $f_r = s \, f$ By the transformer EMF equation (rotor essentially behaves like a short-circuited secondary of a rotating transformer): $$E_{2r} = 4.44 \, f_r \, N_2 \, \phi_m = 4.44 \, (s f) \, N_2 \, \phi_m = s \cdot E_2$$ And rotor reactance at slip: $$X_{2r} = 2 \pi f_r L_2 = 2 \pi (s f) L_2 = s \cdot X_2$$ Total rotor impedance per phase: $$Z_{2r} = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$ Rotor current per phase: $$I_2 = \frac{E_{2r}}{Z_{2r}} = \frac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$$ Rotor power factor: $$\cos \phi_2 = \frac{R_2}{Z_{2r}} = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Complete Torque Equation (Step-by-Step, No Steps Skipped) **Step 1 — Mechanical Power Developed (Power in the Air-Gap Transferred to Rotor):** Air-gap power per phase = $I_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s$ (this is the total per-phase power crossing the air-gap; the term $R_2/s$ is the *equivalent resistance* that includes the mechanical load). Total 3-phase air-gap power: $$P_{ag} = \frac{3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2}{s}$$ Of this air-gap power: - Fraction $s$ is lost as **rotor copper loss** ($I_2^2 R_2$ per phase × 3) - Fraction $(1 - s)$ is converted to **gross mechanical power** $P_m$ Therefore: $$P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_{ag} = (1 - s) \cdot \frac{3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2}{s}$$ **Step 2 — Convert Mechanical Power to Torque:** Mechanical power = Torque × Angular velocity of rotor $$P_m = T \cdot \omega_r = T \cdot \omega_s (1 - s)$$ Equating with the previous expression: $$T \cdot \omega_s (1 - s) = (1 - s) \cdot \frac{3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2}{s}$$ **Step 3 — Cancel $(1 - s)$ on both sides:** $$T \cdot \omega_s = \frac{3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2}{s}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $I_2$:** $$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{I_2^2 \, R_2}{s} \\ &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot \left( \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}} \right)^2 \\ &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} \\ &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\,T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}\,}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Slip at Maximum Torque & Value of $T_{max}$ The torque equation is: $$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$$ To find the slip at which $T$ is maximum, differentiate $T$ with respect to $s$ and set $\frac{dT}{ds} = 0$: $$\frac{dT}{ds} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) \cdot 1 - s \cdot (2 s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2} = 0$$ Numerator must equal zero: $$R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$ $$R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$ $$R_2^2 = s^2 X_2^2$$ $$\boxed{\,s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $s_m$ back into the torque expression to get $T_{max}$:** $$R_2 = s_m X_2 \implies R_2^2 + s_m^2 X_2^2 = s_m^2 X_2^2 + s_m^2 X_2^2 = 2 s_m^2 X_2^2$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} T_{max} &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s_m \, E_2^2 \, (s_m X_2)}{2 s_m^2 X_2^2} \\ &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s_m^2 X_2 \, E_2^2}{2 s_m^2 X_2^2} \\ &= \frac{3}{2 \, \omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{X_2} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\,T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \, \omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{X_2} \quad \text{(independent of rotor resistance)}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight (huge KTU exam implication):** $T_{max}$ does **not** depend on $R_2$, but the *slip at which it occurs* **does** depend on $R_2$. This is why adding external rotor resistance in slip-ring motors **shifts the maximum-torque point toward higher slip** (good for starting high-inertia loads) without reducing the maximum torque itself. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: General Torque Ratio (Useful for Solving Numericals) Dividing the general torque equation by the maximum torque expression: $$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{ \dfrac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2} }{ \dfrac{E_2^2}{2 X_2} } = \frac{2 s \, X_2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$$ Substitute $R_2 = s_m X_2$: $$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 s \, X_2 \cdot s_m X_2}{s_m^2 X_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2} = \frac{2 s \, s_m}{s_m^2 + s^2} = \frac{2}{\frac{s}{s_m} + \frac{s_m}{s}}$$ $$\boxed{\,\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\frac{s}{s_m} + \frac{s_m}{s}}\,}$$ This single formula is **sufficient to solve almost every KTU torque-numerical** in Module 2. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Power Flow Inside a Three-Phase Induction Motor ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Stator Core
3-Phase Winding
415 V 50 Hz"] --> B["Rotating Magnetic Field
RMF at Ns = 120f / P"] B --> C["Air-Gap Power Transfer
Pag = 3 I2 squared R2 / s"] C --> D["Rotor Copper Loss
Pcu = s . Pag"] C --> E["Gross Mechanical Power
Pm = 1 - s . Pag"] E --> F["Shaft Mechanical Power
Pout = Pm - Friction - Windage"] D --> G["Heat Dissipation
Cooling Fins / Fan"] F --> H["Mechanical Load
Pump / Fan / Compressor"] B -.->|Induces EMF| I["Rotor Circuit
R2 + j s X2"] I --> C ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Power enters as **electrical input** at the stator, becomes **air-gap power** via induction, splits into **rotor copper loss** (heat) and **gross mechanical power**, which finally exits as **shaft power** to the load. This single diagram is worth 3–5 marks in KTU ESE if reproduced neatly. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Function of Each Constructional Component ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Frame["Mechanical Structure"] F1["Cast Iron Frame
Mechanical Strength + Cooling"] end subgraph StatorSection["Stator (Stationary)"] S1["Laminated Si-Steel Core
Eddy Current Reduction"] S2["3-Phase Distributed Winding
120 degree Electrical Displacement"] S3["Terminal Box
Star or Delta Connection"] end subgraph Airgap1["Air Gap (0.4 to 4 mm)"] AG["Magnetic Field Coupling Zone"] end subgraph RotorSection["Rotor (Rotating)"] R1["Shaft
Forged Steel"] R2["Laminated Rotor Core
Slots for Conductors"] R3A["Option A: Squirrel Cage
Al Bars + End Rings"] R3B["Option B: Wound Rotor
3-Phase Winding + Slip Rings"] end subgraph BrushGear["External Access (Wound Rotor Only)"] BG1["Carbon Brushes"] BG2["External Rotor Resistance
Starting / Speed Control"] end F1 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S2 --> AG AG --> R2 R2 --> R3A R2 --> R3B R3B --> BG1 BG1 --> BG2 R2 --> R1 ``` --- ### 4.3 Conceptual Torque-Slip Characteristic (Block-Level Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Slip s = 0
Theoretical
Ns"] -->|"Region 1
Low slip"| B["Stable Operating Zone
Near Synchronous Speed
Motoring Mode"] B -->|"Region 2
Slip increases"| C["Point of Max Torque
Sm = R2 / X2
Breakdown Torque"] C -->|"Region 3
Higher slip"| D["Pull-out Point
Instability"] D -->|"Region 4
Slip approaches 1"| E["s = 1
Standstill
Starting Torque Ts"] E -->|"Reverse RMF"| F["Negative Slip
Generating / Braking Mode"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **The slope near $s = 0$ is steep** (normal running), and **the slope near $s = 1$ depends on $R_2$** — high rotor resistance gives high starting torque but poor running efficiency. This trade-off is the central engineering compromise in induction motor design. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]** **"Why is an induction motor called an 'asynchronous' machine? Define slip. A 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor runs at 1440 rpm. Calculate its slip."** **Model Answer:** An induction motor is called **asynchronous** because its rotor can never rotate at the synchronous speed of the stator's rotating magnetic field — there is always a small relative speed difference between them. The induction principle (Faraday's law) requires this difference; if the rotor reached synchronous speed, no relative motion would exist, no EMF would be induced, no rotor current would flow, and consequently no torque would be developed. Hence the rotor always *asynchronously* lags the field. **Slip ($s$)** is defined as the ratio of the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor to the synchronous speed: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ **Given:** $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $N_r = 1440$ rpm Synchronous speed: $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ Slip: $$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 = 4\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Memorise:** $s = 0.04$ corresponds to full-load for a 4-pole, 50 Hz standard industrial motor. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 Marks]** **"List any six applications of three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors. Why are they preferred for fixed-speed industrial drives?"** **Model Answer:** **Six Applications of 3-Phase Squirrel-Cage Induction Motors:** 1. **Centrifugal pumps** (water supply, irrigation, boiler feed pumps) 2. **Industrial fans and blowers** (HVAC, induced-draft fans in boilers) 3. **Air and gas compressors** (refrigeration, pneumatic systems) 4. **Conveyor belts** (mining, packaging, assembly lines) 5. **Machine tool spindles** (lathes, milling machines, drill presses) 6. **Mixers, agitators, and crushers** (chemical, food, and cement industries) **Reasons for preference in fixed-speed drives:** - **Self-starting** (no auxiliary starting device needed for DOL start) - **Robust, brushless, almost maintenance-free** construction - **Low initial cost** and **high reliability** - **Constant speed** characteristic close to synchronous (slip only 2–5 %) - **No risk of sparking** — suitable for hazardous / explosive environments - **Wide range of standard ratings** available off-the-shelf (0.5 kW to several MW) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Squirrel-Cage / Wound-Rotor / Torque-Slip Analysis* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and principle of operation of a **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor**. Discuss the production of a **rotating magnetic field** with a mathematical proof. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, **415 V** induction motor has the following per-phase standstill rotor quantities referred to stator: $E_2 = 100$ V, $R_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 2 \, \Omega$. For a slip of **4 %**, calculate: (i) Synchronous speed $N_s$ (ii) Rotor speed $N_r$ (iii) Rotor frequency $f_r$ (iv) Rotor induced EMF $E_{2r}$ and reactance $X_{2r}$ (v) Rotor current $I_2$ and power factor $\cos \phi_2$ (vi) Developed torque $T$ **(7 marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Construction & Principle:** [See SECTION 1 and SECTION 2 of these notes for the complete construction description (stator, rotor types) and SECTION 3.1 for the full mathematical proof of RMF production. The student should reproduce the waveform diagram, flux-resolution equations, and the final conclusion: $\phi_{res} = \frac{3}{2}\phi_m = $ constant, with $\theta = \omega t$.] **Key steps to write in exam:** 1. State the three instantaneous currents and fluxes with 120° time displacement. 2. Resolve fluxes along orthogonal axes. 3. Derive $\phi_X = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ and $\phi_Y' = \frac{3}{2}\phi_m \sin(\omega t)$. 4. Show $\phi_{res} = \frac{3}{2}\phi_m$ (constant) and $\theta = \omega t$ (rotating at $\omega$). 5. Conclude: this rotating field cuts stationary rotor conductors, induces EMF, current flows, Lorentz force produces torque. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $E_2 = 100$ V, $R_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 2 \, \Omega$, $s = 0.04$ **(i) Synchronous speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **(ii) Rotor speed:** $$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$ **(iii) Rotor frequency:** $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$ **(iv) Rotor EMF and reactance (running):** $$E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2 = 0.04 \times 100 = 4 \text{ V per phase}$$ $$X_{2r} = s \cdot X_2 = 0.04 \times 2 = 0.08 \, \Omega \text{ per phase}$$ **(v) Rotor current and power factor:** $$Z_{2r} = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.08)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.0064} = \sqrt{0.2564} = 0.5064 \, \Omega$$ $$I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{Z_{2r}} = \frac{4}{0.5064} = 7.90 \text{ A (per phase)}$$ $$\cos \phi_2 = \frac{R_2}{Z_{2r}} = \frac{0.5}{0.5064} = 0.9873 \text{ (lagging)}$$ **(vi) Developed torque:** First, synchronous angular speed: $$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$ Using torque formula: $$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$ $$T = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times (100)^2 \times 0.5}{(0.5)^2 + (0.08)^2}$$ $$T = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 10000 \times 0.5}{0.2564}$$ $$T = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{200}{0.2564} = \frac{3}{157.08} \times 780.03 = 14.90 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 14.9 \text{ N·m}}$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Starting Methods & Maximum Torque* **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of the **torque-slip characteristic curve**, explain the following: starting torque, pull-out (maximum) torque, full-load torque, and slip at maximum torque. Show mathematically that **maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance** but the **slip at which it occurs is directly proportional to rotor resistance**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, **6-pole, 50 Hz** induction motor has a rotor resistance per phase of $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance per phase of $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$. Determine: (i) Slip at maximum torque (ii) The value of additional rotor resistance required to **double the starting torque** (i.e., make $T_{st}' = 2 T_{st}$) (iii) The new slip at maximum torque after adding this resistance (iv) Comment on the effect of adding rotor resistance on starting current and starting torque. **(7 marks)** **Complete Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Torque-Slip Characteristic Explanation:** The torque-slip curve of an induction motor is divided into four key regions: 1. **Region 1 (Low slip, $s \approx 0$ to $s_{fl}$):** Stable motoring region. The motor runs near synchronous speed. Torque is approximately proportional to slip. Normal operating point lies here. 2. **Region 2 ($s = s_{fl}$):** Full-load point where developed torque equals load torque. 3. **Region 3 ($s = s_m$):** **Maximum torque / pull-out torque**. Beyond this, any further increase in load causes the motor to stall. 4. **Region 4 (High slip, $s \to 1$):** Starting condition. Torque at $s = 1$ is the **starting torque**. **Mathematical proof** — see SECTION 3.4 of these notes. From the condition $\frac{dT}{ds} = 0$ we obtained: $$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{X_2}$$ **Conclusion:** - $T_{max}$ depends only on $E_2$ and $X_2$ (stator supply and motor design) — **independent of $R_2$**. - $s_m$ is **directly proportional to $R_2$** — doubling $R_2$ doubles the slip at which max torque occurs. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_2 = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$ **(i) Slip at maximum torque (original):** $$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2 = 20\%$$ **(ii) Additional rotor resistance to double starting torque:** Starting torque (original, $s = 1$): $$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2 \, E_2^2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$ Let new total rotor resistance be $R_2' = R_2 + R_{ext}$. New starting torque: $$T_{st}' = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2' \, E_2^2}{(R_2')^2 + X_2^2}$$ For $T_{st}' = 2 \, T_{st}$: $$\frac{R_2'}{(R_2')^2 + 1^2} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.2}{(0.2)^2 + 1^2} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.2}{0.04 + 1} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.2}{1.04} = \frac{0.4}{1.04}$$ $$\frac{R_2'}{(R_2')^2 + 1} = 0.3846$$ Let $y = R_2'$. Then: $$0.3846 \, y^2 - y + 0.3846 = 0$$ Multiply by 10000 / 3846 (or solve via quadratic formula): $$y = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4 \times 0.3846 \times 0.3846}}{2 \times 0.3846}$$ $$y = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.5917}}{0.7692} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{0.4083}}{0.7692} = \frac{1 \pm 0.6390}{0.7692}$$ Two solutions: - $y_1 = \dfrac{1.6390}{0.7692} = 2.131 \, \Omega$ (larger root → double starting torque) - $y_2 = \dfrac{0.3610}{0.7692} = 0.4693 \, \Omega$ (smaller root → also doubles starting torque but only in the "high-torque" region) **Practical choice:** $R_2' = 2.131 \, \Omega$ (the higher value, as it is achievable and stable). Additional external resistance required: $$R_{ext} = R_2' - R_2 = 2.131 - 0.2 = 1.931 \, \Omega \approx 1.93 \, \Omega$$ **(iii) New slip at maximum torque:** $$s_m' = \frac{R_2'}{X_2} = \frac{2.131}{1.0} = 2.131$$ Since $s_m' > 1$, this means **maximum torque is not reached in the motoring region** — the new characteristic has the entire $s = 0$ to $s = 1$ region on the rising side, with starting torque itself approaching the maximum torque. **(iv) Effect of adding rotor resistance:** - **Starting torque:** **Increases** significantly (in our case, doubled). - **Starting current:** **Decreases** — because the added resistance dominates the impedance at $s = 1$. - **Slip at max torque:** **Increases** (shifts to the right on the slip axis). - **Maximum torque:** **Remains unchanged** (independent of $R_2$). - **Running efficiency:** Slightly decreases at normal slip because more rotor copper loss occurs. - **Speed regulation:** Improves under load transients. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert $f$ to per-phase or vice versa:** The torque equation uses **per-phase EMF** $E_2$, not line voltage. Mixing up 415 V and 240 V costs full marks. > 2. **Using $N_s$ in rad/s but writing $2\pi N_s$ incorrectly:** $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60}$, **not** $2\pi N_s$. > 3. **Forgetting to square $E_2$** in torque formula: $E_2^2$, not $E_2$. > 4. **Stopping derivation at $s_m = R_2/X_2$** without substituting back to get $T_{max}$ — the question asks for $T_{max}$, so the second step is mandatory. > 5. **Confusing air-gap power and mechanical power** in power-flow diagram (SECTION 4.1) — remember $P_{ag} = P_{cu,rotor} + P_m$ and $P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag}$. > 6. **Stating "induction motor is self-starting" without explaining why** — the RMF itself provides the starting torque; you must link to the principle of operation. > 7. **Saying "rotor current is DC"** — it is **AC** at frequency $f_r = s f$. This is a very common blunder. > 8. **Wrong polarity in Star-Delta starter** — if connected in reverse, the motor will run in the **reverse direction**. Always double-check. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rules for KTU 2024 ESE — Induction Motor Module:** - ✅ Induction motor is **self-starting** because the RMF is already rotating at the moment of switch-on. - ✅ Rotor frequency = **$s f$**, rotor EMF = **$s E_2$**, rotor reactance = **$s X_2$** — all quantities scale linearly with slip. - ✅ Slip $s$ is always between **0 and 1** in motoring mode. - ✅ **$T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$** — it depends only on $E_2$ (stator supply) and $X_2$ (rotor leakage reactance). - ✅ **$s_m = R_2 / X_2$** — slip at max torque is directly proportional to rotor resistance. - ✅ In a **squirrel-cage motor**, you cannot add external resistance — so starting torque is limited. This is why high-starting-torque applications use slip-ring motors. - ✅ **Star-Delta starting** reduces starting current to **1/3** of direct-on-line, and starting torque to **1/3** of DOL — useful only for light-load-start applications. - ✅ **Autotransformer starting** can be set to tap (say 50 %, 65 %, 80 %) — starting current and torque both reduce as the **square of the tapping ratio**. - ✅ **Rotor resistance starting** (slip-ring) increases starting torque AND decreases starting current — the only method that does both favourably. - ✅ Rotor copper loss = $s \times P_{ag}$ (i.e., a fraction $s$ of air-gap power is always lost as heat — fundamental thermodynamic penalty of induction). - ✅ Gross mechanical power = $(1 - s) \times P_{ag}$ — the remaining useful power. - ✅ Three-phase induction motor is the **most manufactured electrical machine in the world** — over **90 %** of all industrial motors. - ✅ Always remember: rotor **never** reaches synchronous speed under any condition (only theoretically at no-load friction = 0). - ✅ Energy flow: **Input → Stator Cu + Stator Core + Friction/Windage losses → Air-gap power $P_{ag}$ → Rotor Cu loss ($s P_{ag}$) + Mechanical power $P_m$ → Shaft output $P_{out}$**. ---

topics.title: Fundamentals of Motors Used in EVs, Motor Control for BLDC Motors, Analyse EVs Based on Power Sources and Calculate Range of an EV. Perform Motor Power and Torque Calculations to Select a Motor to Build Their Own EV

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamentals of Motors Used in Electric Vehicles (EVs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** An **Electric Vehicle (EV) motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device specifically engineered to deliver high starting torque, wide constant-power operating range, high efficiency (>90%), and excellent regenerative braking capability for traction applications.

An EV motor is the **heart of the electric powertrain**. Unlike industrial motors, traction motors must perform across a highly dynamic load profile — accelerating a 1500 kg car from standstill, cruising on highways, and recovering energy during braking. The four most dominant motor technologies in modern EVs are:

| Motor Type | Symbol | Defining Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Brushless DC Motor** | BLDC | Permanent magnet rotor, trapezoidal back-EMF |
| **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor** | PMSM | Sinusoidal back-EMF, FOC control |
| **Induction Motor** | IM | Squirrel-cage rotor, no magnets, rugged |
| **Switched Reluctance Motor** | SRM | Salient-pole rotor, no rotor windings/magnets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an EV motor like the human heart. Just as the heart pumps blood with varying intensity (resting vs. running) and never stops, the EV motor delivers mechanical "blood-flow" (torque) in bursts during acceleration and steady streams during cruising. The **controller** is the brain that decides how hard the heart should pump at any instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight:** Tesla Model 3 uses a **PMSM** (rear) and **IM** (front); Tata Nexon EV uses a **PMSM**; Tesla Model S Plaid uses **carbon-wrapped rotor PMSM**; Mahindra eVerito uses a **3-phase AC Induction motor**.

---

## 1.2 BLDC Motor Control — Intuitive Overview

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** is a synchronous motor with permanent magnets on the rotor and a wound stator. "Brushless" means the commutation — the switching of current between phases — is done **electronically** by an **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)** instead of mechanical brushes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a 360° revolving door at a hotel. A traditional brushed DC motor has a *doorman inside* (brushes) physically pushing people (current) to the next section. A BLDC motor **removes the doorman entirely** — a smart computer outside (ESC) decides exactly when to open each section based on door position sensors. Result: no friction, no wear, higher efficiency.

The motor requires **rotor position feedback** (via Hall-effect sensors or sensorless back-EMF detection) to commutate the six switches of a three-phase inverter at precisely the right electrical angle.

---

## 1.3 EV Classification Based on Power Source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** EVs are classified by the **energy source** that ultimately powers the traction motor. This is the most common academic classification scheme tested in KTU examinations.

| EV Type | Abbreviation | Power Source | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery Electric Vehicle** | BEV | Rechargeable Li-ion battery only | Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3 |
| **Plug-in Hybrid EV** | PHEV | Battery + ICE (plug-in chargeable) | Toyota Prius Prime |
| **Hybrid EV** | HEV | Battery + ICE (no plug) | Toyota Camry Hybrid |
| **Fuel Cell EV** | FCEV | Hydrogen fuel cell | Hyundai Nexo, Toyota Mirai |
| **Solar EV** | SEV | Photovoltaic panels + battery | Lightyear 0 (concept) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of these as different "diets" for a car. **BEV** eats only electricity from the wall socket. **HEV** snacks on petrol but recharges its small battery from the engine. **PHEV** eats both — petrol *and* wall-plug electricity. **FCEV** "drinks" hydrogen and exhales water vapour. **SEV** photosynthesises sunlight like a plant.

---

## 1.4 EV Range — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** **EV Range** is the total distance (in km) an electric vehicle can travel on a single full charge of its battery, under specified driving conditions (NEDC / WLTP / EPA standard cycles).

The standard test cycles differ significantly — **WLTP** is more realistic than the older **NEDC**, and **EPA** is the most conservative (lowest range). KTU problems typically assume a single **energy consumption constant** in Wh/km unless stated otherwise.

---

## 1.5 Motor Power and Torque Calculations — Intuition

**Power** is the rate of doing work (W or kW). **Torque** is the rotational equivalent of force (N·m). For a vehicle wheel:

$$P = T \cdot \omega$$

where $\omega$ is angular velocity in **rad/s**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine pedalling a bicycle. **Torque** is how *hard* you push the pedal (force × crank length). **Power** is how *fast* you turn the crank while pushing that hard. A cyclist on a flat road needs less torque but more pedalling speed (power). Climbing a hill needs huge torque at low pedalling speed (high power too, but limited by muscle).

The **tractive force** equation — the single most important equation in EV design — captures every resistance a car must overcome to move:

$$F_{tr} = m \cdot a + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

where every term represents a force fighting motion: **inertia**, **hill climb**, **rolling resistance**, and **aerodynamic drag**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a BLDC Motor

A 3-phase BLDC motor has **three stator windings (A, B, C)** physically displaced by **120° electrical**. The rotor carries **surface-mounted or interior permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB — Neodymium Iron Boron, $B_r \approx$ **1.2 T**).

**Sequence of commutation (six-step trapezoidal):**

1. The **ESC** energises two phases at a time (one high-side MOSFET, one low-side).
2. The **third phase is OFF** and is used to sense **back-EMF** for sensorless commutation.
3. There are **6 commutation steps per electrical revolution** (60° each).
4. Each step is triggered by rotor position feedback from **3 Hall-effect sensors** (mounted 120° apart) or by back-EMF zero-crossing detection.
5. Electrical speed $f_e$ relates to mechanical speed $n_m$ by $f_e = (P/2) \cdot n_m$ where $P$ is the number of poles.

> [!TIP]
> **Why BLDC is dominant in 2/3-wheelers:** High power density, simple 6-step control (cheap microcontroller like STM32 or TI C2000), excellent efficiency at low-to-mid speeds. KTU frequently tests the relationship between **electrical and mechanical degrees**.

---

## 2.2 Tractive Force Breakdown — Why Each Term Exists

| Term | Physical Meaning | When It Dominates |
|---|---|---|
| $m \cdot a$ | **Inertial force** — Newton's 2nd law for acceleration | 0 → 60 km/h launch |
| $m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | **Gravitational component** along slope | Hill climbing |
| $m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta$ | **Rolling resistance** of tyres on road | All speeds (constant) |
| $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | **Aerodynamic drag** — proportional to $v^2$ | Highways > 60 km/h |

**Constants to remember (highlighted in bold as per protocol):**

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
- Air density at sea level: $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$
- Rolling resistance coefficient (car tyre on asphalt): $C_r \approx \mathbf{0.015}$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient (typical sedan): $C_d \approx \mathbf{0.30}$

---

## 2.3 EV Range — Theoretical Derivation Path

The range of an EV is governed by the **total usable battery energy** divided by the **energy consumed per kilometre**:

$$R = \frac{E_{batt}}{\varepsilon_{aux} \cdot e_c}$$

where:
- $E_{batt} = V_{batt} \cdot C_{batt}$ in **Wh** (battery nominal energy)
- $e_c$ = tractive energy per km in **Wh/km**
- $\varepsilon_{aux}$ = auxiliary load factor (HVAC, lights, electronics)

A more practical engineering form:

$$R = \frac{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inverter} \cdot \eta_{transmission} \cdot V_{batt} \cdot C_{batt}}{e_c \cdot 1000}$$

The drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{total} \approx \mathbf{0.85 \text{ to } 0.92}$ for modern EVs.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** Memorise this table — it covers >85% of numerical questions from this module.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Power, Torque, Angular velocity | W, N·m, rad/s | $\omega = \tfrac{2\pi n}{60}$ for RPM |
| 2 | $F_t = m a + m g \sin\theta + m g C_r \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | Total tractive force | N | All four forces needed for hill start |
| 3 | $T_{wheel} = F_t \cdot r$ | Wheel torque | N·m | $r$ = tyre radius |
| 4 | $T_{motor} = \tfrac{T_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}}$ | Motor torque after gearbox | N·m | $G$ = gear ratio |
| 5 | $n_{motor} = G \cdot n_{wheel}$ | Motor RPM from wheel RPM | rpm | $G$ > 1 = reduction |
| 6 | $P_{motor} = \tfrac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{motor}}$ | Required motor power | W | $v$ in m/s |
| 7 | $R = \tfrac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{total}}{e_c}$ | EV range | km | $E_{batt}$ in Wh, $e_c$ in Wh/km |
| 8 | $E_{batt} = V_{batt} \cdot C_{batt}$ | Battery energy | Wh | $C$ in Ah, $V$ in V |
| 9 | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | BLDC back-EMF | V | $K_e$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) |
| 10 | $T = K_t \cdot I$ | BLDC electromagnetic torque | N·m | $K_t = K_e$ in SI units |
| 11 | $E_{specific} = \tfrac{E_{batt}}{m_{batt}}$ | Battery specific energy | Wh/kg | Li-ion ≈ **150–250 Wh/kg** |
| 12 | $f_e = \tfrac{P}{2} \cdot \tfrac{n_m}{60}$ | Electrical frequency of BLDC | Hz | $P$ = number of poles |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tata Motors EV division** uses tractive-force analysis to size the PMSM in the Nexon EV.
- **ISRO's lithium-ion cells** for satellites use the same $E_{batt} = V \cdot C$ energy equation.
- **Formula Student EV teams** use $P = T \cdot \omega$ to design drivetrains.
- **BLDC controllers** in drone ECs (Electronic Speed Controllers) use the exact six-step commutation taught above — drones are a **multirotor application** of the same theory.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Vehicle Velocity to Motor RPM

**Step 1 — Convert linear velocity to wheel angular velocity.**

The wheel rolls without slipping. A point on the rim covers a distance equal to the arc length:
$$v = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}$$

**Step 2 — Convert wheel angular velocity to motor angular velocity using the gearbox ratio G.**

A reduction gearbox of ratio $G$ means the motor spins $G$ times faster than the wheel:
$$\omega_{motor} = G \cdot \omega_{wheel}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RPM.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_{wheel} &= \frac{v}{r_{wheel}} \quad \text{[rad/s]} \\
n_{wheel} &= \frac{60 \cdot \omega_{wheel}}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \cdot v}{2\pi \cdot r_{wheel}} \quad \text{[RPM]} \\
n_{motor} &= G \cdot n_{wheel} = \frac{60 \cdot G \cdot v}{2\pi \cdot r_{wheel}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.**

Let $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, $r_{wheel} = 0.3$ m, $G = 8$.

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{wheel} &= \frac{60 \times 25}{2\pi \times 0.3} = \frac{1500}{1.885} \approx 796 \text{ RPM} \\
n_{motor} &= 8 \times 796 = 6368 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches real EV motors which spin at **6000–15000 RPM**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tractive Force on a Slope

**Step 1 — Resolve gravity along the slope.**

For an incline of angle $\theta$, the component of weight pulling the car downhill is $mg \sin\theta$. The motor must supply this *uphill* to maintain constant speed.

**Step 2 — Resolve normal force (used in rolling resistance).**

The normal force on a slope is $N = mg \cos\theta$. The rolling resistance force is:
$$F_{roll} = C_r \cdot N = C_r \cdot m g \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive force** (neglecting drag for simplicity here):

$$F_t = m a + m g \sin\theta + m g C_r \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.**

A 1500 kg car accelerating at $1.5$ m/s² up a 10° slope with $C_r = 0.015$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{inertia} &= 1500 \times 1.5 = 2250 \text{ N} \\
F_{slope} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(10°) = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.1736 = 2555 \text{ N} \\
F_{roll} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times \cos(10°) = 217 \text{ N} \\
F_t &= 2250 + 2555 + 217 = 5022 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **force the tyres must deliver to the road** — the starting point for motor sizing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Motor Power

**Step 1 — Power at the wheel-road contact patch.**

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 2 — Account for drivetrain losses** (gearbox, inverter, motor internal losses).

$$P_{motor,electrical} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{inverter}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**

Using the 5022 N from §3.2 at $v = 25$ m/s with $\eta_{total} = 0.88$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= 5022 \times 25 = 125{,}550 \text{ W} \approx 125.6 \text{ kW} \\
P_{motor,electrical} &= \frac{125{,}550}{0.88} \approx 142{,}670 \text{ W} \approx 143 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

This justifies the 100–150 kW motors used in mid-size EVs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: EV Range — Complete Analytical Walkthrough

**Given Data:**
- Battery: 400 V nominal, 50 kWh usable
- Vehicle mass: 1600 kg
- $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.3$ m², $C_r = 0.012$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.88$
- Cruise speed: 80 km/h $= 22.22$ m/s (no acceleration, level road)

**Step 1 — Compute tractive force at constant cruise.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{aero} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times (22.22)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times 493.8 \\
&= 194.6 \text{ N} \\
F_{roll} &= m g C_r = 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 188.4 \text{ N} \\
F_t &= 194.6 + 188.4 = 383 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute tractive power at wheel.**

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = 383 \times 22.22 = 8510 \text{ W} = 8.51 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute electrical power drawn from battery.**

$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta} = \frac{8.51}{0.88} = 9.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute energy per km consumed.**

$$e_c = \frac{P_{batt}}{v} = \frac{9670 \text{ W}}{22.22 \text{ m/s}} = 435.2 \text{ J/m} = 120.9 \text{ Wh/km}$$

**Step 5 — Compute range.**

$$R = \frac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta}{e_c} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.88}{120.9} = 364 \text{ km}$$

This matches the **real-world WLTP range of Tata Nexon EV (Long Range) ≈ 437 km** (their cycle is more optimistic; our calculation assumed constant 80 km/h, no regen benefit).

---

## 3.5 BLDC Torque–Speed–EMF Relationship (Derivation)

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law applied to a rotating PM.**

The flux linking one phase varies sinusoidally (or trapezoidally) as the rotor turns. The induced EMF (per phase) is:
$$e(t) = K_e \cdot \omega \cdot f(\theta_e)$$

For ideal BLDC, the average EMF over 60° conduction is:
$$E_{avg} = \frac{2 \cdot E_{peak}}{\pi} = K_e \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — KVL on a phase winding (during conduction).**

$$V_{DC} = E + I \cdot R_{phase} + L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

At steady state, $L \cdot di/dt \to 0$, so:
$$V_{DC} \approx E + I \cdot R_{phase}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for current.**

$$I = \frac{V_{DC} - K_e \cdot \omega}{R_{phase}}$$

**Step 4 — Torque produced.**

$$T = K_t \cdot I = K_t \cdot \frac{V_{DC} - K_e \cdot \omega}{R_{phase}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical insight:** When $\omega = 0$ (standstill), $T = K_t \cdot V_{DC}/R_{phase}$ — **maximum starting torque**. As $\omega$ rises, torque falls linearly. This is the **natural constant-torque region** of a BLDC.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Motor for an EV

| Decision Parameter | Engineering Question | KTU Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Power** | What is the maximum power for 0–100 km/h? | $P = F_t \cdot v / \eta$ |
| **Peak Torque** | What torque to launch from rest? | $T = F_t \cdot r / (G \cdot \eta)$ |
| **Top Speed Power** | Power needed to cruise at $v_{max}$? | $P = F_{aero+roll} \cdot v_{max} / \eta$ |
| **Max Motor RPM** | For $v_{max}$: | $n_{motor} = 60 G v_{max} / (2\pi r)$ |
| **Battery Energy** | For target range $R$ | $E_{batt} = R \cdot e_c / \eta$ |
| **Battery Capacity** | In Ah | $C = E_{batt} / V_{batt}$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EV Powertrain Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat1[Li-ion Battery Pack] --> bms1[Battery Management System BMS]
    bms1 --> inv1[Power Inverter DC to AC]
    inv1 --> mcu1[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    mcu1 --> mtr1[BLDC or PMSM Traction Motor]
    mtr1 --> gbx1[Single Speed Gearbox Ratio G]
    gbx1 --> whl1[Drive Wheels with Tyre Radius r]
    whl1 --> rd1[Road Surface]
    whl1 -. Regenerative Braking .-> mtr1
    mtr1 -. Three Phase AC .-> inv1
    inv1 -. DC Link .-> bms1
    rd1 -. Rolling Resistance Fr .-> whl1
    rd1 -. Aerodynamic Drag Fd .-> whl1
    rd1 -. Hill Component Fg .-> whl1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The dotted feedback lines show the **regenerative braking loop** (energy flows backward to recharge the battery) and the **road reaction forces** (rolling, drag, gravity) that the motor must overcome.

---

## 4.2 BLDC Six-Step Commutation Timing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as Hall Sensors
    participant C as ESC Controller
    participant A as Phase A
    participant B as Phase B
    participant C1 as Phase C
    Note over H,C1: Electrical angle 0 to 60 degrees
    H->>C: Hall pattern 101
    C->>A: Switch ON High
    C->>C1: Switch ON Low
    C->>B: OFF
    Note over H,C1: Current flows A to C
    Note over H,C1: Electrical angle 60 to 120 degrees
    H->>C: Hall pattern 100
    C->>A: Switch ON High
    C->>B: Switch ON Low
    C->>C1: OFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At every 60° electrical rotation, the controller turns ON a different pair of phases in a defined sequence (A→B→C). This creates a **rotating magnetic field** that drags the permanent-magnet rotor along — the essence of brushless operation.

---

## 4.3 Motor Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Define EV Use Case] --> q1A{Required Range km}
    q1A -- Greater than 200 --> bevA[Choose BEV with Li-ion Battery]
    q1A -- Less than 50 AND urban --> hevA[Choose HEV with Small Battery]
    q1A -- Heavy duty long haul --> fcevA[Choose FCEV with H2 Tank]
    startA --> q2A{Required Peak Power kW}
    q2A -- Less than 20 --> bldcA[Select BLDC for 2W 3W]
    q2A -- 50 to 150 --> pmsmA[Select PMSM for Passenger Car]
    q2A -- Greater than 200 --> imA[Select High Power IM or Dual Motor]
    startA --> q3A{Budget Constraint}
    q3A -- Low --> imB[AC Induction Motor cheapest]
    q3A -- High --> pmsmB[PMSM high efficiency]
    startA --> q4A{Rare Earth Availability}
    q4A -- Constrained --> srmA[Switched Reluctance Motor no magnets]
    q4A -- Available --> pmB[PM based motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This flowchart maps the four most common KTU-style design questions: range requirement, peak power, budget, and supply-chain constraints. Each path leads to an industry-justified motor technology choice.

---

## 4.4 BLDC Motor Controller — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr1[DC Battery Vdc] --> inv2[3 Phase MOSFET Inverter]
    inv2 --> phA[Phase A Winding]
    inv2 --> phB[Phase B Winding]
    inv2 --> phC[Phase C Winding]
    phA --> rot1[PM Rotor]
    phB --> rot1
    phC --> rot1
    rot1 --> hall2[Hall Position Sensors 3]
    hall2 --> mcu2[Microcontroller STM32 or TI C2000]
    mcu2 --> inv2
    thr1[Throttle Input Pedal] --> mcu2
    mcu2 --> pwm2[PWM Generator 20 kHz]
    pwm2 --> inv2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The dashed feedback line from rotor to Hall sensors to microcontroller to inverter is the **closed-loop commutation loop**. Without it, the BLDC motor cannot self-start. This is the key difference from a brushed DC motor where the brushes perform commutation mechanically.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Compare BLDC motor and PMSM for EV applications. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BLDC | PMSM |
|---|---|---|
| Back-EMF waveform | Trapezoidal | Sinusoidal |
| Control scheme | 6-step commutation | Field-Oriented Control (FOC) |
| Torque ripple | Higher | Lower (smoother) |
| Cost of controller | Lower (simple) | Higher (DSP needed) |
| Use case | 2W, 3W, drones | Premium cars (Tesla, Tata Nexon) |

**Advantage of BLDC:** Simple, low-cost 6-step control electronics → cheaper for mass-market 2/3 wheelers.
**Advantage of PMSM:** Smoother torque, higher efficiency at high speed → preferred for premium passenger cars.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for comparison table, 1 mark each for advantages)*
