### Question A (14 Marks): Motor Sizing for a Custom EV **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**Question (a) [7 marks]:**
A custom 3-wheeler EV is to be designed with the following specifications:
- Total mass $m = 600$ kg (including 150 kg battery)
- Tyre radius $r = 0.25$ m
- Rolling resistance coefficient $C_r = 0.018$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.4$, frontal area $A = 1.8$ m²
- Gear ratio $G = 6$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.85$
- Required top speed $v_{max} = 60$ km/h
- Required 0–30 km/h acceleration time = 6 seconds
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Calculate: **(i)** the **peak tractive force** required during 0–30 km/h acceleration on a **level road**, and **(ii)** the **motor peak power** and **peak torque** required.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the acceleration.**

$$a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{30 \text{ km/h}}{6 \text{ s}} = \frac{8.333 \text{ m/s}}{6 \text{ s}} = 1.389 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute all tractive force components during launch (level road, so $\sin\theta = 0$).**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{inertia} &= m \cdot a = 600 \times 1.389 = 833.3 \text{ N} \\
F_{roll} &= m g C_r = 600 \times 9.81 \times 0.018 = 105.95 \text{ N} \\
F_{aero} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.4 \times 1.8 \times (8.333)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.4 \times 1.8 \times 69.44 \\
&= 30.6 \text{ N} \quad \text{(negligible at low launch speed)} \\
F_{t,peak} &= 833.3 + 105.95 + 30.6 = 969.9 \text{ N} \approx 970 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute wheel torque required.**

$$T_{wheel} = F_t \cdot r = 970 \times 0.25 = 242.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute motor torque after gearbox losses.**

$$T_{motor} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta} = \frac{242.5}{6 \times 0.85} = \frac{242.5}{5.1} = 47.55 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute wheel and motor RPM at top speed.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{wheel} &= \frac{60 \times v}{2\pi r} = \frac{60 \times 16.67}{2\pi \times 0.25} = \frac{1000}{1.571} = 636.6 \text{ RPM} \\
n_{motor} &= G \times n_{wheel} = 6 \times 636.6 = 3820 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute peak motor power.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel,peak} &= F_t \cdot v = 970 \times 8.333 = 8083 \text{ W} \approx 8.08 \text{ kW} \\
P_{motor,peak} &= \frac{P_{wheel,peak}}{\eta} = \frac{8.08}{0.85} = 9.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer (Part a):** Peak tractive force ≈ **970 N**, peak motor torque ≈ **47.5 N·m**, peak motor power ≈ **9.5 kW**, max motor speed ≈ **3820 RPM**.

---

**Question (b) [7 marks]:**
Using the data above and assuming a **Li-ion battery** with nominal voltage $V = 72$ V, cell-level specific energy = 180 Wh/kg, and target **range of 80 km** with average energy consumption $e_c = 110$ Wh/km. Calculate: **(i)** the **required battery energy in kWh**, **(ii)** the **battery capacity in Ah**, and **(iii)** the **battery mass in kg**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required battery energy.**

$$E_{batt} = R \cdot e_c / \eta = 80 \times 110 / 0.85 = 8800 / 0.85 = 10{,}353 \text{ Wh} \approx 10.35 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Battery capacity in Ah.**

$$C = \frac{E_{batt}}{V} = \frac{10{,}353}{72} = 143.8 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Battery mass.**

$$m_{batt} = \frac{E_{batt}}{E_{specific}} = \frac{10{,}353}{180} = 57.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check.** Total vehicle mass was 600 kg including 150 kg battery — our calculated 57.5 kg battery leaves 92.5 kg margin for chassis + passengers. This is feasible. ✓

**Final Answer (Part b):** $E_{batt} \approx$ **10.35 kWh**, $C \approx$ **143.8 Ah**, $m_{batt} \approx$ **57.5 kg**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): EV Range Analysis and Source Comparison **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Question (a) [7 marks]:**
A BEV uses a 350 V, 100 Ah Li-ion battery pack. The vehicle consumes an average of **150 Wh/km** at a steady speed of 50 km/h on a level road. The drivetrain efficiency is 88%, and the auxiliary load (HVAC + electronics) draws a constant **400 W**.
**Calculate:** **(i)** the **total range**, **(ii)** the **range without considering auxiliary load** (i.e., ideal range), and **(iii)** the **percentage loss in range** due to the auxiliary load.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Battery total energy.**

$$E_{batt} = V \times C = 350 \times 100 = 35{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 35 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Usable energy for traction (full efficiency).**

$$E_{usable} = E_{batt} \times \eta = 35{,}000 \times 0.88 = 30{,}800 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 3 — Ideal range (no aux load).**

$$R_{ideal} = \frac{E_{usable}}{e_c} = \frac{30{,}800}{150} = 205.3 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Energy consumed per hour by aux load.**

$$E_{aux,per\,km} = \frac{400 \text{ W}}{50 \text{ km/h}} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ Wh/km}$$

**Step 5 — Effective consumption rate.**

$$e_{c,eff} = 150 + 8 = 158 \text{ Wh/km}$$

**Step 6 — Actual range with aux load.**

$$R_{actual} = \frac{30{,}800}{158} = 194.9 \text{ km}$$

**Step 7 — Percentage loss.**

$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{205.3 - 194.9}{205.3} \times 100 = \frac{10.4}{205.3} \times 100 = 5.07\%$$

**Final Answer (Part a):** $R_{ideal} \approx$ **205 km**, $R_{actual} \approx$ **195 km**, percentage loss $\approx$ **5.07%**.

---

**Question (b) [7 marks]:**
**Compare BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV** based on the following criteria (use a comparison table):
(i) Primary energy source, (ii) Tailpipe emissions, (iii) Typical range, (iv) Refuelling/charging time, (v) One example vehicle.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Energy source** | Grid electricity → Battery | Petrol + Regenerative Braking | Grid + Petrol (dual) | Hydrogen tank + Fuel Cell |
| **(ii) Tailpipe emissions** | **Zero** | Low (still burns petrol) | Very Low | **Zero** (only water vapour) |
| **(iii) Typical range** | 200–600 km | 800–1000 km | 500–800 km | 400–650 km |
| **(iv) Refuelling time** | 30 min–8 hr (charging) | 3 min (petrol) | 3 min + 3 hr | **5 min** (H₂ filling) |
| **(v) Example** | Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3 | Toyota Camry Hybrid | Toyota Prius Prime, MG Hector Plus | Hyundai Nexo, Toyota Mirai |

**Discussion points expected in 14-mark answer:**

- BEVs are best for **urban daily commute** if charging infrastructure exists.
- HEVs are a **transitional technology** — no charging plug needed.
- PHEVs offer **flexibility** for users who can't always charge at home.
- FCEVs offer **fast refuelling** like petrol cars, but H₂ infrastructure is scarce.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per parameter row + 2 marks for engineering discussion of trade-offs)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** before using $P = F \cdot v$. Always divide by 3.6.
> 2. **Confusing $T = F \cdot r$ with $T = F/r$** — torque is force TIMES radius, not divided.
> 3. **Not dividing by drivetrain efficiency** when going from wheel power to motor power.
> 4. **Mixing up NEDC and WLTP** — KTU problems accept both if you state the cycle you used.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $P/2$** for electrical frequency in BLDC.
> 6. **Including regenerative braking gain** when not stated — assume one-way energy flow unless told otherwise.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- ✅ **EV motor types:** BLDC, PMSM, IM, SRM — BLDC and PMSM dominate modern passenger EVs.
- ✅ **BLDC needs rotor position feedback** — Hall sensors (sensored) or back-EMF zero-crossing (sensorless).
- ✅ **Six-step commutation** has **6 states per electrical revolution**; each state energises 2 of 3 phases.
- ✅ **EV classes by power source:** BEV (battery only), HEV (engine + regen, no plug), PHEV (engine + plug-in battery), FCEV (hydrogen), SEV (solar).
- ✅ **Tractive force equation** has 4 components: inertia, gravity-slope, rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag.
- ✅ **Aerodynamic drag dominates at highway speeds** — it scales as $v^2$.
- ✅ **Rolling resistance dominates at low speeds** — it is constant with speed.
- ✅ **EV Range formula:** $R = E_{batt} \cdot \eta / e_c$ — memorise this in Wh.
- ✅ **Power–Torque relationship:** $P = T \cdot \omega$ — convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi n/60$.
- ✅ **Battery specific energy** of Li-ion ≈ **150–250 Wh/kg** (NMC chemistry).
- ✅ **Air density** $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ at sea level — always state this constant.
- ✅ **Rolling resistance coefficient** $C_r \approx 0.015$ for car tyres on asphalt.
- ✅ **Drag coefficient** of a typical sedan $C_d \approx 0.30$ (Tesla Model 3 = 0.23, very low).
- ✅ **Always convert km/h to m/s** by dividing by 3.6 when using SI force-power equations.
- ✅ **Typical EV motor speed** range: 6000–15000 RPM with single-speed reduction gearbox ($G \approx 7$–$10$).
- ✅ **PMSM is preferred for premium cars**; **IM is preferred for rugged low-cost** applications; **SRM is the future** (no rare-earth magnets).
- ✅ **Drive cycle matters:** EPA < WLTP < NEDC in reported range.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Breakers - SF6, Vacuum, Air Blast
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circuit Breakers - SF6, Vacuum, Air Blast

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — CIRCUIT BREAKERS: SF₆, VACUUM & AIR BLAST

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Circuit Breaker?

A **Circuit Breaker (CB)** is an automatically operated electrical switch designed to protect an electrical circuit from damage caused by **overcurrent / overload / short-circuit conditions**. Unlike a fuse (which operates once and must be replaced), a circuit breaker can be reset (manually or automatically) to resume normal operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A circuit breaker is a piece of equipment used to **make and break** a circuit, both under normal and abnormal (fault) conditions, by means of an arc-quenching medium.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the three modern types of circuit breakers studied under Module 2 are classified by their **arc-quenching medium**:

| Breaker Type | Arc Quenching Medium | Symbol/State |
|---|---|---|
| **SF₆ Circuit Breaker** | Sulphur Hexafluoride gas (high pressure) | SF₆ (g) |
| **Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB)** | Vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr) | Vacuum |
| **Air Blast Circuit Breaker (ABCB)** | Compressed high-pressure air blast | Compressed air |

### 1.2 The Central Problem: Arc Formation

When the contacts of a circuit breaker separate while carrying load or fault current, the **air/vacuum/gas between them ionizes** and an **electric arc** is struck. This arc must be extinguished as quickly as possible — otherwise, it will:
- Cause contact erosion / melting
- Damage adjacent insulation
- Allow the fault current to persist, threatening system stability

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
> Imagine you are dragging two wet wooden sticks apart over a campfire. As they slowly separate, a bright flame (the "arc") jumps between them — it sustains itself because the hot ionized gases conduct electricity easily. To kill that flame, you either:
> 1. **Suck away** the hot gases (this is what **Air Blast** does — blows cold air),
> 2. **Replace the air with a gas that doesn't ionize** (this is what **SF₆** does — a superior dielectric), or
> 3. **Pull the sticks apart in an empty vacuum** — with no gas to ionize, no flame can exist (this is what a **Vacuum CB** does).
>
> Each method achieves the **same goal — quenching the arc — by a different physical trick.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Remember)

- **Dielectric strength of SF₆ ≈ 2.5 to 3 times that of air** at the same pressure.
- **Dielectric strength of vacuum ≈ 30 times that of air** at small gap (< 1 mm).
- **Vacuum pressure** in a VCB interrupter: **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr** (≈ 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ Pa).
- **Air blast pressure** in ABCB: typically **20–30 bar** (axial blast) or **5–6 bar** (cross blast).
- **SF₆ gas pressure** in puffer-type CB: **4–6 bar (gauge)** at 20 °C.
- **Arc temperature**: **10,000 K to 20,000 K** during fault interruption.

### 1.4 Why Different Media? (The "Why" Question)

The choice of arc-quenching medium depends on:
- **Voltage class** (LV, MV, HV, EHV)
- **Breaking capacity** (MVA or kA rating)
- **Speed of operation** required
- **Maintenance philosophy** (oil CBs are now obsolete for environmental reasons; SF₆/Vacuum/Air Blast dominate modern grids)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Note:** SF₆ is a potent greenhouse gas (Global Warming Potential ≈ 23,500 times that of CO₂ over 100 years). Therefore, modern KTU syllabus stresses **sealed pressurized systems** to prevent SF₆ leakage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Arc Extinction — Universal Principles

Regardless of the medium used, an arc in a circuit breaker is extinguished based on one (or a combination) of these three principles:

1. **Lengthening the arc** (increasing arc length → higher arc resistance → lower current → extinction at current zero).
2. **Cooling the arc** (reducing the temperature of the arc column → de-ionization of the medium).
3. **Splitting the arc into multiple smaller arcs** (using splitter plates in air-magnetic and oil CBs — not the focus here, but the principle is common).

At every **current zero crossing** (50 Hz → 100 half-cycles per second), the arc momentarily tries to re-ignite. The medium must provide sufficient **dielectric strength** to prevent **re-strike**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept — Rate of Rise of Recovery Voltage (RRRV):**
> After current zero, the voltage across the CB contacts rises rapidly. If the **dielectric strength of the gap rises faster than the recovery voltage**, the arc is **successfully interrupted**. Otherwise, it **re-strikes**.

## 2.2 SF₆ Circuit Breaker

### 2.2.1 Properties of SF₆ Gas

SF₆ is a colourless, odourless, non-toxic, non-flammable, and chemically stable gas. Its unique properties make it the best gaseous dielectric:

| Property | Value / Behaviour | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Molecular weight** | 146 g/mol | Heavier than air → sinks in leaked state |
| **Dielectric strength** | **2.5 – 3 × air** at 1 atm | Better insulation in smaller volume |
| **Arc-quenching ability** | ~100 × air | Superior interruption of high fault currents |
| **Recombination after arc** | Extremely fast | Rapid dielectric recovery |
| **Chemical stability** | Inert up to 500 °C | No carbon deposits on contacts |
| **Heat transfer** | 2 × air | Effective arc cooling |

### 2.2.2 Construction of a Puffer-Type SF₆ CB

The modern SF₆ CB is of the **puffer type** (also called **"double-pressure"** in older designs, but puffer dominates today).

**Main components:**
1. **Fixed contact** & **Moving contact** — made of copper-tungsten (Cu-W) alloy.
2. **Insulating nozzle** (PTFE / Teflon) — directs SF₆ flow over the arc.
3. **Puffer cylinder (piston)** — attached to the moving contact.
4. **SF₆ gas reservoir** — at high pressure.
5. **Operating mechanism** — spring / hydraulic.

### 2.2.3 Working Principle

1. On receiving a trip command, the moving contact begins to separate.
2. Simultaneously, the **piston compresses SF₆ gas** inside the cylinder.
3. The high-pressure SF₆ is **forced through the nozzle**, blasting the arc.
4. The gas **absorbs heat** (high specific heat), **elongates the arc**, and **de-ionizes** the gap.
5. At current zero, the dielectric strength of fresh SF₆ between the contacts is greater than the recovery voltage → **arc is quenched**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Fact:** SF₆ molecules are **electronegative** — they readily **attract free electrons** to form negative ions. These heavy negative ions cannot sustain ionization, hence the **fast dielectric recovery**.

## 2.3 Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB)

### 2.3.1 Why Vacuum?

A vacuum has **practically zero gas molecules** → no medium to ionize → no arc can be sustained in the traditional sense. However, during separation, **metal vapour** from the contacts sustains a brief "metallic arc."

### 2.3.2 Construction

- **Sealed glass / ceramic envelope** containing the contacts.
- **Two contacts** — one fixed, one moving — shaped with special **"butt" or "axial magnetic field" geometry**.
- **Metallic bellows** — allow the moving contact to translate while preserving vacuum.
- **Vapor shield** — surrounds the contacts; condenses back the metal vapour.

### 2.3.3 V-I Characteristics of a Vacuum Arc

The vacuum arc is unique — its voltage drop is **almost independent of current** over a wide range:

$$
V_{arc} \approx 20 \text{ to } 40 \text{ V (constant, low value)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This constant low arc voltage means that **the energy dissipated in the arc is proportional to current** — making VCBs ideal for **medium-voltage (up to 36 kV) applications**.

### 2.3.4 Contact Materials

Pure tungsten is not used (brittle). Common materials:
- **Cu-Cr (Copper-Chromium)** — most common today.
- **Ag-WC (Silver-Tungsten Carbide)** — for high current.
- **Cu-Bi** — newer alloys.

### 2.3.5 Working

1. Contacts separate inside vacuum.
2. A small amount of **contact material vaporizes** → sustains a **metallic vapour arc**.
3. At current zero, the metal vapour **rapidly condenses** on the vapor shield.
4. The vacuum between contacts is **restored in microseconds (≈ 5–10 µs)**.
5. Dielectric strength recovers faster than recovery voltage → **arc is extinguished**.

## 2.4 Air Blast Circuit Breaker (ABCB)

### 2.4.1 Principle

A high-pressure **air blast** (from a compressor + reservoir) is directed across the arc to:
- **De-ionize the arc path** (blow away ionized particles),
- **Cool the arc** rapidly,
- **Lengthen the arc** by the kinetic energy of the air stream.

### 2.4.2 Types of Air Blast CBs

| Type | Description | Voltage Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial Blast** | Air flows **along the axis** of the arc (parallel to arc). | EHV / HV (66 kV to 765 kV) |
| **Cross Blast** | Air flows **perpendicular** to the arc. | MV (up to 33 kV) |

### 2.4.3 Construction of Axial-Blast ABCB

- **Main contacts** (silver-faced) — carry current in closed position.
- **Arc contacts** (tungsten) — carry the arc during opening.
- **Insulating nozzle** (PTFE) — shapes the air blast.
- **Air reservoir** (compressed air at 20–30 bar).
- **Blast valve** — releases air when contacts open.

### 2.4.4 Working (Axial Blast)

1. Trip signal opens main contacts → current transfers to arc contacts.
2. Arc contacts separate → an arc is struck.
3. **Blast valve opens** → high-pressure air rushes axially through the nozzle.
4. Air **drags the arc into the arc chute**, where it is **cooled and de-ionized**.
5. At current zero, dielectric strength builds up → **arc is quenched**.
6. **Air continues to flow** for a few cycles, **preventing restrike**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM ESSENTIAL:** Memorize the units and the variable names. Marks are lost in KTU papers for missing units in numerical answers.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{dv}{dt} \bigg\vert_{t=0}$ | Rate of Rise of Recovery Voltage | V/µs or kV/µs |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{C \cdot R}$ | RRRV for purely resistive fault (simplified) | V/s |
| 3 | $R_{arc} = \dfrac{k \cdot l}{i^n}$ (Notch–Edwards or Ayrton-like empirical form) | Arc resistance where $l$ = arc length, $i$ = current, $k, n$ = constants | Ω |
| 4 | $V_{arc} = K \cdot l$ (for steady arc, simplified) | Arc voltage ∝ length | V |
| 5 | $t_{arc} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ (theoretical minimum at current zero) | Minimum theoretical arcing time for 50 Hz | 0.01 s = 10 ms |
| 6 | $\text{SF}_6 \text{ dielectric strength} \approx 3 \times \text{air}$ | Comparative property | — |
| 7 | $\text{Vacuum} \approx 30 \times \text{air}$ (small gap, 1 mm) | Comparative property | — |
| 8 | $P_{SF_6} \approx 4 \text{ to } 6 \text{ bar (gauge)}$ | Operating pressure of puffer CB | bar |
| 9 | $P_{air blast} \approx 20 \text{ to } 30 \text{ bar}$ | Air blast pressure (axial) | bar |
| 10 | $t_{recovery} = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{init}}\right)$ | Dielectric recovery time (simplified exponential model) | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the KTU mark-allocator's check:**
> - $V_{max}$ for line-to-line fault = **$\dfrac{V_L \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}$** (per phase analysis).
> - For KTU numericals, **always** express the final answer with **proper units** (V, kV, µs, kV/µs).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SF₆ CBs** are used in **EHV (Extra High Voltage)** systems — 132 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV substations across India's PGCIL (Power Grid Corporation of India Limited) network.
- **VCBs** dominate **MV (Medium Voltage)** distribution — 11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV indoor switchgear in industrial and commercial premises.
- **ABCBs** are now mostly replaced by SF₆ at HV, but **cross-blast types still exist in some old substations and railway traction** in India (e.g., 25 kV OHE switching).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rate of Rise of Recovery Voltage (RRRV) for a Short-Circuited Line

### Step 1: Define the fault scenario

A **short-circuit fault** occurs at the receiving end of a transmission line. The line has series inductance $L$ and shunt capacitance $C$ to neutral. The circuit breaker contacts open to interrupt the fault.

### Step 2: Equivalent circuit after current zero

The simplified equivalent circuit (lumped) is:

- A charged capacitance $C$ (representing the line capacitance),
- Connected through an inductance $L$ to the source.

This is an **LC oscillatory circuit**.

### Step 3: Differential equation of the circuit

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) at the moment of current interruption (i.e., $i = 0$):

$$
L \dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0
$$

Where $i = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$, and $q$ is the charge on the capacitor. Substituting:

$$
L \dfrac{d^2q}{dt^2} + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve the second-order ODE

The characteristic equation is:

$$
L s^2 + \dfrac{1}{C} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s^2 = -\dfrac{1}{LC} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s = \pm j \omega_0
$$

where the **natural frequency of oscillation** is:

$$
\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

### Step 5: General solution

$$
q(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t) + B \sin(\omega_0 t)
$$

### Step 6: Apply initial conditions

At $t = 0$ (instant of current zero):
- $q(0) = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged in this simplified model)
- $i(0) = 0$ (current is zero at interruption)

Substituting $q(0) = 0$:

$$
A = 0
$$

Differentiating and using $i(0) = 0$:

$$
i(t) = -\omega_0 A \sin(\omega_0 t) + \omega_0 B \cos(\omega_0 t)
$$

At $t = 0$:

$$
i(0) = \omega_0 B = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B = 0
$$

So in this idealized case, $q(t) = 0$ — to introduce initial conditions realistically, we consider the **capacitor initially charged to a value $V_0$** (the recovery voltage) and include a series resistance $R$ to model the damped case.

### Step 7: Damped (real) case

$$
L \dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0
$$

Differentiating once and substituting $i = \dot{q}$:

$$
L \ddot{q} + R \dot{q} + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0
$$

For an **under-damped system** ($R < 2\sqrt{L/C}$), the solution is:

$$
q(t) = e^{-\alpha t} \left[ A \cos(\omega_d t) + B \sin(\omega_d t) \right]
$$

Where:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}, \;\;\; \omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{LC} - \dfrac{R^2}{4L^2}}
$$

### Step 8: Recovery voltage across CB contacts

The voltage across the breaker is the capacitor voltage:

$$
v_{rec}(t) = \dfrac{q(t)}{C} = \dfrac{V_0 \, \omega_0}{\omega_d} \cdot e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)
$$

### Step 9: Rate of rise of recovery voltage (initial value)

Differentiating and evaluating at $t = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{dv_{rec}}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=0} = \dfrac{V_0 \, \omega_0}{C \cdot \omega_d} \cdot \omega_d = \dfrac{V_0}{R \cdot C}
$$

(when $R \ll 2\sqrt{L/C}$, a common assumption in modern systems)

Hence the final, exam-ready formula:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{R \cdot C}\;}
$$

### Step 10: Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** A 33 kV system, $V_{max} = \dfrac{33{,}000 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = 26{,}943 \text{ V}$,
Line $L = 10 \text{ mH}$, $C = 0.01 \text{ µF}$, and effective fault resistance $R = 5 \, \Omega$.

**Compute RRRV:**

$$
\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{R \cdot C} = \dfrac{26{,}943}{5 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{26{,}943}{5 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
= 5.389 \times 10^{11} \text{ V/s} = 0.539 \text{ kV/µs}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert µF to F (multiply by 10⁻⁶) and express RRRV in **kV/µs** for a 5-mark KTU numerical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arc Resistance (Notch–Edwards Empirical Form)

### Step 1: Why an empirical model?

The arc in a circuit breaker is a complex non-linear plasma phenomenon. Empirical formulae (e.g., **Steinmetz's law, Ayrton's equation, and Notch–Edwards model**) are used for practical calculations.

### Step 2: Ayrton's equation (1902)

For a long arc in air:

$$
V_{arc} = A + B \cdot l + \dfrac{C + D \cdot l}{I}
$$

Where:
- $V_{arc}$ = arc voltage (V),
- $I$ = arc current (A),
- $l$ = arc length (mm),
- $A, B, C, D$ = empirical constants (depend on electrode material).

### Step 3: Simplified form for engineering use

At high currents (where $C + D \cdot l \ll I \cdot (A + B l)$), this reduces to:

$$
V_{arc} \approx A + B \cdot l
$$

Thus arc resistance:

$$
R_{arc} = \dfrac{V_{arc}}{I} = \dfrac{A + B \cdot l}{I}
$$

### Step 4: Inverse (Rizzitelli / Cassie-like) form

For an arc being stretched (length growing with time $t$ at velocity $v$):

$$
R_{arc}(t) = \dfrac{k \cdot l(t)}{i(t)^n}
$$

Where:
- $k, n$ are empirical constants,
- $l(t) = v \cdot t$ (linear elongation).

### Step 5: Physical interpretation

- As $l \uparrow$, $R_{arc} \uparrow$ → arc voltage rises → current is forced down.
- As $I \uparrow$, $R_{arc} \downarrow$ (negative resistance behaviour at high currents — a hallmark of arcs).
- At **current zero**, $I \to 0$, so $R_{arc} \to \infty$ — the arc naturally extinguishes if the dielectric strength holds.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (MUST MEMORIZE for KTU)

| Parameter | **SF₆ CB** | **Vacuum CB** | **Air Blast CB** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc quenching medium** | SF₆ gas (4–6 bar) | Vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr) | Compressed air (20–30 bar) |
| **Dielectric strength (relative)** | **3 × air** | **~30 × air** (small gap) | **1 × air** (but high pressure) |
| **Arc type** | Gas arc | Metal-vapour arc | Gas arc |
| **Arc voltage** | High (~25% of system V) | Low (~20–40 V) | Moderate |
| **Breaking capacity** | **Very high** (up to 80 kA, 800 kV) | Moderate (up to 63 kA, 36 kV) | High (up to 40 kA, 400 kV) |
| **Suitable voltage range** | **MV, HV, EHV** (3.3 kV – 765 kV) | **MV** (3.3 kV – 36 kV) | **HV, EHV** (66 kV – 765 kV) |
| **Operating mechanism** | Spring / hydraulic / pneumatic | Spring (with low energy) | Pneumatic (compressed air) |
| **Noise** | Low | Very low | **High** (loud exhaust) |
| **Maintenance** | Low (sealed system) | **Very low** (sealed for life) | Moderate (air filters, compressor) |
| **Environmental concern** | **SF₆ is a GHG** (GWP 23,500 × CO₂) | None | None |
| **Contact life** | Long | **Very long** (no oxidation) | Moderate |
| **Cost** | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Typical applications** | PGCIL EHV substations, GIS | Industrial LV/MV switchgear | Old HV substations, railways |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Puffer-Type SF₆ Circuit Breaker

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

    subgraph Interrupter["Interrupter Unit Sealed SF6 Gas"]

        PISTON[Puffer Piston Coupled to Moving Contact]
    end

    end

    end

    subgraph Recovery["Post Interruption"]

    end
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Process Flow — Arc Extinction in a Circuit Breaker (Universal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Flows Through Closed Contacts] --> B[Fault Occurs or Trip Command Received]
    B --> C[Contacts Begin to Separate]
    C --> D[Arc Strikes Between Contacts]
    D --> E{Choose Quenching Medium}
    E -->|SF6 Gas| F1[Puffer Compresses SF6 through Nozzle]
    E -->|Vacuum| F2[Metal Vapour Arc Forms then Condenses]
    E -->|Compressed Air| F3[Axial or Cross Air Blast Applied]
    F1 --> G1[Arc Cooled Elongated and Deionised]
    F2 --> G2[Vacuum Dielectric Strength Recovers Rapidly]
    F3 --> G3[Arc Dragged into Chute and Extinguished]
    G1 --> H[Current Zero Cross]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I{Dielectric Strength Greater than Recovery Voltage?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Successful Interruption]
    I -->|No| K[Arc Restrikes and Interruption Fails]
    J --> L[Breaker Reset for Next Operation]
    K --> M[Re-evaluate Arc Quenching Parameters]

    classDef fault fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef success fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef failure fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D process
    class E,I decision
    class F1,F2,F3 process
    class G1,G2,G3,H,L process
    class J success
    class K,M failure
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List any three superior properties of SF₆ gas that make it suitable as an arc-quenching medium in modern EHV circuit breakers."**

**Model Answer:**

SF₆ (Sulphur Hexafluoride) is widely used in EHV circuit breakers because of the following properties:

1. **High dielectric strength** — approximately **2.5 to 3 times that of air** at the same pressure. This allows more compact switchgear design at high voltages.

2. **Excellent arc-quenching ability** — about **100 times that of air**. SF₆ molecules are **electronegative**, meaning they capture free electrons to form heavy negative ions, which cannot sustain ionization. This leads to very fast dielectric recovery after current zero.

3. **High thermal conductivity** — approximately **2 times that of air**. This allows the gas to absorb arc heat rapidly, cooling and de-ionizing the arc column effectively.

4. **Chemical inertness and non-toxicity** — SF₆ is chemically stable up to 500 °C and does not produce carbon deposits, ensuring long contact life.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "D-A-Q-C" → Dielectric strength, Arc-quenching, Quiet (non-toxic), Chemical stability.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Why is a Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB) preferred for medium-voltage indoor switchgear applications?"**

**Model Answer:**

A Vacuum Circuit Breaker is preferred for medium-voltage (3.3 kV to 36 kV) indoor switchgear for the following reasons:

1. **Very fast arc extinction** — since there is no gas to ionize, the arc is sustained only briefly by metal vapour from the contacts, which condenses within microseconds after current zero. The dielectric strength recovers in **5 to 10 µs**.

2. **No fire or explosion hazard** — unlike oil CBs, VCBs contain no flammable or explosive medium, making them inherently safe for indoor use.

3. **Maintenance-free operation** — the sealed vacuum interrupter has a long contact life (often the entire equipment lifetime), reducing maintenance costs.

4. **Silent operation and compact size** — ideal for indoor substations, industrial plants, and commercial buildings.

5. **Environmentally friendly** — no greenhouse gas emission (unlike SF₆).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1): SF₆ Circuit Breaker — Construction, Working & Applications**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a puffer-type SF₆ circuit breaker**. Discuss the role of the puffer cylinder and the insulating nozzle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **properties of SF₆ gas** that make it the preferred medium for EHV (Extra High Voltage) circuit breakers. Also, list the **limitations and environmental concerns** of using SF₆.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Construction (4 marks):**

A puffer-type SF₆ circuit breaker consists of the following main parts:

1. **Fixed contact** — made of copper-tungsten (Cu-W) alloy, mounted on the upper terminal. The Cu-W combination provides high electrical conductivity and resistance to arc erosion.

2. **Moving contact** — also Cu-W alloy, attached to a conducting rod that is connected to the lower terminal through a sliding contact.

3. **Insulating nozzle** — made of **PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene / Teflon)**, fixed around the contacts. It directs the SF₆ flow over the arc.

4. **Puffer cylinder / piston** — a cylinder mechanically coupled to the moving contact. When the contact moves, the piston compresses the SF₆ gas.

5. **SF₆ gas reservoir** — filled with SF₆ at **4 to 6 bar (gauge)** at 20 °C.

6. **Operating mechanism** — a spring or hydraulic system that drives the moving contact.

**Working (3 marks):**

When a trip signal is received:

1. The operating mechanism drives the moving contact downward.
2. The contacts separate → an **arc is struck** between them.
3. Simultaneously, the **puffer piston compresses** the SF₆ gas in the cylinder.
4. The high-pressure SF₆ is **forced through the nozzle** and **blasted over the arc**.
5. The gas **absorbs heat** (high specific heat of SF₆) and **elongates** the arc.
6. **Free electrons in the arc** are captured by the electronegative SF₆ molecules → **de-ionization**.
7. At **current zero**, the dielectric strength of the SF₆ gap recovers faster than the recovery voltage → **arc is extinguished**.

> The **insulating nozzle** plays a critical role by:
> - **Shaping** the gas flow for maximum cooling efficiency.
> - **Insulating** the metallic parts from the high-temperature arc.
> - **Confining** the arc to a small region for effective quenching.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Superior properties of SF₆ (5 marks):**

1. **High dielectric strength** — about **2.5 to 3 times** that of air at atmospheric pressure. (Some sources cite up to 5 times at higher pressures.)

2. **Excellent arc-quenching ability** — about **100 times** that of air, due to its electronegative nature. SF₆ rapidly absorbs free electrons, preventing re-ionization after current zero.

3. **High thermal conductivity** — about **2 times** that of air, allowing efficient heat dissipation.

4. **Chemical inertness** — does not react with contact materials (Cu, W) even at high arc temperatures, ensuring long contact life and low maintenance.

5. **Non-flammable and non-toxic** — safe for indoor and outdoor use.

6. **Recombines readily after arc** — the gas self-heals; arc byproducts (e.g., metallic fluorides) recombine when cooled, maintaining gas purity.

7. **Lower operating noise** — no loud exhaust as in air blast CBs.

**Limitations & Environmental Concerns (2 marks):**

1. **Greenhouse gas** — SF₆ has a **Global Warming Potential (GWP) of ~23,500 times that of CO₂** over a 100-year horizon and an atmospheric lifetime of about **3,200 years**.

2. **Cost** — SF₆ is expensive, and the gas must be sealed to prevent leakage.

3. **Decomposition products** — under high-temperature arcing, SF₆ can decompose into **SOF₂, SO₂F₂, HF**, which are toxic and corrosive. These must be removed by **alumina adsorbents** in the gas compartment.

4. **Requires strict sealing** — any leak is environmentally damaging and degrades dielectric performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often mention only the advantages of SF₆ and forget the **environmental concerns**. For full marks (7/7), you MUST discuss **both** the superior properties AND the environmental limitations. Marks are specifically reserved for the GWP value and decomposition products.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): Air Blast vs Vacuum — Comparison & Working**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **construction and working of an Axial-Blast Air Blast Circuit Breaker (ABCB)**. How does it differ from a **Cross-Blast ABCB**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **construction and arc-quenching mechanism of a Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB)**. List its **advantages and limitations** compared to SF₆ and Air Blast CBs.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Construction of Axial-Blast ABCB (4 marks):**

1. **Main contacts** — silver-faced copper contacts, carry the load current in the closed position.
2. **Arc contacts** — made of **tungsten** (high melting point), carry the arc during opening.
3. **Insulating nozzle** — PTFE, shapes the air blast.
4. **Compressed air reservoir** — at **20 to 30 bar (axial)**, charged by an external compressor.
5. **Blast valve** — a trip-actuated valve that releases the compressed air when contacts open.
6. **Arc chute** — downstream of the contacts, where the arc is dragged, cooled, and de-ionized.

**Working (2 marks):**

1. The trip command opens the **main contacts first**.
2. Current shifts to the **arc contacts** (still closed).
3. The **arc contacts open** → an arc is struck.
4. The **blast valve opens simultaneously** → high-pressure air rushes **axially** through the nozzle.
5. The air **drags the arc into the arc chute**, where it is **cooled, elongated, and de-ionized**.
6. At **current zero**, the dielectric strength of the air gap recovers, aided by continued air flow → **arc is extinguished**.

**Difference between Axial and Cross Blast (1 mark):**

| Feature | Axial Blast | Cross Blast |
|---|---|---|
| **Air flow direction** | Parallel to the arc (along its axis) | Perpendicular to the arc |
| **Arc lengthening** | By hot gases being pushed forward | By the arc being bent sideways |
| **Voltage range** | EHV (66 kV – 765 kV) | MV (up to 33 kV) |
| **Air pressure** | 20 – 30 bar | 5 – 6 bar |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Construction of VCB (3 marks):**

1. **Sealed envelope** — made of **glass or ceramic**, hermetically sealed.
2. **Vacuum inside** — pressure of **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr** (≈ 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ Pa).
3. **Fixed contact** — Cu-Cr (Copper-Chromium) alloy, mounted on the upper terminal.
4. **Moving contact** — Cu-Cr alloy, mounted on a shaft that can slide axially.
5. **Stainless steel metallic bellows** — seal the moving contact shaft while allowing axial motion.
6. **Vapor shields** — surround the contacts; condense the metal vapour back after the arc.
7. **External operating mechanism** — spring-operated, requires low energy.

**Arc-Quenching Mechanism (2 marks):**

1. When contacts separate, a brief **metallic vapour arc** is struck (from contact material vaporisation).
2. The arc voltage is **low (~20–40 V)** and **nearly constant** over a wide current range.
3. At **current zero**, the metal vapour rapidly **condenses on the vapor shield**.
4. The vacuum between contacts is **restored in microseconds** — much faster than in any gas or oil CB.
5. The **dielectric strength rises faster than the recovery voltage** → successful interruption.

**Advantages and Limitations (2 marks):**

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Very fast arc extinction (5–10 µs recovery) | Limited to **MV (≤ 36 kV)** — difficult to scale up |
| Maintenance-free (sealed for life) | **High initial cost** of vacuum interrupters |
| No fire / explosion / gas hazard | **Voltage transients** (current chopping) can damage connected equipment |
| Silent operation | Not suitable for very high fault currents beyond 63 kA |
| Long contact life (no oxidation) | Sensitive to over-voltage surges during switching |
| Environmentally safe (no GHG) | Requires special manufacturing (vacuum sealing) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.B):**
> 1. Do NOT confuse **axial blast** with **cross blast** airflow direction — examiners will deduct 1 to 2 marks.
> 2. For VCB, do NOT say "there is no arc" — there IS a brief **metal-vapour arc**. Saying "no arc" is a common mistake and loses marks.
> 3. Always state the **vacuum pressure in Torr** (not just "high vacuum") — the examiner looks for the numerical value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This List**

### ✅ Universal Concepts
- [ ] Circuit breaker = make + break under **normal AND fault** conditions.
- [ ] Arc is the unavoidable consequence of opening contacts under load.
- [ ] Arc extinction relies on **de-ionization + dielectric recovery > recovery voltage**.
- [ ] Current zero of AC = the natural opportunity for arc extinction.
- [ ] RRRV = $\dfrac{dv}{dt}\big\vert_{t=0}$ — must be less than rate of rise of dielectric strength.

### ✅ SF₆ Circuit Breaker
- [ ] Dielectric strength = **3 × air**.
- [ ] Arc-quenching ability = **100 × air**.
- [ ] **Electronegative** — captures free electrons.
- [ ] **Puffer type** dominates modern designs.
- [ ] Operating pressure = **4 to 6 bar (gauge)**.
- [ ] Used in **EHV** (up to 765 kV, 80 kA).
- [ ] **GWP = 23,500 × CO₂** — major environmental drawback.

### ✅ Vacuum Circuit Breaker
- [ ] Vacuum pressure = **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Torr**.
- [ ] **Dielectric strength ≈ 30 × air** at small gaps.
- [ ] Arc voltage = **~20–40 V (constant)** — unique V-I characteristic.
- [ ] Arc type = **metallic vapour** (not gas).
- [ ] Dielectric recovery = **5–10 µs** (fastest of all CBs).
- [ ] Contact material = **Cu-Cr (most common)**.
- [ ] Used in **MV (3.3 kV – 36 kV)** — industrial, indoor.
- [ ] No fire hazard, no gas, **maintenance-free**.

### ✅ Air Blast Circuit Breaker
- [ ] Air pressure = **20–30 bar (axial)**, **5–6 bar (cross)**.
- [ ] Two contact types: **main (silver)** and **arc (tungsten)**.
- [ ] **Axial blast** for EHV; **cross blast** for MV.
- [ ] Air blast drags arc into **arc chute** for cooling and de-ionization.
- [ ] **Continued air flow** after current zero prevents restrike.
- [ ] **Loud operation** (air exhaust noise) — major drawback.
- [ ] Now largely **replaced by SF₆ CBs** at EHV.

### ✅ Comparison Triggers for Exam
- [ ] "Which CB for EHV?" → **SF₆** (and historically, Air Blast).
- [ ] "Which CB for indoor MV?" → **Vacuum**.
- [ ] "Which CB for outdoor HV/EHV with no GHG?" → **Air Blast** (older installations).
- [ ] "Fastest dielectric recovery?" → **Vacuum CB** (5–10 µs).
- [ ] "Most environmentally harmful medium?" → **SF₆** (GWP 23,500).
- [ ] "Best for very high fault currents?" → **SF₆ CB**.

### ✅ Numericals to Practice
- [ ] RRRV computation: $\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{R \cdot C}$.
- [ ] Recovery voltage waveforms (LC oscillation, damped).
- [ ] Comparison of breaking capacities and voltage classes.

---

> 🎓 **End of Notes — Module 2: Circuit Breakers (SF₆, Vacuum, Air Blast)**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Switchgear?

**Switchgear** is a collective term that encompasses all the switching devices, associated equipment, protection devices, control panels, fuses, relays, current/voltage transformers, isolators, and circuit breakers used for controlling, protecting, isolating, and operating electrical power systems in generating stations, transmission networks, substations, distribution networks, and industrial installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Module 2):** Switchgear is the apparatus used for switching, controlling, and protecting the electrical circuits and equipment. It plays a vital role in ensuring the reliable and safe operation of power systems by detecting faults, interrupting fault currents, and isolating faulty sections without disturbing healthy parts of the network.

### 1.2 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standardized Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Switchgear:** The complete assembly of devices designed to control, regulate, switch on/off, and protect electrical equipment from abnormal operating conditions such as short circuits, overvoltages, overloads, and earth faults.

The generic term **"Switchgear"** is derived from two functional words:
- **Switch** → for making or breaking (closing/opening) an electrical circuit.
- **Gear** → the protective and auxiliary equipment associated with the switching action.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of Switchgear

The core functions of switchgear in a power system can be broadly classified into **three vital functions**:

1. **Carrying Function (Normal Operation):**
   - To carry normal load current continuously without overheating.
   - To carry short-circuit current for a brief duration (typically 1 to 3 seconds) without mechanical or thermal damage.

2. **Breaking Function (Fault Clearance):**
   - To break (interrupt) normal as well as abnormal (fault) currents safely.
   - The breaking capacity is expressed in **MVA** (Mega Volt-Amperes) for AC systems and **kA** (kilo-Amperes) for DC systems.

3. **Protective Function (Selective Isolation):**
   - To detect abnormal conditions (overload, short-circuit, earth fault, overvoltage, under-voltage) and automatically isolate only the faulty section, leaving the healthy sections of the network operational.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Rating Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Breaking Capacity (B):** $B = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_{sc} \times 10^{-6} \, \text{MVA}$
> - **Making Capacity:** $1.8 \times \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_{sc} \times 10^{-6} \, \text{MVA}$ (typically 1.8 times the breaking capacity)
> - **Short-time Current Rating:** Typically rated for **1 second, 3 seconds, or 5 seconds** duration.

## 1.4 Classification of Switchgear (By Voltage Class)

| Class | Voltage Range | Typical Equipment | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Voltage (LV) Switchgear** | Up to **1 kV** (typically 415 V, 440 V) | MCBs, MCCBs, ACBs, HRC fuses, contactors | Industrial panels, distribution boards, building wiring |
| **Medium Voltage (MV) Switchgear** | **1 kV to 33 kV** (sometimes up to 36 kV) | VCBs, SF₆ breakers, OCBs, load break switches | Industrial distribution, primary substations, ring main units |
| **High Voltage (HV) Switchgear** | **33 kV to 245 kV** | Bulk oil CBs, minimum oil CBs, air blast CBs, SF₆ | Transmission substations, EHV switching stations |
| **Extra High Voltage (EHV) Switchgear** | **Above 245 kV** (up to 765 kV, 1200 kV) | SF₆ single-pressure puffer breakers, GIS | EHV/UHV transmission, HVDC converter stations |

## 1.5 What is a Circuit Breaker?

A **Circuit Breaker (CB)** is an automatically operated electrical switch designed to protect an electrical circuit from damage caused by overcurrent/overload, short circuits, or earth faults. Unlike a fuse, which operates once and then must be replaced, a circuit breaker can be reset (either manually or automatically) to resume normal operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** A circuit breaker is a switching device capable of making, carrying, and breaking currents under both normal and abnormal circuit conditions (such as short circuits) automatically. It is the most important and expensive component of the switchgear family.

## 1.6 The Arc Phenomenon — A Simple Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Garden Hose Analogy**
> Imagine a high-pressure water pipe (representing the live conductor). If you try to suddenly close the valve (representing the circuit breaker contacts opening), water hammer occurs — a sudden shock wave due to the inertia of the moving fluid. Similarly, when contacts carrying current start to separate, the **inertia of the current** (which is due to the system inductance resisting any change in current, governed by $V = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$) causes the **voltage across the contacts to rise sharply to a very high value**. The dielectric between the contacts (air, oil, SF₆ gas) cannot withstand this voltage, and it ionizes. The ionized path becomes conductive, and a **self-sustaining luminous discharge called an "arc"** is formed between the contacts. The arc is the *worst enemy* of a circuit breaker because it delays the current interruption, damages the contacts, and produces extreme heat.

### 1.7 What is an Electric Arc?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Arc:** A self-sustaining, high-temperature, low-voltage, luminous electrical discharge (plasma) formed between two separating current-carrying contacts of a switchgear, sustained by the thermal ionization of the surrounding dielectric medium. Arc temperatures can reach **20,000 K to 50,000 K** (sun's surface is ~5,800 K).

## 1.8 The Necessity of Arc Interruption

When a circuit breaker interrupts a load or fault current, an arc inevitably forms between the separating contacts. This arc must be extinguished as quickly as possible to:

1. Prevent contact erosion and welding.
2. Prevent damage to adjacent insulation and equipment.
3. Ensure rapid fault clearance and **system stability**.
4. Protect human operators from arc-flash hazards.
5. Limit the let-through energy ($I^2t$) to within equipment thermal limits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students often confuse *current interruption* with *arc extinction*. They are NOT the same. **Current interruption** is the physical event of contact separation; **arc extinction** is the subsequent successful quenching of the plasma channel, which permanently blocks re-ignition. Marks are awarded for distinguishing these in descriptive answers.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Essential Qualities of an Ideal Circuit Breaker

A circuit breaker should possess the following technical qualities, as per KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus:

1. **Zero RRRV (Rate of Rise of Recovery Voltage) at zero current:** For successful arc extinction at natural current zero, the contact gap must develop dielectric strength **faster** than the transient recovery voltage.
2. **Short Break Time:** The operating time from fault detection to arc extinction should be minimal (typically **3 to 5 cycles**, i.e., 60–100 ms for 50 Hz systems).
3. **High Reliability:** Must operate without failure when commanded by the protection relay.
4. **Adequate Making Capacity:** Must withstand the mechanical, thermal, and electromagnetic forces when closing against a fault (peak inrush can be up to **2.55 × RMS fault current**).
5. **Compact Size and Low Maintenance:** Modern design trend.
6. **Economical:** Cost-effective for the voltage class.

## 2.2 Operating Principle of a Circuit Breaker (Step-by-Step)

A circuit breaker works on a sequential logic as follows:

1. **Normal State:** Contacts are closed; current flows through the main contact path.
2. **Fault Occurs:** A fault current (5 to 20 times normal current) begins to flow.
3. **Protection Relay Senses Fault:** The Current Transformer (CT) senses the abnormal current. A relay discriminates between normal and fault conditions.
4. **Trip Signal Sent:** The relay sends a trip pulse to the **trip coil** of the operating mechanism.
5. **Operating Mechanism Releases:** The latch of the operating mechanism is released; stored mechanical energy (in a spring, pneumatic cylinder, or hydraulic accumulator) drives the moving contact away from the fixed contact.
6. **Contacts Separate:** The main contacts open, and an **arc is struck** between them.
7. **Arc is Quenched:** The arc plasma is de-ionized by the chosen interruption medium (oil, air, SF₆, vacuum).
8. **Current Interruption:** At the first natural current zero (for AC), the arc is not allowed to re-strike. The contact gap is permanently non-conductive.
9. **Contacts Reset:** The breaker can be re-closed manually or by auto-reclosing logic.

## 2.3 The Arc Phenomenon — Detailed Physics

### 2.3.1 Arc Initiation Mechanism

When two current-carrying contacts start to separate, the **contact area decreases** while the current remains almost the same (the inductance in the circuit prevents instantaneous current decay). The current density (J = I/A) at the last point of contact becomes extremely high.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism of Arc Initiation (3-step process):**
> 1. **Thermionic Emission:** Due to high current density, the last contact point is heated to its boiling point (~3,000 °C for copper, ~3,400 °C for silver-tungsten). Electrons are emitted.
> 2. **Field Emission:** The small contact gap produces a high electric field (E = V/d), which pulls electrons from the cathode by the tunneling effect.
> 3. **Ionization by Collision:** The accelerated electrons collide with neutral gas molecules, knocking off more electrons (Townsend avalanche). The path becomes conductive → arc plasma.

### 2.3.2 Sustenance of the Arc

Once initiated, the arc is sustained by:
- **Ionization (in):** Collisional ionization, thermionic emission, field emission, photo-ionization.
- **De-ionization (out):** Recombination (electron + ion → neutral atom), diffusion of ions out of the arc column, cooling by convection/conduction.

The arc is stable when **ionization rate = de-ionization rate** (dynamic equilibrium).

### 2.3.3 Properties of the Arc

| Property | Typical Value/Characteristic | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Temperature** | **20,000 K to 50,000 K** | Damages contacts, vaporizes metals |
| **Arc Voltage** | Low (typically 20–100 V/cm of arc length) | Most of the system voltage appears across the network inductance |
| **Arc Current Density** | $10^3$ to $10^4$ A/cm² | Extreme local heating |
| **Arc Resistance** | Increases as current approaches zero | Used for current chopping analysis |
| **Arc Diameter** | Few mm to a few cm | Depends on current and surrounding medium |

## 2.4 The Arc Interruption Process (AC System)

In AC systems, the current passes through natural **zero** values 100 times per second (for 50 Hz). Successful interruption of an AC arc is achieved at or near the **first natural current zero** following contact separation. The process has two distinct phases:

### 2.4.1 Phase 1: High Current Phase (Pre-Zero)

- The arc is in a stable, high-current state.
- **Arc voltage is low**, and a significant amount of energy is dissipated as heat.
- The arc column is **fully ionized** with high conductivity.
- The contact gap is increasing; arc length is increasing.

### 2.4.2 Phase 2: Current Zero Region (The Critical Region)

- The **current is approaching zero**, and so is the **arc power** (P = v × i).
- However, the **arc resistance is increasing rapidly** because R_arc = v/i (where v remains around 5–10 V due to cathode/anode fall, while i → 0).
- At current zero, theoretically, the **arc extinguishes** momentarily.
- The circuit breaker has a **very brief time (microseconds to a few milliseconds)** to:
  - **Remove residual ions/electrons** from the contact gap (de-ionization).
  - **Build up dielectric strength** of the gap faster than the **recovery voltage** builds up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Conditions for Successful AC Arc Extinction (Cassie's and Mayr's Theories):**
> 1. The **Rate of De-ionization (dD/dt) must exceed the Rate of Ionization (dI/dt)** at all instants near current zero.
> 2. The **Dielectric Strength of the gap must grow faster than the Rate of Rise of Recovery Voltage (RRRV)**.

### 2.4.3 Re-strike vs. Successful Interruption

- **If the recovery voltage across the gap rises faster than the dielectric strength of the gap → arc re-strikes → interruption FAILS.**
- **If the dielectric strength of the gap rises faster than the recovery voltage → arc does NOT re-strike → interruption SUCCEEDS.**

This race between **dielectric recovery curve** and **transient recovery voltage (TRV) curve** is the heart of arc interruption theory.

## 2.5 Arc Interruption Methods (Practical Techniques)

### 2.5.1 High Resistance Interruption

The arc resistance is increased to a very high value, forcing the current to decay to zero even in DC systems (and to limit the magnitude in AC systems).

**Methods to increase arc resistance:**
- Lengthening the arc (using longer contact travel).
- Cooling the arc (using oil, compressed air, SF₆).
- Constricting the cross-section of the arc (narrowing the gap).
- Splitting the arc into many smaller arcs in series (arc chutes with splitter plates).

### 2.5.2 Low Resistance (Current Zero) Interruption

This is the standard method for **all modern AC circuit breakers**. The arc is allowed to persist until the natural current zero of the AC waveform. The conditions around current zero are manipulated to prevent re-ignition.

**Methods to enhance dielectric strength after current zero:**
- **Oil blast:** The arc decomposes oil into hydrogen (H₂), which is a good thermal conductor and de-ionizing gas.
- **Air blast:** Compressed air (5–30 bar) blows the arc plasma away, cooling it rapidly.
- **SF₆ gas:** Sulphur hexafluoride is electronegative (captures free electrons), forming heavy negative ions with low mobility.
- **Vacuum:** The contact gap has virtually no gas molecules; the metal vapor arc self-extinguishes.
- **Solid insulation:** For low-voltage breakers, molded plastic enclosures quench the arc.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Breaking Capacity} = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_{sc} \times 10^{-6}$ | MVA rating of a 3-phase CB | MVA |
| 2 | $\text{Making Capacity} = 1.8 \times \text{Breaking Capacity}$ | Peak inrush capacity of CB | MVA |
| 3 | $\text{Peak Fault Current} = 2.55 \times I_{sc,RMS}$ | First peak of asymmetrical short-circuit current | kA |
| 4 | $v_{arc} = k \cdot l \cdot i^{-n}$ | Ayrton's equation for arc voltage (empirical); $k$, $n$ are constants depending on medium | V |
| 5 | $R_{arc} = \dfrac{v_{arc}}{i_{arc}}$ | Instantaneous arc resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $e = \dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{v_{arc}}{L}$ | Rate of change of current in an inductive circuit | A/s |
| 7 | $\text{Energy dissipated in arc} = E = \displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_2} v_{arc} \cdot i_{arc} \, dt$ | Arc energy = thermal stress on contacts | Joules |
| 8 | $\text{RRRV} = \dfrac{dV_{recovery}}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{T_{rrv}}$ | Rate of Rise of Recovery Voltage | kV/μs |
| 9 | $P_{arc} = v_{arc} \cdot i_{arc}$ | Instantaneous power dissipated in the arc | W |
| 10 | $\text{Arc Power Loss} = \dfrac{V_{arc} \times I_{arc}}{2}$ for sinusoidal approximation | Average arc power in AC | W |
| 11 | $\text{Power Frequency Recovery Voltage} = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{max}}{\pi}$ | Average recovery voltage over the half cycle | V |
| 12 | $I_{chopping} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C \cdot V_{0}^{2}}{L}}$ | Current chopping value in vacuum CB (depends on $C$ = stray capacitance, $V_0$ = arc voltage) | A |
| 13 | $V_{TRV} = V_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Transient recovery voltage (exponential approximation) | V |
| 14 | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Time constant of the circuit | s |
| 15 | $t_{arc} = \dfrac{L \cdot i_{0}}{v_{arc}}$ | Approximate arc duration in a DC circuit | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Arc Interruption Theory:**
> - In **EHV transmission** (400 kV, 765 kV), the RRRV can be as high as **5–10 kV/μs**, demanding extremely fast dielectric recovery. This is why **SF₆** and **vacuum** dominate EHV switchgear.
> - In **HVDC** systems, the absence of natural current zero means **forced current zero via resonant commutation circuits** is essential — a direct application of the low-resistance interruption theory.
> - The Ayrton equation helps in **arc modelling** for power system transient studies (EMTP, PSCAD simulations).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Frequency Recovery Voltage (Across CB after Current Zero)

**Given:** A pure inductive circuit (highly inductive fault) carrying a sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ is interrupted at current zero.

**Objective:** Derive the average rate of rise of recovery voltage (RRRV) and the peak recovery voltage.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Write the source voltage expression.**
The source voltage for the AC system is:
$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the load power factor angle. For a highly inductive load, $\phi \approx 90°$.

**Step 2: Express the system equation using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).**

For a circuit with resistance R, inductance L, arc voltage $v_a$, and source voltage $v(t)$:
$$v(t) = R \cdot i + L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + v_a$$

**Step 3: At the instant of current zero, the arc voltage is approximately equal to the source voltage** (because $Ri = 0$ and $L \frac{di}{dt}$ is small at the peak of recovery voltage, and the system is in transient).

The recovery voltage $v_r(t)$ across the breaker contacts after current zero is:
$$v_r(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi) - v_a(t)$$

**Step 4: For a highly inductive circuit, the recovery voltage peak occurs roughly a quarter cycle after current zero.** Approximating the recovery voltage as a triangular wave of peak $V_m$ over a quarter period $T/4$:
$$\text{RRRV} = \frac{V_m}{T/4} = \frac{4 \cdot V_m}{T}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $T = 1/f$:**
$$\text{RRRV} = 4 \cdot V_m \cdot f$$

**Step 6: Express in terms of RMS voltage for engineering convenience:** $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$:
$$\text{RRRV} = 4\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms} \cdot f \quad \text{(V/s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For a 11 kV system at 50 Hz, RRRV ≈ 4√2 × 11000 × 50 = **3.11 kV/μs**. This is the rate at which the dielectric gap must recover to avoid re-ignition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Arc Resistance from Ayrton's Equation

**Background:** The Ayrton equation is an empirical relation derived by Mrs. Hertha Ayrton (1899–1902) based on experimental data for short arcs in air.

**Given:** Arc voltage depends on arc length, current, and the gas medium.

**Step 1: Write the general empirical form:**
$$v_{arc} = A + B \cdot l + \dfrac{C + D \cdot l}{i}$$

where:
- $A$ = constant anode-cathode voltage drop
- $B$ = voltage gradient per unit length of arc (positive column)
- $C, D$ = constants dependent on the medium
- $l$ = length of the arc (cm)
- $i$ = arc current (A)

**Step 2: For simplicity, in many textbook analyses, this is approximated as:**
$$v_{arc} = k \cdot l + \dfrac{c}{i}$$

where $k$ and $c$ are constants for the given arc medium and length.

**Step 3: Compute arc resistance:**
$$R_{arc} = \frac{v_{arc}}{i} = \frac{k \cdot l}{i} + \frac{c}{i^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation:**
- As $i \to 0$ (near current zero), $R_{arc} \to \infty$. This is why the arc is unstable near current zero and can be extinguished.
- This forms the basis of the "**arc becomes unstable and extinguishes**" near current zero.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Dissipated in the Arc During Interruption

**Given:** An arc voltage $v_a(t)$ and arc current $i(t)$ exist during the arcing period from $t_1$ to $t_2$.

**Step 1: Instantaneous power in the arc:**
$$p_{arc}(t) = v_a(t) \cdot i(t)$$

**Step 2: Energy dissipated in the arc (thermal stress on contacts and surrounding medium):**
$$E_{arc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v_a(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3: For a sinusoidal approximation** (arc voltage ≈ constant $V_a$ near peak, current is sinusoidal $i = I_m \sin(\omega t)$):
$$E_{arc} = \int_{0}^{\pi/\omega} V_a \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral:**
$$E_{arc} = V_a \cdot I_m \cdot \left[-\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega}\right]_{0}^{\pi/\omega}$$

$$E_{arc} = V_a \cdot I_m \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(\pi)}{\omega} = V_a \cdot I_m \cdot \frac{2}{\omega}$$

$$E_{arc} = \frac{2 \cdot V_a \cdot I_m}{\omega}$$

**Step 5: Convert RMS current $I_{rms} = I_m/\sqrt{2}$:**
$$E_{arc} = \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot V_a \cdot I_{rms}}{\omega} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot V_a \cdot I_{rms}}{\pi f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** This $E_{arc}$ is the heat energy that the CB contacts and surrounding dielectric (oil, gas) must absorb without damage. Higher $E_{arc}$ means more stress. SF₆ gas has excellent **heat absorption capacity** and is the preferred medium for high-energy arc interruption.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Current Chopping Phenomenon (Vacuum Circuit Breaker)

**Background:** In a vacuum CB, the arc can extinguish *before* the natural current zero due to the superior dielectric strength of vacuum. This is called **current chopping** and it induces a high overvoltage $V_0$.

**Step 1: At the moment of current chopping at value $I_0$,** the energy stored in the circuit inductance is:
$$E_L = \frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot I_0^{2}$$

**Step 2: This energy is transferred to the stray capacitance $C$ of the system** (CB, busbars, cables), charging it to a peak voltage $V_0$.

By conservation of energy:
$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot V_0^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot I_0^{2}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $V_0$ (overvoltage):**
$$V_0 = I_0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the chopping current $I_0$ in terms of $V_0$ (rearranging the same equation):**
$$I_0 = V_0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$$

**Step 5: To limit $V_0$ (the overvoltage), one can either:**
- Increase $C$ (add surge capacitors across the load).
- Reduce $I_0$ (use contact materials with lower chopping tendency, e.g., CuCr).
- Add **surge arresters** (ZnO) across the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical chopping current values:**
> - **Vacuum CB with CuCr contacts:** $I_0 \approx 2$ to $5$ A.
> - Older **Mercury contacts:** $I_0 \approx 0.5$ to 1 A (very high chopping tendency — outdated).
> - **SF₆ / Oil CB:** $I_0 \approx 0$ (they wait for natural current zero, no significant chopping).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Arc Time Constant (Mayr's Arc Model)

**Background:** Mayr's model (1943) describes the arc conductance near current zero using a thermal time constant $\theta$.

**Step 1: Energy balance in the arc column.** Power input from the circuit = power lost to the surroundings:
$$v \cdot i = P_0 + \frac{dQ}{dt}$$

where $P_0$ is the steady-state power loss and $Q$ is the stored heat in the arc column.

**Step 2: Arc conductance $g$ is proportional to stored heat $Q$:**
$$g = g_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{Q - Q_0}{P_0 \cdot \theta}\right)$$

**Step 3: Differentiating and simplifying, the Mayr equation is:**
$$\frac{1}{g} \cdot \frac{dg}{dt} = \frac{1}{\theta} \left(\frac{v \cdot i}{P_0} - 1\right)$$

**Step 4: The condition for arc extinction** is when $g \to 0$ faster than the current, i.e., the **dielectric recovery rate exceeds the recovery voltage rise rate.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cassie's Model vs. Mayr's Model — Analytical Matrix:**

| Feature | Cassie's Model | Mayr's Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Validity Region** | High current (pre-zero region) | Near current zero (low current) |
| **Assumption** | Arc temperature is constant; arc radius varies | Arc radius is constant; temperature varies |
| **Key Equation** | $\dfrac{1}{g} \cdot \dfrac{dg}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_C}\left(\dfrac{v^2}{V_0^2} - 1\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{g} \cdot \dfrac{dg}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_M}\left(\dfrac{v \cdot i}{P_0} - 1\right)$ |
| **Engineering Use** | Predictions during the main arcing period | Predictions around current zero (success/failure) |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Arc Interruption in Different Media

| Parameter | **Air** (Air Blast CB) | **Oil** (Bulk OCB) | **SF₆** (SF₆ CB) | **Vacuum** (VCB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dielectric strength** | Low (1× air) | High (5–10× air) | Very high (2.5–3× air at same pressure) | Highest |
| **Arc quenching speed** | Fast (blast action) | Moderate (H₂ from oil) | Very fast (electronegative) | Very fast |
| **Maintenance** | High | High (oil filtration) | Very low | Very low |
| **Voltage range** | HV, EHV | MV, HV | MV, HV, EHV | LV, MV (up to 36 kV) |
| **Current chopping** | Low | Low | Low | **High** (needs surge protection) |
| **Environmental hazard** | None | Fire hazard, oil disposal | Greenhouse gas (24,000× CO₂) | None |
| **Typical use today** | Rare | Phased out | Dominant for EHV | Dominant for MV distribution |

```mermaid
        O3["H2 bubble surrounds and cools the arc"]
        O4["De-ionization at current zero"]
        O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4
    end

    subgraph typ2["TYPE 2 AIR BLAST CIRCUIT BREAKER ABCB"]
        A1["Contacts separate in air blast chamber"]
        A2["Compressed air at 20 to 30 bar blasts the arc"]
        A3["Arc is elongated and cooled along the nozzle"]
        A4["Dielectric recovery exceeds RRRV at current zero"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    end

    subgraph typ3["TYPE 3 SF6 GAS CIRCUIT BREAKER"]
        S1["Contacts separate in SF6 gas at 4 to 6 bar"]
        S2["Puffer piston compresses SF6 through nozzle"]
        S3["SF6 captures free electrons electronegative"]
        S4["Heavy SF6 ions cannot sustain the arc"]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    end

    subgraph typ4["TYPE 4 VACUUM CIRCUIT BREAKER VCB"]
        V1["Contacts separate inside vacuum bottle 10 to 5 Torr"]
        V2["Metal vapor arc forms from contact boiling"]
        V3["At current zero metal vapor condenses rapidly"]
        V4["Vacuum gap regains full dielectric strength"]
        V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4
    end

    style O4 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,color:#000
```
