## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the different types of faults that can occur in a power system. Which type is most frequent and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The types of faults in a power system are classified as:

**(a) Symmetrical faults (3-phase):** All three phases short-circuited — 5% of faults.
**(b) Unsymmetrical faults:**
- Single Line-to-Ground (L-G): 70%
- Line-to-Line (L-L): 15%
- Double Line-to-Ground (L-L-G): 10%

**The most frequent fault is the Single Line-to-Ground (L-G) fault** because:
- Overhead lines are highly exposed to **lightning strikes**, which usually affect one phase.
- **Tree contact** typically creates a single-phase path to ground.
- **Insulator pollution** failures usually start on one phase.
- Tall towers attract single-phase flashovers.

> [3 marks: 1 mark list + 1 mark frequency + 1 mark reason ✓]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is SCADA? Mention any four applications of SCADA in power systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a computer-based centralized control system that monitors and controls geographically distributed power system assets in real time.

**Four applications of SCADA in power systems:**

1. **Real-time monitoring** of voltage, current, frequency, MW, MVAR at substations.
2. **Remote control** of circuit breakers, isolators, and tap changers from the load despatch centre.
3. **Alarm management** and **event logging** during faults and abnormal conditions.
4. **Automatic load shedding** under under-frequency conditions to maintain grid stability.

*(Other valid points: data logging, energy management, outage management, state estimation.)*

> [3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks applications ✓]

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a SCADA system** used for power system monitoring and control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **role of SCADA in fault detection, isolation, and restoration** in a substation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SCADA Architecture (7 marks):**

A SCADA system for power systems has a **four-level hierarchical architecture**:

**Level 1 — Field Level (Bay Level):**
This consists of physical equipment in the substation:
- **Instrument Transformers:** CTs (current) and PTs (voltage) for measurement.
- **Circuit Breakers (CBs)** and **Isolators** for switching.
- **Power Transformers** with **tap changers**.
- **Sensors:** temperature, oil level, pressure, SF₆ gas density.

**Level 2 — Station Level (Bay Control Level):**
- **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):** Collects digital and analog data from field devices.
- **BCU (Bay Control Unit):** Local control of individual bays.
- **PLCs:** For automated sequences (auto-reclosing, load shedding).
- **IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices):** Numerical relays with built-in communication.

**Level 3 — Communication Level:**
- **Media:** PLCC, OPGW (optical fibre), microwave, GSM.
- **Protocols:** IEC 60870-5-101/104, DNP3, IEC 61850, Modbus.
- Performs **protocol conversion** between field and control centre.

**Level 4 — Control Centre Level (Master Station):**
- **MTU (Master Terminal Unit) / SCADA Server:** Central processing unit.
- **HMI Workstations:** Operator interfaces with single-line diagrams (SLD).
- **Database/Historian:** Long-term storage of all parameters.
- **Application software:** Alarm handling, trending, AGC, OPF, contingency analysis.

> *(Use the diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks. 7 marks: 2 for levels + 2 for components + 2 for diagram + 1 for protocols.)*

---

**(b) Role of SCADA in Fault Management (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pre-fault Continuous Monitoring:**
SCADA continuously polls all RTUs (typically every 2–4 seconds). The HMI displays the **single-line diagram (SLD)** of the substation in real time.

**Step 2 — Fault Detection by IEDs:**
The numerical relay (IED) detects the fault within **20 ms** (1 cycle at 50 Hz). It identifies the type (L-G, L-L, L-L-G) and the fault current magnitude.

**Step 3 — Trip Command and Isolation:**
The IED sends a **trip signal** to the circuit breaker, which opens within **60 ms**, isolating the faulty section. Total fault clearance is **<80 ms** for 110 kV and above (as per CEA standards).

**Step 4 — SCADA Alarm Generation:**
The RTU detects the **breaker status change** and immediately transmits an **unsolicited message** to the MTU. The HMI:
- Shows a **flashing red alarm** on the affected bay.
- Sounds an **audio alert**.
- Logs the event with **millisecond timestamp (SOE — Sequence of Events)**.

**Step 5 — Auto-Reclosing Attempt:**
For transient faults (e.g., L-G due to lightning), SCADA initiates **auto-reclosing** — typically 1-pole or 3-pole with **dead time** of 0.3–1 second. ~80% of L-G faults are cleared this way.

**Step 6 — Restoration and Reconfiguration:**
If auto-reclosing fails, SCADA assists the operator in:
- **Network reconfiguration** — closing alternate feeders to restore supply.
- **Isolating the faulty section** — opening adjacent isolators to make it safe for maintenance crew.
- **Coordinating field crews** — outage management system (OMS) provides the list of affected customers.

**Step 7 — Post-fault Analysis:**
The **SCADA historian** provides:
- **Disturbance records (DR)** and **fault records (COMTRADE files)**.
- **Trend curves** of pre-fault, during-fault, and post-fault parameters.
- **Reliability indices** (SAIDI, SAIFI) updated automatically.

**Engineering Benefit:**
- Reduction in **outage duration by 30–50%** compared to manual systems.
- Improved **operator situational awareness**.
- Compliance with **CEA (Grid Standards) Regulations, 2010**.

> *(7 marks: 1 each for steps + 1 for benefit. Use a timing diagram for full marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **causes of faults** in power systems. Classify them and give examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 11 kV generator has a subtransient reactance of $X_d'' = 0.15$ pu. It is connected through a transformer (50 MVA, 11/66 kV, $X = 0.10$ pu) to a transmission line ($X = 0.25$ pu on 50 MVA, 66 kV base). A 3-phase fault occurs at the far end of the line. Calculate the fault current in kA and the fault MVA. Use 50 MVA as base MVA. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Causes of Faults (7 marks):**

Faults are caused by a combination of natural, equipment-related, and operational factors.

**Category 1 — Environmental / Natural Causes (2.5 marks):**

| Cause | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **Lightning** | Direct strike on towers/conductors | Induces overvoltages up to 1000 kV |
| **Insulator Pollution** | Salt, dust, bird droppings on insulator surface | Coastal Kerala (KSEB lines in Alappuzha, Kollam) |
| **Flooding** | Underground substation flooding | Monsoon-related in Kerala |
| **Cyclones / Wind** | Tower collapse, conductor snapping | Ockhi cyclone (2017, Kerala) |
| **Earthquakes** | Foundation failure, cable rupture | Rare in Kerala but relevant |

**Category 2 — Equipment / Technical Causes (2.5 marks):**

| Cause | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **Insulation Failure** | Aging, overheating, oil leaks | Transformer Bushing failure |
| **Overloading** | Sustained overcurrent heating conductors | Distribution transformer overloading |
| **Improper Earthing** | High earth resistance | Dangerous step potentials |
| **Human Error** | Wrong switching, dropped tools | Misoperation during maintenance |
| **Poor Maintenance** | Loose connections, rodent damage | Cable joint failure |
| **Switching Transients** | VFTO in GIS | Very Fast Transient Overvoltages |

**Category 3 — Operational Causes (1 mark):**

- Loss of synchronism between generators.
- Loss of excitation in alternators.
- Sudden load rejection causing over-speed.
- Prime mover failure.

**Category 4 — External Physical Causes (1 mark):**

- Vehicle/aviation accidents on towers.
- Construction equipment contact.
- Rodent/insect entry into switchgear panels.
- Vandalism/theft of conductors.

> *(7 marks: 2.5 + 2.5 + 1 + 1 split as above. Add a flowchart for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Generator: 11 kV, $X_d'' = 0.15$ pu
- Transformer: 50 MVA, 11/66 kV, $X_{T} = 0.10$ pu
- Transmission Line: $X_L = 0.25$ pu on 50 MVA, 66 kV base
- Base MVA: $MVA_b = 50$ MVA
- Fault location: Far end of transmission line (66 kV side)
- Fault type: 3-phase (symmetrical)

**Step 1: Convert all impedances to common 50 MVA base.**
All values are already on 50 MVA base, so:

$$X_g = 0.15 \text{ pu}$$
$$X_{T} = 0.10 \text{ pu}$$
$$X_L = 0.25 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 2: Compute total Thevenin reactance.**

$$X_{th} = X_g + X_T + X_L$$

$$X_{th} = 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.25 = 0.50 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 3: Compute per-unit fault current.**
For a 3-phase bolted fault, with prefault voltage = 1.0 pu:

$$I_{f(pu)} = \dfrac{V_{pre}}{X_{th}} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.50} = 2.0 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 4: Compute base current at the fault point (66 kV side).**

$$I_b = \dfrac{MVA_b \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 66 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_b = \dfrac{50,000}{1.732 \times 66} = \dfrac{50,000}{114.31}$$

$$I_b = 437.4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5: Compute actual fault current.**

$$I_f = I_{f(pu)} \times I_b = 2.0 \times 437.4 = 874.8 \text{ A} \approx 0.875 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 6: Compute fault MVA.**

$$MVA_f = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_f \times 10^{-3}$$

$$MVA_f = \sqrt{3} \times 66 \times 0.875 \times 10^{-3}$$

Wait, let me recompute using $I_f$ in amperes:

$$MVA_f = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \times 66 \times 10^3 \times 874.8}{10^6} = \dfrac{1.732 \times 66 \times 874.8}{1000}$$

$$MVA_f = \dfrac{1.732 \times 66 \times 0.8748}{1} = \dfrac{100.0}{1} \approx 100 \text{ MVA}$$

**Verification using formula:**
$$MVA_f = \dfrac{MVA_b}{X_{th}} = \dfrac{50}{0.50} = 100 \text{ MVA} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Fault current, $I_f = 0.875$ kA (874.8 A)**
- **Fault MVA = 100 MVA**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting to convert the per-unit reactance to the chosen base MVA. Always check the base MVA of each given reactance.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** Using **line voltage** in the per-unit to actual conversion formula instead of converting to **per-phase voltage**. The base current formula uses $V_L$ (line voltage), but per-phase impedance uses $V_{ph}$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting units in the final answer (kA vs A).
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost):** Not writing the final answer in a **box** as required by KTU valuation norms.
> - **Pro Tip:** Always cross-check using the formula $MVA_f = MVA_b / X_{th(pu)}$ as a sanity check.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 (High-Density Summary)**

**1. Fault Definition & Causes**
- A fault is any abnormal current path causing deviation from normal voltage/current.
- Causes: Environmental (lightning, pollution), Equipment (insulation failure, overloading), Operational (loss of sync), Human (error, vandalism).

**2. Fault Classification — MUST MEMORIZE**
- **Symmetrical (3-phase):** 5% frequency, highest current, balanced analysis.
- **Unsymmetrical:** L-G (70%), L-L (15%), L-L-G (10%).
- **By duration:** Transient (auto-reclose) vs Permanent (manual).
- **By location:** Line, Transformer, Generator, Busbar, Cable.

**3. Symmetrical Components (Fortescue's Theorem)**
- Any unbalanced 3-phase system → 3 balanced systems: positive, negative, zero.
- Operator $a = 1 \angle 120°$; $1 + a + a^2 = 0$.
- Sequence network connections: L-G (series), L-L (Z₁+Z₂), L-L-G (Z₁ + Z₂∥Z₀), L-L-L (Z₁ only).

**4. Per-Unit Method — MUST MEMORIZE FORMULAS**
- $Z_{pu(new)} = Z_{pu(old)} \times (MVA_{b,new}/MVA_{b,old}) \times (V_{b,old}/V_{b,new})^2$.
- $I_{f(pu)} = 1.0 / X_{th(pu)}$.
- $MVA_f = MVA_b / X_{th(pu)}$.
- $I_b = MVA_b / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L)$.

**5. SCADA — Four-Level Architecture**
- Field → Station (RTU/IED) → Communication → Control Centre (MTU/HMI).
- Communication protocols: IEC 60870-5-101/104, DNP3, IEC 61850, Modbus.
- Communication media: PLCC, OPGW, microwave, GSM, 4G.

**6. SCADA in Fault Management — 7-Step Process**
- Continuous polling → IED detection (20 ms) → Trip signal → Breaker opens (60 ms) → SCADA alarm → Auto-reclose attempt → Restoration or manual intervention.

**7. Indian Context — MUST KNOW**
- CEA Safety Regulations 2010.
- KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board Limited) uses SCADA for 110 kV+ substations.
- PowerGrid operates NLDC/RLDCs with EMS.
- Reliability indices: SAIDI, SAIFI, CAIDI, ASAI.

**8. KTU Exam-Specific Tips**
- Always draw a **single-line diagram** for fault calculation problems.
- Always show **base selection** explicitly.
- Always use **boxed final answers**.
- For SCADA questions, include **timing values (ms)** for full marks.
- For unsymmetrical faults, state the **sequence network connection** explicitly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Advice:** Module 2 of PCSFT403 is a **high-weightage** module. Master the **per-unit method** and the **sequence networks** — these appear in nearly every ESE paper. For SCADA, focus on the **practical applications** rather than abstract theory, as KTU examiners value **engineering relevance** over rote memorization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Fuses — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuse (KTU Board Definition):** A fuse is a **sacrificial, single-use, overcurrent protective device** that consists of a fusible element (a calibrated metal strip or wire) designed to melt, vaporize, and physically open the circuit when the current flowing through it exceeds a predetermined safe value for a sufficient duration. It is the **simplest, cheapest, and most reliable** form of circuit protection used in low-voltage and high-voltage electrical installations.

---

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sacrificial Weakest Link"

Think of a fuse as the **deliberately engineered weakest link** in an electrical chain, much like a **pressure-release valve** in a pressure cooker:

| Real-World Analogy | Electrical Equivalent (Fuse) |
|---|---|
| A **sacrificial anode** on a ship's hull that corrodes to protect the ship's iron body | The **fusible element** melts to protect the costly wiring, transformer, or motor downstream |
| A **fuse cartridge** in a firecracker — designed to fail safely at a set temperature | A **fuse link** rated to blow at a fixed current × time product |
| The **weakest glass pane** marked in a building to allow fire-escape breaking | The **lowest-rated fuse** in a circuit hierarchy that breaks first, isolating the fault |
| A **safety fuse wire** in old home wiring that burns out before the house burns down | A **modern HRC cartridge** that interrupts fault current in milliseconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Philosophical Point:** The fuse is a *one-time* device. Once it operates (blows), it must be **replaced**, not reset. This is in direct contrast to a **circuit breaker**, which is reusable. The KTU examiner will often test this contrast: *"Why prefer a fuse over a breaker?"* — Answer: **Cost, simplicity, no maintenance, no moving parts, and inherently current-limiting action.**

---

## 1.2 Functional Requirements of an Ideal Fuse Element (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

An *ideal* fuse element, as per KTU board standards, must satisfy **all** of the following:

1. **Low melting point** — so it operates at moderate overcurrent.
2. **Low resistivity** — so it does not cause unnecessary voltage drop during normal operation.
3. **High conductivity** — to carry rated current without overheating.
4. **No tendency to oxidize** — to maintain stable characteristics over its service life.
5. **Low cost** — since it is a consumable, throw-away device.
6. **Reliable operation** — must blow *only* on fault, not on harmless surges (e.g., motor starting).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Pearl:** The textbook answer to *"Why is silver preferred as a fuse element material?"* must include **all four** of these points: low resistivity, no oxidation, stable time-current curve, and a *predictable* melting point of **961.8 °C**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Ratings and Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Definition | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Rated Current ($I_n$)** | The maximum current the fuse can carry continuously without deterioration | **Amperes (A)** |
| **Rated Voltage ($U_n$)** | The maximum system voltage the fuse can safely interrupt | **Volts (V)** — e.g., 250 V, 415 V, 11 kV |
| **Breaking Capacity (kA)** | The maximum prospective fault current the fuse can safely interrupt without explosion | **kA** — e.g., 80 kA, 120 kA for HRC |
| **Fusing Factor ($f$)** | Ratio of minimum fusing current to rated current | Dimensionless, $f > 1$ |
| **Pre-arcing Time ($t_1$)** | Time from fault initiation to melting of element | Milliseconds (ms) |
| **Arcing Time ($t_2$)** | Time from arc ignition to arc extinction | Milliseconds (ms) |
| **Total Operating Time ($t = t_1 + t_2$)** | Sum of pre-arcing and arcing time | ms |
| **I²t (Ampere-squared-seconds)** | Energy let-through characteristic | A²s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — What Happens *Inside* a Fuse During Operation

The blowing of a fuse is a **three-stage physical process**. KTU examiners frequently test this sequence:

### Stage 1 — Pre-arcing (Heating) Phase
- Fault current $\uparrow$ → $I^2R$ heating in the element $\uparrow$
- Temperature rises until the element reaches its **melting point**.
- Duration: depends on the magnitude of the fault current and the **thermal capacity** of the element.
- This is governed by the equation: 
$$Q = I^2 R t = m \, s \, \Delta\theta$$
- At the end of this stage, the element **melts** and an arc is initiated.

### Stage 2 — Arcing Phase
- After melting, an **electric arc** is established across the break in the element.
- The arc is sustained by the system voltage until the current passes through natural zero.
- The arc voltage opposes the source voltage, **limiting** the fault current.
- This is the *current-limiting* action unique to HRC fuses.

### Stage 3 — Extinction and Recovery
- At current zero, the arc extinguishes.
- The **rate of rise of recovery voltage (RRRV)** must not exceed the dielectric recovery strength of the gap, else **re-ignition** occurs.
- If recovery is successful, the circuit is permanently opened.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HRC fuses are "current limiting":** A high-rupturing-capacity fuse melts and develops an arc voltage *greater* than the system voltage within a few milliseconds. This **opposes** the source EMF, forcing the fault current to a *lower* peak than it would otherwise reach. The fuse thus "limits" the let-through energy.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and memorize every row. Every KTU exam question on fuses will reduce to one or more of these equations.**

| # | Formula / Law | Equation | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Preece's Equation** (for round wire) | $I = a \cdot d^{\,3/2}$ | $I$ = fusing current (A), $d$ = diameter (mm), $a$ = material constant |
| 2 | **Preece's Equation** (alternate form) | $I^2 = k \cdot d^3$ | Equivalent to (1); used to find *minimum* fusing current |
| 3 | **Material Constants (Preece)** | $a_{Cu} = 80.5$, $a_{Ag} = 75.3$, $a_{Al} = 59.2$, $a_{Sn} = 12.8$, $a_{Pb} = 10.8$ | Memorize the **silver** and **tin** constants |
| 4 | **Fusing Factor** | $f = \dfrac{I_{min}}{I_n}$ | Always $f > 1$; a lower value indicates a *better* fuse |
| 5 | **Minimum Fusing Current** | $I_{min} = f \cdot I_n$ | The smallest current that will eventually melt the element |
| 6 | **Actual Fusing Current** | $I_f = k \cdot f \cdot I_n$ | Where $k \approx 1.45$ for silver/copper to account for short-time vs. continuous |
| 7 | **Energy Balance on Element** | $I^2 R t = m \, s \, (\theta_m - \theta_0)$ | Used to derive the fusing time equation |
| 8 | **Time-Current (inverse) relation** | $t \propto \dfrac{1}{(I/I_n)^2}$ | Higher fault current $\Rightarrow$ faster blow |
| 9 | **Let-through Energy (I²t)** | $I^2 t = \text{const}$ for adiabatic heating | Used in **discrimination / selectivity** studies |
| 10 | **Recovery Condition (no re-ignition)** | $\left.\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right\vert_{rec} < \left.\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right\vert_{dielectric}$ | Rate of rise of recovery voltage (RRRV) check |
| 11 | **Fuse Resistance (approx.)** | $R = \rho \dfrac{l}{A}$ | Standard resistivity formula applied to fuse element |
| 12 | **Fusing Factor (typical values)** | HRC: 1.1 – 1.4; Rewirable: 1.25 – 2.0 | Lower is better — HRC fuses are more precise |

---

## 2.3 Fuse Element Materials — Comparative Analysis

| Material | Melting Point (°C) | Resistivity ($\mu\Omega \cdot cm$) | Oxidation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Silver (Ag)** | **961.8** | 1.62 | None | **Best** — HRC cartridge fuses |
| **Copper (Cu)** | 1084.6 | 1.72 | Forms oxide layer at high T | Tinned copper in HRC |
| **Tin (Sn)** | 231.9 | 11.3 | Slight | Tinned fuses, element coatings |
| **Lead (Pb)** | 327.4 | 21.9 | None | Rewirable fuses (low cost) |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | 419.5 | 5.9 | Forms protective oxide | Rewirable fuses |
| **Aluminium (Al)** | 660.3 | 2.65 | Forms oxide | Special applications |
| **Alloy (Tin-Lead, 50:50)** | ~ 230 | ~ 15 | None | Domestic rewirable fuses |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick Question:** *"Why are pure tin/lead wires not used in industrial HRC fuses despite their low melting points?"*  
> **Answer:** Their **high resistivity** causes excessive $I^2R$ losses and voltage drop *during normal operation*, leading to nuisance blowing on harmless current surges.

---

## 2.4 Classification of Fuses (KTU Modular Hierarchy)

```
FUSES
│
├── Low Voltage (LV) Fuses
│     ├── Rewirable (Kit-Kat) Fuse
│     ├── Cartridge Fuse
│     │     ├── D-type
│     │     └── Link-type
│     ├── HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) Fuse
│     │     ├── Blade/Plug-in type (automotive)
│     │     ├── Bolted-tag type
│     │     └── Bottle/High-voltage HRC
│     ├── Drop-out Fuse
│     └── Striker Fuse
│
├── High Voltage (HV) Fuses
│     ├── Cartridge HRC (> 3.3 kV)
│     ├── Liquid Fuse
│     ├── Expulsion Fuse
│     └── HV Drop-out Fuse
│
└── Semiconductor / Ultra-fast Fuses
      ├── Rectifier protection fuses
      └── IGBT/Thyristor protection
```

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Distribution Boards:** Rewirable fuses (now largely replaced by **MCBs** but still tested in the KTU syllabus).
- **Industrial Motor Control Centers (MCCs):** HRC fuses for short-circuit back-up to contactors + overload relays.
- **Semiconductor Power Electronics:** Ultra-fast (rectifier) fuses with $I^2t$ below the SCR/diode's $I^2t$ rating.
- **Distribution Transformers:** HV drop-out fuses act as the *primary-side isolation and overcurrent device* — when they blow, the fuse carrier *drops* out visibly, giving the lineman a clear visual indication.
- **Battery Banks / EV Packs:** DC-rated HRC fuses with high breaking capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Preece's Equation $I = a \cdot d^{3/2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Preece"?** This is the *single most-tested derivation* in the KTU Electrical Technology and Safety module. The 2019, 2021, and 2023 university exams all carried direct or variant questions on this. You will earn **full 7 marks** if you can derive it cleanly.

### Assumptions
1. The fuse element is a **cylindrical wire** of diameter $d$ (m) and length $l$ (m).
2. The element is **uniformly heated** along its length (negligible end-effects).
3. **Steady-state** melting occurs — all the $I^2R$ heat goes into raising the element to its melting point.
4. The element reaches **melting point** $T_m$ from ambient $T_a$, so $\Delta T = T_m - T_a$.

### Step 1 — Resistive Heating Power
The electrical power dissipated as heat in the element is:
$$P = I^2 R$$

### Step 2 — Resistance in Terms of Geometry
For a cylindrical wire:
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{A} = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{\tfrac{\pi}{4}d^2} = \frac{4\rho l}{\pi d^2}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Balance
Heat generated in time $t$ must equal the heat required to raise the element to its melting point:
$$I^2 R \, t = m \, s \, \Delta T$$
where:
- $m$ = mass of the element
- $s$ = specific heat (J/kg·°C)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise

### Step 4 — Mass in Terms of Geometry
$$m = \rho_m \cdot V = \rho_m \cdot A \cdot l = \rho_m \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot l$$
where $\rho_m$ is the material's mass density (kg/m³).

### Step 5 — Substitute into Energy Equation
$$I^2 \cdot \frac{4\rho l}{\pi d^2} \cdot t = \rho_m \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot l \cdot s \cdot \Delta T$$

Cancel $l$ from both sides:
$$I^2 \cdot \frac{4\rho}{\pi d^2} \cdot t = \rho_m \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot s \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $I^2$
$$I^2 = \frac{\rho_m \cdot s \cdot \Delta T \cdot \pi^2}{16 \, \rho \, t} \cdot d^4$$

For a given material and a given operating time $t$, all the constants on the right can be lumped into a single constant $K$:
$$I^2 = K \cdot d^4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \sqrt{K} \cdot d^2$$

But this gives $I \propto d^2$, *not* $I \propto d^{3/2}$!

### Step 7 — Correction: Preece's Empirical Correction
Preece found **experimentally** that the exponent is **3/2**, not 2. The discrepancy arises because the **resistivity $\rho$ itself increases with temperature** in the hot zone, the heat is not perfectly uniformly distributed, and there are **end-effect** losses.

Preece's empirical fit (and the form universally used in KTU textbooks) is:
$$\boxed{I = a \cdot d^{\,3/2}}$$

where $a$ is a constant that depends on the fuse material. Equivalent squared form:
$$I^2 = a^2 \cdot d^3$$

**In KTU notation**, the squared form is written as:
$$\boxed{I^2 = k \, d^3}$$
where $k = a^2$ is the material constant.

### Step 8 — Material Constants (Memorize These)
| Material | $a$ | $k = a^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| Copper | 80.5 | 6480 |
| Silver | 75.3 | 5670 |
| Tin | 12.8 | 164 |
| Lead | 10.8 | 117 |
| Aluminium | 59.2 | 3505 |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Material Constants:** *"Come, See The Little Ants"* → **C**u=80, **S**n=12, **T**in (Sn)=12, **L**ittle (Pb)=10, **A**l=59. (Not exact, but the rough magnitudes help.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Fusing Factor

**Definition:** The fusing factor is the ratio of the *minimum* current that will *just* melt the fuse element to the *rated* current:
$$f = \frac{I_{min}}{I_{rated}}$$

**Physical Meaning:**
- $I_{rated}$ = the current the fuse can carry **indefinitely** without blowing.
- $I_{min}$ = the smallest current that, if it flowed for a *long* time, would eventually melt the element.
- A fusing factor of 1.0 would be **ideal** (no margin) — but practically impossible.
- A *low* fusing factor means the fuse is **more precise** and operates close to its rated current.

**Numerical Example (typical):**
- A 100 A HRC fuse with $f = 1.1$:
$$I_{min} = 1.1 \times 100 = 110 \text{ A}$$

This means the fuse will *just barely* blow if **110 A** flows continuously for an extended period, but it will carry 100 A indefinitely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing *fusing factor* with *safety factor*. The fusing factor is **intrinsic to the fuse design**; safety factor is an engineering choice on top of rated current.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Time-Current Inverse Relationship

Starting from the energy balance in a simplified adiabatic form:
$$I^2 R \, t = \text{const} = W_f$$
where $W_f$ is the energy required to bring the element to its fusing temperature.

Solving for the operating time $t$:
$$t = \frac{W_f}{I^2 R}$$

If we express current in *per-unit* of the rated current $I_n$, and recognizing that $W_f$ and $R$ are *properties of the element* (constants for a given fuse), we get:
$$t = \frac{C}{I^2} \quad \text{where} \quad C = \frac{W_f}{R}$$

Or, normalizing:
$$\boxed{\frac{t}{t_n} = \left(\frac{I_n}{I}\right)^2}$$

This is the **fundamental inverse-square time-current characteristic** of a fuse. The graph is a rectangular hyperbola in the $t$–$I$ plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** If fault current is *doubled*, operating time is *quartered*. If fault current is *tripled*, operating time is *one-ninth*. This is why a fuse blows almost instantly (milliseconds) on a bolted short-circuit, but may take seconds on a moderate overload.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Preece's Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Sample Problem Style:** *"A copper fuse wire of diameter 0.7 mm is to be used on a 220 V supply. Find the minimum fusing current and the current rating of the fuse. Given: material constant $a_{Cu} = 80.5$. Assume fusing factor $f = 1.5$."*

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Preece's equation to find the minimum fusing current:**
$$I_{min} = a \cdot d^{3/2}$$
$$I_{min} = 80.5 \times (0.7)^{3/2}$$

Calculate $(0.7)^{3/2}$:
$$(0.7)^{3/2} = (0.7)^{1.5} = \sqrt{0.7^3} = \sqrt{0.343} = 0.5857$$

Therefore:
$$I_{min} = 80.5 \times 0.5857 = 47.15 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Find the rated current using the fusing factor:**
$$f = \frac{I_{min}}{I_{rated}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{rated} = \frac{I_{min}}{f}$$
$$I_{rated} = \frac{47.15}{1.5} = 31.43 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — State the answer with units and physical interpretation:**
> The **minimum fusing current is ≈ 47.15 A** and the **rated current is ≈ 31.43 A**. A standard 32 A fuse (next higher standard rating) would be the appropriate selection.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Fuse for the Job

| Application | Recommended Fuse Type | Key Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Domestic house wiring | Rewirable (Kit-Kat) | Low cost, easily replaced, low fault level |
| Industrial motor short-circuit back-up | HRC Cartridge (bolted-tag) | High breaking capacity, no maintenance |
| Power semiconductor (SCR/diode) | Ultra-fast / Rectifier fuse | $I^2t$ below device rating |
| Pole-mounted distribution transformer | HV Drop-out fuse | Visible isolation, automatic operation |
| Battery / DC system | DC-rated HRC | DC has no natural current zero → harder to interrupt |
| Capacitor bank | Special HRC with current-limiting | High inrush current during switching |
| Traction / locomotive | High-voltage HRC | Vibration resistance, high kA rating |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Stages of Fuse Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Normal Operation] --> B{Fault Current Detected?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Stage 1: Pre-arcing Phase]
    C --> C1[I²R Heating in Element]
    C1 --> C2[Element Reaches Melting Point]
    C2 --> D[Element Melts and Opens]
    D --> E[Stage 2: Arcing Phase]
    E --> E1[Electric Arc Established]
    E1 --> E2[Arc Voltage > System Voltage]
    E2 --> E3[Current Limited to Cut-off Value]
    E3 --> F{Current Zero Crossing?}
    F -- No --> E3
    F -- Yes --> G[Stage 3: Extinction Phase]
    G --> G1[Dielectric Recovery]
    G1 --> G2{RRRV < Dielectric Strength?}
    G2 -- No --> E3
    G2 -- Yes --> H[Circuit Permanently Isolated]
    H --> I[Fuse Requires Replacement]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — HRC Cartridge Fuse Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ext[Intact HRC Cartridge Assembly]
        E1[End Cap 1] --- C1[Ceramic Body]
        C1 --- F1[Fusible Element Ag or Cu]
        F1 --- Q1[Quartz Sand Filler]
        Q1 --- F2[Second Element Section]
        F2 --- C2[Ceramic Body 2]
        C2 --- E2[End Cap 2]
    end
    F1 -.Melts on Fault.-> ARC[Arc Extinguished by Quartz Sand]
    ARC --> LMT[Current Limited Below Prospective Peak]
    LMT --> SAFE[Downstream Equipment Protected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Functional Points Visible in the Diagram:**
> 1. The **ceramic body** provides mechanical strength and arc containment.
> 2. The **quartz sand** filler absorbs the arc energy and de-ionizes the arc plasma.
> 3. The **silver fusible element** (or tinned copper) provides predictable, repeatable melting.
> 4. The **end caps** (ferrule or bolted-tag) provide electrical connection and mechanical sealing.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — Rewirable vs. HRC Fuse Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Select Protection Device] --> Q1{Fault Level at Bus?}
    Q1 -- Less Than 4 kA --> RW[Rewirable Kit-Kat Fuse]
    Q1 -- Greater Than 4 kA --> Q2{Criticality of Load?}
    Q2 -- Domestic Light Loads --> RW
    Q2 -- Industrial or Motor --> HRC[HRC Cartridge Fuse]
    HRC --> Q3{Need Current Limiting?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> HRC
    Q3 -- No --> CB[Consider Circuit Breaker]
    RW --> CHECK[Verify Breaking Capacity Greater Than Prospective Fault Current]
    HRC --> CHECK
    CHECK --> DONE[Fuse Selected and Installed]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Fuse–Relay Coordination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUPPLY[Source] --> BUS[Distribution Bus]
    BUS --> F1[Fuse on Feeder 1]
    BUS --> F2[Fuse on Feeder 2]
    BUS --> CB[Main Circuit Breaker]
    F1 --> L1[Load 1]
    F2 --> L2[Load 2]
    F1 -- Blows First on Feeder 1 Fault --> ISOL1[Feeder 1 Isolated]
    F2 -- Blows First on Feeder 2 Fault --> ISOL2[Feeder 2 Isolated]
    ISOL1 --> SAFE[Discrimination Achieved]
    ISOL2 --> SAFE
    SAFE --> NOTE[Only the Faulted Feeder Loses Power]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrimination / Selectivity Principle:** For proper coordination, the **I²t (let-through energy)** of the downstream fuse must be *less than* the **I²t pre-arcing** of the upstream protective device. This ensures the smaller fuse blows *first*, isolating only the faulted section — a favourite KTU board topic in Part-B questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part-A carries 3 marks each. Expect **one** Part-A question on fuses per exam paper. Answers should be **3 to 4 lines maximum**, **definition-style**, with no derivations.

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Define a fuse. List any four desirable properties of fuse material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A fuse is a protective device that **automatically opens a circuit** when the current exceeds a predetermined value for a specified time, by means of a fusible element that melts.
> 
> Desirable properties of fuse material:
> 1. **Low melting point** (e.g., tin 232 °C, lead 327 °C) for easy operation.
> 2. **Low resistivity** (e.g., silver 1.62 µΩ·cm) to minimize voltage drop.
> 3. **High conductivity** to carry rated current without overheating.
> 4. **No tendency to oxidize** to maintain stable characteristics (silver is the best in this regard).

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2024]**
**What is fusing factor? Why is a low fusing factor considered superior?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The fusing factor is the **ratio of minimum fusing current to the rated current** of the fuse, i.e., $f = I_{min} / I_n$. It is always greater than 1.
> 
> A **lower fusing factor** (closer to 1) means the fuse operates *closer* to its rated current, providing **better accuracy** and **less nuisance blowing** on harmless overloads. HRC fuses have fusing factors of 1.1–1.4, while rewirable fuses have higher values (1.25–2.0), making HRC the superior choice.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part-B questions carry 14 marks each with internal choice. Below are **two full alternative questions** (OR-style) — exactly as they would appear on the university question paper.

---

### **Question B1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of Preece's equation, derive the relationship between fusing current and fuse-wire diameter. State any three assumptions made. List the material constants for silver, copper, and tin.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 5 A-rated HRC cartridge fuse has a fusing factor of 1.3 and a 5 % safety margin is to be applied. Calculate: (i) the minimum fusing current, (ii) the actual fusing current, and (iii) the minimum prospective current that will operate the fuse in 0.1 s if the I²t let-through of the fuse at 0.1 s is 250 A²s.

---

#### **Solution to B1(a): [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — State Preece's Equation:**
Preece's equation gives the **minimum fusing current** of a cylindrical wire fuse element:
$$I = a \cdot d^{3/2} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I^2 = k \cdot d^3$$
where $a$ (or $k = a^2$) is a constant depending on the fuse material.

**Step 2 — Derivation (Key Steps):**
Starting from the energy balance: the heat generated in the element equals the heat required to raise it to its melting point.

$$I^2 R \, t = m \, s \, \Delta T$$

Substituting $R = \dfrac{4 \rho l}{\pi d^2}$ and $m = \rho_m \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot l$:

$$I^2 \cdot \frac{4\rho l}{\pi d^2} \cdot t = \rho_m \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot l \cdot s \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for $I$ and lumping all material/time constants into a single constant $a$:

$$\boxed{I = a \cdot d^{3/2}}$$

**Step 3 — Assumptions (any three, 1 mark each):**
1. The fuse wire is a **uniform cylinder** of constant cross-section.
2. Heat is **uniformly generated** along the entire length (end-effects neglected).
3. The wire is **electrically and thermally homogeneous** (no joints or weak spots in the middle).
4. The **resistivity is constant** during heating (in reality it varies with temperature — this is why Preece's law is empirical with exponent 3/2, not 2).
5. Steady-state melting — the heat loss to surroundings is **negligible** during the short pre-arcing time.

**Step 4 — Material Constants (3 marks — list all three):**
| Material | Constant $a$ |
|---|---|
| Silver | 75.3 |
| Copper | 80.5 |
| Tin | 12.8 |

---

#### **Solution to B1(b): [7 marks]**

**Given:** $I_n = 5$ A, $f = 1.3$, safety margin = 5 %, $t = 0.1$ s, $I^2t = 250$ A²s.

**(i) Minimum fusing current:**
$$I_{min} = f \times I_n = 1.3 \times 5 = 6.5 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Actual fusing current (with 5 % safety margin):**
A safety margin of 5 % means the actual fusing current is *increased* by 5 % above the theoretical minimum, OR it is the current at which the fuse is *rated to blow* under short-time conditions. The standard interpretation in KTU:
$$I_{actual} = f \times I_n \times (1 + 0.05) = 1.3 \times 5 \times 1.05 = 6.825 \text{ A}$$

(If the alternative interpretation is used: $I_{actual} = 1.45 \times f \times I_n = 1.45 \times 1.3 \times 5 = 9.425$ A — the examiner may accept either with the proper assumption stated.)

**(iii) Minimum prospective current to operate the fuse in 0.1 s:**
Using the I²t let-through:
$$I^2 \cdot t = 250 \text{ A}^2\text{s}$$
$$I^2 = \frac{250}{0.1} = 2500$$
$$I = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \text{ A}$$

> **Answer:** Minimum fusing current = **6.5 A**, Actual fusing current = **6.825 A** (or **9.425 A** per alternative interpretation), Minimum prospective current to operate in 0.1 s = **50 A**.

---

### **Question B2: [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3] (OR alternative to B1)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **High Rupturing Capacity (HRC) cartridge fuse**. State any three advantages of HRC fuses over rewirable fuses.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a time-current characteristic curve, explain the operating principle of a fuse. Distinguish clearly between **pre-arcing time**, **arcing time**, and **total operating time**, and explain what is meant by the **cut-off current**.

---

#### **Solution to B2(a): [7 marks]**

**Construction of HRC Cartridge Fuse:**

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Ceramic body** | Steatite or porcelain | Mechanical strength, heat resistance, arc containment |
| **Fusible element** | Silver or tinned copper | Melts to interrupt fault current |
| **Quartz sand filler** | High-purity SiO₂ | Quenches the arc; absorbs arc energy |
| **End caps / Ferrules** | Brass, nickel-plated | Electrical connection; seal the body |
| **Indicator / striker** | Mechanical flag (optional) | Visual/remote indication of operation |

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. Under **normal current**, the element carries the load without significant heating (temperature stays well below melting point).
2. When a **fault occurs**, the heavy fault current causes rapid $I^2R$ heating.
3. The element **melts** within milliseconds (pre-arcing phase, $t_1$).
4. An **arc** is established across the gap, but the surrounding quartz sand **de-ionizes** the arc plasma.
5. The arc voltage rises rapidly — exceeding the system voltage — which **forces the current down** to zero before it can reach the prospective peak.
6. The current is thus "**cut off**" at a value far below the prospective fault current, and the circuit is permanently opened.

**Three Advantages of HRC over Rewirable Fuses (3 marks):**
1. **High breaking capacity** (up to 120 kA) — safe interruption of very high prospective fault currents without explosion.
2. **Current-limiting action** — the let-through energy is far less, providing better protection for downstream equipment.
3. **No maintenance, no renewal of fuse wire** — once it operates, replace the entire cartridge. Cleaner and safer than rewiring a Kit-Kat.
4. *Bonus*: Stable, repeatable time-current characteristics; non-deteriorating; compact size.

---

#### **Solution to B2(b): [7 marks]**

**Time-Current Characteristic Curve:**

The T-C curve is a plot of **fusing time (seconds, log scale)** on the Y-axis vs. **prospective current (A, log scale)** on the X-axis. It is an **inverse rectangular hyperbola**, asymptoting to the rated current at the bottom (infinite time) and to a few milliseconds at the top (very high fault currents).

**Three Time Zones on the Curve:**

| Region | Current Range | Time Range | Dominant Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lower band** | $I_n$ to $2I_n$ | Minutes to hours | $I^2R$ heating; slow melting |
| **Mid band** | $2I_n$ to $10I_n$ | Seconds | Rapid heating, melting |
| **Upper band** | $> 10I_n$ | < 0.1 s | Pre-arcing dominates; near-instantaneous |

**Time Definitions (2 marks each):**

- **Pre-arcing time ($t_1$):** The interval from the moment the fault current begins to flow until the moment the element actually **melts and breaks**. During $t_1$, the element is being heated. This depends on $I^2R$ heating and the thermal mass of the element.

- **Arcing time ($t_2$):** The interval from the moment the element breaks (and an arc is struck) until the arc is **completely extinguished** at the next current zero. The arc voltage opposes the source voltage, and the arc must withstand the **rate of rise of recovery voltage (RRRV)**.

- **Total operating time ($t = t_1 + t_2$):** The complete clearing time of the fault, measured from fault initiation to permanent circuit interruption.

**Cut-off Current (2 marks):**
> **Cut-off current** is the **maximum instantaneous value of current** actually reached during the interruption process. It is the value at which the fuse "**cuts off**" the current before it can rise to the full prospective peak. It is always **less than** the prospective peak fault current that *would* have flowed if the fuse had not intervened. The ratio of cut-off current to prospective peak is the **cut-off factor** (always < 1 for a current-limiting fuse).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "fusing factor" with "safety factor"** — they are *not* the same. Fusing factor = $I_{min}/I_n$ (intrinsic). Safety factor is a designer-applied multiplier.
> 2. **Forgetting that $I^2t$ is constant only under adiabatic conditions** — in practice, there is heat loss to surroundings, so the value is approximate.
> 3. **Mixing up pre-arcing and arcing time definitions** — pre-arcing is *before* the element melts, arcing is *after* the element melts.
> 4. **Wrong material constant for Preece's equation** — if the question says "silver" you MUST use $a = 75.3$, not copper's 80.5. Read the problem carefully.
> 5. **Forgetting units in Preece's equation** — diameter is in **mm**, not m, for the standard form of the equation.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — at least **3 assumptions** are required for full marks on Preece's equation.
> 7. **Drawing the T-C curve on linear axes instead of log-log** — KTU board standard is log-log scale.
> 8. **Confusing the direction of protection** — a fuse protects the *downstream* equipment, not the *source*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ A fuse is a **sacrificial, single-use, overcurrent protective device** based on **melting of a fusible element**.
- ☐ The ideal fuse material: **low melting point, low resistivity, no oxidation, low cost, reliable**.
- ☐ **Silver** is the best fuse element material (despite high cost) because of low resistivity and zero oxidation.
- ☐ **Rewirable (Kit-Kat) fuses** are for low fault levels (domestic); **HRC fuses** for high fault levels (industrial).
- ☐ **HRC fuses are current-limiting** — they force the current to a *cut-off* value far below the prospective peak.

### Key Definitions
- ☐ **Fusing factor** $f = I_{min} / I_n$ — always $> 1$; lower is better.
- ☐ **Pre-arcing time** — fault initiation to element melting.
- ☐ **Arcing time** — element melting to arc extinction.
- ☐ **Cut-off current** — actual peak current reached; *less* than prospective peak.
- ☐ **I²t (let-through energy)** — measure of energy dissipated in the fault loop; key to *discrimination*.

### Formulas (Memorize Cold)
- ☐ **Preece:** $I = a \cdot d^{3/2}$ or $I^2 = k d^3$ (diameter in **mm**).
- ☐ **Fusing factor:** $f = I_{min} / I_n$.
- ☐ **Time-current:** $t \propto 1 / I^2$ (inverse-square).
- ☐ **Material constants:** $a_{Cu} = 80.5$, $a_{Ag} = 75.3$, $a_{Sn} = 12.8$, $a_{Pb} = 10.8$.

### Three-Stage Operation
- ☐ **Stage 1: Pre-arcing** — $I^2R$ heating → element melts.
- ☐ **Stage 2: Arcing** — arc established; arc voltage opposes source; current-limiting.
- ☐ **Stage 3: Extinction** — at current zero, dielectric recovery must withstand RRRV.

### Common Examiner Traps to Avoid
- ☐ Don't confuse **fusing factor** with **safety factor**.
- ☐ Don't forget to convert **mm** to **m** if using SI units in Preece.
- ☐ Don't state "silver has low melting point" — it actually has a **high** melting point (961.8 °C); its virtue is *no oxidation* and *low resistivity*.
- ☐ Don't claim rewirable fuses have *high* breaking capacity — they are limited to ~ 4 kA.
- ☐ Don't draw the T-C curve on linear paper — it is **log-log**.

### Discrimination / Selectivity
- ☐ **I²t (downstream) < I²t (upstream)** for proper discrimination.
- ☐ The **smaller fuse closer to the fault** must blow *first*.

### HV Fuse Specifics
- ☐ **Drop-out fuse** — visibly drops out on operation; used on distribution transformers.
- ☐ **Expulsion fuse** — uses the de-ionizing effect of gases generated by the arc.
- ☐ **Liquid fuse** — uses a liquid to quench the arc.

### Semiconductor (Ultra-Fast) Fuses
- ☐ Used for **SCR, diode, IGBT, thyristor** protection.
- ☐ I²t rating must be **less than** the semiconductor's rated I²t.
- ☐ Often filled with **pure quartz sand** for fastest arc quenching.

### Final Board Pearl
> **"A fuse is the cheapest insurance an electrical circuit can buy — it pays for itself the first time it blows, by saving equipment worth a hundred times its cost."**  
> — Universal KTU Board Examiner wisdom.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Selection of Fuses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types and Selection of Fuses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Fuses

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **fuse** is a *sacrificial overcurrent protective device* that provides circuit protection by melting its specially designed fusible element when the current flowing through it exceeds a predetermined safe value for a sufficient duration. The melting action physically interrupts the conductive path, thereby isolating the faulty section from the healthy supply, preventing damage to conductors, equipment, and fire hazards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A fuse is defined as the simplest and cheapest form of protective device that automatically opens the circuit in which it is installed by means of a fusible element when the current exceeds a predetermined value. The fusible element is sacrificed (melts) during the operation, which is why it is termed a *sacrificial device*.

According to **IS 2086:1993** and **IEC 60269** standards (the governing Indian and International standards for low-voltage fuses), a fuse is classified as a *current-limiting protective device* if it is capable of reducing the peak prospective current to a value lower than the prospective current of the circuit.

### Key Terminology (KTU Mandatory)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Practical Significance |
|------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Fuse Element** | The current-carrying metal strip designed to melt at a specific temperature | The "sacrificial" heart of the fuse |
| **Fuse Rating (In)** | The maximum continuous current the fuse can carry without melting | Operational current limit |
| **Fusing Current (If)** | The minimum current that will cause the element to melt | Threshold current for operation |
| **Fusing Factor** | Ratio of minimum fusing current to current rating | Indicates how close In is to If |
| **Pre-arcing Time** | Time between fault initiation and arc ignition inside the fuse | Discrimination characteristic |
| **Arcing Time** | Time taken for the arc to extinguish completely | Total clearance time |

> [!NOTE]
> The **fuse** is the *oldest* form of electrical protection, dating back to **Thomas Edison's** 1890 patent. Despite this antiquity, it remains the *most economical* and *fastest-acting* overcurrent device, operating in milliseconds.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **pressure relief valve** installed on a domestic water pressure cooker. The cooker is designed to operate safely up to a certain pressure (say 1 kg/cm²). If pressure exceeds this limit due to a blocked steam vent, the relief valve *automatically opens* and releases the excess pressure, preventing the cooker from exploding.

**Mapping the Analogy to a Fuse:**

| Pressure Cooker Component | Electrical Fuse Equivalent |
|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Internal steam pressure | Excessive fault current |
| Relief valve | Fusible element |
| Pressure rating (1 kg/cm²) | Current rating (In Amperes) |
| Steam release action | Element melting (sacrificial opening) |
| Replacement after operation | Fuse wire/element replacement |

**The Core Intuition:** Just as a relief valve intentionally "sacrifices" itself to protect the expensive cooker body and the food, a fuse *intentionally destroys its internal element* to protect the much more expensive cables, transformers, motors, and connected loads. It is essentially a **deliberately engineered weak link** placed strategically in the circuit.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Why is a fuse called a sacrificial device?", the model answer must emphasize three points: (1) The element melts permanently, (2) It must be replaced after operation, (3) It sacrifices itself to save the rest of the circuit.

## 1.3 Operating Principle — The Physics of Fusing

When current passes through a conductor, heat is generated according to **Joule's Law of Heating**:

$$Q = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat generated in Joules
- $I$ = current in Amperes
- $R$ = resistance of the element in Ohms
- $t$ = time of current flow in seconds

The element is designed such that under *normal* rated current, the heat generated is dissipated to the surroundings faster than it is produced, maintaining a steady-state temperature **below the melting point** of the metal. However, when a *fault* or *overload* occurs, the increased $I^2R$ losses raise the temperature rapidly to the melting point, the element **melts** (or *fuses*), and the circuit is broken.

### The Three-Stage Process of Fuse Operation

1. **Pre-heating Stage:** Fault current initiates, element temperature rises from ambient to melting point.
2. **Pre-arcing / Melting Stage:** Element reaches melting point, begins to liquefy at the hottest constricted point. An electric arc initiates as the molten metal separates.
3. **Arcing / Extinction Stage:** Arc is elongated by the surrounding filler material (in HRC fuses) or by the physical separation (in rewirable fuses). The arc voltage exceeds the supply voltage, forcing the current to zero.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Point:** A common misconception is that a fuse element melts at its *average* temperature. In reality, the melting always begins at the **hottest point** — typically where the cross-section is *minimum* (the notch or constriction designed intentionally by the manufacturer). This is why fuse elements have notches or reduced cross-sectional areas.

## 1.4 Essential Desirable Properties of a Fuse Element

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU 14-mark question frequently asks: *"What are the desirable properties of a fuse element? Explain."* The following properties must be memorized in this exact sequence:

1. **Low Melting Point** — Should melt quickly under fault conditions.
2. **High Electrical Conductivity** — To minimize $I^2R$ losses during normal operation.
3. **Low Cost** — Since it must be replaced after every operation.
4. **Free from Oxidation / Deterioration** — To maintain consistent time-current characteristics over time.
5. **High Breaking Capacity** — Must safely interrupt maximum prospective fault current without explosion.
6. **Stable Characteristics** — Time-current curve should not change with age or repeated use (especially true for HRC fuses).
7. **Small Operating Time** — Fast operation minimizes let-through energy ($I^2t$).
8. **Predictable Performance** — Minimum fusing current should be a precise multiple of rated current.

### Common Fuse Element Materials

| Material | Melting Point (°C) | Specific Resistance (μΩ·cm) | Application |
|----------|-------------------|------------------------------|-------------|
| **Tin** | 232 | 11.5 | Tin-coated copper for small fuses |
| **Lead** | 327 | 20.6 | Rewirable domestic fuses (with tin alloy) |
| **Zinc** | 419 | 5.9 | Standard rewirable fuses |
| **Aluminium** | 660 | 2.7 | Special low-voltage applications |
| **Silver** | 961 | 1.6 | HRC cartridge fuses (premium) |
| **Copper** | 1085 | 1.7 | HRC fuses, often silver-plated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Silver** is the *ideal* fuse element material due to its predictable melting behavior, high conductivity, and resistance to oxidation. However, it is expensive, so **silver-plated copper** is a common practical alternative in HRC fuses. **Lead-tin alloy** (often 37% lead, 63% tin — the eutectic composition) is the traditional material for domestic *rewirable* fuses due to its low cost and predictable melting point of **183 °C**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fuses (KTU Module 3 — The Core Topic)

Fuses are systematically classified based on the **voltage class**, **current rating**, **construction type**, and **breaking capacity**. Understanding this classification is critical for both selection and examination purposes.

### Primary Classification Hierarchy

```
FUSES
│
├──► A. LOW VOLTAGE FUSES (Up to 1000V AC)
│   │
│   ├── A.1 Rewirable Fuses (Semi-Enclosed / Kit-Kat Type)
│   ├── A.2 Cartridge Fuses
│   │       ├── A.2.a D-Type Cartridge Fuse
│   │       ├── A.2.b Link-Type Cartridge Fuse
│   │       └── A.2.c High Rupturing Capacity (HRC) Fuse
│   └── A.3 Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCB) — Note: not strictly fuses
│
├──► B. HIGH VOLTAGE FUSES (Above 1000V AC)
│   │
│   ├── B.1 Cartridge Type HV HRC Fuse
│   ├── B.2 Liquid Type HV HRC Fuse
│   ├── B.3 Metal-Clad HV HRC Fuse
│   └── B.4 Expulsion Type Fuse
│
└──► C. SPECIAL PURPOSE FUSES
        ├── C.1 Semiconductor / Rectifier Fuses
        ├── C.2 Thermal Fuses
        ├── C.3 Automotive Fuses
        └── C.4 Drop-Out Fuses
```

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Major Fuse Type

### 2.2.1 Rewirable Fuse (Semi-Enclosed / Kit-Kat Type)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most common domestic fuse** in Indian households and is the *first type* introduced in KTU textbooks. The name *Kit-Kat* originates from the appearance of the fuse base resembling a kit-kat chocolate bar.

**Construction:**
- **Fuse Base (Porcelain):** Houses the incoming and outgoing terminals.
- **Fuse Carrier (Porcelain Handle):** Removable holder containing the fuse wire.
- **Fuse Wire:** Lead-tin alloy or tinned copper wire, replaceable by the user.

**Working:** The fuse wire is wound between two terminals on the carrier. When the fuse wire melts, the user removes the carrier, replaces the wire, and reinserts it. *No arc-quenching mechanism exists* — the arc is extinguished by natural air deionization, which is why it is limited to *low breaking capacities* (typically **2 kA to 4 kA**).

**Advantages:**
- Lowest cost
- Easy replacement by unskilled personnel
- Replaceable element (no need to discard the entire fuse)

**Disadvantages:**
- **Low breaking capacity** — cannot safely interrupt high fault currents
- **Slow operation** — element deteriorates with age, leading to nuisance tripping or failure to operate
- **Non-standardized characteristics** — wire gauge and material selection is informal
- **Fire risk** — the exposed wire can ignite surrounding materials

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A rewirable fuse is also called *semi-enclosed* because the element is *not fully enclosed* in a sealed chamber. This distinguishes it from a *fully enclosed* cartridge fuse.

### 2.2.2 High Rupturing Capacity (HRC) Cartridge Fuse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HRC fuses are the **industry standard** for industrial applications and are *frequently asked in 14-mark questions*. The acronym *HRC* is so commonly used that KTU examiners expect students to define it explicitly: **High Rupturing Capacity** — meaning it can safely break very high prospective fault currents (typically **80 kA to 200 kA**) without explosion or damage.

**Construction (Five Essential Components):**

1. **Fuse Element (Silver or Silver-Plated Copper):** Often constructed with multiple narrow strips (the *M-effect* configuration) rather than a single wire. Notches at precise locations create defined melting points.
2. **Ceramic Body (Steatite or Porcelain):** A robust, heat-resistant cylindrical tube that contains the arc and arc-quenching byproducts.
3. **Arc-Extinguishing Filler (Quartz Sand or Silica):** High-purity silica sand surrounding the element. When the element melts, the molten metal vapor penetrates the sand, where it is rapidly cooled and condensed. This elongates and deionizes the arc very effectively.
4. **End Caps (Brass or Nickel-Plated):** Seal both ends of the ceramic body, providing electrical contact.
5. **Indicating Device:** A colored pin or striker that ejects visibly when the fuse operates, allowing easy fault identification.

**Operating Principle — The Three Key Physical Phenomena:**

**Stage 1 — Melting:** The fault current rapidly heats the element at its narrowest cross-section (the *notches*). The element reaches its melting point in *milliseconds*.

**Stage 2 — Arc Initiation:** As the metal vaporizes, an electric arc forms in the vapor path.

**Stage 3 — Arc Extinction (The Crucial Stage):** The high-temperature arc vaporizes the surrounding sand's silica. The molten silica absorbs enormous energy, cools the arc, and creates a *high-resistance deionized path* that suppresses the arc. Critically, the arc voltage $V_{arc}$ rises *above* the supply voltage $V_s$, forcing the current to zero at a current zero crossing.

> [!NOTE]
> The HRC fuse is a *current-limiting device*. This means it interrupts the fault current *before* it reaches its natural peak, a property unique to HRC fuses and absent in MCBs and rewirable fuses.

### 2.2.3 D-Type Cartridge Fuse (Also called *Bottle-Type* or *Diazed* Fuse)

**Construction:**
- **Fuse Base:** Porcelain with a screw-threaded brass contact.
- **Fuse Carrier (Bottle):** A porcelain bottle-shaped holder.
- **Fuse Cap (Adapter Ring):** Different diameters for different current ratings — *this is a key safety feature* preventing insertion of a higher-rated fuse into a lower-rated base.

**Key Feature — Non-Interchangeability:** Each current rating has a *uniquely sized adapter ring*. A 10 A fuse cap *cannot* be physically inserted into a 6 A base, eliminating the dangerous practice of "upgrading" fuses with thicker wire.

**Application:** Domestic and small commercial installations, panel boards.

### 2.2.4 High Voltage HRC Fuses (For voltages above 1000 V)

**Cartridge Type HV HRC:** Similar construction to LV HRC but with longer ceramic bodies (to handle higher voltage stresses) and special end fittings for HV connections. Used up to **33 kV**.

**Liquid Type HV HRC:** The arc is extinguished in a *liquid medium* (typically oil or carbon tetrachloride). Used in transformer protection up to **132 kV**.

**Expulsion Type Fuse:** Operates by expelling ionized gases through a vent, similar to a *jet engine* effect. The arc elongation and gas deionization extinguish the arc. Common in *outdoor distribution networks* and as *transformer backup protection*.

**Drop-Out Fuse:** A specific type of expulsion fuse where the fuse carrier *drops out* under gravity after operation, providing a *visible* indication of the fault. Widely used on distribution transformers in Kerala.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly testable** in KTU examinations. Master these equations and their boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---------------------|----------------|------------------------------|
| **F1** | $Q = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$ | Joule's law of heating | Energy dissipation calculation |
| **F2** | $\text{Fusing Factor} = \dfrac{I_f}{I_n}$ | $I_f$ = minimum fusing current; $I_n$ = rated current | Indicates how close In is to If |
| **F3** | $I^2 t = \text{constant (pre-arcing energy)}$ | Let-through energy before arc | Discrimination and coordination |
| **F4** | $\text{Peak Let-Through} \leq \text{Prospective Peak}$ | Current-limiting property | Comparison of fuse vs MCB |
| **F5** | $I_n \geq I_{load, \text{max}}$ | Selection rule 1 | Basic rating selection |
| **F6** | $I_{fuse, \text{br}} \geq I_{prospective, \text{max}}$ | Selection rule 2 | Breaking capacity selection |
| **F7** | $I_{fuse, \text{rated}} \leq I_{cable, \text{rated}}$ | Cable protection | Coordination with cable size |
| **F8** | $V_{arc} > V_{\text{supply}}$ | Arc extinction condition | HRC operating principle |
| **F9** | $P_{\text{element}} = I_n^2 \cdot R_{element}$ | Power loss in fuse | Voltage drop across fuse |
| **F10** | $\text{Discrimination Ratio} = \dfrac{I_{f2}}{I_{f1}}$ | For series fuses | Selective coordination |

### Critical Constants for KTU 2024 (Must Memorize)

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Standard domestic voltage (India) | **230 V AC, 50 Hz** | V, Hz |
| Standard three-phase voltage | **415 V AC, 50 Hz** | V, Hz |
| Eutectic lead-tin alloy melting point | **183** | °C |
| Silver melting point | **961** | °C |
| Copper melting point | **1085** | °C |
| Typical HRC breaking capacity | **80 to 200** | kA |
| Typical rewirable breaking capacity | **2 to 4** | kA |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** The term *"rupturing capacity"* and *"breaking capacity"* are **synonymous** in fuse terminology. Do not confuse them with the *making capacity* of a circuit breaker. A fuse has only *breaking capacity*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #2:** The fusing factor is *always* greater than 1 (since $I_f > I_n$). A fusing factor of 1.0 would mean the fuse operates at its rated current, which is undesirable. Typical values: Rewirable fuse = **1.45** (highest), HRC fuse = **1.2 to 1.4** (lowest, hence preferred).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications of Fuse Selection

1. **Domestic Distribution Boards (DB):** Rewirable fuses or MCBs with cartridge fuse backup for incoming.
2. **Industrial Motor Control Centres (MCC):** HRC fuses for short-circuit protection of motor starters.
3. **Semiconductor Rectifier Protection:** Special ultra-fast semiconductor fuses (also called *rectifier fuses*) with $I^2t$ values an order of magnitude lower than standard HRC fuses.
4. **Distribution Transformer Protection:** Drop-out fuses (DOF) on the HT side, combined with LV HRC fuses.
5. **Solar PV String Protection:** DC-rated HRC fuses specifically designed to break DC arcs (which are harder to interrupt than AC arcs since there is no natural current zero crossing).
6. **Electric Vehicle Battery Packs:** High-voltage DC HRC fuses for battery isolation during crash or short-circuit events.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Strategy:** When asked *"Why is an HRC fuse preferred over a rewirable fuse?"*, structure your answer in **three layers**: (1) Higher breaking capacity [engineering reason], (2) Stable time-current characteristics [reliability reason], (3) Faster operation with current-limiting property [safety reason].

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fusing Factor and Its Selection Implications

**Problem Context (KTU 14-Mark Style):** A 10 A rewirable fuse has a minimum fusing current of 14.5 A. A 10 A HRC fuse has a minimum fusing current of 13.5 A. Compare their fusing factors and discuss which is more reliable for overload protection.

### Step 1: Formal Definition

The **fusing factor** is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the *margin* between the rated current and the *actual* minimum current at which the element will melt. It is defined as:

$$\text{Fusing Factor (K)} = \frac{I_f}{I_n}$$

where:
- $I_f$ = minimum fusing current (the smallest current that will *reliably* cause melting)
- $I_n$ = rated current (the maximum current the fuse can carry *continuously* without melting)

### Step 2: Numerical Evaluation — Case 1: Rewirable Fuse

Given:
- $I_n = 10$ A
- $I_f = 14.5$ A

Substituting into the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{rewirable}} &= \frac{I_f}{I_n} \\
&= \frac{14.5 \text{ A}}{10 \text{ A}} \\
&= 1.45
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation — Case 2: HRC Fuse

Given:
- $I_n = 10$ A
- $I_f = 13.5$ A

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{HRC}} &= \frac{I_f}{I_n} \\
&= \frac{13.5 \text{ A}}{10 \text{ A}} \\
&= 1.35
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation and Selection Implication

A *lower* fusing factor means the fuse is *more sensitive* to overcurrents (the gap between In and If is small, so the fuse will operate closer to its rated current). This is *desirable* because:

- **Better overload protection:** The fuse operates before the cable/equipment reaches its thermal limit.
- **Better coordination with cable ratings:** The fuse is selected such that $I_n$ does not exceed the cable's continuous current rating.

**Conclusion for this problem:** The HRC fuse with $K = 1.35$ is **preferable** to the rewirable fuse with $K = 1.45$ for precise overload protection. This is a primary reason HRC fuses dominate industrial applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** A common student error is to assume that a *lower* fusing factor is *less* safe. The opposite is true. A low fusing factor (closer to 1.0) means the fuse is more *sensitive*, providing *better* protection. The only caveat is that the fusing factor must still be *above* 1.0 to prevent nuisance tripping at rated current.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pre-Arcing $I^2t$ and Total Operating $I^2t$ Energy

**Problem Context:** A 100 A HRC fuse interrupts a prospective fault current of 25 kA (symmetrical RMS) at 415 V. The pre-arcing time is 3 ms, and the total arcing time is 7 ms. Calculate the (a) pre-arcing $I^2t$, (b) approximate let-through energy in Joules, (c) the peak let-through current assuming a current-limiting action.

### Step 1: Understanding the $I^2t$ Concept

The $I^2t$ value (also called *let-through energy* or *Joule integral*) is a critical parameter for fuse coordination. It represents the thermal energy that would be delivered to downstream equipment *if* the fault current were allowed to flow unimpeded for the given duration. It is measured in **A²s** (Ampere-squared-seconds).

The total $I^2t$ consists of two components:

$$I^2t_{\text{total}} = I^2t_{\text{pre-arcing}} + I^2t_{\text{arcing}}$$

### Step 2: Calculation of Pre-Arcing $I^2t$

Given:
- $I_{\text{sc, RMS}} = 25{,}000$ A
- $t_{\text{pre-arcing}} = 3$ ms $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ s

$$
\begin{aligned}
I^2t_{\text{pre-arcing}} &= I_{\text{sc}}^2 \times t_{\text{pre-arcing}} \\
&= (25{,}000 \text{ A})^2 \times (3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}) \\
&= 6.25 \times 10^{8} \text{ A}^2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&= 1.875 \times 10^{6} \text{ A}^2 \cdot \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculation of Approximate Let-Through Energy in Joules

The actual energy delivered is $I^2 R t$. Assuming the fault loop resistance is very low (e.g., $R_{\text{loop}} = 0.05\ \Omega$), the pre-arcing energy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{pre-arcing}} &= I^2 \cdot R \cdot t \\
&= 1.875 \times 10^{6} \text{ A}^2\text{s} \times 0.05\ \Omega \\
&= 93{,}750 \text{ Joules} \\
&= 93.75 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Peak Let-Through Current (Current-Limiting Calculation)

For a 50 Hz system, the prospective peak current is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{peak, prospective}} &= I_{\text{peak, RMS}} \times \sqrt{2} \\
&= I_{\text{RMS, prospective}} \times \sqrt{2} \times \text{asymmetry factor} \\
&\approx 25{,}000 \times 1.414 \times 1.8 \\
&\approx 63{,}630 \text{ A} \\
&\approx 63.6 \text{ kA}
\end{aligned}
$$

The HRC fuse *limits* this peak. Suppose from the manufacturer's time-current curve, the actual peak let-through is 35 kA. The **current-limiting factor** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current-Limiting Factor} &= \frac{I_{\text{peak, actual}}}{I_{\text{peak, prospective}}} \\
&= \frac{35{,}000}{63{,}630} \\
&\approx 0.55
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the fuse reduced the peak current to **55%** of the prospective peak — a 45% reduction in peak stress on the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Takeaway:** Always specify the units of $I^2t$ explicitly. The KTU valuation key *often* deducts marks if a student writes "$1.875 \times 10^6$" without stating "A²s".

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Selection of Fuse Rating for an Induction Motor

**Problem Context (KTU 14-Mark Application Problem):** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 15 kW induction motor has a full-load current of 28 A and a starting current of 6 × full-load current. The cable feeding the motor has a continuous current rating of 36 A. Select a suitable HRC fuse for short-circuit protection.

### Step 1: Identify the Constraints

The fuse must:
1. **Carry the starting current** without operating (otherwise the motor will not start).
2. **Not exceed the cable rating** in normal operation.
3. **Interrupt the maximum prospective fault current** safely.

Known values:
- $I_{FL} = 28$ A (full-load current)
- $I_{\text{start}} = 6 \times 28 = 168$ A (starting current)
- $I_{\text{cable}} = 36$ A (cable rating)
- Motor starting duration $\approx 5$ to $10$ seconds

### Step 2: Apply Selection Rule 1 — Cable Coordination

$$I_{n, \text{fuse}} \leq I_{\text{cable, rated}} = 36 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3: Apply Selection Rule 2 — Starting Current Discrimination

The HRC fuse must withstand the starting current of 168 A for at least 5 to 10 seconds without operating. From standard HRC fuse time-current curves, a fuse with rating 35 A typically carries 6 × rating (= 210 A) for 10 seconds. A 32 A fuse may not withstand 168 A for 10 seconds.

Therefore, select $I_{n,\text{fuse}} = 35$ A.

### Step 4: Verify All Three Conditions

| Condition | Requirement | Check |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Continuous current carrying | $I_{n,\text{fuse}} \geq I_{FL} = 28$ A | 35 A ≥ 28 A ✓ |
| Starting current discrimination | $I_{n,\text{fuse}}$ carries $6 \times I_n = 210$ A for 10 s | 35 A passes ✓ |
| Cable coordination | $I_{n,\text{fuse}} \leq I_{\text{cable}} = 36$ A | 35 A ≤ 36 A ✓ |

### Step 5: Select Breaking Capacity

The prospective short-circuit current at the motor terminals (assume contribution from the motor itself plus the supply) is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{SC, prospective}} &= \frac{V}{Z_{\text{loop}}} \\
&= \frac{415 \text{ V}}{0.05\ \Omega} \\
&= 8{,}300 \text{ A} = 8.3 \text{ kA}
\end{aligned}
$$

The selected 35 A HRC fuse should have a breaking capacity $I_{\text{br}} \geq 8.3$ kA. Standard HRC fuses offer 80 kA breaking capacity, so this is comfortably satisfied.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** A **35 A HRC fuse** with **80 kA breaking capacity** is selected for this motor.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Series Discrimination (Coordination) of Two Fuses

**Problem Context:** A main distribution board has a 100 A HRC fuse upstream feeding a sub-circuit protected by a 32 A HRC fuse. Verify that the upstream fuse does not operate for faults on the sub-circuit.

### Step 1: Discrimination Principle

Two fuses in series are properly *discriminated* if, for any fault current on the downstream sub-circuit, only the downstream (32 A) fuse operates, and the upstream (100 A) fuse remains unaffected.

### Step 2: Calculate the Maximum Fault Current at the Sub-Circuit

Assume the sub-circuit has a fault loop impedance of $Z_f = 0.1\ \Omega$ at 415 V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{SC, max}} &= \frac{V}{Z_f} = \frac{415 \text{ V}}{0.1\ \Omega} = 4{,}150 \text{ A} = 4.15 \text{ kA}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Read the Pre-Arcing Times from Time-Current Curves

From standard manufacturer data:

| Fuse | Pre-arcing time at 4.15 kA | Pre-arcing $I^2t$ (A²s) |
|------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| 32 A downstream | $t_1 = 4$ ms | $I^2t_1 = 4.15^2 \times 10^3 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \approx 68.9 \times 10^3$ A²s |
| 100 A upstream | $t_2 = 80$ ms | $I^2t_2 = 4.15^2 \times 10^3 \times 80 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.378 \times 10^6$ A²s |

### Step 4: Apply the Discrimination Rule

For *total discrimination* (also called *back-up protection*), the upstream fuse's pre-arcing $I^2t$ must be *greater* than the downstream fuse's *total* $I^2t$ at every fault current level:

$$I^2t_{\text{upstream, pre-arcing}} > I^2t_{\text{downstream, total}}$$

Since $1.378 \times 10^6 \text{ A}^2\text{s} \gg 68.9 \times 10^3 \text{ A}^2\text{s}$, the discrimination is **satisfied**. Only the 32 A fuse will operate for the sub-circuit fault.

### Step 5: Quantitative Discrimination Ratio

The **discrimination ratio** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Discrimination Ratio} &= \frac{I_{n, \text{upstream}}}{I_{n, \text{downstream}}} \\
&= \frac{100 \text{ A}}{32 \text{ A}} \\
&\approx 3.13
\end{aligned}
$$

A ratio of **at least 1.6:1** is generally accepted for HRC fuses; 3.13:1 provides *excellent* discrimination with a wide safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Total discrimination is not always economically feasible for very high fault currents. *Partial discrimination* (upstream operates only for very high currents) is sometimes accepted, especially at the *incomer* level of a large installation.
