In practice, mention **5–10% over-compensation** to avoid series resonance. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Arc Suppression Coil (ASC) = Petersen Coil = Resonant Grounding Reactor** — three names for the same device. - ✅ The ASC is an **adjustable iron-core inductor** connected between the **neutral** and **earth**. - ✅ **Tuning Condition:** $\omega L = \dfrac{1}{3\omega C_0}$, equivalently $L = \dfrac{1}{3\omega^2 C_0}$. - ✅ At perfect tuning, $I_C$ (capacitive) cancels $I_L$ (inductive) at the fault → **arc self-extinguishes**. - ✅ Most practical systems operate at **5–10% over-compensation** to avoid dangerous resonance with system capacitance. - ✅ ASC grounding allows **auto-reclosure** to succeed — the line does not need to be manually re-energized. - ✅ ASC is preferred for **11 kV – 132 kV overhead transmission networks** with high earth-fault capacitive currents. - ✅ **Equipment Grounding = Safety of personnel + fault-current path**, **NOT** system neutral stabilization. - ✅ Equipment grounding connects **non-current-carrying metal parts** (enclosures, frames, conduits, cable trays) to earth. - ✅ Equipment grounding conductor is **Green with Yellow stripe** in India (per IS 3043). - ✅ Target resistance: **< 1 Ω industrial, < 5 Ω commercial/domestic**. - ✅ **Touch Potential** $V_t = I_g R_g$ at enclosure; safe limit for indefinite contact is **9 V** (IEEE Std 80). - ✅ **Lower $R_g$ → Higher fault current → Faster breaker trip → Less exposure time → Greater safety.** - ✅ Equipotential **bonding** of all metallic parts is essential to eliminate dangerous step and touch potentials. - ✅ ASC grounding is **NOT** suitable for cable networks with very high capacitance (Petersen coil becomes impractically large). - ✅ Always remember: **System Grounding stabilizes voltage; Equipment Grounding saves lives.**

topics.title: Spark Gap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spark Gap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔌 SPARK GAP — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Spark Gap** is a deliberately engineered air gap between two conducting electrodes, designed such that the intervening dielectric (typically atmospheric air) **breaks down** and becomes conductive only when the voltage across the gap exceeds a **predefined threshold** known as the *Breakdown Voltage* ($V_b$). Below this threshold, the gap behaves as a near-perfect insulator (resistance $\rightarrow \infty$); above it, the gap transitions into a low-impedance plasma arc, safely shunting dangerous over-voltages (such as lightning surges or switching transients) away from protected equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> A spark gap is a *non-linear, voltage-dependent switching element* whose transition state is governed by the **dielectric strength of air** ($E_b \approx 3 \text{ kV/mm}$ or $30 \text{ kV/cm}$ peak) and the **geometry** of the electrodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **floodgate in a dam**:
- When the river level (voltage) is normal, the gate stays shut and water (current) cannot pass.
- The instant the water level rises above a critical mark (breakdown voltage), the gate **automatically opens**, allowing the excess water (current) to rush through a safe spillway to the riverbed.
- Once the flood subsides, the gate closes again.

A spark gap is the *electrical twin* of this floodgate. It remains "closed" (insulating) under normal operating voltage and "opens" (conducts) the moment a transient over-voltage threatens the downstream system. The protected equipment never sees the surge because it is harmlessly diverted to earth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy in safety engineering:**
> The **lightning arrester on a building rooftop** is a practical spark gap. When lightning strikes, the air gap ionizes, conducts the millions of amperes to ground, and protects the structure and its electronics.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Highlighted)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Dielectric strength of dry air (1 atm, 20°C)** | **$E_b \approx 3 \text{ kV/mm}$ (peak)** or $\approx 21.2 \text{ kV/cm}$ (rms) |
| **Standard atmospheric pressure** | **$p = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$** |
| **Temperature (KTB constants for Paschen's Law)** | $A = 112.5 \text{ (ions·m}^{-2}\text{·Pa}^{-1}\text{)}$, $B = 2737.5 \text{ V·m}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$ for air |
| **Typical gap lengths (LV systems)** | $0.5 \text{ mm}$ to $5 \text{ mm}$ |
| **Typical gap lengths (HV/EHV systems)** | $10 \text{ mm}$ to $500 \text{ mm}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Idle State (Normal Voltage):** The electric field $E$ across the gap is below the dielectric strength of air. Very few free electrons exist. The gap acts as a **near-perfect insulator** (resistance $\sim 10^{14} \text{ }\Omega$).
2. **Onset of Ionization:** When the voltage $V$ is gradually increased, free electrons (cosmic-ray generated) gain kinetic energy. Once $E$ approaches the ionization threshold, electrons collide with neutral air molecules, knocking out more electrons — this is the **primary ionization** process.
3. **Townsend Avalanche:** Each newly freed electron in turn ionizes more molecules, producing an **exponential multiplication** of charge carriers. The current rises by many orders of magnitude in microseconds.
4. **Spark Formation (Breakdown):** When the avalanche reaches a critical size, the gas in the gap becomes a **luminous plasma channel** — a spark — and the gap resistance collapses to nearly zero.
5. **Arc Sustenance & Extinction:** The spark is self-sustaining as long as the source supplies enough current. In AC systems, the arc **naturally extinguishes at the current zero crossing**, allowing the gap to reset for the next half-cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" the gap works:** The spark gap is the simplest *self-triggering* over-voltage protector — no auxiliary power, no electronics, no moving parts. Its operation is dictated purely by the physics of gaseous discharge.

## 2.2 Types of Spark Gaps (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Type | Geometry | Application / Characteristic |
|------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Sphere Gap** | Two polished metallic spheres | **Standard** for HV measurement (IEC 60052) |
| **Rod Gap** | Two cylindrical rods with hemispherical ends | **Surge arrester** in transmission lines; simple, robust |
| **Horn Gap** | Two horn-shaped diverging electrodes | **Auto-extinguishing** arc (used in lightning arresters); arc climbs upward and breaks |
| **Needle Gap** | Two sharp needle points | **Low-voltage** labs; non-uniform field, low breakdown |
| **Sphere-Rod Gap** | Hybrid configuration | Intermediate lab measurements |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In all markdown tables below, absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Uniform electric field across gap ($\text{V/m}$) |
| 2 | $V_b = E_b \cdot d$ | Breakdown voltage in uniform field ($\text{V}$) |
| 3 | $V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]}$ | **Paschen's Law** — $p$ in Pa, $d$ in m, $V_b$ in V |
| 4 | $V_m = V_b \cdot k$ | Measured voltage corrected to standard: $V_s = k \cdot V_m$ (sphere gaps) |
| 5 | $\alpha = A \cdot p \cdot e^{-B \cdot p / E}$ | **Townsend's first ionization coefficient** ($\text{m}^{-1}$) |
| 6 | $\gamma_{se}$ | **Secondary ionization coefficient** (cathode emission) — typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| 7 | $t_t = t_s + t_f$ | Total time lag = statistical lag + formative lag |
| 8 | $V_{50\%}$ | 50% disruptive discharge voltage (impulse testing) |
| 9 | $E_b = 3 \text{ kV/mm}$ (peak) | Dielectric strength of air at NTP (uniform field) |
| 10 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Conversion for sinusoidal AC quantities |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lightning Arresters on Transmission Lines:** Rod/horn gaps divert lightning strokes (10–200 kA) safely to earth.
- **Surge Protection in Substations:** Sphere gaps protect switchgear from switching transients.
- **High-Voltage Measurement Laboratories:** Sphere gaps are the *primary standard* for calibrating voltmeters (used by PTB, NPL, NABL-accredited labs).
- **Triggering Circuits:** Spark gaps in pulsed-power systems (e.g., Marx generators) trigger high-current discharges.
- **Automotive Ignition Systems:** Spark plugs in IC engines are miniature spark gaps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Paschen's Law from Townsend's Theory

The breakdown condition in a gas gap is derived by combining **Townsend's primary ionization** with **secondary ionization** at the cathode.

### Step 1 — Townsend's Primary Ionization Coefficient

In a uniform electric field $E$, the number of ionizing collisions an electron makes per unit length is:

$$
\alpha = A \cdot p \cdot e^{-B \cdot p / E}
$$

where:
- $A$, $B$ are gas-specific constants (for air: $A \approx 112.5$, $B \approx 2737.5$ in SI units)
- $p$ is gas pressure in Pa
- $E = V/d$ is the electric field in V/m
- $d$ is the gap spacing in m

### Step 2 — Current Growth Across the Gap

If $n_0$ electrons are released at the cathode, the number arriving at the anode is:

$$
n = n_0 \cdot e^{\alpha \cdot d}
$$

The current at the anode is therefore:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{\alpha \cdot d}
$$

### Step 3 — Inclusion of Secondary Ionization

For self-sustained discharge, each primary avalanche must regenerate itself through secondary processes (ions striking the cathode liberate $\gamma_{se}$ new electrons per ion). The total current including secondary effects is:

$$
I = \dfrac{I_0 \cdot e^{\alpha d}}{1 - \gamma_{se} \left(e^{\alpha d} - 1\right)}
$$

### Step 4 — The Self-Sustained Breakdown Condition

Breakdown occurs when the denominator vanishes (current tends to infinity even with $I_0 \to 0$):

$$
1 - \gamma_{se} \left(e^{\alpha d} - 1\right) = 0
$$

$$
\gamma_{se} \left(e^{\alpha d} - 1\right) = 1
$$

$$
e^{\alpha d} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\alpha d = \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Substituting $\alpha$ from Step 1

$$
\left(A \cdot p \cdot e^{-B \cdot p / E}\right) \cdot d = \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)
$$

$$
A \cdot p \cdot d \cdot e^{-B \cdot p \cdot d / V} = \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)
$$

(Since $E = V/d$, we have $p/E = p \cdot d / V$)

### Step 6 — Solving for Breakdown Voltage $V_b$

Taking the natural log of both sides again:

$$
\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{V_b} = \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]
$$

Rearranging for $V_b$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]}\,}
$$

This is **Paschen's Law**. It shows that for a given gas, $V_b$ is a function of the product $p \cdot d$ only — a fundamental result for spark gap design.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A parallel-plate spark gap has a spacing of $d = 5 \text{ mm}$ in air at standard atmospheric pressure. Using the dielectric strength of air as $E_b = 3 \text{ kV/mm}$ (peak), calculate the peak and RMS breakdown voltages.

**Step 1 — Identify the electric field at breakdown:**

$$
E_b = 3 \text{ kV/mm} = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the peak breakdown voltage:**

$$
V_{b,\text{peak}} = E_b \cdot d = (3 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m})
$$

$$
V_{b,\text{peak}} = 15 \times 10^3 \text{ V} = 15 \text{ kV}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RMS:**

$$
V_{b,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{b,\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{1.4142}
$$

$$
V_{b,\text{rms}} \approx 10.61 \text{ kV}
$$

**Result:** A 5 mm air gap breaks down at **15 kV peak** or **10.61 kV RMS**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Spark Gap Geometries

| Property | Sphere Gap | Rod Gap | Horn Gap | Needle Gap |
|----------|-----------|---------|----------|------------|
| **Field uniformity** | Highly uniform (small gaps) | Moderately non-uniform | Non-uniform, diverging | Strongly non-uniform |
| **Breakdown voltage precision** | $\pm 3\%$ (standard) | $\pm 8\%$ | $\pm 10\%$ | $\pm 15\%$ |
| **Used for measurement?** | ✅ Yes (primary standard) | ❌ No (protection only) | ❌ No | ❌ No (lab only) |
| **Arc self-extinction (AC)** | Possible but slow | Slow | ✅ Excellent (arc rises) | Slow |
| **Corona onset** | Low | Moderate | High | Very low |
| **Common use** | HV calibration | Transmission-line arrester | Lightning arrester | Lab HV test |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Spark Gap Protection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Source / Line] --> B[Protected Equipment Transformer]
    A --> C[Spark Gap Electrodes]
    C -->|Breakdown arc| D[Earth Ground]
    B --> E[Load]
    C -.->|Idle: insulation| D

    classDef sourceStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef gapStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef earthStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef loadStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000

    class A sourceStyle
    class C gapStyle
    class D earthStyle
    class B,E loadStyle
```

**Interpretation:**
- The HV source feeds **both** the protected equipment and the spark gap in parallel.
- Under normal voltage, the gap is *idle* (dotted line = no conduction).
- When $V > V_b$, the gap fires, short-circuiting the surge to earth and protecting the transformer/load.

---

## 4.2 Horn Gap Arrester — Auto-Extinguishing Action

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HGA["Horn Gap Arrester Schematic"]
        L1[Line Conductor] --> P1[Horn Electrode Upper]
        P1 --> P2[Horn Electrode Lower]
        P2 --> G1[Earth]
        P1 -. Arc path .-> P2
    end

    subgraph ARCPATH["Arc Rise Mechanism"]
        direction TB
        A1[Hot arc column] -->|rises due to buoyancy| A2[Arc lengthens]
        A2 -->|voltage drops below supply| A3[Arc extinguishes]
    end

    classDef hornStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef earthStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef arcStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF

    class P1,P2 hornStyle
    class G1 earthStyle
    class A1,A2,A3 arcStyle
```

**Interpretation:**
- A horn gap is essentially two diverging electrodes connected between line and earth.
- When the arc strikes, the hot ionized column **rises due to buoyancy** (like hot air rising).
- The arc stretches along the diverging horns, increasing its length and resistance.
- The arc voltage exceeds the supply voltage → arc **self-extinguishes**, restoring insulation.

---

## 4.3 Sphere Gap Measurement Setup (HV Laboratory)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[HV Test Transformer] --> R[Current Limiting Resistor]
    R --> S1[Sphere 1 Cathode]
    S1 <-. gap d .-> S2[Sphere 2 Anode]
    S2 --> M[Measuring Voltmeter / Sphere Gap Standard]
    S2 --> GND[Earth]
    HV --> GND

    classDef xfmrStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef sphereStyle fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#696969,color:#000
    classDef measStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef gndStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    class HV xfmrStyle
    class S1,S2 sphereStyle
    class M measStyle
    class GND gndStyle
```

**Interpretation:**
- A **current-limiting resistor** $R$ is essential to prevent the HV transformer from being short-circuited at breakdown.
- The voltmeter reads the RMS voltage at the moment of sparkover.
- The gap distance $d$ is adjusted until breakdown occurs at a known voltmeter reading → used to **calibrate** the voltmeter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define a spark gap. State any two applications of spark gaps in electrical safety systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A spark gap is a pair of conducting electrodes separated by an air (or gas) dielectric such that the gap remains insulating under normal voltage but breaks down and conducts when the voltage across it exceeds the **dielectric strength** of the intervening medium. *(2 marks)*
**Applications:** *(1 mark — any two)*
1. Over-voltage protection in **lightning arresters** on transmission lines.
2. **Sphere gap** as a primary standard for HV voltage measurement in calibration laboratories.
3. Triggering element in **pulsed-power** circuits (e.g., Marx generators, ignition systems).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**What is Paschen's Law? Write the mathematical form of Paschen's Law for air.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Paschen's Law** states that the breakdown voltage $V_b$ of a gas in a uniform-field gap is a function only of the product of gas pressure $p$ and gap spacing $d$, and is independent of the field and the gap considered separately. *(2 marks)*

**Mathematical form:**

$$
V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]}
$$

For air, $A \approx 112.5$ and $B \approx 2737.5$ in SI units. *(1 mark)*

---

## 🅱️ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions for Module 3 follow the pattern: **Choice between two 14-mark questions**, each with two 7-mark sub-parts. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **horn gap arrester**. Why does the arc self-extinguish in AC systems? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A horn gap arrester consists of two diverging horn-shaped metal electrodes — one connected to the **line** and the other to **earth** — with a small gap at the bottom. The horns diverge upward like a "V".

**Working:**
1. Under normal line voltage, the gap remains insulating.
2. When an over-voltage (lightning or switching surge) arrives, the gap breaks down and an arc strikes across the narrowest part.
3. The **hot arc column** heats the surrounding air, becomes buoyant, and **rises upward** along the diverging horns.
4. As the arc climbs, its **length increases**, hence its resistance increases and the arc voltage rises.
5. The arc continues to stretch until the arc voltage exceeds the supply voltage — at this point, the arc **extinguishes** automatically.

**Why it self-extinguishes in AC:**
At the **current zero crossing** of the AC waveform, the arc attempts to re-strike. By this time, the arc has stretched sufficiently, so the available supply voltage is insufficient to re-ignite the gap. The arc is therefore quenched. *(7 marks full)*

---

**Q. (b)** Derive Paschen's Law for a uniform-field gas discharge. A sphere gap of diameter 12.5 cm is used to measure the breakdown voltage of air. At what spacing will the gap break down at 50 kV (rms)? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** (See full derivation in Section 3.1 above — write the same stepwise derivation showing Townsend's primary ionization, secondary ionization, and the breakdown condition leading to Paschen's Law.) *(4 marks)*

**Numerical Part:**
For a **12.5 cm diameter sphere gap**, the IEC 60052 standard table gives the **50% breakdown voltage** vs spacing. The relevant entry is:

| Sphere Diameter | Gap Spacing | Breakdown Voltage (rms) |
|-----------------|-------------|-------------------------|
| 12.5 cm | 3.0 cm | 50 kV |

(Students may also be expected to use the empirical relation $V_b \approx E_b \cdot d$ with $E_b = 3 \text{ kV/mm}$. The peak voltage is $V_{peak} = 50 \sqrt{2} = 70.7 \text{ kV}$, hence the required spacing is $d = 70.7/3 \approx 23.6 \text{ mm} \approx 2.36 \text{ cm}$.)

**Answer:** The sphere gap will break down at **50 kV (rms)** when the spacing is approximately **2.3 – 3.0 cm** (depending on the standard table used). *(3 marks)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Townsend mechanism of spark breakdown** in a uniform-field gap. Define the primary ($\alpha$) and secondary ($\gamma$) ionization coefficients. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Townsend breakdown mechanism is a two-stage gaseous discharge process:

**Stage 1 — Primary Ionization (Electron Avalanche):**
A free electron liberated at the cathode is accelerated by the applied field $E$. If its kinetic energy exceeds the ionization energy of the gas molecule, it produces one new electron + one positive ion. The original electron plus the new one continue to ionize, leading to an **electron avalanche**. The number of new ionizing collisions per unit length is characterized by **Townsend's first ionization coefficient** $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = A \cdot p \cdot e^{-B \cdot p / E} \quad (\text{m}^{-1})
$$

**Stage 2 — Secondary Ionization (Cathode Emission):**
The positive ions produced during the avalanche drift toward the cathode. On impact, they liberate additional electrons from the cathode surface. The number of secondary electrons produced per incident ion is the **secondary ionization coefficient** $\gamma_{se}$ (typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ for air). Other secondary processes include photoemission and metastable-atom bombardment.

**Self-Sustained Condition:**
For self-sustained discharge, the secondary electrons must regenerate the primary avalanche. The condition is:

$$
\gamma_{se} \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) = 1
$$

When this is satisfied, the gas in the gap becomes a **luminous spark/arc** and breakdown occurs. *(7 marks full)*

---

**Q. (b)** A parallel-plate spark gap has a uniform field of strength $3 \text{ kV/mm}$. If the gap spacing is 8 mm, calculate (i) the peak breakdown voltage, and (ii) the RMS breakdown voltage. What is the **time to breakdown** if the statistical lag is 1.2 µs and the formative lag is 0.3 µs? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Peak Breakdown Voltage:**

$$
V_{b,\text{peak}} = E_b \cdot d = (3 \text{ kV/mm}) \times (8 \text{ mm}) = 24 \text{ kV}
$$

**(ii) RMS Breakdown Voltage:**

$$
V_{b,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{b,\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{24}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 16.97 \text{ kV}
$$

**(iii) Time to Breakdown:**

$$
t_t = t_s + t_f = 1.2 \text{ µs} + 0.3 \text{ µs} = 1.5 \text{ µs}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $V_{b,\text{peak}} = 24 \text{ kV}$
- $V_{b,\text{rms}} \approx 16.97 \text{ kV}$
- $t_t = 1.5 \text{ µs}$ *(7 marks full)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ conversion:** Students often give the breakdown voltage in *peak* when the question asks for *RMS*, or vice versa. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\gamma$:** $\alpha$ is the **primary** ionization coefficient (per unit length, in $\text{m}^{-1}$); $\gamma_{se}$ is the **secondary** ionization coefficient (dimensionless ratio). Mixing them up costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping derivation steps:** In Part B derivations, students who write only the final Paschen's Law formula **without the intermediate Townsend steps** lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Unit errors in $E_b$:** Always use **$3 \text{ kV/mm}$** for air, not $3 \text{ kV/cm}$. This is a **1-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Ignoring current-limiting resistor:** In sphere-gap circuit diagrams, examiners expect the **limiting resistor $R$** in series; omitting it costs 1 mark.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Spark gap = pair of electrodes in air** that conducts only when $V > V_b$.
- 🌩️ **Primary use:** over-voltage / surge / lightning protection.
- 📏 **Dielectric strength of air:** $E_b = 3 \text{ kV/mm}$ (peak) = $30 \text{ kV/cm}$ (peak).
- 🔢 **Breakdown voltage (uniform field):** $V_b = E_b \cdot d$.
- 📐 **Paschen's Law:** $V_b$ depends on the product $p \cdot d$, not on either separately.
- 🧊 **Breakdown condition:** $\gamma_{se}(e^{\alpha d} - 1) = 1$.
- ⏱️ **Time lag:** $t_t = t_s + t_f$ (statistical + formative).
- 🔵 **Sphere gap** = primary HV standard (IEC 60052); **rod gap** = protection; **horn gap** = auto-extinguishing.
- 🔁 **Horn gap self-extinguishes** because the arc rises due to buoyancy, lengthens, and at the AC current zero, the available voltage is insufficient to re-ignite.
- 🛡️ **Sphere gap circuit** always needs a **current-limiting resistor** to protect the HV source.
- 📊 **Conversion rule:** $V_{peak} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ (sinusoidal AC).
- 🚫 **Common error:** confusing peak and rms values in numerical problems.
- 🧪 **Constants for Paschen's Law (air):** $A \approx 112.5$, $B \approx 2737.5$ (SI units).
- 🎯 **For 50% breakdown voltage (impulse):** use $V_{50\%}$ — the standard tabulated value for sphere gaps.
- 🧯 **After breakdown**, the arc is sustained only while current flows; in DC it persists, in AC it quenches at the zero crossing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surge Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surge Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surge Protection** is the engineered practice of safeguarding electrical and electronic equipment against **transient overvoltages (surges)** by deploying coordinated Surge Protective Devices (SPDs) that divert, limit, or shunt unwanted surge energy to earth, thereby clamping the voltage to a level below the equipment's **Withstand Voltage (UW)** or **Basic Insulation Level (BIL)**.

A **surge (transient)** is a short-duration, high-magnitude voltage or current disturbance superimposed on the nominal power waveform, typically lasting from microseconds (μs) to a few milliseconds (ms), with rise times in the nanosecond to microsecond range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Surge protection design is governed by **IEC 61643-11 / IEC 61643-12** (Low-voltage SPDs) and **IS 15086 / IEEE C62.41** for surge environment classification.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Relief Valve"

Imagine a domestic water pipeline designed to carry water at **2 bar pressure**. If a water-hammer effect suddenly pushes **20 bar** through the line, the pipes will burst. A **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** opens at a preset threshold, diverts the excess water safely to a drain, and then closes automatically.

| Domestic Water Analogy | Electrical Surge Protection |
|---|---|
| Normal water flow at 2 bar | Nominal mains voltage (230 V / 415 V) |
| Water-hammer surge (20 bar) | Lightning / switching transient (≥ 1.5 kV) |
| Pressure Relief Valve | Surge Protective Device (SPD) |
| Drain | Earth/Ground busbar |
| Pipe burst | Equipment insulation failure |

**The "pressure" is voltage; the "excess flow" is current; the "drain" is the earth electrode.**

### 1.3 Why Surge Protection is Mandatory (KTU Industrial Context)

Modern electronic loads — PLCs, VFDs, medical equipment, IT servers, IoT controllers — use semiconductor devices rated for **1.5 kV to 6 kV** impulse withstand (per IEC 60664-1). Even a **6 kV** ring-wave transient can permanently damage an SMPS. Hence surge protection is no longer optional — it is a **statutory safety requirement** under the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Safety Regulations, 2023** and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constant to remember:**
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - **Nominal system voltages in India:** $V_L = 230\text{ V}$ (single phase), $V_{LL} = 415\text{ V}$ (three phase)
> - **Standard impulse waveform:** **1.2/50 μs** (voltage) and **8/20 μs** (current) per IEEE C62.41
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Surges (Per IEEE C62.41 / IEC 61643)

Surge events are classified into three primary categories based on origin:

- **Lightning-Induced Surges (Type I / External):**
  * **Direct strike** — extremely high energy (≥ 100 kA peak).
  * **Indirect strike (induced coupling)** — capacitive/inductive coupling onto power/data lines within a **few hundred metres** of the strike point.
  * Up to **6 kV / 3 kA** can be coupled onto low-voltage lines.

- **Switching Transients (Type II / Internal):**
  * Generated by **inductive load switching** (motors, transformers, capacitor banks, contactors).
  * Typical magnitude: **1.5 kV to 6 kV**, duration: 0.1 ms to 10 ms.
  * Most common cause of equipment failure in industrial plants.

- **Electrostatic Discharge (ESD) (Type III):**
  * Human-body discharge to terminals of equipment.
  * Very short duration (ns range), low energy but high voltage (≥ 15 kV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Approximately **65% of all surge-related equipment failures** are caused by INTERNAL switching transients, NOT lightning. This is a frequently asked question in KTU University exams.

### 2.2 Surge Protective Devices (SPDs) — Working Principles

An SPD is a nonlinear voltage-limiting device that exhibits **high impedance** at normal operating voltage and **low impedance** during a surge, thereby clamping the voltage.

#### Core SPD Components

- **Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV):** Zinc-oxide (ZnO) ceramic grain structure. Conducts when terminal voltage exceeds its **varistor voltage (Vv)**. Most widely used; clamping voltage 1.5x to 2x rated.
- **Gas Discharge Tube (GDT):** Inert gas-filled ceramic tube. Ionizes and arcs at breakdown voltage. Highest surge current capacity (≥ 100 kA) but slower response (μs).
- **Transient Voltage Suppressor (TVS Diode):** Avalanche-mode semiconductor. Fastest response (ps to ns) but lowest energy absorption. Used for PCB-level protection of ICs.
- **Spark Gap:** Air arc chamber; archaic but used in HV substations.

### 2.3 SPD Classification (IEC 61643-11 Type 1 / 2 / 3)

| SPD Type | Installation Point | Test Waveform | Typical Application | Energy Handling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** (Class I) | Main service entrance, before meter | 10/350 μs | Lightning equipotential bonding | ≥ 25 kA (Iimp) |
| **Type 2** (Class II) | Sub-distribution boards | 8/20 μs | Switching surge protection | 20 kA – 40 kA (In) |
| **Type 3** (Class III) | Near terminal equipment (≤ 10 m cable) | 1.2/50 μs + 8/20 μs | Fine protection for IT/electronics | 1.5 kA – 5 kA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coordination Rule:** Type 1 → Type 2 → Type 3 must be installed in series with a **minimum cable length of 10 metres** (or a decoupling inductor) between successive stages to prevent unwanted interaction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surge Energy Dissipated in SPD | $W = \int V(t) \cdot I(t)\, dt$ | Joules (J) | Time integral of power |
| 2 | MOV Varistor Voltage | $V_v = V_{N} \times \sqrt{2} \times \alpha$ | V | $\alpha \in [1.4, 1.6]$ typical |
| 3 | Clamping Voltage Ratio | $K_c = \dfrac{V_{clamp}}{V_{ref}}$ | dimensionless | Should be $\leq 2$ for IT loads |
| 4 | Lightning Strike Current (realistic) | $I_{imp} = 25$ to $100$ kA | kA | $10/350$ μs waveform |
| 5 | Switching Surge Voltage | $V_s = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Back-EMF of inductance |
| 6 | Let-Through Voltage | $V_{LT} = V_{clamp} + L_{wire} \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Includes wiring inductance |
| 7 | Earth Electrode Resistance (NEC/KTU) | $R_e \le 5\ \Omega$ for SPDs | Ω | $\le 1\ \Omega$ for substations |
| 8 | Coordination Decoupling Length | $l_{min} = \dfrac{U_{w2} - U_{w1}}{k} \ge 10$ m | m | $k$ = inductance per metre |
| 9 | Decoupling Inductor (if short cable) | $L = \dfrac{U_{w2} - U_{w1}}{\dfrac{di}{dt}}$ | H (Henry) | Substitute inductor |
| 10 | Surge Impedance (line) | $Z_0 \approx 30 \text{ to } 600\ \Omega$ | Ω | Depends on cable geometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU-Pitfall:** Use `\vert` (not `|`) in handwritten exam scripts for absolute value. In markdown tables above, no vertical bars are used inside cells to avoid rendering issues.

### 2.5 Zones of Lightning Protection (LPZ Concept — IEC 62305-4)

The **Lightning Protection Zone (LPZ)** concept divides a building into nested volumes:

- **LPZ 0A:** External, direct strike possible. Full lightning current.
- **LPZ 0B:** External, screened from direct strike. Partial lightning current.
- **LPZ 1:** Internal, downstream of Type 1 SPD. Limited surge.
- **LPZ 2:** Internal, downstream of Type 2 SPD. Further limited.
- **LPZ 3:** Equipment terminal, downstream of Type 3 SPD. Within equipment immunity.

Boundary crossing from LPZ $n$ to LPZ $n+1$ **requires** a coordinated SPD.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inductive Switching Surge Magnitude

When an inductive circuit (motor winding, transformer primary, relay coil) is interrupted, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high voltage across the switch contacts.

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law applied to a coil:**

$$
\varepsilon = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — At the moment of switching (t = 0⁺):**

The current $i$ falls from $I_0$ to zero in a time $\Delta t$. Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{di}{dt} \approx \dfrac{I_0}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the EMF equation:**

$$
V_s = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt} = L \cdot \dfrac{I_0}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical evaluation:**

Consider a small motor with $L = 0.5$ H operating at $I_0 = 5$ A, switched off in $\Delta t = 1$ μs (typical contactor opening time):

$$
V_s = \dfrac{0.5 \times 5}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2.5}{10^{-6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ V}
$$

**Result:** A **2.5 MV transient** can be generated by switching a 5 A inductive load! (In practice, arc suppression, snubbers, and SPDs limit this to < 6 kV at the equipment terminals.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **motor starters, contactor coils, and solenoid valves** MUST have either an RC snubber, a free-wheeling diode (DC), or a varistor across the coil.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Let-Through Voltage Including Wiring Inductance

**Step 1 — Define the SPD clamping voltage:**

When the SPD activates, it clamps the voltage at the device terminal to $V_{clamp}$.

**Step 2 — Account for the connecting wire:**

The wire connecting the SPD to the line has an inductance $L_w$. The surge current has a rate of rise $\frac{di}{dt}$.

**Step 3 — Voltage across the wire:**

$$
V_{wire} = L_w \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Total let-through voltage at the protected equipment:**

$$
V_{LT} = V_{clamp} + V_{wire}
$$

$$
V_{LT} = V_{clamp} + L_w \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration:**

Assume $V_{clamp} = 1.2$ kV, $L_w = 1\ \mu\text{H/m} \times 0.5$ m of lead length, and $\frac{di}{dt} = 1$ kA/μs = $10^{9}$ A/s:

$$
V_{wire} = (0.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10^{9} = 500\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_{LT} = 1200 + 500 = 1700\ \text{V} = 1.7\ \text{kV}
$$

**Key Takeaway:** **Minimize SPD lead length** — every 10 cm of lead can add 100 V of let-through voltage during a fast surge. KTU rule of practice: lead length **< 0.5 m total**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Dissipated in an MOV During a Surge

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power in the SPD:**

$$
P(t) = V(t) \cdot I(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Total energy absorbed:**

$$
W = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} V(t) \cdot I(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Approximation using RMS-equivalent values for an 8/20 μs waveform:**

A standard $8/20\ \mu s$ current pulse of peak $I_p = 20$ kA has an equivalent RMS duration. Empirically, the energy can be approximated by:

$$
W \approx 0.5 \cdot V_{clamp} \cdot I_p \cdot \tau_{eq}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $V_{clamp} = 1.5$ kV, $I_p = 20$ kA, $\tau_{eq} = 20$ μs:**

$$
W \approx 0.5 \times 1500 \times 20000 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
W \approx 0.5 \times 1500 \times 20000 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
W \approx 0.5 \times 1500 \times 0.4 = 0.5 \times 600 = 300\ \text{J}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule:** An SPD selected for a service entrance should be rated for **at least 1.5 × maximum single-strike energy** to account for end-of-life derating. For multi-stroke lightning events (typically 3–5 strokes per flash), use a **Class II tested** device with $W \ge 1000$ J.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SPD Selection Decision Tree

| Decision Criteria | Preferred SPD Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Building with external lightning rod | Type 1 + Type 2 | Iimp test (10/350 μs) required |
| Industrial plant with motors / drives | Type 2 | 8/20 μs switching surges dominant |
| IT server room, hospital | Type 2 + Type 3 cascade | Sensitive electronics, fine protection |
| Solar PV DC side | Type 2 DC-rated (V DC > Voc × 1.2) | DC arc has no zero crossing |
| Telecom / signal line | GDT + TVS hybrid | Low capacitance, fast response |
| Outdoor CCTV / PoE | GDT + TVS on data pairs + MOV on power | Mixed environment |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Coordinated SPD Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UTIL[Utility 11kV OR 415V Incoming Line] --> MAIN[Main Service Entrance Panel MCCB AND Meter]
    MAIN --> T1[Type 1 SPD 10 by 350 microsec Class I Tested 25kA]
    T1 --> T2A[Distribution Board A]
    T1 --> T2B[Distribution Board B]
    T2A --> T2_1[Type 2 SPD 8 by 20 microsec 40kA]
    T2B --> T2_2[Type 2 SPD 8 by 20 microsec 40kA]
    T2_1 --> LOAD1[Motor Drive VFD]
    T2_1 --> LOAD2[PLC Panel]
    T2_2 --> LOAD3[HVAC Load]
    T2_2 --> LOAD4[Lighting Circuit]
    T2_1 --> T3A[Type 3 SPD Local Strip 1.5kA]
    T2_2 --> T3B[Type 3 SPD Local Strip 1.5kA]
    T3A --> IT1[IT Server Rack]
    T3A --> IT2[Medical Equipment]
    T3B --> IT3[Workstation PC]
    T3B --> IT4[CCTV NVR]
    T1 --> EARTH[Earth Electrode Re less than 5 Ohm Star Point]
    T2_1 --> EARTH
    T2_2 --> EARTH
    T3A --> EARTH
    T3B --> EARTH
    UTIL -.Lightning.-> ROOF[Building Rooftop Air Termination]
    ROOF -.Down Conductor.-> EARTH
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SPD Internal Topology (MOV + GDT + TVS Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LIN[L Live In] --> F1[Backup Fuse 32A gG]
    F1 --> M1[First Stage GDT Gas Discharge Tube]
    M1 --> M2[Second Stage MOV Metal Oxide Varistor]
    M2 --> M3[Third Stage TVS Diode Transient Suppressor]
    M3 --> LOUT[L Live Out to Load]
    LIN --> NIN[N Neutral In]
    NIN --> NOUT[N Neutral Out to Load]
    M1 --> EARTH2[PE Protective Earth]
    M2 --> EARTH2
    M3 --> EARTH2
    M3 --> SIG[Optional Signal Line to PE via TVS]
    LOUT --> EQUIP[Protected Equipment PLC VFD Server]
    NOUT --> EQUIP
    EARTH2 --> GND[Common Earth Busbar]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lightning Protection Zone Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LPZ0A[LPZ 0A External Direct Strike Zone] --> BOUND1[Boundary 0A to 0B Type 1 SPD at Service Entry]
    BOUND1 --> LPZ0B[LPZ 0B External Screened Partial Current]
    LPZ0B --> BOUND2[Boundary 0B to 1 Type 1 or Type 2 SPD]
    BOUND2 --> LPZ1[LPZ 1 Internal Main Distribution Switch Room]
    LPZ1 --> BOUND3[Boundary 1 to 2 Type 2 SPD at Sub DB]
    BOUND3 --> LPZ2[LPZ 2 Floor Distribution Zone]
    LPZ2 --> BOUND4[Boundary 2 to 3 Type 3 SPD at Terminal]
    BOUND4 --> LPZ3[LPZ 3 Equipment Terminal Sensitive Load]
    LPZ0A -.Coupling.-> SIG1[Signal Line Induction]
    SIG1 --> BOUND2
```

### 4.4 Textual Schematic — Installation Wiring Rules (KTU Practical Standard)

```
   [LINE IN]                                          [LINE OUT]
   L -----O-----[ FUSE 32A ]-----+-----[ MOV 1 ]-----+-----> to LOAD
                                |                    |
   N -----O---------------------+-----[ MOV 2 ]-----+-----> to LOAD
                                |                    |
   PE ----O---------------------+-----[ SHORT LEAD ]+-----> EARTH BUS
                                |                    |
                  (Lead length Lw < 0.5 m total)
                  (V-Shape OR Kelvin Wiring Recommended)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Wiring Rule:** Use **"V-wiring" or "Kelvin connection"** — the earth lead of the SPD must branch from the earth busbar as close as possible to the LINE/NEUTRAL incoming terminals. This minimizes the loop area and hence minimizes $L_w \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$ in the let-through voltage equation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]** — *Define a surge. List the three main types of surges and the typical magnitude of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **surge (transient overvoltage)** is a short-duration, high-magnitude disturbance superimposed on the nominal AC waveform, lasting μs to ms, with rise times in the ns–μs range.

- **Lightning-induced surge (Type I):** $\le 6$ kV / 3 kA at the service entrance (induced) and up to 100 kA for direct strike.
- **Switching transient (Type II):** $1.5$ to $6$ kV, 0.1–10 ms duration.
- **ESD (Type III):** Up to 15 kV, nanosecond duration. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — *Differentiate between Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 SPDs with respect to test waveform and installation point.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Type 1 (Class I):** Test waveform **10/350 μs** simulating direct lightning; installed at **main service entrance** before the meter.
- **Type 2 (Class II):** Test waveform **8/20 μs** simulating induced/switching surges; installed at **sub-distribution boards**.
- **Type 3 (Class III):** Test waveform **1.2/50 μs + 8/20 μs** combined; installed within **10 m of the terminal equipment** for fine protection. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

---

#### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Predicted Pattern)]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)** as a surge protective device. Mention the typical clamping ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 415 V, three-phase industrial motor has a contactor coil with $L = 0.2$ H, current $I_0 = 4$ A, switched off in $\Delta t = 2\ \mu s$. Calculate the peak switching surge voltage. Justify whether a Type 2 SPD is sufficient or a Type 1 SPD is needed. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

An MOV is a non-linear resistor fabricated from sintered **zinc-oxide (ZnO)** grains doped with bismuth, cobalt, and manganese oxides. At the grain boundaries, a **depletion layer** with very high resistance exists at low voltages. When the applied voltage exceeds the **varistor voltage ($V_v$)**, the depletion layer breaks down (Zener-like avalanche), and the device transitions from ~$10^{9}\ \Omega$ to a few ohms, shunting surge current.

The voltage-current characteristic follows:

$$
I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}
$$

where $\alpha \approx 30$ to $50$ for a good MOV, giving a sharp knee.

**Clamping ratio:** $K_c = \dfrac{V_{clamp}}{V_{ref}} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$.

After the surge, the depletion layer re-forms and the device returns to high-impedance state. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the inductive switching formula:

$$
V_s = L \cdot \dfrac{I_0}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$
V_s = 0.2 \times \dfrac{4}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{0.8}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.4 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_s = 400\ \text{kV (peak open-circuit induced voltage)}
$$

**Step 3:** Realistic interpretation:

In practice, arc across contactor and nearby circuit capacitance limit this to $\sim 4$ kV at the coil terminals. The **dominating mechanism** is the back-EMF of coil $L$ when interrupted.

**Step 4:** SPD Selection:

Since the surge is **internal switching** with a **0.5 μs–1 ms** rise time and $\le 6$ kV magnitude, this is a **8/20 μs class** event. Therefore a **Type 2 SPD is sufficient**, installed at the sub-distribution board feeding the motor starter. Type 1 is NOT needed (no direct lightning exposure on this panel). A **RC snubber or varistor across the contactor coil** is also recommended for local protection. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Pattern)]**

**(a)** What is meant by **coordination of SPDs**? With a block diagram, explain the three-stage cascade protection concept with reference to LPZ zones. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An SPD with $V_{clamp} = 1.2$ kV is connected to the line using 0.5 m of lead wire. The surge current has a rise rate of 1 kA/μs. Calculate the let-through voltage at the protected equipment. What installation practice will reduce this value? **[7 Marks]**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Coordination of SPDs** is the engineered practice of installing multiple SPDs (Type 1, 2, 3) in a **series cascade** with appropriate decoupling (cable length ≥ 10 m or decoupling inductor) so that each stage progressively reduces the surge energy without one stage interfering with the next.

**Three-Stage Cascade (refer to diagram in Section 4.3):**

- **Stage 1 (Type 1, LPZ 0 → LPZ 1):** Diverts the bulk of lightning current (10/350 μs) at the service entrance.
- **Stage 2 (Type 2, LPZ 1 → LPZ 2):** Clamps the residual 8/20 μs switching and induced surge at sub-distribution.
- **Stage 3 (Type 3, LPZ 2 → LPZ 3):** Fine clamping (1.5 kV residual) at the equipment terminal strip.

The boundary between successive stages must include at least 10 m of cable (or a discrete inductor) to provide series decoupling impedance, so that Stage 1 fully conducts before Stage 2 triggers. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Let-through voltage formula:

$$
V_{LT} = V_{clamp} + L_w \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute wire inductance:

$$
L_w = 1\ \mu\text{H/m} \times 0.5\ \text{m} = 0.5\ \mu\text{H}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute wire voltage drop:

$$
V_{wire} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{10^{3}\ \text{A}}{10^{-6}\ \text{s}} = 0.5 \times 10^{3} = 500\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4:** Total let-through voltage:

$$
V_{LT} = 1200 + 500 = 1700\ \text{V} = 1.7\ \text{kV}
$$

**Step 5:** Best installation practice:

Use **Kelvin (V-shaped) wiring** to keep the SPD earth lead as short and straight as possible (target **≤ 0.25 m**). Alternatively, use a **flat copper braid** of length < 0.1 m. If the lead cannot be shortened, insert a **decoupling inductor** in the line path. Reducing lead length to 0.1 m lowers $V_{wire}$ to 100 V, giving $V_{LT} = 1.3$ kV. **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often write "SPD = surge arrester" — this is WRONG. A **surge arrester** is for HV outdoor lines (11 kV, 33 kV), while an **SPD** is for LV (≤ 1000 V) installations.
> 2. Confusing **8/20 μs** (current) and **1.2/50 μs** (voltage) waveforms is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 3. Forgetting to **multiply by $\sqrt{2}$** when converting RMS to peak varistor voltage.
> 4. Omitting units in the final answer (mandatory 0.5 mark deduction as per KTU valuation key).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Surge = transient overvoltage**, μs to ms duration.
- **Three origins:** Lightning (Type I), Switching (Type II), ESD (Type III).
- **SPD ≠ Surge Arrester** (LV vs HV).
- **Test waveforms:** Type 1 → **10/350 μs**, Type 2 → **8/20 μs**, Type 3 → **1.2/50 μs + 8/20 μs**.
- **Internal switching** causes **65%** of equipment failures.
- **Switching surge formula:** $V_s = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$.
- **Let-through voltage:** $V_{LT} = V_{clamp} + L_w \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$.
- **Keep SPD lead length ≤ 0.5 m** (Kelvin/V-wiring); ideally < 0.25 m.
- **Earth resistance:** $R_e \le 5\ \Omega$ (LV SPD), $\le 1\ \Omega$ (substations).
- **Coordination rule:** Type 1 → 10 m → Type 2 → 10 m → Type 3.
- **LPZ concept:** IEC 62305-4 defines nested zones; each boundary needs an SPD.
- **Standards to remember:** IEC 61643-11, IEC 62305-4, IEEE C62.41, IS 15086, NBC 2016.
- **MOV = ZnO ceramic, non-linear I–V, $\alpha$ ≈ 30–50, clamping ratio 1.5–2.0**.
- **GDT = highest current, slowest response (μs)**.
- **TVS = fastest (ps), lowest energy, used on PCBs**.
- **Always specify test waveform AND $I_n$/$I_{imp}$ rating** when answering KTU numericals.
- **Multiply RMS by $\sqrt{2}$ to get peak** when calculating varistor voltage.
- **CEA Safety Regulations 2023 + NBC 2016** make surge protection a statutory requirement for buildings > 15 m height.
- **For solar PV DC side:** use DC-rated SPD with $V_{DC} \ge 1.2 \times V_{oc}$.
- **Signal/data line protection:** GDT + TVS hybrid, low capacitance (≤ 5 pF for Ethernet).
- **Final design step:** verify $V_{LT} \le U_w$ (equipment impulse withstand) per IEC 60664-1.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lighting Arrester
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lightning Arrester

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ LIGHTNING ARRESTER — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lightning Arrester** is a protective device installed on electrical power systems, transformers, buildings, and switchgear equipment to divert transient over-voltages (such as those caused by lightning strikes or switching surges) safely to earth, thereby limiting the amplitude of these voltage surges to a level that does not damage the insulation of the protected equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** A lightning arrester is a surge protective device (SPD) connected between the line conductor and earth, which possesses a non-linear voltage–current (V–I) characteristic. Under normal operating voltage, it remains practically an open circuit (high impedance), but during surge conditions, it momentarily becomes a low-impedance path to discharge the surge current to ground, and automatically restores to its high-impedance state after the surge passes.

**Synonyms accepted in KTU exams:** Surge Arrester, Surge Diverter, Lightning Diverter.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Pressure Relief Valve"**
>
> Imagine a water pipeline carrying water at normal pressure. A **pressure relief valve** sits on the pipe and is closed during normal flow. However, if a sudden pressure surge (water hammer) occurs, the valve **automatically opens**, releasing the excess pressure to a safe drain, and then **closes again** once the pressure normalizes. A lightning arrester does exactly the same thing — but with **electricity and voltage** instead of water and pressure.

### Why Lightning Arresters are Critical
Lightning can produce voltages of the order of **hundreds of kilovolts to several megavolts** with currents up to **200,000 A** within a few microseconds. Without protection, this would:
- Puncture the insulation of transformers, generators, and cables
- Cause flashovers in substations
- Damage consumer equipment
- Endanger human life

The arrester **clips** the voltage to a safe value and **drains** the surge energy to earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Principle in One Line:**
> *Normal condition → HIGH IMPEDANCE (blocks current) | Surge condition → LOW IMPEDANCE (diverts surge to ground) | Post-surge → Returns to HIGH IMPEDANCE.*

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|
| Lightning stroke current (typical) | **10 kA to 200 kA** |
| Lightning voltage (typical) | **100 kV to 1000 kV** |
| Surge duration | **1 µs to 100 µs** |
| Arrester response time | **< 100 ns** (for modern ZnO arresters) |
| Normal system voltage | **415 V / 11 kV / 33 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV** |
| Earth resistance required | **< 1 Ω** (substation), **< 5 Ω** (general), **< 10 Ω** (domestic) |

---

## 1.4 Function of a Lightning Arrester (KTU Board-Examiner Style)

The four primary functions of a lightning arrester are:

1. **To intercept the lightning stroke** (direct or indirect).
2. **To provide a low-impedance path** for the surge current to earth.
3. **To limit the voltage** across the protected equipment to a safe value (called the *discharge voltage* or *residual voltage*).
4. **To interrupt the power-frequency follow current** after the surge has passed and restore normal conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> A lightning arrester is **NOT** a lightning rod in the strict sense. A **lightning rod (air termination)** intercepts the strike and conducts it down, while a **lightning arrester (surge diverter)** protects electrical equipment from surge voltages traveling along conductors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a lightning arrester is based on the **non-linear V–I characteristic** of certain materials (silicon carbide, zinc oxide). Let us break it down into the four operating stages:

### **Stage 1 — Normal Operating Condition**
- The system operates at its normal line-to-earth voltage (e.g., 11 kV / √3 = **6.35 kV** for an 11 kV system).
- The arrester has a **very high resistance** (in megohms) and behaves almost like an open circuit.
- **Negligible current (microamperes)** flows to earth — known as the *leakage current*.

### **Stage 2 — Surge Arrival (Transient Over-voltage)**
- A lightning surge or switching surge raises the voltage across the arrester above its **sparkover voltage** (for gap-type) or **breakdown voltage** (for non-linear resistance type).
- The arrester impedance **collapses almost instantaneously** (within nanoseconds to microseconds).
- The surge current is shunted to earth, while the voltage across the arrester terminals is limited to the **residual voltage (Vᵣ)** or **discharge voltage**.

### **Stage 3 — Follow Current / Power-Frequency Current**
- After the surge current has been discharged, the system still applies normal line voltage across the arrester.
- A small **power-frequency current (50/60 Hz)**, called the *follow current* or *arc current*, continues to flow.
- For the arrester to function properly, this follow current must be **extinguished** at the next current zero.

### **Stage 4 — Recovery / Sealing**
- Once the follow current is interrupted, the arrester **recovers its high-resistance state**.
- The system returns to its normal operating condition without any manual intervention.

---

## 2.2 Essential Characteristics of an Ideal Lightning Arrester

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An ideal lightning arrester must possess:
> 1. **Zero impedance during surge** → divert all surge current to ground.
> 2. **Infinite impedance at normal voltage** → no leakage / no power loss.
> 3. **Instantaneous response** → operate within nanoseconds.
> 4. **Ability to interrupt follow current** at current zero.
> 5. **High thermal capacity** → absorb surge energy without damage.
> 6. **Non-linear V–I characteristic** → sharp knee in the curve.

---

## 2.3 Types of Lightning Arresters (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### **A. Based on Construction / Technology**

| Type | Full Form | Key Feature | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rod Gap Arrester** | Rod Gap Diverter | Simplest; two rods with an air gap | Substations (backup), rural |
| **Sphere Gap Arrester** | Sphere Gap Diverter | Two spherical electrodes; uniform field | High-voltage labs, low-current |
| **Horn Gap Arrester** | Horn Gap Diverter | Horn-shaped electrodes; arc rises & stretches | Old systems, 11–33 kV |
| **Valve-Type Arrester (SiC)** | Silicon Carbide Arrester | Non-linear SiC resistor blocks; series gaps | Up to 220 kV (older systems) |
| **Metal Oxide Arrester (ZnO)** | Zinc Oxide Arrester | No series gaps; highly non-linear | Modern, 11 kV to 1200 kV |

### **B. Based on Application**

- **Station Class Arrester** — for substation equipment (highest protection).
- **Intermediate Class Arrester** — for distribution transformers.
- **Distribution Class Arrester** — for overhead lines and pole-mounted transformers.
- **Secondary Class Arrester** — for low-voltage (240/415 V) consumer installations.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the essential formulas used in arrester design and selection. All formulas are **dimensionally consistent** and verifiable.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Discharge Voltage (Vᵣ)** | $V_r = I_s \times R_a$ | kV | Product of surge current and arrester impedance |
| 2 | **Voltage Protection Level** | $V_{pl} = \max(V_{so}, V_r)$ | kV | Maximum of sparkover & discharge voltage |
| 3 | **System Line-to-Earth Voltage** | $V_{LE} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | V or kV | $V_L$ = line-to-line voltage |
| 4 | **Maximum Continuous Operating Voltage (MCOV)** | $MCOV \geq \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \times 1.05$ | kV | 5% margin for over-voltages |
| 5 | **Arrester Energy Absorption** | $W = \int_{0}^{t} V_r(t) \cdot I_s(t) \, dt$ | kJ | Energy dissipated during surge |
| 4 | **Earthing Resistance (general)** | $R \leq \dfrac{50}{I_g}$ | Ω | $I_g$ = maximum fault current (A) |
| 6 | **Earth Electrode Resistance (Rod)** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{a}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ω | $\rho$ = soil resistivity, $L$ = length, $a$ = radius |
| 7 | **Earth Electrode Resistance (Plate)** | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4r}\left[1 + \dfrac{r}{2h}\right]$ | Ω | $r$ = plate radius, $h$ = depth |
| 8 | **Number of rods in parallel (for low R)** | $R_n = \dfrac{R_1}{n} \times F$ | Ω | $F$ = usage factor (typically 1.2 to 1.7) |
| 9 | **Surge Impedance of Line** | $Z = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Ω | For travelling wave calculations |
| 10 | **BIL (Basic Insulation Level)** | $BIL = k \times V_L$ | kV | $k$ depends on insulation class (4 to 6) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Note:** All voltage formulas use kV when dealing with high-voltage systems (≥ 1 kV). Use consistent SI prefixes throughout. Never confuse **line-to-line** voltage with **line-to-earth** voltage in earthing and arrester calculations.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lightning arresters are the **backbone of surge protection** in:
- EHV (Extra High Voltage) substations (400 kV, 765 kV)
- Distribution networks (11 kV, 33 kV)
- Solar PV farms and wind turbine installations
- Railway traction systems (25 kV OHE)
- Data centres and IT infrastructure (Type 1/2/3 SPDs)
- Domestic buildings (BS 7671 / IS 732 compliance)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Tip:** In KTU exams, when asked to "select" or "rate" an arrester, always state the **system voltage**, **MCOV**, **discharge class**, and **earth resistance value** — these are the four golden parameters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Earth Resistance of a Rod Electrode

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU Module 3 (Earthing & Lightning Protection).

### **Statement:**
Derive an expression for the resistance of a rod electrode driven vertically into the soil of resistivity $\rho$.

### **Assumptions:**
- The rod is cylindrical, of length $L$ and radius $a$, with $L \gg a$.
- The current flows radially outward into the soil.
- The soil is homogeneous and isotropic.
- The remote earth acts as the return conductor.

### **Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Consider a thin shell of soil of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ at distance $r$ from the rod. The current $I$ flows outward through this shell.

The resistance of the elemental shell is:

$$dR = \rho \cdot \frac{dr}{A(r)}$$

The area of the shell at radius $r$ is the surface area of a cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$A(r) = 2 \pi r L$$

Substituting:

$$dR = \rho \cdot \frac{dr}{2 \pi r L}$$

Integrate from the surface of the rod (radius $a$) to the remote earth (radius $\infty$):

$$R = \int_{a}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dr}{2 \pi L \, r}$$

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \int_{a}^{\infty} \frac{dr}{r}$$

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \Big[ \ln(r) \Big]_{a}^{\infty}$$

Since the integration extends to infinity (theoretically), we use a practical far-field distance of $\infty$, but the result simplifies to:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{a}\right) - 1 \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:**
>
> $$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{a}\right) - 1 \right] \quad \text{(ohms)}$$
>
> This is the **standard formula** for a single vertical rod earth electrode. It is also known as the **IEEE/IS 3043 formula**.

### **Assumptions Clarification:**
- The expression $-1$ comes from Dwight's correction for the rod tip and finite electrode geometry.
- Valid when $L \gg a$ (e.g., $L = 3$ m, $a = 0.0125$ m, ratio = 240:1 ✓).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Earth Resistance of a Plate Electrode

### **Statement:**
Derive an expression for the resistance of a plate electrode buried in soil of resistivity $\rho$.

### **Assumption:**
The plate is circular of radius $r$, buried at depth $h$ in homogeneous soil.

### **Derivation:**

For a circular plate, the resistance is given by the formula:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{4r} \left[ 1 + \frac{r}{2h} \right]$$

If the plate is square of side $b$, the equivalent radius is $r = b/\sqrt{\pi}$, giving:

$$R = \frac{\rho}{4b} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{b}{h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The resistance of a plate is generally **lower** than a rod for the same material quantity, making plate electrodes preferred where low resistance is critical. However, rods are easier to install and maintain.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 33 kV substation has a soil resistivity of **200 Ω·m**. A vertical rod electrode of length **3 m** and diameter **25 mm** is used. Calculate the resistance to earth. If the maximum allowable earth resistance is **1 Ω**, how many such rods in parallel (with a usage factor of 1.5) are required?

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate resistance of a single rod.**

Given:
- $\rho = 200$ Ω·m
- $L = 3$ m
- $a = \dfrac{25}{2} \times 10^{-3} = 0.0125$ m
- $F = 1.5$ (usage factor)

Using the rod formula:

$$R_1 = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{a}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Compute $\dfrac{4L}{a}$:

$$\frac{4L}{a} = \frac{4 \times 3}{0.0125} = \frac{12}{0.0125} = 960$$

Compute $\ln(960)$:

$$\ln(960) = 6.866$$

Apply the formula:

$$R_1 = \frac{200}{2 \pi \times 3} \left[ 6.866 - 1 \right]$$

$$R_1 = \frac{200}{18.85} \times 5.866$$

$$R_1 = 10.61 \times 5.866$$

$$R_1 = 62.25 \ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of rods in parallel for $R_n \leq 1$ Ω.**

The parallel combination formula with usage factor $F$:

$$R_n = \frac{F \cdot R_1}{n}$$

$$n = \frac{F \cdot R_1}{R_n} = \frac{1.5 \times 62.25}{1}$$

$$n = 93.4$$

Since $n$ must be a whole number:

$$n = 94 \text{ rods}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Approximately **94 rods** would be required to achieve an earth resistance of 1 Ω, which is impractical. In real substation design, **chemical earthing (earth enhancement compounds like bentonite, Marconite, or GEM)** is used, which reduces soil resistivity and brings down the required number to **2–10 rods**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Arrester Rating

The following table is the **KTU Board Examiner's standard analytical tool** for selecting an arrester:

| System Voltage (Line-to-Line) | MCOV (≥ V_L / √3 × 1.05) | Rated Voltage (kV rms) | Discharge Class | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **415 V** | 0.252 kV | 0.28 kV | Secondary | Building service entrance |
| **11 kV** | 6.67 kV | 9 kV | Distribution | Pole-mounted transformer |
| **22 kV** | 13.33 kV | 18 kV | Distribution | HT consumer |
| **33 kV** | 20.0 kV | 30 kV | Intermediate | Primary substation |
| **66 kV** | 40.0 kV | 60 kV | Station | EHV substation |
| **110 kV** | 66.7 kV | 96 kV | Station | Grid substation |
| **220 kV** | 133.3 kV | 198 kV | Station | Transmission |
| **400 kV** | 242.5 kV | 360 kV | Station | EHV grid |

> [!TIP]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (for KTU):**
> - **Rated voltage of arrester** $\approx$ **1.5 × MCOV** (for effectively earthed systems)
> - **Rated voltage of arrester** $\approx$ **1.4 × V_L / √3** (rounded up to next standard)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Lightning Arrester Protection Scheme

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **complete functional architecture** of a lightning protection system, showing how the arrester interfaces with the line, the protected equipment, and the earth electrode.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    id1["LIGHTNING SOURCE\nAtmospheric Discharge"]
    id2["OVERHEAD LINE / PHASE CONDUCTOR\nTransmits Surge Wave"]
    id3["LIGHTNING ARRESTER\nNon Linear Device"]
    id4["PROTECTED EQUIPMENT\nTransformer or Cable"]
    id5["EARTH ELECTRODE\nRod or Plate"]
    id6["REMOTE EARTH\nReference Potential"]
    id7["SURGE COUNTER\nOptional Accessory"]
    id8["DISCHARGE PATH\nTo Ground"]

    id1 -- Voltage Surge Travels --> id2
    id2 -- Connects to --> id3
    id3 -- Limits Voltage --> id4
    id3 -- Shunts Surge To --> id7
    id3 -- Diverts Current To --> id5
    id5 -- Dissipates Energy Into --> id6
    id7 -- Records Number of Strikes --> id6
    id4 -- Continues Normal Operation --> id2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The lightning source induces a voltage surge on the line. The arrester senses the over-voltage, switches to low-impedance mode, and routes the surge through the surge counter (which records the strike) into the earth electrode, which dissipates the energy into remote earth. The protected equipment sees only the clipped (residual) voltage.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Construction of a ZnO Lightning Arrester

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    idA["LINE TERMINAL\nTop of Arrester"]
    idB["SILICONE RUBBER HOUSING\nWeather Proof Shell"]
    idC["ZNO BLOCKS\nNon Linear Resistance"]
    idD["ALUMINIUM SPACER\nMechanical Support"]
    idE["EARTH TERMINAL\nBottom of Arrester"]
    idF["PRESSURE RELIEF DEVICE\nSafety Venting"]

    idA --> idB
    idB --> idC
    idC --> idD
    idD --> idC
    idD --> idE
    idC -.-> idF
    idF -.-> idE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Notice that a **modern ZnO arrester has NO series spark gap**. The zinc oxide blocks themselves provide the non-linear V–I characteristic. The pressure relief device is a safety feature that vents internal gases in case of arrester failure (overload), preventing explosion.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Arrester Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    idRoot["TYPES OF LIGHTNING ARRESTERS"]
    idR1["GAPPED ARRESTERS"]
    idR2["GAPLESS ARRESTERS"]

    idRoot --> idR1
    idRoot --> idR2

    idR1A["Rod Gap"]
    idR1B["Sphere Gap"]
    idR1C["Horn Gap"]
    idR1D["Valve Type SiC"]

    idR1 --> idR1A
    idR1 --> idR1B
    idR1 --> idR1C
    idR1 --> idR1D

    idR2A["Metal Oxide ZnO"]
    idR2B["Polymer Housed ZnO"]

    idR2 --> idR2A
    idR2 --> idR2B
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparative V–I Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    idA["LINEAR RESISTOR\nOhms Law V equals IR"]
    idB["ZNO ARRESTER\nHighly Non Linear"]
    idC["SIC ARRESTER\nModerately Non Linear"]
    idD["ROD GAP ARRESTER\nVoltage Switched"]

    idA -- "Linear" --> idB
    idB -- "Sharper Knee" --> idC
    idC -- "Has Series Gap" --> idD
    idD -- "Abrupt Sparkover" --> idA
```

> [!TIP]
> **V–I Curve Interpretation:** On a V–I plot, the linear resistor is a straight line through the origin. The ZnO arrester has the **sharpest knee** (ideal characteristic), meaning it switches from OFF to ON state most efficiently. The SiC arrester is moderately non-linear, while the rod gap has an **abrupt sparkover** followed by a low arc voltage.

---

## 4.5 Schematic — Earthing System Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    id1["EQUIPMENT BODY\nTo Be Earthed"]
    id2["EARTHING CONDUCTOR\nGI or Copper Strip"]
    id3["TEST LINK\nInspection Disconnect"]
    id4["EARTH ELECTRODE\nRod or Plate"]
    id5["EARTHING COMPOUND\nBentonite or Marconite"]
    id6["SOIL MASS\nSurrounding Earth"]

    id1 -- Connects To --> id2
    id2 -- Through --> id3
    id3 -- Down To --> id4
    id4 -- Embedded In --> id5
    id5 -- Surrounded By --> id6
    id4 -- Current Flows To --> id6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a lightning arrester. State its function.**

**Model Answer:**

A lightning arrester is a protective device used in power systems to safeguard electrical equipment from transient over-voltages caused by lightning strikes or switching surges. It is connected between the line conductor and earth.

**Functions:**
1. It provides a low-impedance path to earth for surge currents.
2. It limits the voltage across the protected equipment to a safe value (residual voltage).
3. It interrupts the power-frequency follow current after the surge is cleared.
4. It restores the system to normal operation automatically.

*(3 marks — Definition: 1, Functions: 2)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Compare gap-type and gapless (ZnO) lightning arresters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Gap-Type Arrester (SiC) | Gapless Arrester (ZnO) |
|---|---|---|
| Series spark gap | Yes | No |
| V–I characteristic | Moderately non-linear | Highly non-linear |
| Follow current | Must be interrupted by gap | Self-extinguishing |
| Response time | Slower (microseconds) | Faster (nanoseconds) |
| Maintenance | Frequent | Almost zero |
| Modern use | Legacy systems | Universal today |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Horn Gap Lightning Arrester**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A horn gap arrester consists of two horn-shaped metal electrodes separated by a small air gap. The horns are mounted on insulators and the gap is connected between the line and earth. The arrangement is such that the distance between the horns gradually increases upward.

**Working:**
1. Under normal voltage, the gap remains non-conducting.
2. When a surge voltage appears, the air gap breaks down (sparkover), and an arc is established.
3. The arc heats the surrounding air, which rises upward.
4. As the arc travels upward into the diverging section of the horns, its length increases.
5. The increased arc length raises the arc resistance, and at the next current zero, the arc is **extinguished**.
6. The arrester resets to its high-impedance state.

**Advantages:** Simple, no replacement needed.
**Disadvantages:** Risk of arc persistence at high follow currents; not suitable for EHV.

*(7 marks — Sketch: 2, Construction: 2, Working: 3)*

**Q. (b)** Derive the expression for the **earth resistance of a vertical rod electrode** and explain the factors affecting it. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** (as derived in Section 3.1 above)

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{a}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Factors Affecting Earth Resistance:**
1. **Soil resistivity ($\rho$)** — directly proportional; lower resistivity = lower resistance.
2. **Length of rod ($L$)** — inversely proportional; longer rods = lower resistance.
3. **Radius of rod ($a$)** — log-dependence; slightly lower resistance for larger radius.
4. **Moisture content of soil** — wet soil = lower resistance.
5. **Temperature** — frozen soil has very high resistivity.
6. **Depth of burial** — deeper = cooler and more moist = lower resistance.
7. **Number of rods in parallel** — reduces resistance but with diminishing returns (usage factor $F$).
8. **Salt/chemical treatment** — adding bentonite or salt reduces soil resistivity.

*(7 marks — Derivation: 4, Factors: 3)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the construction, working, and advantages of a **Zinc Oxide (ZnO) Lightning Arrester** with a V–I characteristic curve. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A ZnO arrester is a **gapless** arrester made of a series of zinc oxide non-linear resistance discs housed in a porcelain or polymer insulator. The ZnO blocks are connected between the line and earth terminals. No spark gaps are used. A pressure relief vent is provided for safety.

**Working:**
- Under normal voltage, the ZnO block has a very high resistance (in the order of **MΩ**), allowing only a tiny leakage current (microamperes).
- When the voltage exceeds the knee-point voltage, the resistance falls sharply (to a few ohms), and the surge current is shunted to earth.
- After the surge, the voltage drops, the resistance returns to its high value, and the follow current is naturally suppressed (no need for gap to interrupt).

**V–I Characteristic:**
The curve has three distinct regions:
1. **Pre-breakdown region** (high slope) — very small leakage current at normal voltage.
2. **Knee region** — sharp transition; resistance falls rapidly.
3. **Up-turn region** (low slope) — high current, low voltage; saturation behaviour.

**Advantages:**
1. Highly non-linear — superior protection.
2. No series gap — faster response.
3. No follow current — no arc to extinguish.
4. Compact and lightweight.
5. Long life, low maintenance.

*(7 marks — Construction: 2, Working: 3, Advantages: 2)*

**Q. (b)** A substation has a soil resistivity of **150 Ω·m**. A vertical rod electrode of length **2.5 m** and diameter **20 mm** is installed. Calculate: (i) the resistance of a single rod, and (ii) the number of rods required in parallel to achieve an earth resistance of **2 Ω**, assuming a usage factor of **1.4**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $\rho = 150$ Ω·m
- $L = 2.5$ m
- Diameter = 20 mm $\Rightarrow a = 0.01$ m
- $R_n = 2$ Ω, $F = 1.4$

**Step 1 — Resistance of a single rod.**

$$\frac{4L}{a} = \frac{4 \times 2.5}{0.01} = 1000$$

$$\ln(1000) = 6.908$$

$$R_1 = \frac{150}{2 \pi \times 2.5} \left[ 6.908 - 1 \right]$$

$$R_1 = \frac{150}{15.708} \times 5.908$$

$$R_1 = 9.549 \times 5.908$$

$$R_1 = 56.42 \ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Number of rods in parallel.**

$$R_n = \frac{F \cdot R_1}{n}$$

$$n = \frac{F \cdot R_1}{R_n} = \frac{1.4 \times 56.42}{2}$$

$$n = 39.5 \approx 40 \text{ rods}$$

**Conclusion:** **40 rods** in parallel are required to achieve 2 Ω earth resistance. (In practice, chemical earthing would bring this down to 4–6 rods.)

*(7 marks — Formula: 2, Calculation single rod: 2, Calculation parallel: 2, Result: 1)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing earthing resistance formula with plate vs rod** — many students use the rod formula for plates or vice versa. Always check the electrode type.
> 2. **Forgetting the usage factor $F$** in parallel-rod calculations. The naive formula $n = R_1 / R_n$ gives an **unrealistic** (low) answer and is **incorrect** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Mixing line-to-line and line-to-earth voltages** in MCOV calculations — this is a **2–3 mark** deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit of $\rho$ (Ω·m) vs $R$ (Ω)** — they are dimensionally different and must be carefully written.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — KTU examiners expect "Consider a vertical rod in homogeneous soil..." before starting the derivation.
> 6. **Confusing "sparkover voltage" with "discharge voltage"** — sparkover is across the gap; discharge is across the non-linear resistor.
> 7. **In numerical problems, not writing the formula first** — a step-marker is mandatory: "Using $R = ...$, we have..."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Lightning Arrester)**

- ⚡ **Lightning Arrester** = Surge Protective Device (SPD) connected between line and earth.
- ⚡ **Function** = Divert surge, limit voltage, interrupt follow current, restore normal state.
- ⚡ **Operating States** = OFF (normal) → ON (surge) → OFF (recovery).
- ⚡ **V–I Characteristic** = Non-linear (highly so for ZnO).
- ⚡ **Modern Arrester** = **Metal Oxide (ZnO), gapless, with pressure relief vent**.
- ⚡ **Older Arrester** = SiC with series gap; requires gap to interrupt follow current.
- ⚡ **Horn Gap** uses arc-stretching principle; **Sphere Gap** uses uniform field; **Rod Gap** is the simplest.
- ⚡ **Discharge voltage** = $V_r = I_s \times R_a$ (surge current × arrester impedance).
- ⚡ **MCOV** $\geq 1.05 \times V_L / \sqrt{3}$.
- ⚡ **Rod Resistance Formula** = $\dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln(4L/a) - 1\right]$ — the **most important formula** for Module 3.
- ⚡ **Plate Resistance Formula** = $\dfrac{\rho}{4r}\left[1 + \dfrac{r}{2h}\right]$.
- ⚡ **Parallel Rods** = $R_n = \dfrac{F \cdot R_1}{n}$; **usage factor $F$ = 1.2 to 1.7**.
- ⚡ **Acceptable earth resistance** = **< 1 Ω** (substation), **< 5 Ω** (general), **< 10 Ω** (domestic).
- ⚡ **Earthing compounds** like **bentonite, Marconite, GEM** reduce soil resistivity dramatically.
- ⚡ **Surge counter** is an optional accessory to record the number of arrester operations.
- ⚡ **IS 3070** is the relevant Indian Standard for lightning arresters; **IS 3043** is for earthing.
- ⚡ **KTU Hot Words** for 14-mark answers: "construction, working, advantages, V–I characteristics, neat sketch, comparison with...".

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Power Strategy for KTU ESE Module 3:**
> 1. Memorize the **rod resistance derivation** — it is worth a full 7-mark question almost every year.
> 2. Practice **one numerical** on parallel rods (it is more frequent than single-rod numericals).
> 3. Be ready to draw a **horn gap or ZnO arrester diagram** with proper labeling.
> 4. Always end a 14-mark answer with a **comparison table or advantages/disadvantages** — this guarantees the final 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grounding of Line Structure. Effect of Electric and Magnetic Fields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grounding of Line Structure. Effect of Electric and Magnetic Fields

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Grounding of Line Structure (Tower / Pole Grounding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
