> *Grounding of a line structure is the deliberate electrical connection of a transmission tower or pole (and all associated metallic hardware such as cross-arms, insulator strings, shield wires, and body extensions) to the general body of earth using low-impedance earth electrodes, so as to safely dissipate lightning surges, short-circuit currents, and static charges into the soil without creating hazardous step or touch potentials.*

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the earth electrode system at the base of each tower forms the **Earth Electrode Subsystem**, and the combined tower-footing resistance must be kept within **10 Ω (for 66 kV and below)**, **5 Ω (for 132 kV)**, and as low as **1 Ω to 2 Ω (for 400 kV and 765 kV EHV/UHV lines)**, per **IS 3043:1987 (Reaffirmed 2018)** and **IEEE Std 80-2013**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightning Escape Route":**
> Imagine the tower as a tall metal umbrella standing in an open field during a thunderstorm. The umbrella (tower) gets hit by lightning thousands of times in its lifetime. Without a drain pipe (grounding), the water (current) would have nowhere to go — it would splash sideways onto people, animals, or equipment touching the tower.
> **Grounding = installing the drain pipe.** The earth electrode is the pipe, the soil is the surrounding ground, and the "smoothness" of water flow is what we call *low earth resistance*. A smaller resistance means the lightning current enters the earth quickly and harmlessly.

---

### 1.2 Effect of Electric and Magnetic Fields (EMF) from High-Voltage Lines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Every high-voltage transmission line acts as a source of two distinct quasi-static fields around it — an Electric Field (E-field) produced by the line voltage, and a Magnetic Field (B-field or H-field) produced by the line current. At power frequency (50 Hz in India), these are termed Extremely Low Frequency (ELF) fields. Their intensities diminish with distance from the conductors and are governed by Maxwell's quasi-static equations.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Invisible "Aura" Around Power Lines:**
- The **Electric field** is like the *static cling* you feel when you walk under old CRT TVs — voltage is there even if no current flows. Switch off a line with no load → voltage still present → E-field still present, but **B-field ≈ 0**.
- The **Magnetic field** is like the *invisible push* around a current-carrying wire — only exists when current flows. Under no-load conditions, current is negligible → B-field is negligible, but **E-field persists**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Confusion:**
> "Switching off a line removes EMF." — **Wrong.** Switching off the line removes current (so magnetic field disappears) but **voltage still exists** until the line is earthed on both ends through maintenance grounds. Hence, *dead lines can still be electrically hazardous*.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | **50 Hz** | Indian Power Grid |
| E-field at edge of ROW (400 kV) | ≤ **10 kV/m** | ICNIRP 2010 |
| B-field at edge of ROW (400 kV) | ≤ **100 μT** | ICNIRP 2010 |
| Tower Footing Resistance (400 kV) | ≤ **10 Ω** (preferred ≤ 1 Ω) | IS 3043 |
| Soil Resistivity (typical India) | **100 to 1000 Ω·m** | varies with moisture |
| ROW — Right of Way (400 kV) | **46 m** width | CEA Regulations |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Methods of Grounding Line Structures

KTU 2024 syllabus mandates coverage of the following four methods:

**A. Driven Rod (Pipe / Rod Electrode)**
- A galvanized iron (GI) pipe of **40 mm to 50 mm diameter**, length **2 m to 3 m**, driven vertically into the soil at the tower footing.
- Used when soil resistivity is uniform up to ~3 m depth.
- Multiple rods in parallel reduce equivalent resistance.

**B. Counterpoise (Buried Horizontal Wire)**
- A GI / copper strip buried **0.5 m to 1.0 m** below ground, laid radially from the tower base.
- Length typically **15 m to 60 m**, sometimes up to **120 m** for high-resistivity rocky terrain.
- Used where deep driven rods cannot be installed (rocky strata).

**C. Butt Plate (Foundation Plate Electrode)**
- A GI plate (typically **600 mm × 600 mm × 6 mm**) embedded directly in the tower concrete foundation.
- Provides large contact area with moist soil surrounding the footing.
- Most common in modern lattice tower construction.

**D. Pipe-Cum-Counterpoise (Hybrid)**
- Combination of driven rod(s) + radial counterpoise wires.
- Standard recommendation for **400 kV D/C towers** in Indian terrain.

---

### 2.2 Step Potential vs Touch Potential vs Mesh Potential

This is the **highest-weightage topic** in KTU Module 3 for tower grounding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical Safety Voltages in a Substation / Tower Footing Area:**
> - **Step Potential (Eₛ):** Voltage between the feet of a person (assumed step = **1 m**) standing on the ground during a fault.
> - **Touch Potential (Eₜ):** Voltage between a hand touching a faulty structure and the feet of that person on the ground.
> - **Mesh Potential (Eₘ):** Touch voltage available within an equipotential grounding mesh — *lower than open-area touch potential*.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value bars and divisions in this table use `\vert` and `\mid` (not `\vert x\vert` syntax that could break markdown) and `\dfrac` for clarity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resistance of single driven rod | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ω | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m), $L$ = rod length (m), $d$ = rod diameter (m) |
| 2 | Resistance of horizontal strip (counterpoise) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{\sqrt{hd}}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{2h}{L}\right]$ | Ω | $h$ = burial depth (m) |
| 3 | Resistance of butt plate (square) | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{8r} + \dfrac{\rho}{4r}$ | Ω | $r$ = equivalent radius of plate (m) |
| 4 | Resistance of $n$ rods in parallel | $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | Ω | Valid if spacing $\geq$ rod length |
| 5 | Step potential | $E_s = \dfrac{\rho I_s}{2\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | V | $r_1, r_2$ = distances from fault point |
| 6 | Touch potential | $E_t = \dfrac{\rho I_s}{2\pi r_t}$ | V | $r_t$ = distance from structure to feet |
| 7 | Ground Potential Rise (GPR) | $\mathrm{GPR} = I_f \times R_e$ | V | $I_f$ = fault current (A), $R_e$ = earth resistance |
| 8 | Allowable touch voltage (IEEE 80) | $E_{t,\text{allow}} = (1000 + 1.5\,c\,s\,\rho_s)\dfrac{0.116}{\sqrt{t_s}}$ | V | $c$ = surface layer derating, $s$ = shock duration, $\rho_s$ = surface resistivity |
| 9 | Allowable step voltage | $E_{s,\text{allow}} = (1000 + 6\,c\,s\,\rho_s)\dfrac{0.116}{\sqrt{t_s}}$ | V | Higher because feet separated |
| 10 | Conductor surface E-field (single wire) | $E = \dfrac{V}{r\,\ln(D/r)}$ | V/m | $D$ = distance to return conductor/image |
| 11 | Magnetic field (B) near single wire | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0\,I}{2\pi r}$ | T (Tesla) | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| 12 | Magnetic field magnitude (3-phase line) | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \sqrt{3}\,I}{2\pi D}$ | T | For balanced 3-phase at distance $D$ |
| 13 | Corona inception gradient (Peek's) | $E_0 = m \, g_0 \, \delta \, r \ln(D/r)$ | kV/cm | $m$ = irregularity factor |
| 14 | Power loss due to corona | $P = \dfrac{244}{\delta}(f+25)\!\left(\dfrac{V}{\sqrt{3}}\!\log_{10}\!\dfrac{D}{r}\right)\!^2 \times 10^{-5}$ | kW/km | Empirical, for fair weather |

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Field Practice

- **For Transmission Engineers:** Tower footing resistance directly controls the back-flashover rate of the line during lightning. Every 1 Ω reduction can cut outage frequency by ~10 % in high Keraunic zones (Kerala = ~70 thunderstorm days/year).
- **For Safety Engineers:** Step and touch potentials determine whether a substation operator survives a fault. Kerala's laterite + high-rainfall soil makes touch potentials particularly dangerous — hence **gravel surfacing (ρs ≈ 3000 Ω·m)** is mandatory at substation switchyards.
- **For Civil/Structural Engineers:** Tower design must include a *grounding study* before foundation work — especially relevant in coastal Kerala (sandy, high-resistivity soil).
- **For Public Health Officials:** ICNIRP limits dictate minimum building setback distances from EHV corridors (e.g., **minimum 35 m** for 220 kV, **minimum 46 m** for 400 kV).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resistance of a Driven Rod (Hemispherical Current Dissipation Model)

**Assumption:** Current diverges radially from the rod tip into a *hemisphere of soil* of increasing radius.

$$
\text{Resistance of thin shell of radius } x \text{ and thickness } dx:
$$

$$
dR = \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^{2}}
$$

(Here, $2\pi x^{2}$ is the surface area of the hemisphere at radius $x$, and $dx$ is the thickness.)

$$
\text{Total resistance from rod tip to infinity:}
$$

$$
R = \int_{L/2}^{\infty} \dfrac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^{2}}
$$

(We start integration at $x = L/2$ because current effectively begins to spread from the midpoint of the rod.)

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[-\dfrac{1}{x}\right]_{L/2}^{\infty}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[0 - \left(-\dfrac{2}{L}\right)\right]
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_{\text{approx}} = \dfrac{\rho}{\pi L}\,}
$$

**More accurate form (Sunde's formula — used in practice):**

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Numerical Validation Example:**

A driven rod of length $L = 3$ m, diameter $d = 50$ mm = 0.05 m, in soil with $\rho = 200$ Ω·m.

$$
R = \dfrac{200}{2\pi (3)}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 3}{0.05}\right) - 1\right]
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{200}{18.85}\left[\ln(480) - 1\right]
$$

$$
R = 10.61 \times \left[6.173 - 1\right]
$$

$$
R = 10.61 \times 5.173 = 54.9\ \Omega
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A single rod of 3 m gives 55 Ω in 200 Ω·m soil — *far above the 10 Ω required for a 66 kV tower*. The student must conclude: **Multiple rods in parallel + counterpoise are essential.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Magnetic Field Around a Long Straight Current-Carrying Conductor

**Starting point — Ampère's Law:**

$$
\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = \mu_0 \, I_{\text{enclosed}}
$$

For a circular Amperian loop of radius $r$ around an infinite straight wire, by symmetry $\vec{B}$ is tangential and uniform on the loop:

$$
B \cdot (2\pi r) = \mu_0 \, I
$$

$$
\boxed{\,B = \dfrac{\mu_0\, I}{2\pi r}\,}
$$

Substituting $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A:

$$
B = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \, I}{r} \quad [\text{T}]
$$

**Example:** A 400 kV line carries 1000 A. Find B at edge of ROW, $r = 23$ m.

$$
B = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000}{23} = 8.7\ \mu\text{T}
$$

This is **well below** the ICNIRP occupational limit of **1000 μT** and public limit of **200 μT** — confirming modern HV lines are generally safe for EMF exposure at the edge of ROW.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Ground Potential Rise (GPR) and Step Potential

When a fault current $I_f$ flows through earth resistance $R_e$:

$$
\text{GPR} = V_{\text{ground}} = I_f \cdot R_e
$$

The voltage decreases with distance from the fault point (logarithmic decay in uniform soil):

$$
V(r) = \dfrac{\rho \, I_f}{2\pi r}
$$

**Step Potential** (between two feet separated by 1 m, at distances $r_1$ and $r_2 = r_1 + 1$):

$$
E_s = V(r_1) - V(r_2) = \dfrac{\rho \, I_f}{2\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_s = \dfrac{\rho \, I_f}{2\pi\, r_1 (r_1+1)}\,}
$$

**Touch Potential** (hand on faulty structure at $r_t = 0$, feet at $r = 1$ m):

$$
E_t = V(0) - V(1) \approx \dfrac{\rho \, I_f}{2\pi \cdot 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_t \approx \dfrac{\rho \, I_f}{2\pi}\,}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Kerala Laterite Soil):**

- Fault current: $I_f = 5000$ A
- Earth resistance: $R_e = 5\ \Omega$
- Soil resistivity: $\rho = 300\ \Omega\cdot$m
- Person standing 3 m from tower foot.

$$
\text{GPR} = 5000 \times 5 = 25{,}000\ \text{V} = 25\ \text{kV}
$$

$$
E_s = \dfrac{300 \times 5000}{2\pi \times 3 \times 4} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{75.4} \approx 19{,}900\ \text{V}
$$

$$
E_t = \dfrac{300 \times 5000}{2\pi \times 1} = 238{,}700\ \text{V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Mark-Loss Alert:** The touch potential is **LETHAL** (>> 100 V safe limit). This is exactly why gravel surfacing + equipotential mesh grids are mandatory in substations — they raise the surface layer resistivity $\rho_s$ and limit dangerous voltage differences.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: E-field vs B-field

| Property | Electric Field (E) | Magnetic Field (B) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source quantity** | Voltage (V) | Current (I) |
| **Exists at no-load?** | **YES** | **No** (I ≈ 0) |
| **Shut down line?** | Still present until earthed | Disappears immediately |
| **Typical magnitude (at edge of 400 kV ROW)** | **3 to 10 kV/m** | **5 to 30 μT** |
| **Standard formula** | $E = V/(r\,\ln(D/r))$ | $B = \mu_0 I / (2\pi r)$ |
| **Shielding** | Easily shielded (Faraday cage) | **Difficult** — passes through most materials |
| **Biological concern** | Direct body current induction | Induced eddy currents in body |
| **Mitigation** | Increase height, use compact towers | Phase arrangement, transpositions |
| **Governed by** | Maxwell–Gauss equation | Maxwell–Ampère equation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Tower Grounding System (Block-Level Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords).
> - Labels contain **no markdown formatting** (no `**bold**`).
> - Pure text uppercase labels for maximum rendering compatibility.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[LIGHTNING STRIKE EVENT] --> B[TOWER STRUCTURE AND SHIELD WIRE]
    B --> C[TOWER FOOTING RESISTANCE R_E]
    C --> D[EARTH ELECTRODE SUBSYSTEM]
    D --> D1[DRIVEN PIPE ROD TYPE]
    D --> D2[BUTT PLATE TYPE]
    D --> D3[COUNTERPOISE TYPE]
    D --> D4[HYBRID PIPE PLUS COUNTERPOISE]
    D1 --> E[SOIL VOLUME DISSIPATION]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[SAFE EQUIPOTENTIAL GROUND PLANE]
    F --> G[STEP POTENTIAL CONTROL]
    F --> H[TOUCH POTENTIAL CONTROL]
    F --> I[GRAVEL SURFACE LAYER]
    G --> J[PERSONNEL SAFETY COMPLIANCE]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    C --> K[FAULT CURRENT MONITORING]
    K --> L[GPR CALCULATION]
    L --> M[IS 3043 AND IEEE 80 VERIFICATION]
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — EMF Generation and Mitigation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    HV[HIGH VOLTAGE TRANSMISSION LINE] --> E1[ELECTRIC FIELD GENERATION]
    HV --> M1[MAGNETIC FIELD GENERATION]
    E1 --> E2[INDUCED VOLTAGE ON NEARBY OBJECTS]
    E1 --> E3[CORONA DISCHARGE AND RADIO INTERFERENCE]
    E1 --> E4[BIOLOGICAL EXPOSURE AT ROW EDGE]
    M1 --> M2[INDUCED CURRENT IN PARALLEL CONDUCTORS]
    M1 --> M3[EDDY CURRENT HEATING IN METAL PIPES]
    M1 --> M4[BIOLOGICAL EXPOSURE AT ROW EDGE]
    E2 --> MIT1[MITIGATION INCREASE TOWER HEIGHT]
    E3 --> MIT2[MITIGATION USE BUNDLED CONDUCTORS AND POLISH]
    M2 --> MIT3[MITIGATION PHASE TRANSPOSITION]
    M4 --> MIT4[MITIGATION ENFORCE ICNIRP 2010 LIMITS]
    MIT1 --> SAFE[COMPLIANCE WITH EMF SAFETY STANDARDS]
    MIT2 --> SAFE
    MIT3 --> SAFE
    MIT4 --> SAFE
    E4 --> MON[HUMAN EXPOSURE MONITORING]
    M3 --> MON
    MON --> SAFE
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Grounding Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Tower Grounding Design] --> Q1{Soil Resistivity?}
    Q1 --> Q1A[Below 100 Ohm m]
    Q1 --> Q1B[100 to 500 Ohm m]
    Q1 --> Q1C[Above 500 Ohm m]
    Q1A --> M1[Single Driven Rod]
    Q1B --> M2[Multiple Rods in Parallel]
    Q1C --> Q2{Bedrock Depth?}
    Q2 --> Q2A[Deep Bedrock]
    Q2 --> Q2B[Shallow Bedrock]
    Q2A --> M3[Long Counterpoise 60 to 120 m]
    Q2B --> M4[Butt Plate in Concrete Foundation]
    Q2A --> M5[Hybrid Pipe plus Counterpoise]
    M1 --> CHECK[Verify R less than 10 Ohm]
    M2 --> CHECK
    M3 --> CHECK
    M4 --> CHECK
    M5 --> CHECK
    CHECK --> CHECK2{R less than target?}
    CHECK2 --> YES[Design Approved]
    CHECK2 --> NO[Add More Electrodes]
    NO --> CHECK
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** When KTU asks "Draw a block diagram of tower grounding system," do NOT attempt to draw the physical tower. Use a **functional block diagram** like the ones above — it earns full marks and avoids drawing errors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is meant by *grounding of line structure*? Why is it essential for transmission towers?

> **Model Answer:**
> Grounding of a line structure is the electrical interconnection of a transmission tower and all its metallic components (cross-arms, insulator hardware, shield wire, body extensions) to the general mass of earth through low-impedance earth electrodes such as driven rods, butt plates, or counterpoise wires.
>
> **Importance (3 reasons to score full marks):**
> 1. Provides a **low-impedance discharge path** for direct lightning strikes hitting the shield wire or tower top.
> 2. Prevents dangerous **flashovers** (back-flashover from tower to insulator string) during surges.
> 3. Keeps **touch and step potentials** at the tower base within safe human-tolerable limits during fault conditions.
>
> *(Word count ≈ 70, ideally for a 3-mark answer)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *Step Potential* and *Touch Potential* with reference to tower grounding.

> **Model Answer (Comparison Table Form — examiner-friendly):**
>
> | Feature | Step Potential | Touch Potential |
> |---|---|---|
> | Definition | Voltage difference between the two feet of a person (1 m apart) on the ground | Voltage difference between a hand touching a faulty structure and the person's feet |
> | Typical formula | $E_s = \dfrac{\rho I_f}{2\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_1+1}\right)$ | $E_t = \dfrac{\rho I_f}{2\pi\, r_t}$ |
> | Severity | Generally less severe | Generally more severe (longer current path through body) |
> | Mitigation | Equipotential grading rings | Surface gravel, insulated gloves, mesh grids |
> | Allowable value (IEEE 80) | Higher | Lower (stricter) |

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — KTU ESE Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **different methods of grounding transmission line towers**. Compare their relative effectiveness in (i) low-resistivity alluvial soil and (ii) rocky high-resistivity soil.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **resistance of a single driven rod** electrode. A 3 m long, 40 mm diameter GI rod is driven into soil of resistivity 250 Ω·m. Calculate its resistance. Comment on whether this is acceptable for a 132 kV tower. **(7 marks)**

> **Complete Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Methods of Grounding:**
>
> 1. **Driven Rod:** GI pipe 40–50 mm dia, 2–3 m long, driven vertically. Resistance $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(8L/d) - 1\right]$. Suitable for soft, deep, uniform soil.
> 2. **Counterpoise:** GI wire buried 0.5–1 m deep, radiating from tower base for 15–60 m. Effective in rocky or shallow-bedrock areas.
> 3. **Butt Plate:** GI plate (600 × 600 × 6 mm) embedded in tower foundation concrete. Provides large soil contact through moist concrete.
> 4. **Pipe-Cum-Counterpoise (Hybrid):** Combination for EHV/UHV towers — best overall performance.
>
> **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Method | Alluvial (ρ < 200 Ω·m) | Rocky (ρ > 1000 Ω·m) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Driven rod | Excellent (R ≈ 20–30 Ω) | Poor (R > 200 Ω) |
> | Counterpoise | Acceptable | Excellent (when long enough) |
> | Butt plate | Good | Moderate |
> | Hybrid | Excellent | Good |

> **(b) Derivation:** (as in Section 3.1 above — full derivation expected)
>
> **Numerical Calculation:**
>
> Given: $L = 3$ m, $d = 0.040$ m, $\rho = 250$ Ω·m
>
> $$
> R = \dfrac{250}{2\pi(3)}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 3}{0.040}\right) - 1\right]
> $$
>
> $$
> R = \dfrac{250}{18.85}\!\left[\ln(600) - 1\right] = 13.26 \times \left[6.397 - 1\right] = 71.6\ \Omega
> $$
>
> **Comment:** For 132 kV tower, the prescribed tower footing resistance is ≤ 5 Ω. Since 71.6 Ω >> 5 Ω, **a single rod is NOT acceptable**. We must use:
> - 4 such rods in parallel at spacing ≥ 6 m → R ≈ 71.6 / 4 ≈ 17.9 Ω (still high)
> - Plus counterpoise wires to bring it below 5 Ω
>
> **Recommended Solution:** Pipe-cum-counterpoise design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **origin and effects of electric and magnetic fields** around high-voltage transmission lines. State the **ICNIRP 2010 reference levels** for public and occupational exposure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV, 50 Hz, three-phase transmission line carries a balanced load of 400 A per phase. The conductors are arranged in a horizontal configuration with spacing of 6 m and a mean height of 12 m above ground. Calculate the **magnetic field strength** at a point on the ground directly below the middle conductor and at the edge of the right-of-way (taken as 20 m from the centre line). Assume the line is transposed. **(7 marks)**

> **Complete Model Solution:**
>
> **(a)** *(As in Section 1.2 and 2.4 above — mention both fields, sources, persistence after switch-off, biological concerns, and standards.)*
>
> **ICNIRP 2010 Limits:**
>
> | Parameter | Public | Occupational |
> |---|---|---|
> | E-field (50 Hz) | 5 kV/m | 10 kV/m |
> | B-field (50 Hz) | 200 μT | 1000 μT |
>
> **Effects:** Induced currents in human body, interference with cardiac pacemakers (below 0.5 mT), video display distortion (above 1 μT), and long-term epidemiological concerns.

> **(b) Numerical Solution:**
>
> Given: $V = 220$ kV, $I = 400$ A, phase spacing = 6 m, mean height = 12 m.
>
> **Step 1 — Position of conductors (above ground):**
> - Phase A: $x_A = -6$ m, $y = 12$ m
> - Phase B: $x_B = 0$ m, $y = 12$ m
> - Phase C: $x_C = +6$ m, $y = 12$ m
>
> **Step 2 — Distance from observation point to each conductor image (using image theory for ground effect):**
> For a horizontal line with height $h$, the image lies at depth $h$ below ground, so effective distance from observation point on ground is $\sqrt{x^2 + (2h)^2}$.
>
> At point below middle conductor (x = 0):
> - $r_A = r_C = \sqrt{6^2 + 24^2} = \sqrt{36 + 576} = \sqrt{612} = 24.74$ m
> - $r_B = 24$ m
>
> **Step 3 — For a balanced 3-phase transposed line, the magnetic field at ground level:**
>
> $$
> B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot I}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{r_A^2} + \dfrac{1}{r_B^2} + \dfrac{1}{r_C^2} - \dfrac{1}{r_A r_B} - \dfrac{1}{r_B r_C}}
> $$
>
> $$
> B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 400}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{612} + \dfrac{1}{576} + \dfrac{1}{612} - \dfrac{1}{24.74 \times 24} - \dfrac{1}{24 \times 24.74}}
> $$
>
> $$
> B = 8 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \sqrt{(0.001634 + 0.001736 + 0.001634 - 0.001685 - 0.001685)}
> $$
>
> $$
> B = 8 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \sqrt{0.001634} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.0404
> $$
>
> $$
> B \approx 3.23 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{T} = 3.23\ \mu\text{T}
> $$
>
> **(At edge of ROW, x = 20 m):** Repeating with $r_A = \sqrt{26^2 + 24^2} = \sqrt{676 + 576} = \sqrt{1252} = 35.38$ m, $r_B = \sqrt{20^2 + 24^2} = 31.24$ m, $r_C = \sqrt{14^2 + 24^2} = 27.78$ m:
>
> $$
> B_{\text{ROW}} \approx 2.18\ \mu\text{T}
> $$
>
> **Conclusion:** Both values are **far below** ICNIRP 2010 public limit of 200 μT. The line is **EMF-safe** at the ROW edge.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing GPR with Touch Potential** — GPR is the *absolute* voltage of the grounded structure with respect to remote earth; Touch potential is the *difference* between structure and a person's feet. They are NOT the same.
> 2. **Forgetting the image-conductor effect** for magnetic field calculations — at ground level, image currents at depth $h$ contribute to the B-field; neglecting this gives ~30 % error.
> 3. **Wrong units in Peek's formula** — Use kV/cm for $E_0$, cm for $r$, cm for $D$. Students mix units and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting $\rho_s$ (surface layer resistivity)** in allowable touch voltage formula — this is the **gravel surfacing** term and is a frequent KTU question.
> 5. **Stating "switching off removes EMF"** — Voltage may still be present on a de-energised line until maintenance earths are applied; magnetic field vanishes but electric field may persist.

---

### Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Grounding** = low-impedance path from tower to earth; target R ≤ 10 Ω for 66 kV, ≤ 5 Ω for 132 kV, ≤ 1–2 Ω for 400 kV+.
- [x] **Methods**: Driven rod, Butt plate, Counterpoise, Hybrid (pipe + counterpoise).
- [x] **Driven rod formula** $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\!\left[\ln(8L/d) - 1\right]$; horizontal strip has extra $\ln(L/\sqrt{hd})$ term.
- [x] **Three safety voltages**: Step < Touch < Mesh; mitigated by gravel + equipotential mesh.
- [x] **GPR** $= I_f R_e$; sets the absolute hazard ceiling; Touch = GPR at human reach.
- [x] **Electric field** $E = V / (r\,\ln(D/r))$ — exists even at no-load, easily shielded.
- [x] **Magnetic field** $B = \mu_0 I / (2\pi r)$ — zero at no-load, hard to shield.
- [x] **3-phase balanced field** uses image-conductor at depth $2h$; reduces field near ground.
- [x] **ICNIRP 2010**: Public 5 kV/m + 200 μT; Occupational 10 kV/m + 1000 μT (50 Hz).
- [x] **Corona** governed by Peek's formula $E_0 = m g_0 \delta r \ln(D/r)$ — power loss ~ 1–2 kW/km for 220 kV.
- [x] **Indian ROW widths**: 46 m (400 kV), 35 m (220 kV), 23 m (110 kV), 15 m (66 kV).
- [x] **Standards**: IS 3043:1987, IEEE Std 80-2013, ICNIRP 2010, CEA (Safety) Regulations 2010.
- [x] **Kerala-specific concern**: Laterite soil + 70+ thunderstorm days → aggressive grounding design + lightning arrester mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Safety Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Human Safety Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Human Safety Aspects in Electrical Engineering?

In the context of **Electrical Technology and Safety (PCSFT403)**, *Human Safety Aspects* refer to the systematic study of how electric current interacts with the human body, the physiological effects produced, the conditions that aggravate or mitigate those effects, and the engineering safeguards (earthing, insulation, protective devices, safe work practices) designed to prevent injury or death.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 3 Anchor):** *Human Safety Aspects* is the discipline that analyses the **electrical hazards to living beings** — primarily electric shock, burns, arc flash, and indirect secondary injuries (falls, muscle contraction-induced trauma) — and prescribes the **threshold limits, protective schemes, and first-aid protocols** to keep humans safe in the vicinity of energized electrical systems.

The governing principle can be expressed by the classical electro-physiological relation:

$$I_{body} = \frac{V_{touch}}{Z_{body}}$$

where a small denominator (wet skin, broken skin) and a large numerator (line voltage, induced voltage) combine to produce fatal currents.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Body as a Resistor in a Water Circuit"

Imagine your body as a **sponge sitting in a bathtub of water**.

- The **water pressure** at the tap is the *voltage* of the electrical source.
- The **diameter of the pipe** connecting the sponge to the tap is the *resistance* — a wide pipe (low resistance, e.g., wet/broken skin) lets a lot of current through; a narrow pipe (high resistance, e.g., dry calloused skin) restricts it.
- The **actual flow through the sponge** is the *current* through your body.
- A tiny trickle of water (1 mA) is just a tingle; a fire-hose jet (>100 mA) tears the sponge apart.

The key engineering insight is this: **voltage alone does not kill — current does.** And because the body is a *resistor* (with non-linear, voltage-dependent resistance), small reductions in voltage or small additions of series resistance (gloves, shoes, mat) can move you from the *fibrillation* zone into the *safe* zone.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Constants (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Conventional safe AC voltage limit (dry skin)** | **< 50 V AC** |
| **Conventional safe DC voltage limit (dry skin)** | **< 120 V DC** |
| Threshold of perception | ~**1 mA** |
| Maximum let-go current (adult male) | ~**10 mA AC**, **50 mA DC** |
| Ventricular fibrillation threshold | ~**100 mA AC for > 0.2 s** |
| Body resistance (dry skin) | ~**100 000 Ω** |
| Body resistance (wet skin) | ~**1 000 Ω** |
| Internal body resistance (skin bypassed) | ~**300 Ω** |
| Standard mains frequency (India) | **50 Hz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Line (Dec 2022/2023 pattern):** *"It is the **current** and the **duration**, not the voltage, that determine the severity of an electric shock."* Memorise this — it appears in nearly every KTU answer script.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Influencing the Severity of Electric Shock on the Human Body

The physiological damage produced by an electric shock is governed by **five primary factors**, all of which are testable in KTU examinations.

**Factor 1 — Magnitude of Current (I)**
- Governed by Ohm's law: $I = \dfrac{V}{R_{body}}$
- Below **1 mA**: imperceptible.
- **1–5 mA**: perception threshold; mild tingling.
- **5–15 mA**: muscular control starts to be affected.
- **15–50 mA** ("**let-go threshold**"): involuntary muscle contraction; victim cannot release the conductor.
- **50–100 mA**: extreme pain, breathing difficulty, possible temporary paralysis.
- **>100 mA for > 200 ms**: **ventricular fibrillation** — chaotic, uncoordinated ventricular contraction that is often fatal without defibrillation.
- **> 5 A**: severe burns, sustained myocardial contraction, possible internal organ damage.

**Factor 2 — Path of Current through the Body**
- Current from **hand to hand** passes through the heart and lungs → highest fatality.
- Current from **hand to foot** also crosses the heart.
- Current from **foot to foot** is generally less dangerous (no direct heart path), but induces loss of balance and secondary fall injuries.

**Factor 3 — Duration of Contact (t)**
- A short, sharp shock at 100 mA may be survivable; the same current sustained for several seconds is almost always fatal.
- The empirical **electro-physiological damage relation** is:
$$E_{damage} \propto I^{2} \cdot t$$
- i.e., the energy delivered (I²R × t) determines burn and tissue damage.

**Factor 4 — Frequency of Supply (f)**
- **50/60 Hz** is the most dangerous range (close to the natural resonance of biological tissue).
- **DC** requires roughly 2× the magnitude of AC to produce the same physiological effect (let-go ~2×, fibrillation ~3–4×).
- **High frequency (> 100 kHz)**: produces only surface heating (used in diathermy/medical equipment) — very low fibrillation risk.

**Factor 5 — Resistance of the Body (R)**
- R is **not constant** — it varies with:
  - Skin condition (dry, wet, broken, sweating, oily).
  - Contact area (larger area → lower R).
  - Applied voltage (higher V breaks down the skin's dielectric → R drops).
- Two-resistor body model: $R_{body} = R_{skin(in)} + R_{internal} + R_{skin(out)}$
- $R_{internal} \approx 300\ \Omega$ (blood + muscle, mostly resistive).
- $R_{skin} \approx 10^{4}$ to $10^{5}\ \Omega$ when dry; collapses to a few hundred ohms when wet or punctured.

### 2.2 The Three Hazardous Voltage Forms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU asks this almost every year: classify hazardous voltages into **touch voltage, step voltage, and induced/ transferred voltage**. Get this table cold.

| Hazard Type | Definition | When It Occurs |
|---|---|---|
| **Touch Voltage ($V_{touch}$)** | Voltage between an energized object and the feet of a person in contact with it. | Standing near a faulty appliance, hand on chassis. |
| **Step Voltage ($V_{step}$)** | Voltage between the feet of a person standing near an energized ground point. | Walking near a downed conductor or earthed fault. |
| **Transferred (Induced) Voltage** | Voltage transferred from a primary circuit to a secondary, de-energized circuit (e.g., via capacitive/inductive coupling, or via a separate earth reference). | Telecom line parallel to HV line, neutral-to-earth rise. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}}$ | Current through body via Ohm's law | Almost every shock problem |
| 2 | $R_{body} = 2 R_{skin} + R_{internal}$ | Two-skin + internal resistance model | Body resistance problems |
| 3 | $V_{step} = E \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{d_{step}}{r}$ | Simplified step voltage in soil (E = step potential gradient) | Earthing design |
| 4 | $E_{damage} = I^{2} R_{body}\, t$ | Energy dissipated in body (J) | Burn/severity problems |
| 5 | $V_{safe} < 50\ \text{V AC}$, $< 120\ \text{V DC}$ | IEC 60479 / IS 732 safety limit | Defining SELV/PELV |
| 6 | $t_{fuse} \le \dfrac{I_{fibrillate}^{2}}{I_{actual}^{2}} \cdot 0.2\ \text{s}$ | Empirically adjusted fuse clearing time | Coordination with shock limits |
| 7 | $I_{let\text{-}go,\,AC} \approx 10\ \text{mA}$ | Adult male let-go threshold | Standard reference value |
| 8 | $R_{earth} \le \dfrac{V_{touch(max)}}{I_{trip}}$ | Maximum permissible earth resistance for protection coordination | Earth electrode design |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX rule applied in the table above:** All "absolute-value / magnitude" bars (e.g., in $\vert V \vert$) are written using `\vert` or `\mid` — never the bare vertical pipe `|` — so the markdown table parser is not corrupted.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The study of human safety aspects is not academic — it directly drives:

- **IEC 60664 / IS 732** — insulation coordination and clearance distances.
- **IEC 60479** — current-time-effect curves on humans and livestock.
- **IE Rules 1956 (India)** — statutory limits for earthing resistance (e.g., < 1 Ω for HT substations, < 5 Ω for major plants).
- **NFPA 70E / IEEE 1584** — arc-flash energy calculations and PPE category selection.
- **Product design** — leakage-current limits in medical equipment (IEC 60601-1: < 10 µA patient auxiliary current).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Current Through a Human Body (Two-Skin + Internal Resistance Model)

The human body, for shock-current analysis, is modelled as a **series combination of three resistors**: the skin at the entry point, the internal tissues (blood + muscle), and the skin at the exit point.

**Step 1 — Define the model resistors**

Let:
- $R_{s,in}$ = resistance of the skin at the entry contact
- $R_{int}$ = resistance of the internal path (blood, muscle, organs) ≈ **300 Ω**
- $R_{s,out}$ = resistance of the skin at the exit contact

**Step 2 — Apply the series-resistance rule**

Because the same current flows through all three elements (Kirchhoff's Current Law at the contact nodes), the total body resistance is the algebraic sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{body} &= R_{s,in} + R_{int} + R_{s,out}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Specialize to the "skin bypassed" case (worst case)**

In the worst credible shock scenario — wet, broken, or punctured skin — the skin resistance drops to a negligible value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{s,in} &\rightarrow 0 \\
R_{s,out} &\rightarrow 0 \\
\therefore\ R_{body,\,min} &= R_{int} \approx 300\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ohm's law for body current**

The body current under a touch voltage $V_{touch}$ is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{body} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}} \\
I_{body,\,max} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{int}} = \frac{V_{touch}}{300}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU 14-mark standard)**

> A worker touches a **230 V AC** live conductor with **wet hands**. Each skin contact is approximately **500 Ω**, and the internal body resistance is **300 Ω**. Calculate (a) the body current, and (b) whether the shock is in the fibrillation zone. Assume the person cannot let go for **2 seconds**.

*Solution:*

(a) Total body resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{body} &= R_{s,in} + R_{int} + R_{s,out} \\
&= 500 + 300 + 500 \\
&= 1\,300\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Body current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{body} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}} = \frac{230}{1\,300} \\
&\approx 0.1769\ \text{A} = 176.9\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) Comparison with the fibrillation threshold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{body} &= 176.9\ \text{mA} \;>\; I_{fibrillate} = 100\ \text{mA} \\
\therefore\ &\text{Ventricular fibrillation is LIKELY. The case is fatal.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Dissipated in the Human Body

The thermal and electro-chemical damage to tissue is proportional to the **Joule heating energy** delivered:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{damage} &= I^{2}\, R_{body}\, t \quad \text{[Joules]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Continuing the worked example with $t = 2$ s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{damage} &= (0.1769)^{2} \times 1\,300 \times 2 \\
&= 0.03129 \times 1\,300 \times 2 \\
&= 81.36\ \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 81 J is roughly the kinetic energy of a 1 kg object dropped from 8 m — an intuitive sense of how much "destruction" this small electrical current can unleash inside the body.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Current vs Physiological Effect (AC 50 Hz, hand-to-hand path)

| Current Range | Physiological Effect | KTU-Required Classification |
|---|---|---|
| **0 – 1 mA** | No sensation | Below perception threshold |
| **1 – 5 mA** | Slight tingling | Perception |
| **5 – 15 mA** | Mild shock, involuntary muscle movement | Minor shock |
| **15 – 50 mA** | Cannot let go; muscle freeze ("**let-go threshold**") | Dangerous |
| **50 – 100 mA** | Severe pain, breathing difficulty | Very dangerous |
| **100 mA – 5 A (sustained > 0.2 s)** | **Ventricular fibrillation** | Lethal |
| **> 5 A** | Sustained cardiac contraction, severe burns, organ damage | Lethal / severe trauma |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Mark-Loss Trap:** Many students write "*higher voltage = more dangerous*". This is incomplete. The KTU answer key requires: *"The **current**, the **path**, and the **duration** — not the voltage alone — determine fatality. Higher voltage is more dangerous *only because* it forces more current through a non-linear body resistance.*"

### 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Permissible Earth Resistance

For a protective device (RCD/MCB/ELCB) rated to trip at $I_{trip}$ in time $t \le 0.2$ s, and with a maximum permissible touch voltage $V_{touch,\,max}$ (typically **50 V AC** in dry conditions, **25 V AC** in wet/agricultural premises per IS 732), the **maximum permissible earth resistance** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{earth,\,max} &= \frac{V_{touch,\,max}}{I_{trip}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked example:** A 30 mA RCD in a domestic bathroom. Permissible touch voltage in wet conditions = **25 V AC**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{earth,\,max} &= \frac{25}{0.030} \\
&= 833.33\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Practical installations target a much lower value (**< 5 Ω** for sub-stations, **< 1 Ω** for HT towers) to leave a wide safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of Human Shock Pathway

The following diagram shows the **functional flow** of how a hazardous voltage becomes a physiological effect on the body, and where engineering safeguards intercept the chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Energized Conductor or Fault] --> B[Touch Contact: Hand / Skin]
    B --> C{Contact Condition}
    C -- Dry skin --> D1[R_body approx 100 000 ohm]
    C -- Wet skin --> D2[R_body approx 1 000 ohm]
    C -- Broken skin --> D3[R_body approx 300 ohm]
    D1 --> E[Current I = V / R_body]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Path through Body]
    F --> G1[Hand to Hand: crosses heart]
    F --> G2[Hand to Foot: crosses heart]
    F --> G3[Foot to Foot: less cardiac risk]
    G1 --> H[Physiological Effect]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I1[1 mA: Perception]
    H --> I2[10 mA: Let-go threshold]
    H --> I3[100 mA: Ventricular Fibrillation]
    H --> I4[5 A: Burns and organ damage]
    I1 --> J[Safeguards]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K1[Earthing and Grounding]
    J --> K2[Double Insulation]
    J --> K3[RCD / ELCB / MCB]
    J --> K4[SELV: Less than 50 V AC]
    J --> K5[PPE: Gloves, Mats, Shoes]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Layered Protection Model (Defence-in-Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1: Insulation and Enclosure] --> L2[Layer 2: Safe Design and SELV]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Earthing and Bonding]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Overcurrent Protection MCB]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Residual Current Device RCD]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: PPE and Training]
    L6 --> L7[SURVIVAL OF THE OPERATOR]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — First-Aid Decision Tree for Electric Shock

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Electric Shock Incident] --> S1{Is source still energized?}
    S1 -- YES --> S2[DO NOT touch victim directly]
    S2 --> S3[Switch off MCB or remove plug]
    S1 -- NO --> S4[Is victim conscious?]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 -- YES --> S5[Keep victim warm, monitor pulse, call doctor]
    S4 -- NO --> S6[Check breathing and pulse]
    S6 -- NO --> S7[Begin CPR immediately]
    S6 -- YES --> S8[Recovery position, call ambulance]
    S7 --> S9[Continue CPR until medical help arrives]
    S8 --> S9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is meant by the "let-go current"? State its typical value for an adult male at 50 Hz AC.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **let-go current** is the magnitude of electric current through the human body above which the victim loses voluntary muscular control and **cannot release** the conductor he/she has grasped, due to sustained involuntary contraction of the flexor muscles.
> For an adult male at **50 Hz AC**, the typical let-go current is **≈ 10 mA**.
> *(1 mark definition, 1 mark physiological cause, 1 mark value)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]** *Differentiate between "touch voltage" and "step voltage" with one example each.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | Touch Voltage | Step Voltage |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Voltage between an energized object (e.g., a faulty appliance chassis) and the feet of a person in contact with it. | Voltage between the two feet of a person standing on the ground near an energized earth point (e.g., a downed conductor). |
> | **Hazard path** | Hand-to-foot through heart. | Foot-to-foot, lower cardiac risk but causes loss of balance. |
> | **Example** | Touching a washing-machine cabinet with a Line–Earth fault. | Walking near a broken HT line lying on the ground. |
> *(1 mark each for definition, hazard path, example.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer (14 marks, with internal choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]**

(a) **Explain the various factors that affect the severity of an electric shock on the human body.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **A person with wet hands accidentally touches a 230 V AC live wire. If the contact resistance at each hand is 400 Ω and the internal body resistance is 300 Ω, calculate the current through the body and the energy dissipated in 3 seconds. Comment on the safety of the victim.** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Factors affecting severity (7 marks):**

1. **Magnitude of current (I)** — the single most critical factor. Governed by Ohm's law, $I = V/R_{body}$. The current determines the physiological zone:
   - 1 mA: perception;
   - 10 mA: let-go threshold;
   - 100 mA: ventricular fibrillation threshold.

2. **Path of current** — hand-to-hand and hand-to-left-foot paths cross the heart, and are the most lethal. Foot-to-foot is comparatively less fatal (no direct cardiac path) but causes secondary injuries (falls).

3. **Duration of contact (t)** — energy dissipated $E = I^2 R t$. Even a small current sustained for long periods causes burns. KTU rule: 100 mA for **> 0.2 s** is fibrillation-prone.

4. **Frequency of supply** — 50/60 Hz is the worst range. DC requires ~2× the current for the same effect; very high frequencies (> 100 kHz) produce only surface heating and are used medically (diathermy).

5. **Body resistance (R)** — non-linear and voltage-dependent. Dry skin ≈ 10⁵ Ω; wet skin ≈ 10³ Ω; punctured skin ≈ 300 Ω. Higher voltage breaks down skin, reducing R and increasing I.

6. **Physical condition of the victim** — health, body mass, cardiac condition, fatigue, and presence of moisture all amplify the risk.

*(1 mark for naming each factor, 0.5 mark for physiological effect, 0.5 mark for example/value. Distribute 7 marks across the six factors.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given:
- $V = 230\ \text{V AC}$
- $R_{s,in} = R_{s,out} = 400\ \Omega$
- $R_{int} = 300\ \Omega$
- $t = 3\ \text{s}$

Step 1 — Total body resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{body} &= R_{s,in} + R_{int} + R_{s,out} \\
&= 400 + 300 + 400 \\
&= 1\,100\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Body current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{V}{R_{body}} = \frac{230}{1\,100} \\
&\approx 0.209\ \text{A} = 209\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Energy dissipated in 3 s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= I^{2}\, R_{body}\, t \\
&= (0.209)^{2} \times 1\,100 \times 3 \\
&= 0.0437 \times 1\,100 \times 3 \\
&\approx 144.2\ \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Safety comment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 209\ \text{mA} \;>\; 100\ \text{mA}\ \text{(fibrillation threshold)} \\
\therefore\ &\text{Victim is in the LETHAL zone. Immediate CPR / defibrillation required.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark total R, 1 mark current, 1 mark energy, 1 mark safety conclusion, rest for stepwise clarity.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model / Dec 2022 Pattern]**

(a) **With a neat sketch, explain the human-body electrical model. Show how the total body resistance is computed in dry and wet conditions.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **Define step voltage. A sub-station earth grid has a step-potential gradient of 5 kV/m. Compute the step voltage for a worker whose stride length is 0.8 m, and state whether the worker is safe, given that the safe step-voltage limit is 50 V AC.** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Human-body model (7 marks):**

The human body is modelled as a **series combination** of three resistive elements:

- $R_{s1}$: skin resistance at the entry contact (hand or foot).
- $R_{int}$: internal resistance of the body — blood, muscle, organs (≈ 300 Ω).
- $R_{s2}$: skin resistance at the exit contact.

```
   Hand (Entry)              Internal Body            Foot (Exit)
   ─────┬──────          ──────────────────         ─────┬──────
        │                                              │
       ( ) R_s1         ( ) R_int ≈ 300 Ω            ( ) R_s2
        │                                              │
   ─────┴──────          ──────────────────         ─────┴──────
       Source                                       Earth return
```

Total body resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{body} = R_{s1} + R_{int} + R_{s2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dry condition:** Each skin resistance may be $10^4$ to $10^5\ \Omega$ → $R_{body} \approx 100\ 000\ \Omega$ → safe.

**Wet condition:** Skin resistance drops to ~1 000 Ω → $R_{body} \approx 1\ 600\ \Omega$ → dangerous.

**Skin bypassed (worst case):** $R_{body} \to R_{int} \approx 300\ \Omega$ → lethal currents easily reached.

*(2 marks diagram, 2 marks formula, 2 marks dry case, 1 mark wet case.)*

**(b) Step voltage problem (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Step voltage is the voltage between the feet of a person standing on the ground in the vicinity of an energized earthed conductor, separated by one step length (conventionally 1 m).*

Given:
- Step potential gradient: $E = 5\ \text{kV/m} = 5\,000\ \text{V/m}$
- Stride length: $d_{step} = 0.8\ \text{m}$

Step voltage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{step} &= E \times d_{step} \\
&= 5\,000 \times 0.8 \\
&= 4\,000\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Safety comparison:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{step} = 4\,000\ \text{V} &\;\gg\; V_{safe} = 50\ \text{V AC} \\
\therefore\ &\text{The worker is in EXTREME DANGER. The earth grid is inadequately designed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design corrective action (1 mark):** Reduce the step-potential gradient by:
- increasing the number of earth rods,
- adding a high-resistivity surface layer (gravel / crushed rock) of resistivity $\rho_{s} > 3\,000\ \Omega\text{-m}$ over the earth grid.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark comparison, 1 mark verdict, 1 mark remedy.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. Writing "voltage kills" — wrong; current kills. Voltage is the *cause*, current is the *agent*.
> 2. Forgetting units on $R_{body}$ and $I_{body}$ — instant 0.5 mark deduction in numericals.
> 3. Using $R_{body} = 1\,000\ \Omega$ as a *constant* for all problems — wrong; it depends on skin condition, contact area, and applied voltage.
> 4. Conflating "let-go" with "perception" — these are different thresholds; percept ≈ 1 mA, let-go ≈ 10 mA.
> 5. Not stating the contact **duration** while commenting on fibrillation — a current of 100 mA is safe if cleared in 10 ms, lethal if sustained for 2 s.
> 6. Step-voltage derivations without the unit $\text{V/m}$ for the potential gradient — full mark lost on the definition.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **It is the current, not the voltage, that kills.** Duration ≥ 0.2 s at 100 mA AC causes ventricular fibrillation.
- **Let-go current** (50 Hz, adult male) ≈ **10 mA**; perception threshold ≈ **1 mA**.
- **Body resistance model:** $R_{body} = 2 R_{skin} + R_{int}$, with $R_{int} \approx 300\ \Omega$.
- **Dry skin** ≈ 100 kΩ; **wet skin** ≈ 1 kΩ; **skin bypassed** ≈ 300 Ω.
- **Touch voltage** = hand-to-object-to-foot voltage; **step voltage** = foot-to-foot voltage near a ground fault.
- **Frequency effect:** 50/60 Hz is the most dangerous; DC is ~2× safer; HF (> 100 kHz) causes only surface heating.
- **Energy in body:** $E = I^{2} R_{body}\, t$ — used for burn-damage analysis.
- **Safe limits (IEC 60479 / IS 732):** < **50 V AC**, < **120 V DC** in dry conditions; **25 V AC** in wet premises.
- **Protective measures (in order of importance):** insulation, enclosure, earthing, MCB/fuse, RCD/ELCB, SELV, PPE.
- **Maximum earth resistance:** $R_{earth,\,max} = V_{touch,\,max} / I_{trip}$ — used to verify protective-device coordination.
- **First aid:** Switch off supply first → check breathing/pulse → CPR if no pulse → call ambulance. **Never touch a still-energized victim.**
- **KTU Viva/Interview favourites:** (i) Why is 50 Hz the most dangerous frequency? (ii) Why does a bird on a single wire not get shocked? (iii) Why do we use AC for distribution in spite of DC's shock safety? (iv) How does an RCD detect a human shock current?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Current and Voltage on Human Beings. Electrical Accident - Safety Precaution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔌 MODULE 3: EFFECT OF CURRENT & VOLTAGE ON HUMAN BEINGS, ELECTRICAL ACCIDENTS & SAFETY PRECAUTIONS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Shock** is the physiological response or injury sustained by the human body when an electric current passes through it. The severity depends primarily on the **magnitude of current**, **path of current**, **duration of exposure**, and **frequency**, rather than the voltage alone.

**Effect of Current on Human Body** refers to the various physiological reactions (perception, muscular contraction, ventricular fibrillation, burns, and death) produced in the human body when electric current of different magnitudes flows through it.

**Effect of Voltage on Human Body** refers to how the magnitude of voltage (along with body resistance) determines the current that flows through the body via Ohm's Law:

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{body}}$$

**Electrical Accident** is any unexpected, undesirable event caused directly or indirectly by electric current, resulting in injury, burns, fatality, or property damage.

**Safety Precaution** refers to a set of engineered controls, procedures, PPE (Personal Protective Equipment), and administrative measures designed to prevent electrical accidents and protect human life.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 💡 **Think of electricity flowing through the body like water flowing through a pipe:**

| Element | Analogy | Real Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage** | Water pressure in the pipe | The "push" that drives current |
| **Current** | Actual water flow rate | The real danger (causes damage) |
| **Body Resistance** | Pipe narrowness | Higher resistance = less current |
| **Current Path** | Direction of water flow | Hand-to-hand crosses the heart |
| **Duration** | How long water flows | Even small currents kill if held long |

> **Plain English Explanation:** Imagine your body is a piece of meat (high water and salt content = conductive). When voltage is applied, current flows through your tissues, muscles, nerves, and heart. The current interferes with the body's natural electrical signals (the heart uses ~1 mA, the brain uses micro-ampere-level signals). So even a small external current can scramble these critical signals, leading to muscle lock, heart fibrillation, or death.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Typical Human Body Resistance (dry skin):** $\mathbf{100{,}000\ \Omega}$
- **Typical Human Body Resistance (wet skin):** $\mathbf{1{,}000\ \Omega}$
- **Threshold of Perception (AC 50 Hz):** $\mathbf{\sim 1\ mA}$
- **Let-Go Threshold (AC 50 Hz, men):** $\mathbf{\sim 10\ mA}$
- **Let-Go Threshold (DC):** $\mathbf{\sim 30\ mA}$
- **Ventricular Fibrillation Threshold (AC):** $\mathbf{100\ mA - 5\ A}$
- **Standard Mains Frequency (India):** $\mathbf{50\ Hz}$
- **Standard Touch Voltage Limit (IEC 60990):** $\mathbf{50\ V\ AC}$

### 1.4 Fundamental Insight: Current vs. Voltage — What Really Kills?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ⚠️ **Critical Misconception:** "High voltage is dangerous, low voltage is safe" — **This is FALSE**.
> - A **12 V battery** with **wet hands** (R = 1000 Ω) gives I = 12/1000 = **12 mA** → already above the let-go threshold!
> - A **230 V supply** through dry skin (R = 100,000 Ω) gives I = 230/100,000 = **2.3 mA** → only a tingle!
> - **CURRENT (in mA) is the killer, not voltage.** Voltage is merely the driving force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic: What Happens When Current Flows Through the Body?

### Step 1: Current Enters the Body
When a person touches a live conductor, current enters at the **contact point** (typically a hand), travels through the body tissues (which contain ions and water — making them conductive), and exits at another contact point (other hand, feet, etc.).

### Step 2: Current Distribution
The current does **not** take one single path — it distributes across all tissues inversely proportional to their resistance. However, it predominantly follows the **path of least resistance**. Critical organs in the path (heart, brain, lungs) suffer the most damage.

### Step 3: Physiological Effects on Muscles
- Currents as low as **10 mA** cause the muscles to involuntarily contract.
- This is called **"muscular tetanization"** — the hand "freezes" onto the conductor.
- The person cannot voluntarily release the wire — this is the **Let-Go Threshold**.

### Step 4: Effect on the Heart (Most Critical)
- The heart's natural pacemaker uses electrical signals (~1 mA, ~1 Hz).
- External AC current (50 Hz) at **100 mA+** for >0.2 seconds can disrupt the heart's rhythm.
- This causes **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)** — the heart quivers instead of pumping → **death within minutes** if not defibrillated.

### Step 5: Burns and Tissue Damage
- Power dissipated in the body: $P = I^2 \times R$
- Heat generated: $H = I^2 R t$ (Joule's Law)
- This causes internal burns along the current path and severe contact burns at entry/exit points (called **"Joule Burns"** or **"Electrical Burns"**).

### Step 6: Secondary Injuries
- A person shocked while on a ladder may fall → **secondary trauma**.
- Violent muscle contraction may cause **dislocations** or **bone fractures**.

## 2.2 Physiological Effects of Current Magnitude (50 Hz AC)

| Current Magnitude | Physiological Effect | Sensation |
|---|---|---|
| **< 1 mA** | Below threshold of perception | No sensation |
| **1 mA** | Threshold of perception | Mild tingling |
| **1–5 mA** | Perception increases | Slight shock, finger tremor |
| **5–15 mA** | Mild shock, involuntary muscle action | Painful shock, can let go |
| **15–50 mA** | Muscular tetanization | **Cannot let go** — painful |
| **50–100 mA** | Severe tetanization, breathing difficulty | Respiratory muscle paralysis |
| **100 mA – 5 A** | **Ventricular fibrillation** | **Potentially FATAL** |
| **> 5 A** | Heart paralysis (sustained contraction) | Severe burns, fatal if sustained |
| **> 15 A** | Severe burns, tissue destruction | Internal organs charred |

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Exam-Critical)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{V}{R_{body}}$ | Ohm's law for body current | I in A, V in V, R in Ω |
| 2 | $P = I^2 R_{body}$ | Power dissipated in body | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $H = I^2 R_{body}\, t$ | Heat generated in body (Joule heating) | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $V_{touch} \leq 50\ V$ (AC) | Safe touch voltage limit (IEC 60990) | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $I_{let\text{-}go} = 10\ mA$ (AC) | Let-go threshold for adult male | mA |
| 6 | $I_{VF} \geq 100\ mA$ | Ventricular fibrillation threshold | mA |
| 7 | $R_{body} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ (wet) | Wet skin resistance | Ω |
| 8 | $R_{body} = 100{,}000\ \Omega$ (dry) | Dry skin resistance | Ω |
| 9 | $E_{fibrillation} = I \cdot t = $ const. | Energy required for VF (Dalziel's law) | A·s |
| 10 | $I_{trip} \leq 30\ mA$ | RCD/ELCB trip current (livesaving) | mA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 📌 **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` for absolute values in all formulas to avoid breaking markdown tables (as per V10 protocol). Example: Use $R_{body} \mid_{wet} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ — never use raw `|` inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RCD/ELCB Design:** Earth Leakage Circuit Breakers are designed to trip at **30 mA** to prevent VF.
- **Substation Safety:** Safe approach distances (e.g., 1 m for 11 kV) prevent arc flash.
- **Medical Equipment:** Patient auxiliary currents are limited to **10 µA** (CF-type devices).
- **Earthing Design:** Reduces touch voltage to safe levels (≤ 50 V).
- **Insulation Coordination:** Double insulation in Class II appliances prevents shock.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: Body Current Under Different Conditions

### Problem Statement:
A worker touches a **230 V AC** live wire. Calculate the current flowing through his body under:
- (a) Dry skin condition
- (b) Wet skin (sweaty) condition
- (c) Wet skin with a cut wound (R = 500 Ω)

Comment on the severity in each case.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- Voltage applied, $V = 230\ V$
- Dry body resistance, $R_{dry} = 100{,}000\ \Omega$
- Wet body resistance, $R_{wet} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$
- Cut-wound resistance, $R_{cut} = 500\ \Omega$

**Step 1: Recall Ohm's Law**

The current through the body is given by:

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{body}}$$

This is a direct application of Ohm's Law, where the human body acts as a non-linear resistor (resistance decreases with voltage and breaks down at high V).

**Step 2: Case (a) — Dry Skin**

Substitute the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{dry} &= \frac{V}{R_{dry}} \\
&= \frac{230}{100{,}000} \\
&= 2.3 \times 10^{-3}\ A \\
&= 2.3\ mA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret Result (a)**

$2.3\ mA$ is **above the threshold of perception (1 mA)** but **well below the let-go threshold (10 mA)**. The worker will feel a definite tingle/shock but can release the wire.

**Step 4: Case (b) — Wet/Sweaty Skin**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{wet} &= \frac{V}{R_{wet}} \\
&= \frac{230}{1{,}000} \\
&= 0.23\ A \\
&= 230\ mA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret Result (b)**

$230\ mA$ is **well above the ventricular fibrillation threshold (100 mA)**. This is potentially **FATAL** within seconds. Muscular tetanization will lock the hand onto the wire.

**Step 6: Case (c) — Cut Wound**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{cut} &= \frac{V}{R_{cut}} \\
&= \frac{230}{500} \\
&= 0.46\ A \\
&= 460\ mA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Interpret Result (c)**

$460\ mA$ is **catastrophically high** — over 4× the VF threshold. Causes severe burns, immediate VF, and almost certain death within 1–2 seconds.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Heat Energy Dissipated in the Body

### Problem Statement:
A person receives a current of $I = 100\ mA$ through a body resistance of $R = 2{,}000\ \Omega$ for a duration of $t = 1\ s$. Calculate the heat dissipated and the resulting temperature rise if 1 kg of tissue is involved (specific heat $c = 3{,}500\ J/kg\cdot K$).

### Solution:

**Given:**
- Current, $I = 100\ mA = 0.1\ A$
- Resistance, $R = 2{,}000\ \Omega$
- Time, $t = 1\ s$
- Mass of tissue, $m = 1\ kg$
- Specific heat, $c = 3{,}500\ J/kg\cdot K$

**Step 1: Apply Joule's Law of Heating**

The heat energy dissipated in a resistive medium is:

$$H = I^2 R t$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= (0.1)^2 \times 2{,}000 \times 1 \\
H &= 0.01 \times 2{,}000 \\
H &= 20\ J
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Heat Equation**

The temperature rise is given by:

$$\Delta T = \frac{H}{m \cdot c}$$

**Step 4: Substitute to Find ΔT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{20}{1 \times 3{,}500} \\
\Delta T &= 5.71 \times 10^{-3}\ K \\
\Delta T &\approx 0.0057\ ^\circ C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the Result**

Although the temperature rise in tissue is small, the **localized heating at contact points** (entry/exit wounds) and along the current path is intense due to high current density. This causes **Joule burns** — sometimes called "exit wounds" because the current is forced to converge at the small contact area.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Safe Touch Voltage (IEC 60990)

### Derivation Logic:
The IEC standard assumes a body resistance of $R = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ (worst case wet condition) and a let-go threshold current of $I_{max} = 50\ mA$. Therefore, the maximum safe touch voltage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{touch,max} &= I_{max} \times R_{body} \\
V_{touch,max} &= 0.05 \times 1{,}000 \\
V_{touch,max} &= 50\ V\ (AC)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why **50 V AC** is the universally accepted threshold for "Extra-Low Voltage" (ELV) in safety standards.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: AC vs. DC Effects

| Parameter | AC (50 Hz) | DC |
|---|---|---|
| **Threshold of Perception** | ~1 mA | ~5 mA |
| **Let-Go Threshold** | 10 mA | 30 mA |
| **Ventricular Fibrillation** | 100 mA (high risk) | 300 mA (less likely) |
| **Muscular Tetanization** | Severe (sustained) | Single convulsion |
| **"Let-Go" Sensation** | Cannot release | Repulsive — usually releases |
| **Burns** | Internal & external | Severe at contact points |
| **Rescue** | De-energize fast | De-energize fast |

> [!NOTE]
> 📌 **Why is AC more dangerous at 50 Hz?** At 50 Hz, the current reverses direction 100 times per second. This matches the natural firing rate of certain nerves, causing them to fire continuously → sustained tetanization. DC is more "pushy" and usually causes a single convulsion that throws the person away.

## 3.5 AC Frequency Effects on Human Body

| Frequency | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|
| **DC (0 Hz)** | Single convulsion, heating |
| **10–100 Hz** | **Maximum danger zone** (includes 50 Hz mains) |
| **1–10 kHz** | Reduced tetanization but burns increase |
| **> 100 kHz** | Surface heating only, minimal shock |
| **RF/Microwave** | Diathermy (medical use) — heating only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ✅ **Key Takeaway:** The 50/60 Hz mains frequency is in the **most dangerous band** of the electromagnetic spectrum for human exposure. This is why mains electricity causes so many fatalities.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define "Let-Go Threshold" current. State its typical value for a 50 Hz AC supply.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The Let-Go Threshold is the maximum value of current at which a person holding a live conductor can still voluntarily release the conductor using muscles directly stimulated by the current.
- **Typical Value (1 Mark):** For 50 Hz AC, the let-go threshold is approximately **10 mA for adult males** and about **5–7 mA for adult females**.
- **Effect (0.5 Mark):** Above this value, muscular tetanization occurs, and the person cannot release the conductor, leading to prolonged exposure and increased risk of fatality.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Briefly explain why AC at 50 Hz is considered more dangerous than DC at the same voltage level.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Frequency Effect (1.5 Marks):** At 50 Hz, the alternating current reverses direction 100 times per second. This frequency closely matches the natural firing rate of certain human nerves, causing sustained muscular contraction (tetanization).
- **Muscle Response (1 Mark):** In DC, the body experiences a single convulsion and the person is usually repelled, allowing release. In AC at 50 Hz, muscles lock and the person cannot let go.
- **Let-Go Comparison (0.5 Mark):** Let-go threshold for AC is 10 mA, while for DC it is 30 mA — confirming AC is 3× more dangerous at lower currents.

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — KTU ESE INTERNAL CHOICE)

### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the various physiological effects of electric current on the human body, with a neat sketch/diagram indicating the current thresholds and corresponding effects. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Introduction (1 Mark):**
The human body, due to its high water and electrolyte content, is a good conductor of electricity. When current flows, it disrupts the natural electrical signals of the nervous system and causes tissue heating. The severity depends on the magnitude, frequency, path, and duration of current.

**(ii) Threshold Effects — Tabulation (4 Marks):**

| Current (50 Hz AC) | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|
| < 1 mA | No sensation |
| 1–5 mA | Threshold of perception, mild tingling |
| 5–15 mA | Painful shock, muscle control retained |
| 15–50 mA | Muscular tetanization, **cannot let go** |
| 50–100 mA | Severe pain, respiratory paralysis |
| 100 mA – 5 A | **Ventricular fibrillation** (FATAL) |
| > 5 A | Heart paralysis, severe burns |

**(iii) Critical Effects — Explanation (1.5 Marks):**
- **Ventricular Fibrillation:** Disorganized heart rhythm at 100 mA for >0.2 s.
- **Burns:** I²R heating causes entry/exit wounds and internal tissue damage.
- **Tetanization:** Sustained muscle contraction at >15 mA.
- **Secondary Injuries:** Falls, fractures, eye damage from arc flash.

**(iv) Path and Duration Factors (0.5 Mark):**
- Hand-to-hand path crosses the chest and heart (most dangerous).
- Even small currents (50 mA) for several seconds can be fatal.

---

**Q. (b)** A worker with wet hands touches a 230 V AC live wire. If his body resistance is 1000 Ω, calculate:
   (i) The current through his body
   (ii) The power dissipated in the body
   (iii) The heat generated in 5 seconds
   (iv) Comment on the physiological effect. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Voltage, $V = 230\ V$
- Body resistance, $R = 1{,}000\ \Omega$
- Time, $t = 5\ s$

**(i) Current through body (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{V}{R} \\
&= \frac{230}{1{,}000} \\
&= 0.23\ A = 230\ mA
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Power dissipated (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= I^2 R \\
&= (0.23)^2 \times 1{,}000 \\
&= 0.0529 \times 1{,}000 \\
&= 52.9\ W
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Heat generated in 5 seconds (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= P \times t = I^2 R t \\
&= 52.9 \times 5 \\
&= 264.5\ J
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Physiological Effect (1.5 Marks):**
Since $I = 230\ mA$ is **more than twice the ventricular fibrillation threshold (100 mA)**, the worker will experience:
- Severe tetanization (cannot release wire)
- Immediate ventricular fibrillation
- **High probability of FATALITY within seconds**
- Localized burns at contact points

**Conclusion:** 230 mA is in the **lethal zone** — immediate medical intervention (CPR + AED) is required.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q. (a)** Classify various types of electrical accidents. Discuss the major causes of electrical accidents in industries and homes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification of Electrical Accidents (3 Marks):**

**1. Direct Electrical Injuries:**
- **Electrocution (Fatal Shock):** Caused by current through vital organs.
- **Electric Shock (Non-Fatal):** Pain, muscle contraction, throw-off.
- **Electrical Burns:** Entry/exit wounds, arc burns.
- **Arc Flash:** UV radiation and intense heat from arcing.
- **Arc Blast:** Pressure wave from arc explosion.

**2. Indirect Electrical Injuries:**
- **Fires:** Caused by overheating or short circuits.
- **Explosions:** In flammable atmospheres.
- **Equipment Damage:** Loss of property and production.

**3. Secondary Injuries:**
- **Falls** from ladders/poles due to shock.
- **Bone fractures** from muscle contraction.
- **Eye injuries** from bright arc flash.

**(ii) Major Causes (4 Marks):**

| Cause | Description |
|---|---|
| **Unsafe Acts** | Working on live wires, bypassing safety |
| **Unsafe Conditions** | Poor maintenance, faulty wiring |
| **Lack of Earthing** | No fault current path |
| **No RCD/ELCB** | Leakage current not detected |
| **Wet Conditions** | Reduced body resistance |
| **Poor Training** | Unskilled workers handling HV |
| **DIY Repairs** | Untrained persons doing electrical work |
| **Use of Damaged Cords** | Exposed wires, cracked insulation |
| **Overloaded Circuits** | Fires and overheating |
| **Lack of PPE** | No gloves, mats, or tools |

---

**Q. (b)** List and explain any **seven (7) important safety precautions** to be followed while working with electrical installations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (1 Mark Each):**

1. **De-energize Before Work:** Always switch off the supply and isolate the circuit before any maintenance. Use a **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** system to prevent accidental re-energization.

2. **Use Proper Earthing:** All metallic enclosures of equipment must be connected to a low-resistance earth to provide a safe path for fault currents and operate protective devices.

3. **Install RCD/ELCB:** A Residual Current Device (30 mA) should be installed on all domestic and industrial circuits. It trips within 30 ms, saving lives by preventing VF.

4. **Wear Appropriate PPE:** Use **insulated rubber gloves (Class 0 for 1 kV, Class 2 for 17 kV)**, **insulating mats**, **safety boots**, **face shield**, and **flame-retardant clothing**.

5. **Maintain Safe Distance:** For high-voltage lines, maintain a minimum approach distance (e.g., 1 m for 11 kV, 2 m for 33 kV). Use barriers and warning signs.

6. **Use Insulated Tools:** Always use tools rated for the voltage level. Standard tools are NOT safe for live work.

7. **Avoid Working Alone:** Always have a trained colleague nearby who can switch off the supply and call for help in case of an accident.

8. **Never Work in Wet Conditions:** Moisture drastically reduces body resistance. Ensure hands, floor, and clothing are dry.

9. **Use Permit-to-Work System:** For HV work, issue a written permit specifying the work, isolation, and precautions.

10. **Regular Inspection and Testing:** Periodically test insulation resistance, earth continuity, and RCD operation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS (KTU VALUATION KEY PATTERNS):**

1. **Conflating Voltage and Current Danger (–2 marks):** Writing "high voltage is dangerous" without emphasizing that **CURRENT is the actual killer**. Always mention Ohm's Law: $I = V/R$.

2. **Forgetting Body Resistance Values (–1 mark):** Not specifying $R = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ (wet) vs $R = 100{,}000\ \Omega$ (dry) when calculating body current.

3. **Missing Units in Calculations (–1 mark):** Always write units (mA, A, V, Ω, J) — partial credit is denied for unit-less numerical answers.

4. **Skipping the Path of Current Discussion (–1 mark):** Path through chest = most dangerous. Many students miss this crucial factor.

5. **No Mention of Frequency Dependence (–1 mark):** Failing to explain why 50 Hz is in the most dangerous band of the frequency spectrum.

6. **Mixing Up Let-Go Threshold Values (–1 mark):** Writing 30 mA for AC instead of 10 mA. **10 mA = AC**, **30 mA = DC**.

7. **No Mention of Time Factor (–1 mark):** Even 100 mA for 0.2 s causes VF. Always mention **duration of exposure** alongside current magnitude.

8. **Generic Safety Precaution Answers (–1 mark):** Writing "be careful" or "use gloves" without specific technical justification. Mention **Class rating, RCD trip current (30 mA), LOTO procedures**, etc.

9. **Forgetting Power/Heat Calculation Steps (–1 mark):** In numericals, students often write only the final answer. Show all steps: $I = V/R \rightarrow P = I^2R \rightarrow H = P \times t$.

10. **Missing Diagram in Part B (a) (–1 to 2 marks):** A current-vs-effect table or curve is **mandatory** in 7-mark theoretical questions. Always include a structured table.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST)

> [!NOTE]
> 🎯 **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — MEMORIZE THESE:**

✅ **Ohm's Law for Body:** $I = V/R_{body}$ — the foundation equation for all shock problems.

✅ **Body Resistance:** Dry = **100,000 Ω**, Wet = **1,000 Ω**, with wound = **500 Ω**.

✅ **Threshold of Perception:** AC = **1 mA**, DC = **5 mA**.

✅ **Let-Go Threshold:** AC = **10 mA**, DC = **30 mA**.

✅ **Ventricular Fibrillation Threshold:** **100 mA for 0.2 s** at 50 Hz.

✅ **Most Dangerous Frequency Band:** **10–100 Hz** (includes 50 Hz mains).

✅ **Safe Touch Voltage:** **≤ 50 V AC** (IEC 60990 standard).

✅ **RCD Trip Setting:** **30 mA in 30 ms** (livesaving standard).

✅ **Most Dangerous Path:** Hand-to-hand (crosses heart and lungs).

✅ **Key Formula:** Heat dissipated $H = I^2 R t$ (Joule's Law).

✅ **Power Dissipated:** $P = I^2 R$ (causes burns).

✅ **Types of Electrical Accidents:** Direct, Indirect, Secondary injuries.

✅ **Five Major Safety Controls:** Engineering, Administrative, PPE, Emergency Response, Training.

✅ **LOTO:** Lockout-Tagout is mandatory for safe electrical work.

✅ **PPE Essentials:** Insulated gloves (Class 0/2/4), mats, boots, face shield, FR clothing.

✅ **First Aid Priority:** De-energize → Don't touch victim → Use insulated stick → CPR + AED → Call emergency (108/112).

✅ **Recovery Position:** Used for unconscious but breathing victims.

✅ **CPR Rate:** 100–120 compressions per minute.

✅ **AED Use:** Apply as soon as possible for ventricular fibrillation.

✅ **Indian Standard Frequency:** **50 Hz**; Voltage = **230 V single phase, 415 V three phase**.

✅ **Dalziel's Equation:** $I_{VF} = \dfrac{k}{\sqrt{t}}$ where $k$ is a body weight constant — fibrillation current decreases with longer exposure.

✅ **Mnemonic — "PDCM" for Severity Factors:** **P**ath, **D**uration, **C**urrent magnitude, **M**uscle condition (wet/dry).

✅ **Indian Regulation:** Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Safety Regulations 2010 — mandatory for all electrical installations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Shocks and Their Prevention. Insulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Electric Shocks and Their Prevention. Insulation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Electric Shock — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > An **electric shock** is the physiological stimulation, excitation, or damage caused when an **electric current** flows through the human body or any living tissue due to accidental contact with a live (energised) conductor or due to an unintended potential difference between two body parts. The effect of an electric shock is determined primarily by the **magnitude of current** (not voltage alone) that actually passes through the body, which is governed by **Ohm's Law**: $$I_{body} = \frac{V_{contact}}{R_{body}}$$ where $V_{contact}$ is the voltage to which the body is exposed and $R_{body}$ is the total resistance offered by the current path through the body. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Water-Pipe" Analogy** > Think of electricity as **water flowing through pipes**. *Voltage* is the **water pressure** pushing the water, *current* is the **amount of water flowing per second**, and *resistance* is the **narrowness of the pipe**. Your body is like a **leaky, wet pipe** — if you touch a live wire, current "leaks" through you to ground (like water flowing out of a crack).
