## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Connection of a Protective Relay in a Power System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source[Three Phase Power Source] --> bus[Bus Bar]
    bus --> cta[CT Primary Winding]
    cta --> cts[CT Secondary 5A]
    cts --> relay[Protective Relay]
    relay --> trip[Trip Coil of Circuit Breaker]
    trip --> cb[Main CB Contacts Open Faulty Section]
    bus -.unfaulted.-> load[Healthy Load Continues]
    relay --> flag[Mechanical Flag Indicator]
    relay --> aux[Auxiliary Contacts]
    classDef primary fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef relay fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class source,bus,cta,cb primary
    class relay,trip control
    class cts,flag,aux relay
    class load primary
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Current flows from the source through the bus to the load. A CT is installed to "sense" the line current. The CT secondary is wired to the relay. When the relay senses an abnormal condition, it energises the trip coil, which opens the circuit breaker, isolating the fault. The **healthy load** continues to receive power.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Numerical (Digital) Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputCT[CT Secondary Current Signal] --> analog[Analog Signal Conditioning]
    inputPT[PT Secondary Voltage Signal] --> analog
    analog --> anti[Anti-Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    anti --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter]
    adc --> dsp[DSP / Microprocessor Core]
    dsp --> fft[DFT / FFT Algorithm Computes Phasors]
    fft --> algo[Protection Algorithm Compares Against Settings]
    algo --> logic[Trip Decision Logic]
    logic --> trip[Output Trip Contact]
    logic --> alarm[Alarm Output]
    logic --> display[LCD Display + LED Indicators]
    algo --> memory[Fault Recording Memory]
    memory --> comm[Communication Port IEC 61850]
    comm --> scada[SCADA System]
    classDef sig fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class inputCT,inputPT,analog,anti,adc sig
    class dsp,fft,algo,logic,memory proc
    class trip,alarm,display,comm,scada out
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic (Mermaid Equivalent) — Induction Disc Overcurrent Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cc[Current Coil on Upper Magnet] --> flux1[Alternating Flux phi1]
    pc[Voltage Coil Phase Shifting Winding] --> flux2[Alternating Flux phi2]
    flux1 --> disc[Aluminium Rotating Disc]
    flux2 --> disc
    disc --> spindle[Spindle with Moving Contact]
    spindle --> fixed[Fixed Contact Trip Output]
    spring[Control Spring Restrain] --> spindle
    brake[Permanent Magnet Brake Provides Damping] --> disc
    spindle --> flag[Mechanical Trip Indicator Flag]
    classDef coil fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef flux fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class cc,pc coil
    class flux1,flux2 flux
    class disc,spindle,fixed,spring,brake,flag mech
```

> **Operating principle:** The two fluxes (one from the current coil, one from the voltage/phase-shift coil) are displaced in time. Their interaction with the aluminium disc produces a **driving torque proportional to I²**, causing the disc to rotate. The control spring provides a **restraining torque**. When the operating torque exceeds the spring torque plus friction, the disc rotates to close the trip contact.

---

### 4.4 Flowchart — Continuity Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Identify the Component or Conductor to Test] --> B[Step 1 Isolate Component Remove from Circuit]
    B --> C[Step 2 Verify Circuit is De Energised Using Voltage Tester]
    C --> D[Step 3 Set Multimeter to Continuity Mode or Lowest Ohms Range]
    D --> E[Step 4 Connect Probes to Both Ends of the Conductor]
    E --> F{Reading and Buzzer}
    F -->|0 to 5 Ohms Buzzer ON| G[Result CONTINUOUS Component is Good]
    F -->|OL or Infinite Buzzer OFF| H[Result OPEN CIRCUIT Component is Broken]
    F -->|High Resistance 5 to 1000 Ohms| I[Result PARTIAL Loose Joint or Corrosion Repair Required]
    G --> J[Record and Reconnect]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[End of Test]
    classDef good fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,J,K good
    class G good
    class H bad
    class I warn
    class F good
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Differential Protection (Merz-Price Scheme)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Protected Element e.g. Transformer] --> CT1[CT1 at Terminal 1]
    P1 --> CT2[CT2 at Terminal 2]
    CT1 --> relay1[Relay Operating Coil Direction A]
    CT2 --> relay2[Relay Operating Coil Direction B]
    relay1 --> opd[Operating Quantity equals I1 minus I2]
    relay2 --> opd
    opd --> trip{Operating Quantity Exceeds Setting?}
    trip -->|Yes| cb[Trip Circuit Breaker]
    trip -->|No| nc[No Action Healthy State]
    classDef eq fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class P1,CT1,CT2 eq
    class relay1,relay2,opd sens
    class trip,cb dec
    class nc out
```

> **Operating logic:** Under normal conditions, the current entering (I₁) equals the current leaving (I₂). The phasor sum in the relay's operating coil is zero, so no torque is produced. During an *internal fault*, the balance is broken, current circulates inside the relay coil, producing a strong operating torque → CB trips.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

---

**Q1. Define a protective relay. List any four essential qualities it must possess.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A protective relay is an automatic device that senses abnormal conditions (faults) in an electrical power system and commands the associated circuit breaker to trip, thereby isolating the faulty section from the healthy network.

**Four Essential Qualities:**

1. **Reliability** – Must operate *every time* there is a fault within its zone and *never* operate when there is no fault. It is the most important quality.
2. **Selectivity (Discrimination)** – Should isolate *only* the faulty section, leaving the rest of the system undisturbed.
3. **Speed** – Should operate as fast as possible (ideally within one cycle) to minimise equipment damage and system disturbance.
4. **Sensitivity** – Should respond to the *smallest* fault current in its zone of protection.

(Other accepted qualities: Stability, Simplicity, Economy, Adequateness.)

---

**Q2. What is a continuity test? Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A continuity test is an electrical test performed using a multimeter or a buzzer-type continuity tester to verify whether an *unbroken (low-resistance) electrical path* exists between two points in a circuit.

**Working:** A small DC voltage is applied between the two test probes. If the path is continuous (R < ~30 Ω), the buzzer sounds and the meter shows a low resistance reading. If the path is broken (R = ∞), the meter shows OL and the buzzer remains silent.

**Two Applications:**

1. Testing of **fuses, switches, MCBs, and relay contacts** to ensure they are not open when they should be closed.
2. Verifying the **earthing conductor (PE)** integrity in domestic and industrial wiring for **electric-shock safety**.

(Other valid answers: cable core testing, motor winding continuity, PCB track testing, etc.)

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Slightly Modified to Match 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of an induction-disc type overcurrent relay** with a neat diagram. Discuss its **inverse-time operating characteristic**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
The induction-disc overcurrent relay consists of the following main parts:

1. **Electromagnet (Upper and Lower):** Two electromagnets are mounted on a frame. The *upper* magnet carries the **current coil** (operating coil) connected to the secondary of a CT. The *lower* magnet carries a **voltage coil or phase-shifting winding** which is energised from the same CT (or a separate PT in some designs).
2. **Aluminium Disc:** A thin aluminium disc is mounted on a vertical spindle between the two magnets. The disc is free to rotate in a horizontal plane.
3. **Control Spring:** A spiral spring attached to the spindle provides a *restraining torque* proportional to the angular displacement.
4. **Permanent Magnet (Braking):** A small permanent magnet placed near the disc provides a *damping torque* proportional to the disc's speed, preventing hunting and oscillations.
5. **Trip Contacts:** A *moving contact* on the spindle and a *fixed contact* complete the trip circuit when the disc has rotated through a set angle (typically 360° or so).
6. **Plug Setting Bridge & TMS Dial:** Two adjustable settings — the **current setting** (50%, 75%, 100%, 125%, 150%, 175%, 200% of CT secondary 5 A) and the **time multiplier setting** (0.1 to 1.0).

**Working Principle:**
The two electromagnets produce two alternating fluxes, $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$, **displaced in time-phase** by an angle $\theta$. These fluxes induce eddy currents in the aluminium disc. The interaction between the eddy currents and the fluxes produces a **driving torque** on the disc, given by:

$$T_{op} = K_1 \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot \sin\theta$$

Since both fluxes are produced by the same relay current $I$, $T_{op} \propto I^2$.

This torque rotates the disc against the spring. When the current exceeds the **pickup value**, the operating torque overcomes the spring torque, friction, and damping, and the disc rotates to close the trip contacts — sending a trip command to the circuit breaker.

**Inverse-Time Characteristic:**
The operating time is **inversely proportional** to the current magnitude:
- At currents *just above* pickup (PSM = 1 to 3), the relay takes *long* time to operate.
- At very high currents (PSM > 10), the relay operates in *less than 0.1 s* (becoming effectively instantaneous).

The empirical IEC 60255-151 **Standard Inverse** formula:

$$t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{PSM^{0.02} - 1} \text{ seconds}$$

This is the heart of *discrimination by time* on radial feeders — upstream relays have higher TMS so they operate last, while downstream relays (closer to fault) operate first.

**Neat Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.3.)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of protective relays** based on:
**(i) Construction (Operating Principle)** and **(ii) Function / Application**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Based on Construction (Operating Principle):**

1. **Electromagnetic Relays** (the classical, mechanical type):
   - **Attracted-Armature Type** — A ferromagnetic armature is pulled towards a magnetic core when the current exceeds a threshold. Used in DC circuits and auxiliary protection. *Operating time: instantaneous* (5–20 ms).
   - **Solenoid Type** — A movable iron plunger is drawn into a solenoid. Used as an *instantaneous high-set* element.
   - **Induction-Disc Type** — Aluminium disc driven by two phase-shifted fluxes. Provides *inverse-time* characteristic. Most common for overcurrent.
   - **Induction-Cup Type** — A cylindrical rotor (cup) is used instead of a disc. Provides *higher torque* and *better accuracy* — used in directional and differential relays.
   - **Balanced-Beam Type** — A beam is held in equilibrium by two opposing forces. Any imbalance (as in differential protection) tips the beam.

2. **Static (Solid-State) Relays** — Use diodes, transistors, op-amps, comparators. *No moving parts.* Faster, no contact wear, but sensitive to voltage transients.

3. **Digital / Numerical Relays** — Use microprocessors / DSPs. Perform *Fourier analysis* on sampled signals. Provide *multi-function protection, self-testing, fault recording, IEC 61850 communication.*

**(ii) Based on Function / Application:**

| Relay | Function | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Overcurrent Relay (OCR) | Trips on current exceeding setting | Feeders, transformers |
| Earth-Fault / EFR | Detects zero-sequence current | Single line-to-ground faults |
| Differential Relay | Compares currents entering & leaving | Generators, transformers, bus-bars |
| Distance (Impedance) | Measures Z = V/I | Long transmission lines |
| Directional Relay | Trips only for power in one direction | Parallel feeders, ring mains |
| Under/Over-Voltage | Voltage deviations | Generators, bus-bars |
| Buchholz Relay | Gas & oil surge detection | Oil-immersed transformers |
| Reverse-Power Relay | Detects power flow reversal | Generators (anti-motoring) |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** A **single-phase 11 kV / 230 V, 500 kVA transformer** is protected by a **Differential Relay** (Merz-Price scheme). The CT ratios are:
- HV side: **100 / 5 A**
- LV side: **1500 / 5 A**
A **fault current of 2000 A** flows in the HV winding.

Calculate:
- **(i)** The secondary current of each CT.
- **(ii)** The operating current of the differential relay.
- **(iii)** Will the relay trip if its setting is 0.5 A? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — CT secondary current on HV side:**

$$I_{sec,HV} = \dfrac{I_{primary,HV}}{CT_{ratio,HV}} = \dfrac{2000}{100/5} = \dfrac{2000 \times 5}{100} = 100 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — CT secondary current on LV side (transformer action):**

Turns ratio of transformer = 11000 / 230 ≈ 47.83. For a fault of 2000 A on the HV side, the corresponding LV current is:

$$I_{primary,LV} = \dfrac{2000}{47.83} \approx 41.82 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{sec,LV} = \dfrac{41.82}{1500/5} = \dfrac{41.82 \times 5}{1500} = 0.1394 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Operating current of the differential relay (after current balancing):**

Before computing the operating current, we need to balance the secondary currents. With a **balancing CT (auxiliary CT) or a tapped restraint winding**, the relay designer scales the LV-side secondary current to match the HV-side. Assuming the scheme is properly balanced at rated current, the operating current is the **phasor difference**:

$$I_{op} = \vert I_{sec,HV} - I_{sec,LV,balanced} \vert$$

For a *properly balanced* scheme (both sides equal at rated current), the LV-side current after balancing equals 100 A (mirroring HV side). So during an *external fault*, $I_{op} = 0$. During an *internal fault* on the HV winding, the difference is the HV fault current scaled to the relay side:

$$I_{op} = 100 \text{ A} \quad \text{(after balancing, internal fault case)}$$

> **Practical interpretation:** In a Merz-Price scheme, the operating coil sees the *spill* current. For an internal fault, this is essentially the full CT secondary current.

**Step 4 — Will the relay trip?**

Relay setting = 0.5 A. Operating current = 100 A.

$$\because 100 \text{ A} \gg 0.5 \text{ A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{YES, the relay WILL trip.}$$

$$\boxed{\text{The relay operates decisively, isolating the faulted transformer.}}$$

> **Engineering note:** Differential relays are *unit protection* — they only see faults *inside* the protected zone (transformer windings). For external faults on the LV side, the spill current is near-zero (only CT ratio errors and saturation cause small spill), and the relay must remain stable. This is achieved by adding a *bias* characteristic.

---

**(b)** Explain in detail the **classification of protective relays based on function** with at least **five examples** and their **principle of operation**. Also explain the **essential qualities** of a protective relay. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Refer to the *Function / Application* classification table in Section 5 Part B Question A(b). Provide the following five examples with one-line principles:

1. **Overcurrent Relay (OCR):** Compares line current with a *pickup setting*. Operates when current exceeds setting. Time-current characteristic can be definite or inverse (IDMT).

2. **Differential Relay (Merz-Price):** Compares *current entering* a protected zone with *current leaving* it. Operates on *imbalance* (difference). Used for generators, transformers, bus-bars.

3. **Distance (Impedance) Relay:** Measures apparent impedance $Z = V / I$ seen from the relay location. Operates when $Z$ falls below a set value (= fault lies within the protected line length). Provides *zone-based* protection (Zone 1, 2, 3).

4. **Earth-Fault Relay:** Uses a *zero-sequence CT* (or residual connection) to detect the sum $I_a + I_b + I_c$. In a healthy system, this sum is zero. During a line-to-ground fault, the sum equals $3 I_0 \neq 0$, and the relay operates.

5. **Buchholz Relay:** Installed in the *oil pipeline* between the transformer tank and the conservator. Accumulates gas bubbles generated by *incipient faults* (overheating, partial discharge). When gas accumulation exceeds a threshold, the relay trips. Sudden oil surge (e.g., short-circuit) trips a *second* element.

**Essential Qualities of a Protective Relay:**

1. **Reliability** — *Most important*. Must operate correctly every time.
2. **Selectivity / Discrimination** — Operates only for its own zone's fault.
3. **Speed** — As fast as possible (ideally < 1 cycle).
4. **Sensitivity** — Detects the smallest fault in its zone.
5. **Stability** — Stable for *through-faults* outside its zone.
6. **Economy** — Cost-effective for the value of the equipment protected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3)**
>
> 1. **Unit errors in IDMT numericals** — Confusing primary and secondary currents. Always reduce to CT secondary first, then compute PSM.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert % to a fraction** — A "current setting of 125%" means 1.25 × CT secondary, not 125 A.
> 3. **Reporting "the relay trips" without the operating equation** — A 7-mark numerical must show the **formula substitution AND the final numeric answer**. Marks are split 2 (formula) + 3 (computation) + 2 (interpretation).
> 4. **In continuity test theory, omitting the "isolate the component" step** — The most common 1-mark loss in a 3-mark short answer.
> 5. **Calling a relay a "circuit breaker"** — A relay is a *sensing and decision-making* device; the breaker is the *switching* device. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 6. **Forgetting IEC 60255-151** in the answer for inverse-time — If you write just "inverse time" without the formula, you lose at least 2 marks in a 7-mark question.
> 7. **Mixing up induction disc vs. induction cup** — Disc → non-directional overcurrent. Cup → directional/differential. Many students swap them.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Continuity Test & Protective Relays)**

- [ ] A **continuity test** verifies an unbroken electrical path. Always **de-energise** and **isolate** the component first.
- [ ] Expected reading: **~ 0 Ω** for healthy conductor; **OL** for open circuit; **high Ω** for loose joint.
- [ ] **Earth continuity** test: maximum permitted resistance is **≤ 0.1 Ω** for PE conductor, **≤ 1 Ω** for overall earth path.
- [ ] A **protective relay** is an automatic sensing & decision device; the **circuit breaker** is the switching device.
- [ ] **Six essential qualities:** Reliability, Selectivity, Speed, Sensitivity, Stability, Economy (in that order of importance).
- [ ] **CT secondary standard: 5 A** (sometimes 1 A for long runs). **PT secondary standard: 110 V (L-N)**.
- [ ] **Pickup current (I_p)** = threshold at which relay just operates. **Reset current (I_r)** < I_p. **Pickup ratio** = I_r / I_p > 0.9.
- [ ] **PSM = (I_fault,secondary) / (I_pickup,secondary)**. Computed always on the CT secondary side.
- [ ] **IDMT Standard Inverse:** $t = \dfrac{0.14 \times TMS}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$ seconds. **MEMORISE THIS.**
- [ ] **Induction disc relay** → non-directional overcurrent; **Induction cup relay** → directional / differential.
- [ ] **Differential (Merz-Price) protection** uses $I_{op} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$. Operates only for *internal* faults.
- [ ] **Distance relay** uses $Z = V / I$. Zone 1 = 80% of line, Zone 2 = 100%, Zone 3 = 120–150% (backup).
- [ ] **Buchholz relay** is a *gas-actuated* relay in oil-immersed transformers. Two elements: gas accumulation + oil surge.
- [ ] **Three generations of relays:** Electromagnetic (1st) → Static (2nd) → Numerical/Digital (3rd/4th).
- [ ] KTU 2024 standard answer format for a relay question: **Definition + Diagram + Working principle + Equation + Application**.
- [ ] Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (not bare `|`) when writing mathematical magnitude in exam sheets, to avoid confusion with CT ratios.
- [ ] Static and numerical relays **do NOT have** moving parts — note this distinction in theory questions.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Minute Recall Mnemonics:**
> - **Relay Qualities** → **"R-S-S³-E"** → **R**eliability, **S**electivity, **S**peed, **S**ensitivity, **S**tability, **E**conomy.
> - **IDMT Characteristic Curve** → *"Steeper curve = longer time at low PSM; flat curve = instantaneous at high PSM."*
> - **Earth Fault Detection** → *"Sum of three phase currents = 0 in a healthy system, 3I₀ in a faulted system."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirement of Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Requirement of Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **protective relay** is a device that senses an abnormal or fault condition in an electrical power system and, upon detection, automatically initiates the opening of one or more circuit breakers to isolate the faulty section. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a relay is defined as an **electrically operated automatic switch** that monitors a system continuously and acts as the "decision-making brain" of any protection scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Module 3):**
> *A relay is a device that detects the fault and subsequently commands the circuit breaker to trip, thereby isolating the faulty section from the healthy portion of the system.*

The relay does **not** interrupt the fault current directly. It only commands the **trip coil** of a circuit breaker, which then performs the heavy-duty interruption. This separation of *sensing* (relay) and *switching* (circuit breaker) is the core philosophy of modern switchgear.

### 1.2 Why is a Relay Required? — The Logical Necessity

Imagine a 132 kV transmission line carrying **2000 A** of load current. A short-circuit fault may produce currents of **20,000 A to 40,000 A** for a duration of milliseconds. No human operator, no fuse, and no manual switch can react with the **speed**, **accuracy**, and **reliability** required. This is the fundamental reason a relay is **non-negotiable** in any modern power system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Fundamental Engineering Reasons a Relay is Mandatory:**
> 1. **Speed** — Faults must be cleared in 50–100 ms to prevent insulation damage, fire, and equipment explosion.
> 2. **Selectivity** — Only the faulty zone should be disconnected, leaving the rest of the grid energized.
> 3. **Sensitivity** — Even high-impedance faults (sluggish, low-current faults) must be detected.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fire Alarm" Model

Think of a relay as a **highly intelligent smoke detector** in a building:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Power System Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Smoke Sensor | Detects smoke particles | **Current Transformer (CT)** senses fault current |
| Alarm Panel | Decides whether to ring | **Relay** evaluates the abnormal signal |
| Sprinkler | Actually puts out the fire | **Circuit Breaker** interrupts the fault |
| Battery Backup | Keeps system alive during failure | **Auxiliary DC supply** to the relay |

Just as a smoke detector must be **reliable, fast, and selective** (it should ring only for real fires, not for burnt toast), a protective relay must have the same qualities. If the detector fails, the entire building burns. If the relay fails, the entire substation explodes.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exams:** Always remember this hierarchy — *CT/PT (Sense) → Relay (Decide) → Trip Coil (Command) → Circuit Breaker (Act)*. Examiners love to test this four-stage chain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Essential Requirements of a Protective Relay

The KTU 2024 syllabus, in alignment with standard switchgear practice, mandates that every protective relay must satisfy the following **six essential qualities**. Each is a board-favourite for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

#### (i) Reliability

The relay **must** operate whenever a fault occurs within its zone of protection, and **must not** operate for any normal condition or external fault. Reliability is the single most important property.

$$
\text{Reliability} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Number of operating mechanism components}}
$$

Electromagnetic and static (electronic) relays are inherently more reliable than complex electromechanical types due to fewer moving parts.

#### (ii) Selectivity (Discrimination)

The relay should distinguish between:
- **Internal faults** (within its zone) → must trip
- **External faults** (outside its zone) → must remain stable

This is achieved by **current-time grading** — the relay nearest to the fault operates first, and the back-up relay operates only if the primary fails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrimination Rule of Thumb:**
> Operating time of primary relay $<$ Operating time of back-up relay. The difference is called the **Discrimination Time** or **Grading Margin**, typically **0.3 s to 0.5 s**.

#### (iii) Speed

The shorter the fault-clearing time, the lesser the damage. The relay's speed is quantified by its **operating time** $t_{op}$, which is the interval between the instant of fault occurrence and the closing of the trip-coil contacts.

| Fault Type | Acceptable Clearing Time |
|---|---|
| HV/EHV Transmission (400 kV) | $\leq$ **80 ms** |
| HV (132 kV / 220 kV) | $\leq$ **100 ms** |
| MV Distribution (11 kV / 33 kV) | $\leq$ **150–300 ms** |

#### (iv) Sensitivity

A relay must operate for the **smallest possible fault** within its zone. Sensitivity is expressed as the **Minimum Operating Current** or **Pickup Current** $I_{pickup}$.

$$
\text{Sensitivity Factor} = \frac{I_{\text{min fault}}}{I_{\text{pickup}}}
$$

A sensitivity factor $\geq$ **1.5** is generally considered acceptable in industry.

#### (v) Stability

A relay must remain **stable** (inert) for all fault currents originating **outside** its protective zone. This is also called the **Through-Fault Stability**. A relay that operates for a through-fault is said to have "lost stability."

#### (vi) Adequate Burden & Ratings

The relay's impedance (called its **burden**) must be low enough that the CT does not saturate and produces accurate secondary current. The relay must also withstand:
- **Short-time thermal rating** (1 s or 3 s)
- **Dynamic rating** (peak value of the first fault current loop)
- **Making capacity** of the contacts ($\geq$ **30 A** for trip coil closing)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pickup Current | $I_{pickup} = I_{CT,sec} \times \text{Pickup setting}$ | A | The current at which relay starts to operate |
| 2 | Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM) | $PSM = \dfrac{I_{fault,sec}}{I_{pickup}}$ | dimensionless | $\text{PSM} \geq 1$ for operation |
| 3 | Operating Time (Inverse) | $t = \dfrac{0.14 \times TMS}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$ | seconds | Standard Inverse (IEC 60255) |
| 4 | Operating Time (Very Inverse) | $t = \dfrac{13.5 \times TMS}{PSM - 1}$ | seconds | Very Inverse Characteristic |
| 5 | Operating Time (Extremely Inverse) | $t = \dfrac{80 \times TMS}{PSM^{2} - 1}$ | seconds | Used for EHV lines |
| 6 | Relay Burden | $B = I_{sec}^{2} \times Z_{relay}$ | VA | Should be $< I_{CT}$ rating |
| 7 | Time Multiplier Setting | $TMS = \dfrac{t_{actual}}{t_{curve}}$ | dimensionless | Range typically 0.05 to 1.0 |
| 8 | Discrimination Margin | $\Delta t = t_{backup} - t_{primary}$ | seconds | $\Delta t \geq 0.3$ s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Many students write $\vert PSM - 1 \vert$ in the denominator. Since PSM is always $\geq$ 1 for relay operation, the absolute value is unnecessary. Use $(PSM - 1)$ directly in equations, but if you must use the modulus symbol in tables, write it as $\vert PSM - 1 \vert$ with $\vert$ and not the pipe character.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The relay is the cornerstone of **Zone-Based Protection** philosophy used worldwide. Without relays, the interconnected national grid of India (the "One Nation One Grid") would be physically impossible. Relays enable:

1. **Auto-Reclosing** on EHV lines
2. **Distance Protection** (Zones 1, 2, 3) for transmission lines
3. **Differential Protection** (87T, 87B) for transformers and busbars
4. **Backup Protection** through definite-time and inverse-time grading

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The IDMT (Inverse Definite Minimum Time) Operating Equation

The IDMT relay is the most common relay in KTU problems. Its operating time is governed by a strict mathematical relationship between **PSM** and **Time Multiplier Setting (TMS)**.

#### Starting Point — The Empirical Law

For a given relay family, the operating time at a reference PSM (usually PSM = 10) is constant, call it the **characteristic constant $K$**. For a standard inverse relay, $K$ is conventionally taken as **0.14** in the IEC 60255 standard.

The relationship is empirically found to be:

$$
t = K \cdot TMS \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{PSM^{\alpha} - 1} \right]
$$

where $\alpha$ is the characteristic exponent defining the relay family:

- $\alpha = 0.02$ → **Standard Inverse (SI)**
- $\alpha = 1$ → **Very Inverse (VI)**
- $\alpha = 2$ → **Extremely Inverse (EI)**

#### Worked Example — Standard Inverse Time Calculation

**Problem:** A 5 A, 3-second IDMT relay has TMS = 0.8. The fault current is 4000 A in primary, with a CT ratio of 400/5. Calculate the operating time.

**Step 1: Determine secondary fault current**

The CT ratio is 400/5 = 80. The secondary current is the primary current divided by the CT ratio:

$$
I_{sec} = \frac{I_{primary}}{CTR} = \frac{4000 \text{ A}}{80} = 50 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2: Determine pickup current**

The pickup is the current at which the relay begins to operate. With a plug setting of, say, 100% (i.e., 5 A in the 5 A relay):

$$
I_{pickup} = 5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)**

$$
PSM = \frac{I_{sec}}{I_{pickup}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Standard Inverse IDMT equation**

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \times TMS}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}
$$

Substitute $TMS = 0.8$ and $PSM = 10$:

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.8}{10^{0.02} - 1}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
10^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \cdot \ln 10} = e^{0.02 \cdot 2.3026} = e^{0.04605} = 1.04713
$$

$$
10^{0.02} - 1 = 1.04713 - 1 = 0.04713
$$

Therefore:

$$
t = \frac{0.112}{0.04713} = 2.376 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t \approx$ **2.38 s** (this is the actual operating time of the relay at the given fault current).

### 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity Factor for a Relay

The sensitivity of a relay measures its ability to detect the smallest fault in its zone. Mathematically:

**Step 1: Define the minimum fault current**

For a single line-to-ground fault at the farthest end of the protected zone, the fault current is the smallest. Let this be $I_{f,min}$.

**Step 2: Define the pickup current**

The pickup is the smallest current the relay is calibrated to detect, $I_{pickup}$.

**Step 3: Form the ratio**

$$
\text{Sensitivity Factor} = \frac{I_{f,min}}{I_{pickup}}
$$

For reliable operation, the KTU Board typically requires:

$$
\text{Sensitivity Factor} \geq 1.5
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Discrimination Time (Grading Margin)

When two relays are connected in series (e.g., at substation A and substation B), the relay at A must operate first. The grading margin ensures the back-up relay does not operate prematurely.

**Step 1: Define the time difference**

$$
\Delta t = t_{backup} - t_{primary}
$$

**Step 2: Account for practical delays**

In real systems, the operating time of the circuit breaker ($t_{CB}$) and overshoot time ($t_{o} \approx 0.05$ s) are added:

$$
\Delta t = t_{CB} + t_o + \text{safety margin}
$$

$$
\Delta t = 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.15 = 0.30 \text{ s}
$$

This 0.30 s is the **KTU-standard grading margin**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Relay Requirements

| S.No. | Requirement | What it ensures | Quantitative Measure | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability | Operates when required | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | Catastrophic equipment damage |
| 2 | Selectivity | Trips only the faulty zone | Grading margin (0.3–0.5 s) | Blackout of healthy sections |
| 3 | Speed | Clears fault quickly | Operating time (ms) | Insulation failure, fire |
| 4 | Sensitivity | Detects smallest fault | Sensitivity factor ($\geq 1.5$) | Undetected incipient faults |
| 5 | Stability | No operation for through-faults | Stability ratio (PSM$>$1.5 for back-up) | False tripping, cascade failure |
| 6 | Adequate Rating | Withstands fault current | Short-time rating (1 s, 3 s) | Relay contact welding |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic for the Six Requirements: "R-S-S-S-S-A"** →
> **R**eliability, **S**electivity, **S**peed, **S**ensitivity, **S**tability, **A**dequate Rating

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Protection Scheme

The following block diagram illustrates the chain of components from fault sensing to fault interruption, showing exactly where the relay fits in the power-system protection architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwr1[Power System Bus 132kV]
    ctIn[Current Transformer CT]
    relay1[Protective Relay Decision Unit]
    tripCoil[Trip Coil 110V DC]
    cbMain[Vacuum Circuit Breaker]
    aux1[Auxiliary DC Supply Battery Bank]
    annun1[Annunciator and Flag Indicator]
    scada1[SCADA RTU Telemetry]
    
    pwr1 -- Primary Current --> ctIn
    ctIn -- Secondary Current 5A --> relay1
    relay1 -- Trip Command --> tripCoil
    tripCoil -- Mechanical Force --> cbMain
    cbMain -- Interrupts Fault --> pwr1
    aux1 -- DC Power --> relay1
    aux1 -- DC Power --> tripCoil
    relay1 -- Status Signal --> annun1
    annun1 -- Alarm --> scada1
    
    style pwr1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style ctIn fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
    style relay1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:3px
    style tripCoil fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style cbMain fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
    style aux1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The relay (red, central) is the decision-making unit. It receives input from the CT and sends output to the trip coil. The CB does the actual heavy switching. The DC supply keeps the relay alive even during a station blackout.

### 4.2 Hierarchical Architecture of Relay Requirements

The following diagram shows how the six essential requirements are interdependent and form a hierarchy of design priorities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootReq[Essential Requirements of Protective Relay]
    req1[1 RELIABILITY]
    req2[2 SELECTIVITY]
    req3[3 SPEED]
    req4[4 SENSITIVITY]
    req5[5 STABILITY]
    req6[6 ADEQUATE RATING]
    outA[Design Outcome Safe Dependable Protection]
    
    rootReq --> req1
    rootReq --> req2
    rootReq --> req3
    rootReq --> req4
    rootReq --> req5
    rootReq --> req6
    req1 --> outA
    req2 --> outA
    req3 --> outA
    req4 --> outA
    req5 --> outA
    req6 --> outA
    
    req1 -.depends on.-> req5
    req2 -.depends on.-> req3
    req3 -.conflicts with.-> req4
    
    style rootReq fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style outA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style req1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style req2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style req3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style req4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style req5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style req6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow of a Relay During a Fault

The following diagram explains the logical sequence of decisions a relay executes from the moment the fault current begins to flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[CT senses current]
    cmp1{Current greater than Pickup}
    psmlCheck{PSM greater than 1.0}
    timeDel[Wait for inverse time delay t]
    tripOut[Issue Trip Command to CB]
    cbOp[CB opens and isolates fault]
    resetAct[Reset and await next fault]
    noOp1[No operation relay stays stable]
    
    start1 --> cmp1
    cmp1 -- No --> noOp1
    cmp1 -- Yes --> psmlCheck
    psmlCheck -- No --> noOp1
    psmlCheck -- Yes --> timeDel
    timeDel --> tripOut
    tripOut --> cbOp
    cbOp --> resetAct
    noOp1 --> resetAct
    
    style start1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style cmp1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px
    style psmlCheck fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px
    style tripOut fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style cbOp fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style noOp1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **For Drawing in Exams:** If Mermaid cannot be used, draw a simple block diagram with four blocks: *CT → Relay → Trip Coil → Circuit Breaker*, and label the arrow from CT as "I_secondary = 5A" and from Trip Coil as "Trip Pulse 110V DC". This simple 4-block sketch alone can fetch **3 to 4 marks** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a protective relay. Why is it considered the brain of the protection scheme?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A protective relay is an automatic device that continuously monitors an electrical quantity (current, voltage, frequency, impedance) and, upon detecting an abnormal condition, causes a circuit breaker to trip and isolate the faulty section.

It is called the "brain" of the protection scheme because it performs three critical cognitive functions: (1) **Sensing** the input from CTs/PTs, (2) **Comparing** the input against a preset threshold (pickup), and (3) **Deciding** whether to send a trip command to the circuit breaker. Without the relay, the CB cannot decide *when* to operate, and the CT cannot decide *where* the fault is.

*(Word limit: ~50 words. Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sensing role, 1 mark for decision role.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six essential requirements of a protective relay.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Reliability** – Must operate when required
2. **Selectivity / Discrimination** – Trips only the faulty zone
3. **Speed** – Minimum operating time (typically $<$ 100 ms for EHV)
4. **Sensitivity** – Detects even the smallest fault
5. **Stability** – No operation for through-faults
6. **Adequate Rating** – Withstands short-circuit currents

*(1 mark for listing 6 requirements correctly; 1 mark for any two brief explanations; 1 mark for logical order.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the six essential requirements of a protective relay with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 A, 3-second IDMT overcurrent relay has a plug setting of 125% and TMS = 0.6. The CT ratio is 400/5. A fault current of 5000 A occurs in the primary. Determine the operating time of the relay using the standard inverse formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

### Part (a) — Six Essential Requirements (7 marks)

The six essential requirements of a protective relay are:

**1. Reliability (1 mark):**
The relay must operate consistently whenever a fault occurs within its zone and must not fail to operate. Reliability is achieved by simple design, fewer moving parts, and using proven electromagnetic or numerical (microprocessor-based) technology. Example: A numerical relay with self-diagnostic features.

**2. Selectivity / Discrimination (1 mark):**
The relay must identify the faulty section and isolate only that section, leaving the rest of the system healthy. This is achieved by current-time grading or impedance-based zoning. Example: In a radial feeder, the relay nearest to the fault trips first; the upstream relay acts as back-up.

**3. Speed (1 mark):**
The relay should operate as fast as possible to limit damage from $I^{2}R$ heating, electromagnetic forces, and arc energy. For a 400 kV system, the total fault-clearing time must be $\leq$ **80 ms** (3 cycles). The operating time $t_{op}$ is the interval from fault inception to trip-coil energization.

**4. Sensitivity (1 mark):**
The relay must detect the minimum fault current within its zone. Sensitivity is the ratio:

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{I_{\text{min fault}}}{I_{\text{pickup}}} \geq 1.5
$$

Example: A relay with pickup 1 A can detect a 1.5 A fault — this is the lower sensitivity limit.

**5. Stability (1 mark):**
The relay must remain stable (inert) for through-faults, i.e., faults occurring *outside* its protective zone. A relay that trips for an external fault is said to have "lost stability." Stability is ensured by proper current-transformer knee-point voltage selection and restraining coils in differential relays.

**6. Adequate Rating (2 marks):**
The relay must withstand:
- **Short-time thermal current** for 1 s or 3 s (typically 25 kA to 40 kA for HV systems)
- **Dynamic peak current** (typically 2.55 $\times$ short-time current)
- **Contact making capacity** $\geq$ 30 A at 110 V DC for trip coil energization
- **Burden** $\leq$ CT secondary rating (typically 5 VA, 10 VA, 15 VA)

### Part (b) — IDMT Operating Time Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify the parameters (1 mark)**

Given:
- Relay rating = 5 A, 3 s
- Plug setting = 125% → $I_{pickup} = 5 \times 1.25 = 6.25$ A
- TMS = 0.6
- CT ratio = 400/5 = 80
- Primary fault current $I_p = 5000$ A

**Step 2: Compute the secondary fault current (1 mark)**

$$
I_{sec} = \frac{I_{primary}}{CTR} = \frac{5000}{80} = 62.5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Plug Setting Multiplier (1 mark)**

$$
PSM = \frac{I_{sec}}{I_{pickup}} = \frac{62.5}{6.25} = 10
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Standard Inverse IDMT equation (2 marks)**

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \times TMS}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}
$$

Substituting $TMS = 0.6$ and $PSM = 10$:

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.6}{10^{0.02} - 1} = \frac{0.084}{1.04713 - 1} = \frac{0.084}{0.04713}
$$

**Step 5: Final answer (2 marks)**

$$
t = 1.783 \text{ s} \approx 1.78 \text{ seconds}
$$

The relay operates in approximately **1.78 seconds** at this fault level.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional role of a relay in a power-system protection scheme. Also discuss the difference between primary and back-up protection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IDMT relay is set with pickup at 50%, TMS = 0.5, on a 5 A relay. The CT ratio is 600/5. A three-phase fault of 8000 A occurs. Calculate (i) the secondary current, (ii) the PSM, and (iii) the operating time using the standard inverse equation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Primary vs Back-up Protection (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The relay sits in the middle of the protection chain:

```
[Power System] → [CT/PT] → [Relay] → [Trip Coil] → [Circuit Breaker] → back to [Power System]
                  (Senses)   (Decides)  (Commands)       (Acts)
```

The relay receives the scaled-down current (5 A secondary for 400 A primary, for example) from the Current Transformer. The relay then compares this with its pickup setting. If the current exceeds pickup, the relay waits for the inverse-time delay (or acts instantly for instantaneous elements) and then sends a trip pulse to the trip coil. The trip coil energizes and releases the closing mechanism of the Circuit Breaker, which physically opens the contacts to interrupt the fault.

**Primary vs Back-up Protection (4 marks):**

| Feature | Primary Protection | Back-up Protection |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone** | Operates for faults *within* its own zone | Operates for faults *within* an adjacent zone, *only if* the primary fails |
| **Speed** | Faster (typically 0.1–0.3 s) | Slower (typically 0.3–0.8 s) |
| **Discrimination** | Zone 1, Zone 2 of distance relay | Adjacent zone's primary relay |
| **Reliability role** | First line of defense | Second line of defense |
| **Example** | Feeder relay at Substation A | Transformer HV relay at Substation B |

If the primary relay or its associated CB fails, the back-up relay provides protection — but with a deliberate time delay to allow the primary to act first.

### Part (b) — Three-part IDMT Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute pickup current (1 mark)**

$$
I_{pickup} = 5 \text{ A} \times 50\% = 2.5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2: Compute secondary fault current (2 marks)**

$$
I_{sec} = \frac{I_{primary}}{CTR} = \frac{8000}{120} = 66.67 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Compute PSM (2 marks)**

$$
PSM = \frac{I_{sec}}{I_{pickup}} = \frac{66.67}{2.5} = 26.67
$$

**Step 4: Compute operating time using Standard Inverse equation (2 marks)**

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.5}{(26.67)^{0.02} - 1}
$$

Compute $(26.67)^{0.02}$:

$$
26.67^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \times \ln 26.67} = e^{0.02 \times 3.283} = e^{0.06566} = 1.06787
$$

Therefore:

$$
t = \frac{0.07}{1.06787 - 1} = \frac{0.07}{0.06787} = 1.031 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:**
(i) $I_{sec} = 66.67$ A  
(ii) $PSM = 26.67$  
(iii) $t \approx 1.03$ seconds

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> **Pitfall 1:** Forgetting to convert primary current to secondary current. Always divide by CT ratio first. *Loss: 1–2 marks per question.*
> 
> **Pitfall 2:** Writing the relay rating as pickup current. A 5 A relay *can* be set to pickup at 50%, 75%, 100%, 125%, or 150% of 5 A. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 
> **Pitfall 3:** Using $10^{0.02}$ incorrectly. Many students round it to 1.05, but the actual value is **1.04713**. This error accumulates. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 
> **Pitfall 4:** In 14-mark questions, students skip the **block diagram** (worth 3 marks) and write only text. Always include a labeled diagram. *Loss: up to 3 marks.*
> 
> **Pitfall 5:** Confusing **pickup current** with **operating current**. Pickup is the *threshold* at which the relay *begins* to move. Operating current is the *actual* current during operation (which equals fault current in practice). *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Requirement of Relay**

- [x] **Relay Definition:** An automatic device that detects fault and commands the circuit breaker to trip. (KTU Board favourite, 2–3 marks)

- [x] **Six Essential Requirements:** **R**eliability, **S**electivity, **S**peed, **S**ensitivity, **S**tability, **A**dequate Rating. Mnemonic: **R-S-S-S-S-A** (4 marks guaranteed if you list all six with one-line explanations)

- [x] **Block Diagram:** Always draw CT → Relay → Trip Coil → CB. (3 marks in any 14-mark question)

- [x] **Pickup Current:** $I_{pickup} = \text{Relay CT secondary rating} \times \text{Plug Setting (\%)}$ (1 mark per question)

- [x] **PSM Formula:** $PSM = I_{sec} / I_{pickup}$ — PSM must be $\geq 1$ for relay operation (1 mark)

- [x] **IDMT Standard Inverse Equation:** $t = (0.14 \times TMS) / (PSM^{0.02} - 1)$ — memorize the constants 0.14 and 0.02 (3–4 marks)

- [x] **Discrimination Margin:** $\Delta t = t_{backup} - t_{primary} \geq 0.3$ s (1 mark for the 0.3 s value)

- [x] **Sensitivity Factor:** $\geq 1.5$ for adequate sensitivity (1 mark for the 1.5 value)

- [x] **CT Role:** Senses the primary current and scales it to 5 A (or 1 A) secondary. Burden should be $\leq 5$ VA. (1 mark)

- [x] **Trip Coil Voltage:** 110 V DC (Indian standard) for HV substations. (1 mark for block diagram)

- [x] **Total Fault-Clearing Time:** 80 ms (EHV), 100 ms (HV), 150–300 ms (MV) (1 mark)

- [x] **IEC 60255 Standard:** Reference standard for inverse-time relays (1 mark if asked for standards)

- [x] **Common Numerical Answer Traps:** For PSM = 10, $t$ for TMS = 1.0 is exactly 2.97 s. For PSM = 20, $t$ for TMS = 1.0 is 2.07 s. Memorize these two — they appear in every question paper.

- [x] **Distinction to Remember:** Relay does **not** break the fault current — it only **commands** the CB. The CB **acts**. (3-mark question, every year)

- [x] **Why not a fuse?** A fuse is single-use, slow, inaccurate, and cannot be coordinated. A relay is reusable, fast, precise, and graded. (3-mark question)

- [x] **Hierarchy:** Sensing (CT/PT) → Decision (Relay) → Action (CB). This is the order for any protection scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Protection
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Protection

# Types of Protection – KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In power system engineering, **Types of Protection** refers to the systematic classification of relay-based and device-based schemes employed to detect, isolate, and clear abnormal operating conditions (faults) in electrical equipment and networks. The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies these protections into categories based on the **nature of the fault** (overcurrent, earth fault, differential, distance, etc.), the **protected zone** (generator, transformer, transmission line, motor, busbar), and the **operating principle** (electromagnetic, static, numerical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Protection schemes are categorized by the parameter they monitor — current, voltage, frequency, impedance, pressure, or temperature — and the protective zone they safeguard. Each scheme is engineered to ensure selectivity, sensitivity, speed, stability, and reliability (the 5 S's of protection)."*

### 1.2 The Five Pillars of Protection (5 S's)

Any protection scheme, regardless of type, is judged against the following five qualitative criteria:

| Criterion | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Selectivity** | Ability to isolate only the *faulted* section, leaving healthy zones energized. |
| **Sensitivity** | Ability to detect and respond to the smallest fault current within the protected zone. |
| **Speed** | Minimum operating time to clear the fault (typically **< 100 ms** for HV systems). |
| **Stability** | No maloperation during through-faults, transients, or external faults. |
| **Reliability** | Guaranteed operation when required (Dependability) and no operation when not required (Security). |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Immune System of the Human Body**
>
> Imagine your body is a power grid. Different **types of protection** are like different white blood cells:
>
> - **Overcurrent relay (Macrophage)** → Swallows any abnormally large "current particle" (analogous to a bacterial infection).
> - **Differential relay (Antibody)** → Detects a *difference* between the expected and actual "signal" (a virus inside a cell, not outside).
> - **Distance relay (Sentinel Lymph Node)** → Detects threats based on how far they originate, by measuring impedance.
> - **Earth-fault relay (Skin Allergy Test)** → Reacts only when current leaks to "ground" (earth), much like how skin detects external contact.
> - **Buchholz relay (Lung Pressure Sensor)** → Senses abnormal gas pressure inside a transformer "lung."
>
> Just as the immune system uses **multiple overlapping defenses**, a power system uses **graded, coordinated, redundant protection layers** to ensure no single failure can cause a blackout.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards Referenced

- **Standard System Frequencies:** **50 Hz** (India, Europe) and **60 Hz** (USA, Japan)
- **Standard Voltages in India:** **415 V** (LT), **11 kV / 22 kV / 33 kV** (distribution), **110 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV / 765 kV** (transmission)
- **Standard Fault Clearance Time:** **< 100 ms** (3 cycles at 50 Hz) for HV systems
- **Buchholz Relay Oil Velocity Threshold:** **0.6 to 1.0 m/s** (alarm) and **1.0 to 1.5 m/s** (trip)
- **Standard CT Secondary Current:** **1 A or 5 A**
- **Standard PT Secondary Voltage:** **110 V / $\sqrt{3}$ V** (phase), **110 V** (line)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Protection Types

KTU 2024 Scheme organizes Types of Protection into the following principal categories. Each is explained in structured, bulleted logic form.

#### A. Overcurrent Protection (OCP)

- **What it monitors:** Magnitude of line current exceeding a preset Pickup value ($I_p$).
- **Operating principle:** When $I_{\text{line}} > I_p$, the relay operates after a time delay.
- **Two sub-types:**
  - **Definite Time Overcurrent (DTOC):** Fixed operating time, regardless of fault current magnitude.
  - **Inverse Time Overcurrent (IDMT):** Operating time is *inversely proportional* to fault current magnitude.
- **Application:** Feeders, distribution lines, backup protection for transformers and generators.

#### B. Earth-Fault Protection (EFP)

- **What it monitors:** Zero-sequence current ($I_0 = (I_a + I_b + I_c)/3$).
- **Operating principle:** In a healthy system, $I_0 = 0$. During a single line-to-ground (SLG) fault, $I_0 \neq 0$.
- **Application:** Solidly grounded or impedance-grounded systems, residual CT-based schemes.

#### C. Differential Protection (Merz-Price Scheme)

- **What it monitors:** Difference between currents entering and leaving a protected zone.
- **Operating principle:** For an *external* fault or normal operation: $I_{\text{in}} = I_{\text{out}}$, so $\Delta I = 0$. For an *internal* fault: $I_{\text{in}} \neq I_{\text{out}}$, so $\Delta I \neq 0 \rightarrow$ trip.
- **Application:** Generators, transformers, busbars, large motors, short transmission lines.
- **Variant:** Biased (percentage) differential protection uses a restraining coil to prevent maloperation on CT saturation during external faults.

#### D. Distance Protection (Impedance Relay)

- **What it monitors:** Impedance seen by the relay: $Z = V/I$.
- **Operating principle:** Lower impedance implies closer fault. Relay trips if measured impedance falls within a pre-set zone (Zone 1: 80% of line, Zone 2: 100–150%, Zone 3: 120–180%).
- **Application:** Transmission lines (110 kV and above).

#### E. Directional Protection

- **What it monitors:** Direction of power flow (using Voltage + Current phasors).
- **Operating principle:** Relay operates only for faults in the *forward* direction.
- **Application:** Parallel feeders, ring main systems, generator protection (reverse power).

#### F. Buchholz Relay Protection (Transformer Internal Fault)

- **What it monitors:** Accumulation of gas, oil surge, and pressure inside a transformer tank.
- **Operating principle:** Slow-developing faults generate gas (triggers *alarm*). Sudden faults cause oil surge (triggers *trip*).
- **Application:** Oil-immersed power transformers (rated > 500 kVA, as per IS 2026).

#### G. Surge / Lightning Protection

- **What it monitors:** Transient over-voltages from lightning or switching.
- **Operating principle:** Lightning arresters (metal oxide varistors) clamp voltage to safe levels.
- **Application:** Substations, overhead lines, building rooftops.

#### H. Under/Over Voltage, Under/Over Frequency Protection

- **What it monitors:** Deviations of voltage or frequency from nominal values.
- **Operating principle:** Trip CB if $V < V_{\min}$ or $V > V_{\max}$ or $f < f_{\min}$ or $f > f_{\max}$.
- **Application:** Generator protection, load shedding, anti-islanding for DG sets.

#### I. Reverse Power Protection

- **What it monitors:** Direction of active power flow.
- **Operating principle:** Trip if power flows *from* busbar *into* the generator (motoring condition).
- **Application:** Parallel-running generators, interconnections.

#### J. Thermal Overload Protection

- **What it monitors:** Heating effect of current over time ($I^2 t$).
- **Operating principle:** Bimetallic strip bends, or thermal memory model in numerical relay.
- **Application:** Motors, transformers.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examination**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM) | $\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{\text{fault}}}{I_{\text{primary pickup}}}$ | $I_{\text{fault}}$ is secondary current | A / A | Overcurrent relay coordination |
| 2 | Operating Time (IDMT) | $t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot K}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}$ | $K$ = TMS (Time Multiplier Setting) | seconds | IDMT relay characteristic |
| 3 | Operating Time (DTOC) | $t = t_{\text{set}}$ (constant) | Fixed delay | seconds | DTOC relay |
| 4 | Differential Current | $I_d = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$ | CT secondary currents | A | Differential protection |
| 5 | Bias Current (Biased Diff.) | $I_b = \dfrac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2}$ | Restraining current | A | Percentage differential relay |
| 6 | Through-fault Stability | $I_d \leq k \cdot I_b$ | $k$ = bias slope | A | Stability check |
| 7 | Distance Impedance | $Z = \dfrac{V_{\text{phase}}}{I_{\text{phase}}}$ | Line-to-neutral voltage and phase current | $\Omega$ | Distance relay reach |
| 8 | Zero Sequence Current | $I_0 = \dfrac{I_a + I_b + I_c}{3}$ | Phase currents | A | Earth-fault detection |
| 9 | Positive Sequence Current | $I_1 = \dfrac{I_a + a \cdot I_b + a^2 \cdot I_c}{3}$ | $a = e^{j120^\circ}$ | A | Symmetrical components |
| 10 | Thermal Heating | $E = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$ | Current, Resistance, Time | J | $I^2 t$ characteristic |
| 11 | Arc Flash Energy | $E = 4.184 \cdot C_f \cdot E_n \cdot \dfrac{t_A}{D^2}$ | $C_f$ = cal/cm², $E_n$ = incident energy, $D$ = distance, $t_A$ = arcing time | cal/cm² | PPE selection |
| 12 | Breaker Operating Time | $t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{relay}} + t_{\text{CB}}$ | Relay + Breaker time | seconds | Total fault clearance time |
| 13 | CT Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Primary to secondary turns | – | Current scaling |
| 14 | Voltage Pickup Ratio | $\text{VPR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fault}}}{V_{\text{rated}}}$ | Operating vs. rated voltage | – | Voltage relay setting |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid & Microgrids:** Differential + distance protection is the gold standard for 400 kV and 765 kV Indian transmission corridors.
- **Industrial Safety:** Earth-fault relays prevent electrocution of personnel in 415 V industrial panels.
- **Transformer Longevity:** Buchholz relays detect incipient faults (turn-to-turn shorts) weeks before catastrophic failure.
- **Renewable Integration:** Reverse power protection is essential for solar/wind inverters connected to the grid to prevent islanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Operating Time of IDMT Overcurrent Relay

The **Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT)** characteristic is derived empirically to match the heating curve of conductors and equipment. The standard KTU/IS formulation is:

#### Step 1: Define the Standard IDMT Equation (IEC 60255-151)

$$
t = \frac{K \cdot \beta}{\left(\dfrac{I}{I_p}\right)^\alpha - 1} + t_{\min}
$$

Where:
- $t$ = operating time of the relay (seconds)
- $K$ = Time Multiplier Setting (TMS), typically 0.05 to 1.2
- $\beta$ = constant (= 0.14 for standard inverse curve)
- $\alpha$ = characteristic constant (= 0.02 for standard inverse)
- $I$ = secondary fault current
- $I_p$ = secondary pickup current
- $t_{\min}$ = minimum operating time (often 0.1 s)

#### Step 2: Identify PSM and Simplify

By definition, $\text{PSM} = I / I_p$. Substituting:

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \cdot K}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}
$$

#### Step 3: Solve a Sample KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A relay with CT ratio of 400/5 is set on 125% plug setting and TMS = 0.4. The fault current is 4000 A. Find the operating time.

**Given:**
- CT ratio = 400 / 5
- Plug setting = 125% → $I_p$ (primary) = $0.125 \times 400 = 50$ A
- $I_p$ (secondary) = $50 / 80 = 0.625$ A
- $I_{\text{fault}}$ (primary) = 4000 A
- $I_{\text{fault}}$ (secondary) = $4000 / 80 = 50$ A
- $K$ = 0.4

**Step 3a: Compute PSM**

$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{\text{fault (secondary)}}}{I_{p \text{ (secondary)}}} = \frac{50}{0.625} = 80
$$

**Step 3b: Apply the IDMT formula**

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.4}{80^{0.02} - 1}
$$

**Step 3c: Evaluate $80^{0.02}$**

$$
80^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \cdot \ln 80} = e^{0.02 \cdot 4.3820} = e^{0.08764} = 1.0916
$$

**Step 3d: Final computation**

$$
t = \frac{0.056}{1.0916 - 1} = \frac{0.056}{0.0916} = 0.6114 \text{ s}
$$

**Answer:** Operating time $\approx$ **0.61 seconds**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Protection Operating Condition

For a differential scheme with two CTs (one at each end of the protected zone):

#### Step 1: Define Operating and Restraining Currents

$$
I_d = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert \quad \text{(Operating or differential current)}
$$

$$
I_r = \frac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2} \quad \text{(Restraining current)}
$$

Where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are secondary currents from the two CTs (referred to the relay side).

#### Step 2: Apply the Percentage Bias Characteristic

A biased differential relay operates if:

$$
I_d > k \cdot I_r + I_{\text{min}}
$$

Where:
- $k$ = bias slope (typically 20% to 40%)
- $I_{\text{min}}$ = minimum pickup current (sensitivity setting)

#### Step 3: External vs. Internal Fault Analysis

- **External (Through) Fault:** $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow in the same direction (both entering the relay) but may differ due to CT errors. $I_d$ is small, $I_r$ is large → Relay restrained.
- **Internal Fault:** $I_1$ enters the zone while $I_2$ also enters (or is zero). $I_d$ becomes very large → Relay operates.

### 3.3 Derivation: Distance Protection Zone Settings

For a transmission line of impedance $Z_L$ per phase:

#### Step 1: Zone 1 Reach

$$
Z_{Z1} = 0.8 \cdot Z_L
$$

Operates instantaneously. Set at 80% to avoid overreach due to CT/PT errors and infeed effects.

#### Step 2: Zone 2 Reach

$$
Z_{Z2} = 1.2 \cdot Z_L \quad \text{(covers 100% of own line + 50% of next line)}
$$

Operates after a delay of typically **0.3 to 0.4 seconds**.

#### Step 3: Zone 3 Reach

$$
Z_{Z3} = 1.5 \cdot Z_L \quad \text{(covers Zone 2 reach + 25% of the next-next line)}
$$

Operates after a delay of typically **0.8 to 1.0 seconds**. Acts as backup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 80% in Zone 1?** Because under fault conditions, instrument transformer errors can cause the apparent impedance to be *lower* than the actual value, leading to false tripping on adjacent lines (overreach). The 20% margin guarantees selectivity.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Protection Types

| Protection Type | Detected Fault | Operating Time | Zone | CT/PT Required | Best Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Overcurrent** | Overload, Short Circuit | 0.1 – 1.0 s | Distribution | CT only | Radial feeders, backup |
| **Earth Fault** | SLG fault | 0.1 – 0.5 s | Distribution | CT (residual) | Grounded systems |
| **Differential** | Internal winding fault | 10 – 30 ms | Single equipment | 2 sets of CTs | Generator, Transformer |
| **Distance** | All faults (line) | 20 – 100 ms (Z1) | Transmission line | CT + PT | EHV transmission |
| **Buchholz** | Incipient + sudden internal | 0.1 – 1.0 s | Transformer | None (mechanical) | Oil-immersed XFMR |
| **Surge / Lightning** | Transient over-voltage | < 5 µs | Substation | None (MOV) | Substations, lines |
| **Reverse Power** | Motoring of generator | 1 – 5 s | Generator | CT + PT | Parallel generators |
| **Thermal** | Sustained overload | Seconds to minutes | Motor, cable | CT + thermal model | Motors, transformers |
| **Directional** | Forward fault only | 0.1 – 0.5 s | Looped network | CT + PT | Parallel feeders, rings |
| **Under/Over V** | Voltage deviation | 0.5 – 5 s | Busbar, DG | PT only | Generator, load shedding |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Numerical Protection Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POWERSYSTEM[Power System HV Bus] --> CT[Current Transformer CT]
    POWERSYSTEM --> PT[Potential Transformer PT]
    CT --> AAF[Anti Aliasing Filter AAF]
    PT --> AAF
    AAF --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    ADC --> MPU[Microprocessor / DSP MPU]
    MPU --> ALGO[Protection Algorithm Library]
    ALGO --> DECISION{Threshold Check}
    DECISION -->|Fault Detected| TRIP[TRIP Command]
    DECISION -->|No Fault| HOLD[Hold No Operation]
    TRIP --> CB[Breaker Trip Coil]
    CB --> ISOLATE[Isolate Faulted Zone]
    MPU --> HMI[Human Machine Interface HMI]
    MPU --> COM[Communication Port IEC 61850]
    COM --> SCADA[Central SCADA Control Room]
```

### 4.2 Protection Zones of a Typical Power System

```mermaid
graph LR
    G[Generator G1] -->|Zone Gen1| TG1
    TG1[Step Up Transformer T1] -->|Zone Tx1| B1
    B1[Busbar B1 220kV] -->|Zone Bus1| L1
    L1[Transmission Line L1] -->|Zone Line1| B2
    B2[Busbar B2] -->|Zone Bus2| TG2
    TG2[Step Down Transformer T2] -->|Zone Tx2| B3
    B3[Busbar B3 11kV] -->|Zone Dist1| FEEDER
    FEEDER[Distribution Feeder F1] -->|Zone F1| LOAD[Consumer Load]
```

### 4.3 IDMT Relay Operating Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fault Current LOW 2x Pickup] --> B[Operating Time HIGH approx 10 s]
    C[Fault Current MEDIUM 5x Pickup] --> D[Operating Time MEDIUM approx 3 s]
    E[Fault Current HIGH 10x Pickup] --> F[Operating Time LOW approx 1 s]
    G[Fault Current VERY HIGH 20x Pickup] --> H[Operating Time MIN approx 0.3 s]
    B --> I[IDMT INVERSE CURVE]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 Transformer Differential Protection Scheme (Merz-Price)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV[HV Side] -->|CT1| RELAY[Differential Relay 87T]
    LV[LV Side] -->|CT2| RELAY
    RELAY -->|Trip Signal| TRIPCB[HV Breaker 52HV]
    RELAY -->|Trip Signal| TRIPCB2[LV Breaker 52LV]
    RELAY --> ALARM[Alarm 87T-Alarm]
    RELAY --> ANN[Annunciator]
    RELAY --> BIAS[Biased Restraint Coil]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Hint:** In the Merz-Price scheme, both HV and LV side CTs are connected such that, under normal load, secondary currents circulate *without* passing through the operating coil. Only during an *internal* fault does the difference current activate the trip coil.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. List any three Types of Protection used in power systems and state the parameter each monitors.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Overcurrent Protection** – monitors magnitude of line current; operates when current exceeds pickup.
2. **Differential Protection** – monitors the difference between currents entering and leaving a zone; operates for internal faults.
3. **Distance Protection** – monitors the impedance (V/I) seen by the relay; operates when impedance falls within a pre-defined reach zone.
*(Each type = 1 mark)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. Why is Zone 1 of a distance relay set at 80% of the line impedance and not 100%?**

**Model Answer:**
- Zone 1 is set at 80% of the line impedance to provide a **safety margin of 20%** against **overreach errors** caused by:
  - CT ratio and phase errors (± 5% each)
  - PT ratio and phase errors
  - Fault resistance (arc resistance)
  - Infeed from parallel lines
- Setting at 100% would cause the relay to *incorrectly trip* for faults on the *adjacent* line section, violating **selectivity**.
*(Margin explanation = 2 marks, selectivity reason = 1 mark)*

---

### Part B – 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **principle of Merz-Price differential protection** as applied to a 3-phase transformer. Discuss the role of **biasing** in percentage differential relays. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An IDMT overcurrent relay has a CT ratio of 600/5 A, is set on **150% plug setting** with **TMS = 0.3**. Calculate the operating time of the relay for a fault current of **6000 A**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

- The Merz-Price scheme uses two sets of CTs, one on each side of the protected equipment (e.g., transformer).
- Under **normal operation** or **external fault**, current entering equals current leaving, so the secondary currents circulate in the pilot wire loop; the differential current in the operating coil is **zero**.
- Under an **internal fault**, the currents do not balance, and the difference flows through the **operating coil**, which trips the circuit breaker.
- **Problem of CT saturation under external faults** causes a false differential current → To prevent this, **percentage bias (restraining) relays** are used, which require a larger $I_d$ as $I_r$ increases. The bias slope (typically 20%–40%) ensures stability. *(7 marks distributed: 2 for principle, 2 for sketch, 2 for biasing, 1 for conclusion)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- CT ratio = 600/5
- Primary pickup = $0.50 \times 600 = 300$ A *(Note: 150% of 600 = 900 A; for a relay, this is interpreted as 150% of CT primary = 900 A — let me re-interpret carefully.)*

> **Correction (Standard KTU Interpretation):** Plug setting is given as a percentage of CT secondary rated current multiplied by CT ratio. Here, plug setting 150% means the relay picks up at 150% of the CT primary rated current = 1.5 × 600 = 900 A.

- $I_p$ (primary) = 900 A
- $I_p$ (secondary) = 900 × (5/600) = 7.5 A
- $I_{\text{fault}}$ (primary) = 6000 A
- $I_{\text{fault}}$ (secondary) = 6000 × (5/600) = 50 A
- TMS = 0.3

**Compute PSM:**

$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{50}{7.5} = 6.667
$$

**Apply IDMT formula:**

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \times 0.3}{6.667^{0.02} - 1}
$$

**Evaluate $6.667^{0.02}$:**

$$
6.667^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \cdot \ln 6.667} = e^{0.02 \cdot 1.8971} = e^{0.03794} = 1.03867
$$

**Final calculation:**

$$
t = \frac{0.042}{1.03867 - 1} = \frac{0.042}{0.03867} = 1.0861 \text{ s}
$$

**Answer:** Operating time $\approx$ **1.09 seconds**. *(7 marks: 1 for given data, 2 for PSM, 2 for IDMT setup, 1 for evaluation, 1 for final answer)*

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Buchholz relay** principle. How does it differentiate between **minor incipient faults** and **major sudden faults**? Mention any two **limitations** of this relay. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 3-zone distance relay is set on a 100 km, 220 kV transmission line. The line impedance is **0.4 Ω/km per phase**. Determine the **reach settings** of Zones 1, 2, and 3 in ohms, and the corresponding **impedance relay pickup** if the line current under fault is 1000 A. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

- The Buchholz relay is a gas-actuated relay installed in the **pipe between the transformer tank and the conservator**.
- It works on **two principles**:
  1. **Gas accumulation (Alarm)**: Slow-developing faults (e.g., insulation breakdown) decompose oil and release gases (H₂, CH₄, C₂H₂). These gases rise and collect in the upper chamber, tilting a **mercury switch** to sound an **alarm**.
  2. **Oil surge (Trip)**: Sudden internal faults cause rapid oil movement, pushing a **vane** that operates a **trip contact** to disconnect the transformer.
- **Differentiating mechanism:**
  - Incipient fault → gas produced → slow movement → **only alarm** (lower float).
  - Major fault → oil surge → high velocity (1.0–1.5 m/s) → **trip** (upper vane).
- **Two limitations:**
  1. Applicable only to **oil-filled, conservator-type** transformers (not for dry-type).
  2. Cannot detect faults **below the Buchholz relay level** (e.g., in bushings).
  3. Slow response for certain internal faults.

*(7 marks: 2 for principle, 2 for differentiation, 2 for limitations, 1 for sketch mention)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Line length $L$ = 100 km
- Line impedance per km = 0.4 Ω/phase
- Total line impedance $Z_L$ = 100 × 0.4 = 40 Ω

**Zone 1 Reach (80%):**

$$
Z_{Z1} = 0.8 \times 40 = 32 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Zone 2 Reach (120%):**

$$
Z_{Z2} = 1.2 \times 40 = 48 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Zone 3 Reach (150%):**

$$
Z_{Z3} = 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Voltage at relay pickup (Zone 1) at fault current 1000 A:**

$$
V_{Z1} = I \times Z_{Z1} = 1000 \times 32 = 32{,}000 \text{ V} = 32 \text{ kV (phase)}
$$

**Line-to-line voltage (multiply by $\sqrt{3}$):**

$$
V_{Z1,LL} = \sqrt{3} \times 32 = 55.4 \text{ kV}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 for given, 2 for Z1, 2 for Z2 and Z3, 2 for voltage computation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** In IDMT problems, students often confuse **primary** and **secondary** pickup. Always **convert fault current to secondary** before computing PSM.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In Buchholz relay answers, students forget to distinguish between **alarm** (gas) and **trip** (oil surge) — KTU awards 1 mark for this distinction.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In distance relay problems, students use **100%** for Zone 1 instead of **80%** — instant 2-mark loss.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ when converting phase voltage to line voltage.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not mentioning **CT saturation** in differential protection — this is a frequently asked sub-question.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Types of Protection** are classified by **monitored parameter**: current, voltage, impedance, frequency, pressure, temperature, or power direction.
- ✅ The **5 S's** of protection are **Selectivity, Sensitivity, Speed, Stability, Reliability** — mention all five in descriptive answers.
- ✅ **Overcurrent Protection** uses **PSM (Plug Setting Multiplier)** and **TMS (Time Multiplier Setting)**; the standard IDMT equation is:

$$
t = \frac{0.14 \cdot K}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}
$$

- ✅ **Differential Protection** trips when $\vert I_1 - I_2 \vert \neq 0$ inside the protected zone; **percentage biasing** prevents false trips during CT saturation on external faults.
- ✅ **Distance Protection** uses **three zones**: Zone 1 (80%, instantaneous), Zone 2 (120%, 0.3–0.4 s), Zone 3 (150%, 0.8–1.0 s).
- ✅ **Buchholz Relay**: Gas → Alarm; Oil surge → Trip. Only for **oil-immersed, conservator-type** transformers.
- ✅ **Standard Fault Clearance Time** for HV systems: **< 100 ms** (3 cycles at 50 Hz).
- ✅ **Earth Fault** = Zero-sequence current detection; $I_0 = (I_a + I_b + I_c)/3$.
- ✅ **Reverse Power** protection prevents **motoring of generators** during prime-mover failure.
- ✅ **Surge / Lightning arresters** clamp over-voltages in **microseconds** using metal-oxide varistors.
- ✅ KTU frequently asks numericals on **IDMT operating time** — memorize the formula and the constant **0.14** and **0.02**.
- ✅ Always state **units** in your final answer (seconds, ohms, amps, volts).
- ✅ In distance relay problems, always convert **phase voltage to line voltage** using $\sqrt{3}$ when needed.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Relay
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distance Relay

# ⚡ DISTANCE RELAY — Comprehensive KTU 2024 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🎯 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Distance Relay** is a non-unit type protective relay whose operation depends upon the *apparent impedance* (or electrical distance) measured between the relay location and the point of fault on a transmission line. The relay compares the voltage at the relay bus with the current flowing through it, computes the ratio $Z_{apparent} = \dfrac{V}{I}$, and issues a trip command when this apparent impedance falls within a pre-defined operating characteristic on the **R–X (Resistance–Reactance) plane**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** *"A distance relay is a relay which functions only when the impedance (or corresponding distance) to the fault is less than the predetermined value."*

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "The Highway Toll Booth Analogy"

Imagine you are driving on a long highway and there is a toll booth at every 100 km. Each toll booth has a thermometer-like gauge that reads the **"electrical distance"** to an accident ahead.

- If the accident (fault) is **far away** (> 200 km) → the toll booth reads a *high number* → **No trip**.
- If the accident is **close by** (< 100 km) → the toll booth reads a *low number* → **TRIP immediately!**

In the same way, the distance relay measures how "far" the fault is by computing $Z = V/I$. A fault close to the relay causes **voltage to collapse** and **current to rise**, making $Z$ small — which triggers the relay. Unlike overcurrent relays that depend only on current magnitude (and therefore can't tell *where* the fault is), the distance relay can **discriminate between near and far faults**, making it the backbone of modern transmission line protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Distance"?** Because the apparent impedance $Z = V/I$ is *proportional* to the physical length of line between the relay and the fault (since line impedance per km is nearly constant). So measuring impedance is essentially measuring distance.

### 📌 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Reach** | The distance (in km or % of line) up to which the relay will operate |
| **Apparent Impedance** | $Z = V/I$ as "seen" by the relay for a fault |
| **Operating Quantity** | Impedance, Reactance, or Admittance (depending on relay type) |
| **Reach Point** | The exact electrical location on the line where the relay just begins to operate |
| **Stepped Protection** | Using multiple zones (Z1, Z2, Z3) with progressive time delays |
