### 🏗️ Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard Frequencies:** Relay is designed for **50 Hz / 60 Hz** power system operation.
- **CT Ratio (Typical):** 1000/5 A, 2000/1 A — provides proportional current to the relay.
- **VT Ratio (Typical):** 110 kV / 110 V, 220 kV / 110 V.
- **Reach Setting Accuracy:** ±**5%** (as per IEC 60255 standard).
- **Zone 1 Reach:** **80%–90%** of protected line impedance.
- **Zone 2 Reach:** **120%–150%** of protected line impedance.
- **Zone 3 Reach:** **Next adjacent line + 25% of following line**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 Why Do We Need Distance Relays?

Overcurrent relays **cannot distinguish** between a fault at the far end of a long line and a fault at the near end of a short line — both may have similar current magnitudes. Distance relays solve this problem by using **both voltage and current**, giving a measure of impedance proportional to fault location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Working Principle:** $Z_{apparent} = \dfrac{V_{relay}}{I_{relay}}$. When this falls below the *reach setting*, the relay trips.

### ⚙️ Operating Principle — The R–X Diagram

The distance relay plots the apparent impedance on an **R–X plane** (Resistance along horizontal, Reactance along vertical). The locus of all fault points forms a curve/region, and the relay operates when this locus enters its *characteristic curve*.

### 🔬 Types of Distance Relays (KTU Syllabus Core)

| # | Type | Operating Quantity | Characteristic on R-X Plane | Best For |
|---|------|-------------------|----------------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Impedance Relay** | $\vert Z \vert$ | Circle centered at origin | Phase faults, short lines |
| 2 | **Reactance Relay** | $X$ | Straight line parallel to R-axis | **Arc resistance** resistance to faults (high arc resistance) |
| 3 | **Mho (Admittance) Relay** | $\vert Y \vert = \vert I/V \vert$ | Circle **passing through origin** | Long EHV lines; inherently directional |
| 4 | **Quadrilateral Relay** | Combined R & X | Quadrilateral shape | Modern numerical relays; best for arc resistance immunity |

### 🧮 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $Z_{apparent} = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Fundamental apparent impedance | $\Omega$ |
| $Z_{line} = z \times L$ | Line impedance = impedance/km × length | $\Omega$ |
| $R_{reach} = \dfrac{V^2}{P_{max}}$ (approx.) | Approximate reach from load | $\Omega$ |
| $V = I \times Z$ | Voltage across fault | V |
| $X_{reach} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}} \sin\phi$ | Reactance component | $\Omega$ |
| Operating condition for **Impedance Relay** | $\vert Z \vert \leq Z_{setting}$ | — |
| Operating condition for **Reactance Relay** | $X \leq X_{setting}$ | — |
| Operating condition for **Mho Relay** | $\vert Y \vert \leq Y_{setting}$, i.e., $\dfrac{I}{V} \cos(\theta - \phi) \geq Y_{set}$ | — |
| Zone 1 reach | $(0.8 - 0.9) \times Z_{line}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Zone 2 reach | $(1.2 - 1.5) \times Z_{line}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Zone 3 reach | $Z_{line} + Z_{next\,line} + 0.25 \times Z_{next-next}$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Never use bare vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables. Always use `\vert` for absolute value notation to prevent syntax breakage.

### 🔍 Detailed Theory of Each Type

#### (a) **Impedance Relay (Circle Centered at Origin)**
- Measures only the **magnitude** of impedance.
- Has a circular operating region of radius $Z_{setting}$ centered at origin.
- **Non-directional** by itself (operates for faults behind too).
- Requires a directional unit to be made useful.

#### (b) **Reactance Relay (Straight Line)**
- Measures **only the reactance** $X$ component.
- Operates when $X \leq X_{setting}$.
- Characteristic is a horizontal line on R-X plane.
- **Immune to arc resistance** (arc resistance adds to R, not X).
- Best for protection against **high resistance faults** (e.g., ground faults).
- Directional unit must be added separately.

#### (c) **Mho Relay (Admittance Relay — Circle through Origin)**
- Measures **admittance** magnitude: $\vert Y \vert = \vert I/V \vert$.
- Characteristic is a circle of diameter $Z_{setting}$ **passing through origin**.
- **Self-directional** — inherently discriminates forward vs backward faults.
- Most widely used in **EHV transmission lines**.
- Derived from: $I \cos(\theta - \phi) \geq \dfrac{I_{set} V_{set}}{V_{actual}}$ style equation.

#### (d) **Quadrilateral Relay**
- A modern numerical relay characteristic.
- Independent reach settings in R and X axes.
- Forms a quadrilateral shape on R-X plane.
- Maximum flexibility; ideal for **series-compensated lines** and **high arc-resistance scenarios**.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **EHV Transmission Lines** | 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV line protection |
| **Series-Compensated Lines** | Quadrilateral characteristic is preferred |
| **Long Feeders** | Mho relay is standard |
| **Distribution Feeders** | Quadrilateral + ground distance elements |
| **HVDC Systems** | Distance protection for converter transformers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📐 Derivation 1: Apparent Impedance Seen by the Relay

Consider a transmission line of impedance $Z_{line}$ per phase, with a fault at distance $x$ km from the relay. Let the relay bus voltage be $V$ and the current flowing through the relay be $I$.

The voltage at the relay bus equals the voltage drop across the line section from relay to fault:

$$
V = I \times Z_{section}
$$

where $Z_{section} = z \times x$ (impedance per km × fault distance).

The **apparent impedance seen by the relay** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{apparent} &= \frac{V}{I} \\
Z_{apparent} &= \frac{I \cdot z \cdot x}{I} \\
Z_{apparent} &= z \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Voltage and current both have $I$ as common factor. The $I$ cancels, leaving $z \times x$, which is **directly proportional to fault distance $x$**. This is why we call it "distance" relay.

### 📐 Derivation 2: Operating Characteristic of an Impedance Relay

For an impedance relay, the operating condition is that the magnitude of apparent impedance must be less than or equal to the setting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|Z_{apparent}| &\leq |Z_{setting}| \\
\left|\frac{V}{I}\right| &\leq K \\
\frac{V^2}{I^2} &\leq K^2 \\
V^2 &\leq K^2 \cdot I^2 \\
V &\leq K \cdot I
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the equation of a **circle** in the R-X plane centered at origin with radius $K$. Any fault impedance lying inside this circle causes the relay to operate.

### 📐 Derivation 3: Reactance Relay Characteristic Derivation

For a reactance relay, the operating condition is based on the **reactance** component only:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{apparent} &= X_{setting} \\
\frac{V}{I} \sin\phi &= X_{setting}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\phi$ is the phase angle between $V$ and $I$.

Rearranging for the voltage:

$$
V = I \cdot X_{setting} / \sin\phi
$$

This is the equation of a **horizontal straight line** on the R-X plane at $X = X_{setting}$, parallel to the R-axis.

### 📐 Derivation 4: Mho Relay Characteristic Derivation

A Mho relay measures admittance. The operating condition is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|Y| &\geq |Y_{setting}| \\
\left|\frac{I}{V}\right| &\geq K \\
\frac{I}{V} \cos(\theta - \phi) &\geq K
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ is the maximum torque angle (MTA) of the relay and $\phi$ is the phase angle between V and I.

In polar form on the R-X plane, this reduces to the equation of a **circle passing through the origin** with diameter equal to $Z_{setting} = 1/K$.

### 📐 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A 220 kV, 50 Hz transmission line has impedance of $0.4 \, \Omega$/km. The relay is set to cover **80%** of the line. If the line is **100 km long**, calculate:
1. The relay reach setting in ohms.
2. The relay reach in km.
3. The apparent impedance for a fault at 70 km.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Line total impedance:**

$$
Z_{line} = z \times L = 0.4 \times 100 = 40 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Relay reach (80% of line):**

$$
Z_{reach} = 0.8 \times Z_{line} = 0.8 \times 40 = 32 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Relay reach in km:**

$$
\text{Reach (km)} = 0.8 \times L = 0.8 \times 100 = 80 \, km
$$

**Step 4 — Apparent impedance for fault at 70 km:**

$$
Z_{apparent} = 0.4 \times 70 = 28 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$
Z_{apparent} = 28 \, \Omega < Z_{reach} = 32 \, \Omega \Rightarrow \text{RELAY OPERATES!}
$$

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Relay Types

| Parameter | Impedance | Reactance | Mho | Quadrilateral |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----|---------------|
| Measures | $\vert Z \vert$ | $X$ only | $\vert Y \vert$ | R & X separately |
| Shape | Circle (origin) | Horizontal line | Circle (through origin) | Quadrilateral |
| Directional? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Self | ✅ Yes |
| Arc resistance immunity | ❌ Poor | ✅ Best | ⚠ Moderate | ✅ Excellent |
| Used for | Short lines | Ground faults | EHV long lines | Modern numerical |
| Time delay needed | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    E -->|If Z greater than Setting| G[No Trip Signal]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a distance relay. Why is it preferred over an overcurrent relay for transmission line protection?*

**Model Answer:**
A distance relay is a protective relay whose operation depends upon the apparent impedance ($Z = V/I$) between the relay location and the fault point. It is preferred over overcurrent relays for transmission line protection because:

1. Overcurrent relays cannot discriminate between near and far faults (current magnitudes can be similar for both).
2. Distance relays measure impedance, which is **directly proportional to fault distance**, enabling accurate fault location.
3. Their reach setting is independent of source impedance variations.
4. They provide **stepped zones of protection** (Zone 1, 2, 3) with progressive time delays.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the four types of distance relays and state the operating characteristic of each on the R–X plane.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Type | Characteristic on R-X Plane |
|---|------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Impedance Relay | Circle centered at origin with radius equal to reach setting |
| 2 | Reactance Relay | Straight line parallel to R-axis at X = X_setting |
| 3 | Mho (Admittance) Relay | Circle passing through origin with diameter equal to reach setting |
| 4 | Quadrilateral Relay | Quadrilateral shape with independent R and X reach settings |

---

### 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of an **impedance relay** with a neat R–X diagram. Discuss its limitations and explain how an **induction cup type** construction achieves the operating characteristic. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of R–X diagrams, compare the operating characteristics of **impedance, reactance, and mho relays**. State one application of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction:**
The impedance relay is typically built using an **induction cup** type construction with four poles. It has:
- A **current-driven coil** on one pair of poles (producing flux proportional to current $I$).
- A **voltage-driven coil** on the other pair of poles (producing flux proportional to voltage $V$).
- An aluminum cup rotor with a contact mechanism.

**Working Principle:**
The torques produced are:
- Operating torque: $T_{op} \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos(\phi - \theta)$
- Restraining torque: $T_{rest} \propto V^2$ (or $I^2$ depending on design)

where $\theta$ is the relay torque angle and $\phi$ is the phase angle between $V$ and $I$.

The relay operates when:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{op} &\geq T_{rest} \\
V \cdot I \cdot \cos(\phi - \theta) &\geq K_1 V^2 \\
\frac{I}{V} \cos(\phi - \theta) &\geq K_1 \\
\frac{1}{Z} \cos(\phi - \theta) &\geq K_1 \\
Z &\leq \frac{1}{K_1} = Z_{setting}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Limitations:**
1. **Non-directional** — operates for faults on either side of the relay.
2. Affected by **arc resistance** — arc resistance adds to fault impedance, may cause under-reach or over-reach.
3. Reach varies with **source impedance**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Impedance Relay:**
- Operates when $|Z| \leq Z_{setting}$.
- Characteristic: circle centered at origin with radius $Z_{setting}$.
- **Application:** Short transmission lines, phase fault protection (with directional unit added).

**Reactance Relay:**
- Operates when $X \leq X_{setting}$.
- Characteristic: horizontal straight line parallel to R-axis at $X = X_{setting}$.
- **Application:** Ground fault protection where arc resistance is high.

**Mho Relay:**
- Operates when $|Y| \geq Y_{setting}$, equivalently $|Z| \leq |Z_{setting}|$ with directional property.
- Characteristic: circle passing through origin with diameter $Z_{setting}$.
- **Application:** Long EHV transmission lines (inherently directional).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **stepped distance protection** with a clear diagram showing **three zones of protection**. State the reach and time delay setting for each zone. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV, 50 Hz transmission line has a positive sequence impedance of $(0.05 + j0.4) \, \Omega$/km. The line length is 200 km. If the distance relay is set for **80%** of the line in Zone 1:
- (i) Calculate the relay reach in ohms.
- (ii) Calculate the reach in km.
- (iii) Determine whether the relay will operate for a fault at 150 km from the relay.
- (iv) Comment on Zone 2 setting if it must cover the entire protected line plus 50% of the next line of 150 km length. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Stepped Distance Protection Concept:**
To provide complete protection with backup, the distance relay is set in **three concentric zones** with progressively wider reach and longer time delays.

**Zone 1:**
- Reach: **80%–90%** of protected line impedance
- Time delay: **Instantaneous** (~20–40 ms)
- Purpose: Primary protection of own line

**Zone 2:**
- Reach: **120%–150%** of own line (covers full line + 50% of adjacent line)
- Time delay: **0.3 to 0.5 seconds**
- Purpose: Backup for Zone 1 of own line and primary for close-in faults of adjacent line

**Zone 3:**
- Reach: Own line + next line + 25% of next-next line
- Time delay: **1.0 to 1.5 seconds**
- Purpose: Remote backup for adjacent line faults

(Diagram reference: see Diagram 2 in Section 4)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Relay reach in ohms:**
Total line impedance:
$$
Z_{line} = (0.05 + j0.4) \times 200 = (10 + j80) \, \Omega
$$

Magnitude:
$$
|Z_{line}| = \sqrt{10^2 + 80^2} = \sqrt{100 + 6400} = \sqrt{6500} \approx 80.62 \, \Omega
$$

Zone 1 reach (80%):
$$
Z_{reach} = 0.8 \times 80.62 = 64.5 \, \Omega
$$

**(ii) Reach in km:**
$$
L_{reach} = 0.8 \times 200 = 160 \, km
$$

**(iii) Operation check for fault at 150 km:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{apparent} &= (0.05 + j0.4) \times 150 \\
&= (7.5 + j60) \, \Omega \\
|Z_{apparent}| &= \sqrt{7.5^2 + 60^2} = \sqrt{56.25 + 3600} = \sqrt{3656.25} \approx 60.47 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparison: $Z_{apparent} = 60.47 \, \Omega < Z_{reach} = 64.5 \, \Omega$.

✅ **Yes, the relay WILL operate** (fault is within Zone 1 reach).

**(iv) Zone 2 setting:**
Zone 2 must cover full own line + 50% of next line (150 km):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{Zone2} &= Z_{own} + 0.5 \times Z_{next} \\
&= (10 + j80) + 0.5 \times (0.05 + j0.4) \times 150 \\
&= (10 + j80) + 0.5 \times (7.5 + j60) \\
&= (10 + j80) + (3.75 + j30) \\
&= (13.75 + j110) \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnitude:
$$
|Z_{Zone2}| = \sqrt{13.75^2 + 110^2} = \sqrt{189.06 + 12100} \approx 110.86 \, \Omega
$$

Typical Zone 2 setting: ~**1.2 to 1.5 times** Zone 1 setting → here ~**1.72 times**, slightly higher than convention. Practical answer: **Zone 2 reach ≈ 110.86 $\Omega$** covering full line and half of adjacent line.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to magnitude** — Writing $Z_{apparent} = (10 + j80) \, \Omega$ without taking magnitude $\sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Zone 1 reach** — Many students write 100% — but Zone 1 is **deliberately 80%** to avoid over-reaching into adjacent line.
> 3. **Mixing impedance with admittance** — In Mho relay, operating quantity is $|Y|$, **not** $|Z|$ (despite the characteristic looking similar). Direction matters!
> 4. **Forgetting to state units** — $V$ in kV and $I$ in A give $Z$ in $\Omega$, but mixing scales (like V in V and I in kA) gives wrong answers.
> 5. **Not drawing R-X diagram** — A 7-mark question without the diagram loses at least 2 marks. Always include the characteristic on R-X plane.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Distance relay operation** depends on apparent impedance $Z = V/I$ falling below a setting.

✅ **Three main types**: Impedance (non-directional, circle at origin), Reactance (X-only, horizontal line, immune to arc resistance), Mho (self-directional, circle through origin, used for EHV lines).

✅ **Quadrilateral relay** is the modern numerical standard offering independent R and X reach.

✅ **Three Zones of Protection:**
- Zone 1: 80% of line, instantaneous
- Zone 2: 120–150% of line, 0.3–0.5 s delay
- Zone 3: Own + next + 25% of next-next, 1.0–1.5 s delay

✅ **Apparent impedance is proportional to fault distance** — this is the key insight.

✅ **Mho relay is self-directional** — no external directional unit needed.

✅ **Reactance relay is immune to arc resistance** — best for ground faults.

✅ **Impedance relay requires directional unit** — non-directional otherwise.

✅ **Reach Setting Accuracy** is ±5% as per IEC 60255.

✅ **Fault Resistance** (arc) adds to R-component — affects impedance/mho relays more than reactance relay.

✅ Always convert to **magnitude** $|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ before comparison with reach setting.

✅ **Numerical relays** (microprocessor-based) implement all four characteristics digitally with great flexibility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Differential Relay** is a protective relay whose operation is based on the *vector difference* between two (or more) similar electrical quantities (typically currents) entering and leaving the protected zone. It operates when the phasor difference of these quantities exceeds a predetermined threshold value. The relay embodies **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the boundary of the protected zone — under healthy conditions, the sum of currents entering equals the sum leaving, and the relay sees zero spill current.

Mathematically, the operating quantity (spill current) is:

$$I_{spill} = \vert \vec{I}_1 - \vec{I}_2 \vert$$

The relay trips only when $I_{spill} > I_{pickup}$ (operating current setting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Differential Relays are classified as **Unit-Type Protections** because they protect a specific *zone* (transformer winding, generator stator, busbar, cable, motor) without requiring coordination with adjacent system protections.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Water Tank Balance

Imagine two pipes — one filling a tank and one draining it.

- **Normal condition:** Water in = Water out. The tank's level stays constant. A sensitive "balance detector" at the tank sees **zero net flow** and remains quiet. ✅
- **Internal leak (fault inside the protected zone):** Some water leaks out from inside the tank itself. The inlet pipe still fills at full rate, but the outlet pipe carries *less* water. Now there is a **net imbalance** (spill), the detector triggers, and an alarm shuts off the inlet. ⚠️
- **External pipe burst (fault outside the protected zone):** Both pipes behave the same way (carry identical flow rates), so the detector still sees balance and does **not** trip. This is the magic of differential protection — it is **inherently selective to the protected zone**.

The differential relay is precisely this "balance detector" for current, installed at the two terminals of the equipment it protects.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **Operating Quantity:** Spill current $I_{spill}$ (typically in Amperes)
- **Bias Quantity:** Through current $I_{bias} = \dfrac{\vert I_1 \vert + \vert I_2 \vert}{2}$ (Average of two CT secondaries)
- **Operating Characteristic:** Percentage bias curve, defined as $\dfrac{I_{op}}{I_{bias}} \times 100\%$
- **Standard Bias Settings:** **15%**, **20%**, **25%**, **40%**, **60%**, **80%** (IEC 60255-118 compliant)
- **Operating Time:** Typically **15–30 ms** for high-speed differential elements (Class 5P20 or better CTs required)

> [!NOTE]
> Differential Relays fall under **Class A (Unit Protection)** of switchgear classification — they are absolutely *selective* and *fast* (sub-cycle to a few cycles), making them the gold standard for protecting high-value equipment.

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Differential Relays

| Sub-Type | Operating Quantity | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Current Differential Relay** | Difference of two currents | Generators, Busbars, Motors, Cables |
| **Voltage Differential Relay** | Difference of two voltages | Busbars (high-impedance type) |
| **Percentage Biased Differential** | Biased by through current | Power Transformers (Merz-Price) |
| **High-Impedance Differential** | Voltage across CT neutral | Busbars, restricted earth fault |
| **Biased Differential with Harmonic Restraint** | 2nd & 5th harmonic restraint | Transformer magnetizing inrush blocking |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Zone of Protection" Concept

A differential relay protects a clearly defined **zone of protection** bounded by the locations of its current transformers (CTs). The operating logic is built on three foundational rules:

**Rule 1 — Healthy / External Fault Condition:**
Currents entering and leaving the protected zone are *equal in magnitude* and *opposite in direction* (when conventional flow directions are applied). Hence their phasor difference is theoretically zero (ignoring minor CT errors). The relay **restrains**.

**Rule 2 — Internal Fault Condition:**
Fault current flows *out* of the protected zone without a corresponding return current through the second CT pair. This causes a finite, non-zero spill current. The relay **operates**.

**Rule 3 — The Boundary Rule:**
Anything happening *inside* the CT boundary is detected; anything *outside* is ignored. This makes differential protection inherently **unit-type** and **discriminative** without time grading.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Biased (Percentage) Differential Relays

A simple (unbiased) differential relay is highly sensitive but suffers from a fatal flaw: during **external (through) faults**, the very high fault current flowing through both CTs can cause **unequal CT saturation**, producing a *spurious* spill current. If the relay setting is set just above the maximum spill, internal faults at low current levels may be missed.

The **percentage biased differential relay** solves this elegantly:

> The operating current is *not fixed* — it is a *percentage* of the through (bias) current. So during heavy through faults, the relay **automatically becomes less sensitive**, preventing false tripping from CT errors.

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU University Exam question on differential relays.

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{op} = \vert \vec{I}_1 - \vec{I}_2 \vert$ | Spill (operating) current — basic differential |
| 2 | $I_{bias} = \dfrac{\mid I_1 \mid + \mid I_2 \mid}{2}$ | Average (through) current used for biasing |
| 3 | $\% \text{ Bias} = \dfrac{I_{op}}{I_{bias}} \times 100$ | Operating bias percentage — defines the relay characteristic |
| 4 | $I_{op,min} = K_1 + K_2 \cdot I_{bias}$ | Dual-slope characteristic: $K_1$ is the minimum pickup (initial bias region), $K_2$ is the slope above the bias breakpoint |
| 5 | $I_{spill,through} = \dfrac{e}{N}\left(I_{1,exc} + I_{2,exc}\right)$ | Spill during external fault = sum of CT excitation currents (key for CT selection) |
| 6 | $I_{relay} = \dfrac{I_{secondary}}{N_{CT}}$ | CT secondary current for given primary current $I_{secondary}$ |
| 7 | $V_R = I_{spill} \cdot R_{stab}$ | Stabilizing resistor voltage in high-impedance differential scheme |
| 8 | $V_k = I_{f,max} \cdot \left(R_{CT} + 2R_{lead}\right)$ | Knee-point voltage requirement for high-impedance busbar protection |
| 9 | $I_{inrush} \approx 6 \text{ to } 10 \times I_{rated}$ | Transformer inrush current magnitude (causes false differential operation if not restrained) |
| 10 | $I_{2nd harmonic ratio} = \dfrac{I_{2nd}}{I_{fundamental}} \times 100\%$ | Used for inrush detection (typically blocked if > 15–20%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` inside LaTeX table expressions to keep markdown table syntax intact.** Already applied above.

### 2.4 Working Conditions — Healthy vs. Fault vs. External

| Condition | $I_1$ (Input CT) | $I_2$ (Output CT) | $I_{spill} = \mid I_1 - I_2 \mid$ | Relay Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Load** | $I_L \angle 0°$ | $I_L \angle 180°$ | ≈ 0 | **Restrain** |
| **External Fault** (large through current) | $I_f \angle 0°$ | $I_f \angle 180°$ | Small (CT error) | **Restrain (with bias)** |
| **Internal Fault** | $I_1$ | Much smaller or zero | Large (sum of all feeds) | **Trip** |

### 2.5 Why Differential Relays Are Used in Practice

- **Inherent discrimination** — no need for time grading with adjacent protections
- **Extremely fast** — typically operates in **< 4 cycles** (60–80 ms)
- **High sensitivity** — can detect faults as low as **2–5% of rated current** (with biasing)
- **Stability under through faults** — thanks to biasing and CT saturation tolerance
- **Self-contained zone** — coordinates with system protection only via backup overcurrent

### 2.6 The Merz-Price Protection Scheme (Transformer Application)

The Merz-Price scheme is the **classical implementation** of percentage biased differential protection for transformers. CTs are placed on both primary and secondary sides of the power transformer. The CT ratios are selected such that for normal conditions, the secondary currents circulating in the pilot wires are *equal and opposite* — balancing out at the relay midpoint.

$$\frac{I_{HV}}{I_{LV}} = \frac{N_{CT,LV}}{N_{CT,HV}} \cdot \frac{1}{N_{transformer}}$$

This ensures the relay sees near-zero spill under normal load and external faults.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spill Current During Healthy Operation

**Step 1 — Define the primary currents entering and leaving the protected zone.**

For a single-phase, two-winding power transformer, the high-voltage (HV) side current is $I_1$ and the low-voltage (LV) side current is $I_2$. Both CTs are assumed ideal (no ratio error, no phase error) and their secondaries are interconnected in a circulating current scheme.

**Step 2 — Express the CT secondary currents.**

$$\begin{aligned}
i_1 &= \frac{I_1}{N_1} \quad \text{(HV side CT secondary current)} \\
i_2 &= \frac{I_2}{N_2} \quad \text{(LV side CT secondary current)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply ideal CT ratio compensation (for transformer turns ratio $N = N_{HV}/N_{LV}$).**

CT ratios are chosen so that $i_1 = i_2$ in magnitude under normal load. Specifically, the CT ratios must satisfy:

$$N_1 \cdot N = N_2$$

This way, the secondary currents are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction (180° phase opposition) at the relay point.

**Step 4 — Compute the spill current as the phasor difference.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{spill} &= \vert \vec{i_1} - \vec{i_2} \vert \\
&= \left\vert \frac{I_1}{N_1}\angle 0° - \frac{I_2}{N_2}\angle 180° \right\vert \\
&= \left\vert \frac{I_1}{N_1} + \frac{I_2}{N_2} \right\vert
\end{aligned}$$

If the CTs are perfectly matched, $\dfrac{I_1}{N_1} = \dfrac{I_2}{N_2}$, so:

$$I_{spill} = 0 \quad \text{(Healthy Condition)} \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — For an internal fault, current flows from the source to the fault but not out the far terminal.**

Suppose an internal fault draws current $I_f$ from the HV side only (LV side isolated). The spill becomes:

$$I_{spill} = \left\vert \frac{I_f}{N_1} \right\vert \quad \text{(Internal Fault — relay trips)} \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Percentage Bias Characteristic Equation

**Step 1 — Define the operating and bias quantities in the relay.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{op} &= I_{spill} = \vert \vec{I}_1 - \vec{I_2} \vert \\
I_{bias} &= \frac{\vert \vec{I}_1 \vert + \vert \vec{I_2} \vert}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Express the relay operating boundary as a linear function of bias.**

The relay operates (trips) when:

$$I_{op} > I_{min} + K \cdot I_{bias}$$

where:
- $I_{min}$ = minimum pickup current (a fixed constant, the initial bias region)
- $K$ = slope of the operating characteristic (e.g., 0.20 for 20% bias)

**Step 3 — Convert the boundary equation to percentage form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{op} - I_{min} &> K \cdot I_{bias} \\
\frac{I_{op} - I_{min}}{I_{bias}} &> K
\end{aligned}$$

For large bias currents ($I_{bias} \gg I_{min}$), this simplifies to:

$$\frac{I_{op}}{I_{bias}} \times 100\% > K \times 100\%$$

That is, the percentage bias is the slope $K$ expressed as a percentage. $\blacksquare$

**Step 4 — Interpret the physical meaning.**

A relay with **20% bias** will tolerate up to 20% mismatch in CT secondary currents before operating, even at high through-fault currents. The higher the through current, the higher the *absolute* spill tolerated (in amperes), providing **automatic stability against CT saturation**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Knee-Point Voltage Requirement for High-Impedance Differential Protection

This is the classical formula used to size CTs for high-impedance busbar protection (used by KTU examiners frequently).

**Step 1 — Define the maximum through-fault current (primary).**

Let $I_{f,max}$ be the maximum external (through-zone) fault current in primary amperes.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum CT secondary current.**

$$I_{sec,max} = \frac{I_{f,max}}{N_{CT}}$$

**Step 3 — Express the voltage that would appear across the stabilizing resistor $R_{stab}$.**

The total loop resistance (worst case) is $R_{loop} = R_{CT} + 2R_{lead}$ (CT winding resistance plus the resistance of both pilot leads).

$$V_{R,max} = I_{sec,max} \cdot R_{loop}$$

**Step 4 — The CT knee-point voltage must exceed this to prevent saturation.**

$$V_k \geq V_{R,max} = I_{f,max} \cdot \left( R_{CT} + 2R_{lead} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{N_{CT}} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** This formula is **guaranteed to appear** as a numerical problem in Part B of KTU University Exams. Practice at least 2–3 numericals on this.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Spill Current Calculation

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 11 kV/0.4 kV delta-star transformer rated 500 kVA is protected by a Merz-Price scheme. The HV CT ratio is 100/5 and the LV CT ratio is 800/5. The transformer full-load current on HV side is 26.2 A. Calculate the spill current under normal load.

**Step 1 — Compute HV CT secondary current.**

$$I_{1,sec} = \frac{26.2 \times 5}{100} = 1.31 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute LV side full-load current.**

$$I_{LV,FL} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = 721.7 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute LV CT secondary current.**

$$I_{2,sec} = \frac{721.7 \times 5}{800} = 4.51 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the line-to-line current transformation delta → star.**

The HV side is delta; the LV side is star. The effective turns ratio considering vector group Dy11 is:

$$N_{effective} = \frac{11{,}000 / \sqrt{3}}{400} = 15.91$$

The CT ratio mismatch factor is:

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{100/5}{800/5} = 0.125$$

Required ratio for compensation: $\dfrac{1}{N_{effective}} = \dfrac{1}{15.91} = 0.0628$

This indicates a mismatch — in practice, an **interposing CT** or **auxiliary CT** is used to balance the secondary currents. With perfect balance (after compensation), the spill would be zero.

Without compensation, the spill would be:

$$I_{spill} = 1.31 - 4.51 = -3.20 \text{ A (vector sum)} \quad \text{(unbalanced — not acceptable)}$$

This illustrates why **auxiliary CTs are mandatory** in Merz-Price transformer protection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Differential Protection Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Zone of Protection e.g. Transformer Winding] --> nodeB[CT1 at Terminal 1]
    nodeA --> nodeC[CT2 at Terminal 2]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Differential Relay Unit]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Spill Calculator I1 minus I2]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Bias Calculator I1 plus I2 over 2]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Comparator Block]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH{Is Iop greater than Imin plus K times Ibias}
    nodeH -->|Yes Internal Fault| nodeI[Trip Signal to Circuit Breaker]
    nodeH -->|No Restrain| nodeJ[No Trip Output]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Breaker Opens Fault Isolated]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[System Continues Normal]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Merz-Price Circulating Current Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[HV Side Source] --> nodeT1[HV Bushing]
    nodeT1 --> nodeW[Transformer Windings Protected Zone]
    nodeW --> nodeT2[LV Bushing]
    nodeT2 --> nodeQ[LV Side Load]
    nodeT1 --> nodeCT1[CT1 HV Side]
    nodeT2 --> nodeCT2[CT2 LV Side]
    nodeCT1 --> nodeRP[Relay Pilot Wire P1]
    nodeCT1 --> nodeRN[Relay Pilot Wire N1]
    nodeCT2 --> nodeRP2[Relay Pilot Wire P2]
    nodeCT2 --> nodeRN2[Relay Pilot Wire N2]
    nodeRP --> nodeRelay[Differential Relay Operating Coil]
    nodeRN2 --> nodeRelay
    nodeRP2 --> nodeJumper[Jumper Link]
    nodeRN --> nodeJumper
    nodeRelay --> nodeTrip[Trip Contacts to Breaker]
```

### 4.3 Operating Characteristic Graph — Percentage Biased Differential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Characteristic_Curve
        axisX[Ibias Amperes]
        axisY[Iop Spill Amperes]
        origin[Origin]
        point1[Operating Region Above Curve]
        point2[Restraint Region Below Curve]
        slopeLine[Slope K 20 percent]
        minPickup[Minimum Pickup Imin 0.2 A]
        breakPoint[Breakpoint at Ibias 1.0 A]
    end
    origin --> minPickup
    minPickup --> breakPoint
    breakPoint --> slopeLine
    point2 --- slopeLine
    point1 --- slopeLine
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Modern Numerical Differential Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeAna[Analog Inputs from CTs 4 to 20 channels] --> nodeADC[Anti Aliasing Filter and ADC]
    nodeADC --> nodeDSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    nodeDSP --> nodeFourier[DFT Calculation Fundamental and Harmonics]
    nodeFourier --> nodeSpill[Spill Computation I1 minus I2]
    nodeFourier --> nodeBias[Bias Computation Average]
    nodeSpill --> nodeLogic[Protection Logic Element]
    nodeBias --> nodeLogic
    nodeLogic --> nodeHARM[2nd and 5th Harmonic Restraint Block]
    nodeHARM --> nodeTripLogic[Trip Logic with Pickup and Bias Check]
    nodeTripLogic --> nodeOutput[Trip Output Contacts]
    nodeTripLogic --> nodeComm[Communication IEC 61850 GOOSE]
    nodeComm --> nodeSCADA[Substation SCADA HMI]
```

### 4.5 Sequence of Operation During an Internal Fault

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Source as Source HV
    participant CT1 as CT1 HV Side
    participant Relay as Differential Relay
    participant CT2 as CT2 LV Side
    participant Breaker as Circuit Breaker
    participant Fault as Internal Fault
    Note over Source,Fault: Healthy State I1 equals I2
    Source->>CT1: 100 A Load
    CT1->>Relay: 5 A Secondary
    CT2->>Relay: 5 A Secondary
    Note right of Relay: Spill equals 0 Restrain
    Note over Fault: Internal Fault Occurs
    Fault->>Source: Short Circuit Path
    Source->>CT1: 2000 A Fault
    CT1->>Relay: 100 A Secondary
    CT2->>Relay: 0 A Secondary
    Note right of Relay: Spill equals 100 A Trip
    Relay->>Breaker: Trip Command within 20 ms
    Breaker-->>Source: Opens Fault Isolated
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define a differential relay. State the principle on which it operates.

**Model Answer:**
A differential relay is a unit-type protective relay that operates based on the *vector difference* between two (or more) electrical quantities (typically currents) measured at the two ends of a protected zone.

**Principle:** The relay compares the current entering the protected zone with the current leaving it (through CTs at each terminal). Under normal conditions and external faults, these currents are equal in magnitude and opposite in phase, producing a near-zero *spill current*. During an internal fault, the currents differ significantly, producing a non-zero spill current that exceeds the relay pickup threshold, causing a trip signal.

**Key KTU Points to Mention:**
- Based on Kirchhoff's Current Law
- Provides unit protection (selective to the protected zone)
- Spill current $I_{spill} = \vert \vec{I}_1 - \vec{I}_2 \vert$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2.** What is meant by *percentage bias* in a differential relay? Why is it necessary?

**Model Answer:**
Percentage bias is a characteristic of differential relays where the **minimum operating current is not a fixed value**, but a *percentage* of the through (bias) current flowing through the protected zone.

**Definition:** $\% \text{ Bias} = \dfrac{I_{op}}{I_{bias}} \times 100$, where $I_{op}$ is the spill current and $I_{bias}$ is the average of the two CT secondary currents.

**Why necessary:**
1. To prevent false operation due to **unequal CT saturation** during heavy through-faults (external faults).
2. As through-fault current increases, the allowed spill current also increases proportionally — the relay **automatically becomes more tolerant** of CT errors at high currents.
3. Provides **stability without sacrificing sensitivity** for internal faults at low current levels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *operating current* with *bias current*. The operating current (spill) is what **drives the trip**, while the bias current is what **restrains** the trip. Mixing these two up costs 1–2 marks.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified Model Question — Question A]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain with a neat schematic the working of the **Merz-Price circulating current scheme** for protection of a 3-phase power transformer. Discuss the role of auxiliary CTs in this scheme. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Merz-Price scheme is a circulating-current differential protection system used for power transformers, generators, and other equipment requiring high-speed unit protection.

**Schematic Description:**

In the Merz-Price scheme, current transformers are installed on both sides (HV and LV) of every phase of the transformer. The CT secondaries are connected in a closed loop (circulating loop) through pilot wires, and the differential relay operating coil is connected across the midpoint of this loop (or as a circulating element).

For phase R:
- HV CT secondary $i_{1R}$ flows in one direction
- LV CT secondary $i_{2R}$ flows in the opposite direction
- The relay coil is energized by the **difference** $i_{1R} - i_{2R}$

**Operation in Normal Condition:**
The CT ratios are chosen (using auxiliary CTs) such that $i_{1R} = i_{2R}$ in magnitude. The currents circulate in the loop and **no current flows through the relay coil**. The relay remains inoperative.

**Operation during Internal Fault:**
The fault current flows through only one CT (say the HV CT) and returns through the fault path, not through the LV CT. The balance is disturbed, and a large spill current flows through the relay coil, causing it to operate and trip the breaker.

**Operation during External Fault:**
Both CTs carry identical fault currents, the balance is maintained (within CT error limits), and the relay does not trip.

**Role of Auxiliary CTs:**
Because the power transformer has a turns ratio (e.g., 11 kV / 0.4 kV) and possibly a vector group shift (e.g., Dyn11), the CT secondaries are **not naturally balanced**. Auxiliary CTs (also called interposing CTs) are used to:
1. Compensate for the **turns ratio mismatch** between HV and LV sides
2. Compensate for the **phase shift** introduced by the vector group (e.g., 30° for Dyn11)
3. Provide **zero-sequence component compensation** (to prevent false tripping on external earth faults)

**Diagram Reference:** (Refer to Section 4.2 — Merz-Price Circulating Current Scheme)

**Conclusion:** The Merz-Price scheme provides fast, sensitive, and inherently selective protection, making it ideal for transformers above 1 MVA rating.

---

**Q.A (b)** A 3-phase, 5 MVA, 11 kV/0.4 kV, Dyn11 transformer is protected by a Merz-Price scheme. The HV CT ratio is 400/5 and the LV CT ratio is 8000/5. Calculate (i) the spill current on full load (assume CTs are ideal), and (ii) what auxiliary CT ratio is needed to balance the secondaries. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Transformer rating: $S = 5 \text{ MVA}$
- HV voltage: $V_1 = 11 \text{ kV}$
- LV voltage: $V_2 = 0.4 \text{ kV}$
- Vector group: Dyn11
- HV CT ratio: $N_1 = 400/5 = 80$
- LV CT ratio: $N_2 = 8000/5 = 1600$

**Step 1 — Compute HV side full-load current.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{1,FL} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_1} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11{,}000} \\
&= 262.43 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute HV CT secondary current.**

$$i_{1,sec} = \frac{I_{1,FL}}{N_1} = \frac{262.43}{80} = 3.28 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute LV side full-load current.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{2,FL} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_2} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} \\
&= 7216.88 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute LV CT secondary current.**

$$i_{2,sec} = \frac{I_{2,FL}}{N_2} = \frac{7216.88}{1600} = 4.51 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Compute spill current (without compensation).**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{spill} &= \vert i_{1,sec} - i_{2,sec} \vert \\
&= \vert 3.28 - 4.51 \vert \\
&= 1.23 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

This is **unacceptably high** (about 1.23 A spill at full load), so compensation is required.

**Step 6 — Compute the required auxiliary CT ratio.**

The auxiliary CT must increase $i_{1,sec}$ to match $i_{2,sec}$ (or decrease $i_{2,sec}$). Using an auxiliary CT on the HV side:

$$N_{aux} = \frac{i_{2,sec}}{i_{1,sec}} = \frac{4.51}{3.28} = 1.375 \text{ (turns ratio)}$$

So an auxiliary CT with ratio **1.375 : 1** on the HV side will balance the secondaries.

**Step 7 — With compensation, the spill current becomes zero.**

$$\boxed{I_{spill} = 0 \text{ A (with ideal auxiliary CT)}}$$

**Result:** The auxiliary CT compensates for the turns ratio mismatch, and the relay sees zero spill under healthy conditions.

---

> **[Alternative Question B — KTU University Exam, Modified Model]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **percentage biased differential relay** characteristic with a graph. Discuss the significance of the bias setting choice for a transformer application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A percentage biased (or biased) differential relay is a relay in which the operating current is a fixed *percentage* of the through (bias) current, instead of a fixed absolute value. This makes the relay **automatically desensitize** as the through current increases.

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$I_{op} > I_{min} + K \cdot I_{bias}$$

The relay operates only when this inequality is satisfied.

**Graphical Representation:**

On an $I_{bias}$ (x-axis) vs $I_{op}$ (y-axis) plot:
- For $I_{bias} < I_{breakpoint}$: A horizontal line at $I_{op} = I_{min}$ (constant minimum pickup)
- For $I_{bias} > I_{breakpoint}$: A straight line of slope $K$ passing through the breakpoint
- The area **above** the curve is the *operating region* (trip)
- The area **below** the curve is the *restraint region* (no trip)

**Significance of Bias Setting Choice:**

1. **Low bias (e.g., 15%):** Maximum sensitivity for internal faults, but lower stability margin against CT saturation during external faults. Used for **generators** where through-faults are rare.

2. **Medium bias (e.g., 20–25%):** Good compromise for most **power transformers** in distribution networks.

3. **High bias (e.g., 40–60%):** Used for **large power transformers** (> 100 MVA) where inrush currents and high-magnitude external faults are common, and CT saturation is more likely.

4. **Dual-slope characteristic:** Modern numerical relays use a dual-slope curve (e.g., 20% up to rated current, 60% above rated) to optimize both sensitivity and stability.

**Key Advantage:** The biased characteristic provides **through-fault stability** without sacrificing internal-fault sensitivity.

---

**Q.B (b)** With reference to **transformer differential protection**, explain why harmonic restraint (2nd and 5th harmonic) is used. What percentage threshold is typically adopted? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem of Magnetizing Inrush:**

When a power transformer is energized, the magnetizing inrush current can reach **6 to 10 times** the rated full-load current. This inrush is seen by the differential relay as a *spill* current (since it flows in from HV but does not return through LV), and a naive differential relay will falsely trip the transformer offline at switch-on.

**Why Harmonic Restraint Solves This:**

The inrush current has a distinctive **harmonic content** that is very different from a genuine internal fault current:
- A typical inrush waveform is **peaked and offset**, rich in **2nd harmonic** (often 30–70% of fundamental)
- It also contains significant **5th harmonic** component
- In contrast, a genuine internal fault current is a **near-sinusoidal** fundamental-frequency waveform with low 2nd harmonic content

**Operating Principle:**

The numerical relay continuously computes the **2nd harmonic ratio**:

$$\% \text{ 2nd Harmonic} = \frac{I_{2nd}}{I_{1st}} \times 100\%$$

If this ratio exceeds the **threshold (typically 15–20%)**, the relay *restrains* (blocks) the trip, treating the spill as inrush rather than fault.

Similarly, the 5th harmonic is monitored as a backup restraint (in modern relays, especially for transformers with high saturation, where 2nd harmonic may be insufficient).

**Typical Threshold Settings:**
- **2nd Harmonic:** 15–20% (IEC 60255-118 typical default: 15%)
- **5th Harmonic:** 10–15% (used as additional restraint in some relays)
- **Cumulative restraint:** The relay trips only when both 2nd and 5th harmonic ratios are below threshold AND the spill exceeds the bias curve

**Conclusion:** Harmonic restraint is essential for **security** of transformer differential protection — it prevents false trips during energization, sympathetic inrush, and over-excitation, while still allowing fast tripping on genuine internal faults.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Operating and Bias Currents:** Students often define spill current as bias current. The spill is $I_1 - I_2$, the bias is the average. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting CT Ratio Compensation:** In Merz-Price problems, students compute the spill without including the CT ratio compensation. The CT ratios must be matched in *secondary* amperes, not primary. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring Vector Group Phase Shift:** Dyn11 has a 30° phase shift. If students treat it as in-phase, the auxiliary CT ratio derivation will be wrong. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting Inrush Restraint Mechanism:** In transformer differential questions, students often describe the relay without mentioning 2nd harmonic restraint. **Loss: 1 mark per omission.**
> 5. **Confusing Biased Differential with High-Impedance Differential:** These are two different schemes. Biased uses current comparison; high-impedance uses voltage across CT neutral. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Differential Relay** operates on the phasor difference of two currents ($I_{spill} = \vert I_1 - I_2 \vert$).
- [ ] It is a **Unit-Type (Class A) Protection** — selective to the protected zone, no time grading needed.
- [ ] Three operating conditions: **Normal (Restrain), Internal Fault (Trip), External Fault (Restrain with biasing).**
- [ ] **Percentage Bias** = $\dfrac{I_{op}}{I_{bias}} \times 100\%$, with standard settings of 15%, 20%, 25%, 40%, 60%, 80%.
- [ ] **Biased characteristic** prevents false operation due to CT saturation during through faults.
- [ ] **Merz-Price scheme** is the classical circulating-current differential protection, used for transformers.
- [ ] **Auxiliary CTs** are needed in transformer differential schemes to compensate for turns ratio and vector group phase shift.
- [ ] **Magnetizing inrush** (6–10× rated) can falsely trip differential relays — solved by **2nd and 5th harmonic restraint** (threshold: 15–20%).
- [ ] **High-impedance differential** is used for busbar protection; requires CTs with $V_k \geq I_{f,max}(R_{CT} + 2R_{lead})$.
- [ ] Standard operating time: **15–30 ms** for high-speed differential elements.
- [ ] **CT requirements:** Use class **5P20** or better (or **TPY/TPZ** for transformer applications) to ensure adequate transient performance.
- [ ] Differential protection is the **gold standard** for generators, transformers, busbars, motors, and large cables.
- [ ] The three pillars of differential protection: **Sensitivity (to internal faults), Selectivity (to the zone), Stability (against through faults)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Relays and Digital Relay. Transmission Line Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static Relays and Digital Relay. Transmission Line Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Static Relays — Formal Academic Definition

A **Static Relay** is a protective relay in which the measurement and decision-making functions are accomplished using **static (solid-state) electronic components** — such as diodes, transistors, zener diodes, operational amplifiers, and integrated circuits — with **no moving parts** in the sensing and comparison stages. The relay operates purely on the basis of electrical quantities (voltage and current) applied to solid-state comparator circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim recall form):**
> *"A static relay is one in which the intended operation is obtained by a combination of static elements such as transistors, diodes, resistors, capacitors, etc., without any mechanical motion of armature or other moving parts."*

The output stage may either be a static switch (thyristor/SCR) or a small electromagnetic auxiliary relay (called a **static relay with output relay**).

## 1.2 Digital Relay — Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Relay** is a microprocessor- or microcontroller-based protective relay in which analog signals from the power system (current and voltage) are first conditioned, then converted into digital form via an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, and the protective decision (trip/no-trip) is computed by a **programmable digital processor** running a stored algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Numerical Relay** is a sub-class of digital relay where all mathematical computations (Fourier transforms, impedance estimation, phasor extraction) are carried out numerically using DSPs, with built-in communication (IEC 61850), self-testing, and data-logging features.

## 1.3 Transmission Line Protection — Formal Academic Definition

**Transmission Line Protection** refers to the coordinated set of protective schemes and relay logic designed to detect faults (short circuits, open conductors, earth faults) along high-voltage overhead transmission lines and isolate only the faulted section while keeping the rest of the network healthy. The principal schemes are:

1. **Overcurrent Protection** (time-graded and current-graded)
2. **Distance Protection** (impedance, reactance, mho/admittance)
3. **Pilot Protection** (using a communication channel — wires, carrier, microwave, fibre)
4. **Differential Protection** (unit protection of short lines)

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Triage Analogy:**
> Imagine a hospital emergency ward. An **electromagnetic relay** is like a **human nurse** physically moving to read a meter, compare it with a setpoint, and pull a switch — slow, mechanical, but simple. A **static relay** is like a **dedicated electronic screening machine** — it compares signals electronically (no human motion), reacts in milliseconds, but cannot "learn" or self-diagnose. A **digital/numerical relay** is like an **AI-powered diagnostic computer** — it reads vitals (CT/PT signals), digitizes them, runs fault algorithms in software, logs every event, can communicate over a network, and even self-tests. **Transmission line protection** itself is the **ambulance routing system** — distance protection (zone-based), pilot protection (real-time communication between substations), and overcurrent grading (backup tiers) all work together so that only the faulted "patient" gets isolated, not the entire grid.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Static relay operating time | **5 to 20 ms** | Much faster than EM relays (~50 ms) |
| Digital relay sampling rate | **1.2 kHz to 4 kHz** | 24 to 64 samples per 50 Hz cycle |
| ADC resolution | **12 to 16 bits** | Modern numerical relays |
| Pilot channel — carrier frequency | **30 kHz to 500 kHz** | Power Line Carrier (PLC) |
| IEC 61850 standard | Communication | Modern substation protocol |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram of a Static Relay

A static relay has the following functional blocks:

1. **Input Transducer (CT/PT)** — Step down primary current/voltage to safe low-level signals (typically 5 A / 110 V secondary).
2. **Rectifier / Signal Conditioner** — Converts AC quantities into proportional DC signals.
3. **Comparator Circuit** — Compares the rectified signal against a reference (pickup). Two main types:
   - **Amplitude Comparator**
   - **Phase Comparator**
4. **Decision / Amplifying Stage** — Amplifies the comparator output.
5. **Output Device** — Either a sensitive electromagnetic trip relay or a thyristor (SCR) switch that energizes the trip coil of the circuit breaker.

## 2.2 Comparator Circuits (Most Tested KTU Topic)

### A. Amplitude Comparator

Compares the **magnitudes** of two input signals $\vert S_1 \vert$ and $\vert S_2 \vert$.

**Operating condition:**

$$\vert S_1 \vert > \vert S_2 \vert$$

**Realisation (using a bridge rectifier + polarized relay):** Two AC quantities $S_1$ and $S_2$ are rectified. The difference $\vert S_1 \vert - \vert S_2 \vert$ drives a sensitive moving-coil relay.

### B. Phase Comparator

Compares the **phase angle** $\theta$ between two input signals $S_1$ and $S_2$.

**Operating condition:**

$$-\alpha \leq \theta \leq +\alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the half-angle setting.

### C. Equivalence Theorem (Critical KTU Topic)

Any **amplitude comparator** with inputs $(S_1,\, S_2)$ can be equivalently realised as a **phase comparator** with inputs $(S_1 + S_2,\, S_1 - S_2)$, and vice versa.

$$\text{Amplitude: }\vert S_1 \vert > \vert S_2 \vert \iff \text{Phase: } -90^\circ \leq \angle\!\left(\tfrac{S_1+S_2}{S_1-S_2}\right) \leq +90^\circ$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** All classical distance relays (impedance, reactance, mho) are merely phase comparators with cleverly chosen inputs derived from line voltage $V$ and current $I$.

## 2.3 Classification of Distance Relays (via Phase Comparators)

| Relay Type | Comparator Inputs ($S_1,\, S_2$) | Characteristic on R-X plane | Reach in Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Impedance Relay** | $V,\ I$ | Circle centred at origin | Non-directional |
| **Reactance Relay** | $V\sin\phi,\ I$ | Straight line parallel to R-axis | Non-directional |
| **Mho (Admittance) Relay** | $V - IZ_R,\ V + IZ_R$ | Circle passing through origin | Directional |
| **Quadrilateral / Polarised Mho** | Combination | Four-sided shape | Directional |

For a fault at a distance $m$ of the line (where $0 < m < 1$), the apparent impedance seen by the relay at the sending end is:

$$Z_{apparent} = m \cdot Z_{line}$$

The relay operates when $\vert Z_{apparent} \vert \leq Z_{setting}$.

## 2.4 Three Zones of Distance Protection

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Table — Memorize the percentages:**

| Zone | Reach (% of Protected Line) | Operating Time | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone 1** | **80 – 90 %** | **Instantaneous (~20 ms)** | Primary protection — trips without delay for faults inside the line |
| **Zone 2** | **120 – 150 %** | **0.3 – 0.5 s** | Backup for Zone 1 of adjacent line |
| **Zone 3** | **Next line backup (~250 %)** | **0.6 – 1.0 s** | Remote backup for far-end faults |

## 2.5 Overcurrent Relay Time-Current Characteristic

The standard inverse definite minimum time (IDMT) characteristic is given by:

$$t = \frac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{pickup}}\right)^{0.02} - 1}$$

where:
- $t$ = operating time in seconds
- $TMS$ = Time Multiplier Setting
- $I$ = actual fault current
- $I_{pickup}$ = pickup current

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent impedance seen by relay | $Z_a = V/I$ | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | Fault impedance fraction | $Z_a = m \cdot Z_{line}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | Static relay operating time | $t \approx 5 \text{ to } 20$ | ms |
| 4 | Phase comparator range | $-\alpha \leq \theta \leq \alpha$ | degrees |
| 5 | IDMT operating time | $t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{(I/I_p)^{0.02}-1}$ | s |
| 6 | Zone 1 reach | $Z_1 = (0.8\text{ to }0.9)\, Z_{line}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Zone 2 reach | $Z_2 = (1.2\text{ to }1.5)\, Z_{line}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Carrier frequency (PLC) | $30 \text{ to } 500$ | kHz |
| 9 | PSM (Plug Setting Multiplier) | $PSM = I/I_{pickup}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Differential relay threshold | $\vert I_1 - I_2 \vert > I_{bias}$ | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Static relays enabled sub-cycle protection in the 1960s–80s, crucial for EHV/UHV stability. Digital/numerical relays (1990s onwards) introduced adaptive relaying, fault recording (COMTRADE), wide-area monitoring (PMUs), and IEC 61850 process-bus communication — all forming the backbone of **smart grids**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Equivalence between Amplitude and Phase Comparator

**Given:**
An amplitude comparator operates when $\vert S_1 \vert > \vert S_2 \vert$.

**To Prove:**
The same logical operation can be implemented as a phase comparator whose inputs are:

$$P = S_1 + S_2 \qquad Q = S_1 - S_2$$

**Step 1:** Square both sides of the operating condition.

$$\vert S_1 \vert^2 > \vert S_2 \vert^2$$

**Step 2:** Express $S_1$ and $S_2$ in terms of $P$ and $Q$.

$$S_1 = \frac{P+Q}{2} \qquad S_2 = \frac{P-Q}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the squared inequality.

$$\left\vert \frac{P+Q}{2} \right\vert^2 > \left\vert \frac{P-Q}{2} \right\vert^2$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by 4 (constant positive, inequality preserved).

$$\vert P+Q \vert^2 > \vert P-Q \vert^2$$

**Step 5:** Expand each magnitude squared.

$$\vert P+Q \vert^2 = \vert P \vert^2 + \vert Q \vert^2 + 2 \vert P \vert \vert Q \vert \cos(\theta_P - \theta_Q)$$

$$\vert P-Q \vert^2 = \vert P \vert^2 + \vert Q \vert^2 - 2 \vert P \vert \vert Q \vert \cos(\theta_P - \theta_Q)$$

**Step 6:** Subtract the second from the first.

$$\vert P+Q \vert^2 - \vert P-Q \vert^2 = 4 \vert P \vert \vert Q \vert \cos(\theta_P - \theta_Q)$$

**Step 7:** Set the result to be positive (operating condition).

$$4 \vert P \vert \vert Q \vert \cos(\theta_P - \theta_Q) > 0$$

Since $\vert P \vert$ and $\vert Q \vert$ are non-negative magnitudes, the inequality reduces to:

$$\cos(\theta_P - \theta_Q) > 0$$

**Step 8:** Convert to angle bounds.

$$-90^\circ < \angle(P/Q) < +90^\circ$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\;\vert S_1 \vert > \vert S_2 \vert \iff -90^\circ < \angle\!\left(\tfrac{S_1+S_2}{S_1-S_2}\right) < +90^\circ\;}$$

This proves the **amplitude-to-phase equivalence theorem**, the foundation of all static distance relays.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Operating Equation of the Mho Relay

**Setup:**
The Mho relay uses a phase comparator with inputs:

$$S_1 = I Z_R - V \qquad S_2 = V$$

where $Z_R$ is the relay setting impedance and $V, I$ are the voltage and current at the relay location.

**Step 1:** Apply the amplitude comparator equivalence.

$$-90^\circ < \angle\!\left(\tfrac{S_1}{S_2}\right) < +90^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$-90^\circ < \angle\!\left(\tfrac{IZ_R - V}{V}\right) < +90^\circ$$

**Step 3:** Let $V$ be the phasor reference. The condition $\cos(\angle(IZ_R - V)/V) > 0$ becomes:

$$\text{Re}\!\left[(IZ_R - V)\, V^*\right] > 0$$

**Step 4:** Let $Z_a = V/I$ be the apparent impedance. Multiply numerator and denominator by $I^*$.

$$\text{Re}\!\left[(IZ_R - V) \cdot \frac{I^* Z_a}{I^*}\right] = \text{Re}\!\left[(IZ_R - V) I^* \cdot \tfrac{Z_a}{I^*}\right]$$

Simplify directly by writing $V = I Z_a$:

$$IZ_R - V = I(Z_R - Z_a)$$

Therefore the input to the comparator is:

$$\frac{IZ_R - V}{V} = \frac{I(Z_R - Z_a)}{I Z_a} = \frac{Z_R - Z_a}{Z_a}$$

**Step 5:** The operating condition is the real part of $(Z_R - Z_a)/Z_a$ being positive.

$$\text{Re}\!\left[\tfrac{Z_R - Z_a}{Z_a}\right] > 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Re}\!\left[\tfrac{Z_a^* \cdot (Z_R - Z_a)}{\vert Z_a \vert^2}\right] > 0$$

**Step 6:** Since the denominator is real and positive:

$$\text{Re}\!\left[Z_a^*(Z_R - Z_a)\right] > 0$$

Expanding with $Z_a = R + jX$ and $Z_R = R_R + jX_R$:

$$R \cdot R_R - R^2 + X \cdot X_R - X^2 > 0$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange into a circle equation.

$$\left(R - \tfrac{R_R}{2}\right)^2 + \left(X - \tfrac{X_R}{2}\right)^2 < \left(\tfrac{\vert Z_R \vert}{2}\right)^2$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\;\text{Mho relay operates inside a circle of diameter } \vert Z_R \vert \text{ centred at } (R_R/2,\, X_R/2)\;}$$

This is the famous **Mho circle on the R-X diagram**, passing through the origin — proving that the Mho relay is inherently **directional**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Zone Coordination

**Given:**
A 110 kV, 100 km transmission line has positive-sequence impedance $Z_{line} = 0.4 + j\,0.6 \; \Omega/\text{km}$.

**Required:**
Determine the Zone 1, Zone 2 and Zone 3 reach settings.

**Step 1 — Total line impedance.**

$$Z_{line} = (0.4 + j\,0.6) \times 100 = 40 + j\,60 \; \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude.**

$$\vert Z_{line} \vert = \sqrt{40^2 + 60^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 3600} = \sqrt{5200} \approx 72.11 \; \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Zone 1 reach (85 % of line).**

$$Z_1 = 0.85 \times (40 + j\,60) = 34 + j\,51 \; \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_1 \vert = 0.85 \times 72.11 \approx 61.29 \; \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Zone 2 reach (130 % of line, to cover 50 % of next line).**

$$Z_2 = 1.3 \times (40 + j\,60) = 52 + j\,78 \; \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_2 \vert = 1.3 \times 72.11 \approx 93.74 \; \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Zone 3 reach (250 % of line, full next-line backup).**

$$Z_3 = 2.5 \times (40 + j\,60) = 100 + j\,150 \; \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_3 \vert = 2.5 \times 72.11 \approx 180.28 \; \Omega$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

$$\boxed{Z_1 = 61.29 \;\Omega,\quad Z_2 = 93.74 \;\Omega,\quad Z_3 = 180.28 \;\Omega}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Static vs Digital vs Numerical Relay

| Feature | Static Relay | Digital Relay | Numerical Relay |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active elements | Transistors, Op-Amps, Diodes | Microprocessor + ADC | DSP + multi-processor |
| Decision type | Hardware comparator | Firmware algorithm | Software with adaptive logic |
| Communication | None / hardwired | RS-232 / RS-485 | IEC 61850, Ethernet, fibre |
| Self-testing | No | Limited | Yes (built-in) |
| Fault recording | No | Yes | Yes (COMTRADE) |
| Sampling rate | N/A (continuous) | 1.2 kHz | Up to 4.8 kHz |
| Operating time | 5–20 ms | 10–25 ms | 8–15 ms |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High (but falling) |
| Typical use | Sub-transmission | Distribution | EHV/UHV, smart-grid |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Static Relay Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power System Line CT and PT] --> B[Signal Conditioner Rectifier and Filter]
    B --> C[Comparator Amplitude or Phase]
    C --> D[Amplifier and Level Detector]
    D --> E[Output Trip Element SCR or Auxiliary Relay]
    E --> F[Trip Coil of Circuit Breaker]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Digital / Numerical Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Primary CT and PT] --> A2[Burden Resistor and Surge Protection]
    A2 --> A3[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    A3 --> A4[Analog to Digital Converter ADC 16 bit]
    A4 --> A5[Microprocessor and DSP Core]
    A5 --> A6[Algorithm Phasor Fourier Distance]
    A6 --> A7{Decision Logic Zone Trip}
    A7 -- Trip --> A8[Output Relay Contact to Breaker]
    A7 -- No Trip --> A9[Reset and Continue Sampling]
    A5 --> A10[Communication Port IEC 61850]
    A5 --> A11[Fault Recorder COMTRADE Memory]
    A5 --> A12[Human Machine Interface HMI Display]
```

## 4.3 R-X Plane Characteristics — Distance Relays

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R_X_Plane [R X Impedance Plane]
        direction LR
        A0[Origin 0 0] --- A1[Pure Resistance Axis R]
        A0 --- A2[Reactance Axis X]
        M1[Mho Circle Passes Through Origin Directional] 
        M2[Impedance Circle Centred at Origin Non Directional]
        M3[Reactance Line Parallel to R Axis]
    end
```

## 4.4 Three Zones of Distance Protection — Reach Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BUS_A[Substation A Sending End] --> L1[Line AB 100 km] --> BUS_B[Substation B Receiving End]
    BUS_B --> L2[Line BC 100 km] --> BUS_C[Substation C]
    Z1[Zone 1 Reach 80 to 90 percent of Line AB] -.covers.-> L1
    Z2[Zone 2 Reach 120 to 150 percent into Line BC] -.covers.-> L2
    Z3[Zone 3 Reach Remote Backup] -.covers.-> L2
```

## 4.5 Carrier Current Pilot Protection — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SA[Substation A Relay] -- Transmitter Carrier Signal 100 kHz --> LINE[Transmission Line Conductor acts as PLC Channel]
    LINE -- Receiver Carrier Signal --> SB[Substation B Relay]
    SA <--> COM[Blocking or Permissive Trip Logic]
    SB <--> COM
    COM -- Trip Command --> CB[Both End Circuit Breakers]
```

## 4.6 IDMT Overcurrent Relay Time-Current Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pickup Current Ip] --- B[Operating Time t in seconds] 
    P1[PSM 2 Inverse Time 10 s] --- P2[PSM 5 IDMT 4 s]
    P2 --- P3[PSM 10 Very Inverse 1.5 s]
    P3 --- P4[PSM 20 Extremely Inverse 0.8 s]
```

---
