<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain any three advantages of static relays over electromagnetic relays.**

**Model Answer:**

A static relay uses solid-state electronic components (transistors, diodes, op-amps) instead of electromagnetic moving parts. Its advantages over electromagnetic (EM) relays are:

1. **High Speed of Operation** — Operating time is typically 5 to 20 ms, compared to 50 ms for EM relays, which is essential for maintaining power system stability.
2. **No Moving Parts** — Absence of mechanical movement means no wear and tear, longer life, less maintenance, and immunity to vibration and dust.
3. **Low Burden on CT/PT** — Static relays draw very small operating power (a few milliwatts), so the measuring CT/PT does not saturate easily.
4. **High Sensitivity and Accuracy** — Can detect very small fault currents; setting is precise and drift-free.
5. **Compact Size and Economical** — A single static module replaces bulky electromagnetic assemblies.

*(Any 3 points for full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is a numerical relay? List its salient features.**

**Model Answer:**

A numerical relay is a computer-based protective relay in which all the protection functions — analog signal acquisition, phasor estimation (via Discrete Fourier Transform), fault detection, decision logic, recording, and communication — are executed numerically by a microprocessor/DSP.

**Salient Features:**
1. **Multi-function Capability** — A single relay can provide overcurrent, distance, differential, and breaker-failure protection.
2. **Self-testing and Self-diagnosis** — Internal health-check on every power-up.
3. **Communication Protocols** — Supports IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus for SCADA integration.
4. **Fault Recording** — Stores oscillographic disturbance records in COMTRADE format.
5. **Adaptive Settings** — Settings can be changed remotely; multiple setting groups for different operating conditions.
6. **High Accuracy** — Numerical phasor computation eliminates CT/PT ratio errors.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always **draw the block diagram** — without it, expect a 2-mark cut.
> - When asked about distance relay characteristics, **plot on the R-X plane**, do not just describe in words.
> - Mention the **equivalence theorem** explicitly when comparing amplitude and phase comparators.
> - For zone settings, **always express as a percentage of line impedance**, not just as ohmic value.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Static Relays & Comparators**

**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a static relay. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the equivalence between amplitude comparator and phase comparator. Derive the operating condition. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The functional blocks of a static relay are:

1. **Input Transducers (CT/PT)** — Step down line current (typically to 5 A) and voltage (to 110 V) to safe proportional low-magnitude signals.
2. **Rectifier / Signal Conditioner** — Converts AC signals into proportional DC magnitude (for amplitude comparators) using bridge rectifiers and smoothing filters.
3. **Comparator Circuit** — The core decision element. Either an *amplitude comparator* (uses op-amp difference amplifier or diode bridge) or a *phase comparator* (uses coincidence detector or flip-flop).
4. **DC Amplifier / Level Detector** — Boosts the comparator output to a level sufficient to drive the output stage.
5. **Output Element** — A thyristor (SCR) or a sensitive electromagnetic auxiliary relay that closes the trip circuit of the circuit breaker.

**Working:** During a fault, the CT/PT signals change; the rectifier produces a DC voltage proportional to the fault quantity; the comparator matches it against the pre-set reference (pickup); if exceeded, the amplifier triggers the output, which energises the trip coil of the breaker.

*Block diagram reference: Section 4.1 of these notes.*

#### **Model Solution (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.1 (Derivation)** of these notes.

**Final Operating Condition:**

$$\boxed{\;\vert S_1 \vert > \vert S_2 \vert \iff -90^\circ \leq \angle\!\left(\tfrac{S_1+S_2}{S_1-S_2}\right) \leq +90^\circ\;}$$

**Explanation (7 marks worth):**
- An amplitude comparator decides whether one AC signal magnitude exceeds another. A phase comparator decides whether the phase difference between two AC signals lies within a set band.
- The equivalence theorem states that every amplitude comparator can be realised by a phase comparator (and vice versa) using the linear combinations $S_1 + S_2$ and $S_1 - S_2$.
- This is the **mathematical foundation** of all static distance relays (impedance, reactance, mho), because they are all phase comparators with cleverly chosen input combinations derived from $V$ and $I$.
- Numerical example: An impedance relay with inputs $(V, I)$ is simply an amplitude comparator with $\vert V \vert > \vert I \cdot Z_{set} \vert$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Digital Relay & Transmission Line Distance Protection**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the working of a digital/numerical relay. Compare numerical relays with static relays in any four aspects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 kV, 80 km three-phase transmission line has positive-sequence impedance $0.05 + j\,0.4 \; \Omega$/km. The line is protected by a three-zone distance relay. Determine the ohmic reach of Zone 1, Zone 2, and Zone 3. State the operating times typically assigned. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** for the block diagram and **Section 3.4 (Analytical Matrix)** for the comparison.

**Working of a Numerical Relay:**

1. CT/PT step down primary current and voltage.
2. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter** removes high-frequency noise.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter** samples signals at 32 samples/cycle.
4. **DSP/Microprocessor** computes the fundamental-frequency phasor using **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)**:

$$V_k = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v(nT) \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

5. The phasor yields the **apparent impedance** $Z = V/I$.
6. The relay compares $Z$ with the zone settings $(Z_1, Z_2, Z_3)$ and trips the appropriate breaker.
7. All events are recorded (COMTRADE) and communicated via IEC 61850.

**Comparison (any four):**

| Aspect | Static Relay | Numerical Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Decision | Hardware (analog comparator) | Software (algorithm) |
| Phasor extraction | Rectifier-based approximation | Discrete Fourier Transform |
| Communication | Hardwired only | IEC 61850, Ethernet, fibre |
| Self-test | No | Yes |

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Total line impedance.**

$$Z_{line} = (0.05 + j\,0.4) \times 80 = 4 + j\,32 \; \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude.**

$$\vert Z_{line} \vert = \sqrt{4^2 + 32^2} = \sqrt{16 + 1024} = \sqrt{1040} \approx 32.25 \; \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Zone 1 reach (85 %).**

$$Z_1 = 0.85 \times (4 + j\,32) = 3.4 + j\,27.2 \; \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_1 \vert = 0.85 \times 32.25 \approx 27.41 \; \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Zone 2 reach (130 %).**

$$Z_2 = 1.3 \times (4 + j\,32) = 5.2 + j\,41.6 \; \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_2 \vert = 1.3 \times 32.25 \approx 41.92 \; \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Zone 3 reach (250 %).**

$$Z_3 = 2.5 \times (4 + j\,32) = 10 + j\,80 \; \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_3 \vert = 2.5 \times 32.25 \approx 80.62 \; \Omega$$

**Typical operating times:**

| Zone | Reach (ohm) | Operating Time |
|---|---|---|
| Zone 1 | 27.41 Ω | Instantaneous (~20 ms) |
| Zone 2 | 41.92 Ω | 0.3 – 0.5 s |
| Zone 3 | 80.62 Ω | 0.6 – 1.0 s |

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Topic 4:**

- **Static Relay:** Solid-state relay with **no moving parts** in the measurement stage. Operating time **5 to 20 ms**.
- **Digital Relay:** Uses ADC + microprocessor; phasor computed via **DFT/FFT**.
- **Numerical Relay:** Advanced digital relay with **IEC 61850** communication and adaptive settings.
- **Two types of comparators:** Amplitude (magnitude) and Phase (angle).
- **Equivalence Theorem:** $\vert S_1 \vert > \vert S_2 \vert \iff -90^\circ \leq \angle\!\left(\tfrac{S_1+S_2}{S_1-S_2}\right) \leq +90^\circ$.
- **Impedance relay** = amplitude comparator with inputs $(V, IZ_{set})$ → **Circle at origin**.
- **Reactance relay** = phase comparator → **Straight line** parallel to R-axis.
- **Mho relay** = phase comparator with inputs $(IZ_R - V, V)$ → **Circle through origin** (directional).
- **Zone 1 reach = 80–90 %** of line; instantaneous.
- **Zone 2 reach = 120–150 %**; time delay ~0.3–0.5 s.
- **Zone 3 reach = ~250 %**; time delay ~0.6–1.0 s.
- **IDMT equation:** $t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{(I/I_p)^{0.02} - 1}$.
- **Pilot protection** uses a communication channel (PLC, fibre, microwave) for **unit protection**.
- **Carrier frequency band** for Power Line Carrier (PLC) = **30 to 500 kHz**.
- **Apparent impedance seen by relay during fault:** $Z_a = m \cdot Z_{line}$, where $m$ is the per-unit distance to fault.
- For an EHV line, **distance protection is preferred** over overcurrent because of the wide variation in fault current magnitude.
- Always express **reach in %** of line impedance, not just ohms, in board answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety During Installation of Plant and Equipment. Safe Sequences in Installation, Risk During Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety During Installation of Plant and Equipment. Safe Sequences in Installation, Risk During Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Safety During Installation of Plant and Equipment** is defined as the systematic application of engineering principles, regulatory statutes, statutory codes, and organizational safety procedures designed to eliminate, mitigate, or control hazards that arise during the erection, positioning, connection, and commissioning of industrial machinery, electrical apparatus, and process plant.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCSFT403 – Module 4)** syllabus, this topic encompasses three foundational pillars:

1. **Hazard Identification (HI)** during the installation phase.
2. **Safe Sequences of Installation** – the prescribed chronological order of erection activities to minimize compounded risk.
3. **Risk Assessment and Control** during the active installation window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor:** In India, installation safety is governed by the **Factories Act, 1948 (Sec 6, 7, 21, 36, 40–44)**, the **Indian Electricity Rules, 1956**, the **National Electrical Code (NEC) 2023**, and **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** codes such as **IS 5216 (Safety Procedures and Permits)** and **IS 14481 (Installation of Electrical Equipment)**.

**Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Definitions):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Plant** | Any fixed or movable machinery, apparatus, appliance, or assembly of components used in industrial processes. |
| **Equipment** | A specific device or apparatus designed to perform a defined function within the plant. |
| **Hazard** | Any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. |
| **Risk** | A combination of the **probability (likelihood)** of an occurrence of a hazardous event or exposure and the **severity of injury or ill-health** that can be caused by the event or exposure(s). |
| **LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** | An energy-isolation procedure mandated before any installation or maintenance work. |
| **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** | A formal written document authorizing specified work to be carried out safely in defined conditions. |
| **Erection** | The on-site positioning, alignment, bolting, and assembling of plant components. |
| **Commissioning** | The final phase of testing, energizing, and handing over of plant for safe operation. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "Building a House"** 🏠
>
> Think of installing a 50 MW electrical substation or a heavy chemical reactor the same way you would think of building a house:
>
> - **Foundation First (Civil/Structural Readiness):** You cannot put up the walls before the foundation is cured. Similarly, in plant installation, you cannot mount a 100-tonne transformer on an un-cured plinth.
> - **Inspect the Materials (Pre-Installation Checks):** Just as a mason checks each brick for cracks, every component is inspected against the **Material Inspection Report (MIR)** before being lifted.
> - **Use the Right Tools (PPE & Lifting Gear):** A carpenter does not hammer with bare hands or without safety glasses. Likewise, riggers require certified **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** and **lifting tackles**.
> - **Read the Manual First (Method Statement):** Nobody builds a house by guessing the plumbing layout. The **Method Statement** (often called the **Work Instruction**) is the *installation blueprint* for plant erection.
> - **One Mistake, One Collapse:** In both cases, a single skipped step (e.g., not de-energizing a 11 kV busbar, or not curing the foundation) leads to catastrophic failure.

The **safe sequence** is therefore the *correct order of construction* (foundation → structural → mechanical → electrical → instrumentation → commissioning), and the **risk** at every stage is a *predictable* event that can be pre-calculated using a **Risk Score Matrix**.

**Key Standard Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested Values):**
- **Acceptable Risk Score:** $R \leq 4$ (Low Risk) on the standard **5×5 Matrix**.
- **Maximum Allowable Working Height without Fall Protection:** **1.8 m (6 ft)** above ground level.
- **Minimum Approach Distance (MAD) for 11 kV:** **2.5 m** (per IS 5216).
- **Mandatory LOTO Verification:** **Zero-Energy State** confirmed by **3-Phase Voltage Test (V-V-E method)**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of Plant Installation (Logical Breakdown)

Every plant installation, regardless of industry (power, petrochemical, manufacturing), follows a **three-phase chronological model** recognized by KTU:

### **Phase 1: Pre-Installation Phase (Planning & Mobilization)**

This is the *preparation window* – the most safety-critical phase because **90% of installation accidents originate from poor planning**.

**Key Activities (Bulleted Logic – KTU Style):**

- **Risk Assessment (RA) & Job Safety Analysis (JSA):**
  - Identify every task, the hazards, and the controls.
  - Document the **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** register.
- **Method Statement (MS) Preparation:**
  - A step-by-step written procedure describing *how* each installation activity will be performed safely.
  - Includes the **sequence of operations**, the **tools required**, the **PPE matrix**, and the **emergency response plan**.
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW) Issuance:**
  - Issued only after verifying that the area is **isolated, de-energized, and gas-free**.
  - Valid for a **specified time window** (typically a single shift).
- **Lifting Plan & Rigging Study:**
  - Critical for heavy lifts (transformers, turbines, pressure vessels).
  - **Crane selection**, **sling angle calculation**, **center of gravity (CoG) determination**.
  - **Outrigger pad sizing** and **ground bearing capacity (GBC) check**.
- **Site Readiness Verification:**
  - Civil foundations cured for **at least 7 to 28 days** (depending on concrete grade).
  - **Bolted/welded anchor verification** against approved drawings.
  - **Earthing grid** resistance measured: typically $\leq 1 \ \Omega$ for power stations.
- **Toolbox Talk (TBT):**
  - A pre-shift **5–10 minute safety briefing** with the installation crew.
  - Reviews the day's tasks, the hazards, the controls, and the **Stop Work Authority (SWA)**.

### **Phase 2: Installation Phase (Active Erection)**

This is the **physical execution** window where the actual plant and equipment are positioned, aligned, and connected.

**Sub-Activities in KTU-Mandated Safe Sequence:**

1. **Material Receiving & Storage:**
   - Inspect against **Purchase Order (PO)** and **Material Test Certificate (MTC)**.
   - Store in **designated lay-down areas** – never obstruct escape routes.
2. **Mechanical Erection:**
   - Lifting by **certified cranes** with **load-test certificates** valid within the last **12 months**.
   - **Tag-line** used to control swing (never direct hand contact with a suspended load).
   - **Bolting** done using calibrated torque wrenches to **torque values specified in the bolt-tightening schedule**.
3. **Electrical Installation:**
   - **De-energize and isolate** upstream supply (LOTO applied).
   - **Test for dead** using an **approved voltage detector** (V-V-E: Verify-Verify-Earth).
   - **Earth the conductors** (working earths) before any termination work.
   - Cable laying with **minimum bend radius** (typically **12 × Cable Overall Diameter (OD)** for HV cables).
4. **Pipe Work & Process Connections:**
   - **Hydrostatic testing** at **1.5 × design pressure** before final tie-in.
   - **Positive Material Identification (PMI)** for alloy components.
5. **Instrumentation & Controls:**
   - Loop checks, calibration, and **continuity testing**.
6. **Inter-stage Inspections:**
   - **Quality Assurance / Quality Control (QA/QC)** hold points must be cleared before progressing to the next stage.

### **Phase 3: Post-Installation Phase (Pre-Commissioning & Commissioning)**

- **Punch List Generation:** Snag list of incomplete items.
- **Pre-Commissioning Checks:** Insulation Resistance (IR) tests, Earth Resistance tests, Ducter tests, Relay coordination checks.
- **Cold Commissioning:** Energization without load.
- **Hot Commissioning / Trial Run:** With load, monitored closely.
- **Handover & Documentation:** As-built drawings, O&M manuals, **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** certificates.

---

## 2.2 The 5 Pillars of Safe Installation (KTU Mnemonic: **"PRE-ACT"**)

| Pillar | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | **Plan the work** | Method Statement, JSA, Lift Plan |
| **R** | **Recognize hazards** | HAZID register, area classification |
| **E** | **Execute the controls** | PTW, LOTO, barricading, signage |
| **A** | **Audit continuously** | Toolbox talks, supervision, CCTV |
| **C** | **Correct deviations** | Stop Work Authority, incident reporting |
| **T** | **Train and certify** | Riggers certified, operators licensed |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This cheat sheet is **the single most important revision tool** for solving any KTU numerical on this topic. Memorize the **Risk Scoring formula** and the **Fall Factor formula** – they are tested almost every year.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Score (Standard 5×5 Matrix)** | $R = L \times S$ | Dimensionless | $L$ = Likelihood (1–5), $S$ = Severity (1–5) |
| 2 | **Risk Reduction (Hierarchy of Controls)** | $\text{Substitution} > \text{Engineering} > \text{Admin} > \text{PPE}$ | Priority order | Eliminate > Substitute > Isolate > Control > PPE |
| 3 | **Sling Tension (Vertical Lift)** | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \cos\theta}$ | N or kgf | $W$ = Load weight, $n$ = number of legs, $\theta$ = sling angle from vertical |
| 4 | **Sling Capacity Reduction Factor** | $\text{Capacity} = \text{Rated Load} \times k$ | kN | $k = 1.0$ at $90^\circ$, $k = 0.82$ at $60^\circ$, $k = 0.71$ at $45^\circ$ |
| 5 | **Earth Resistance (Target)** | $R_e \leq 1 \ \Omega$ (substation) | Ohm | $\leq 5 \ \Omega$ (general plant) |
| 6 | **Cable Minimum Bend Radius (HV)** | $r_{min} = 12 \times D$ | mm | $D$ = Cable outer diameter |
| 7 | **Hydrostatic Test Pressure** | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$ | bar / MPa | Held for **30 minutes** minimum |
| 8 | **Fall Clearance Required** | $h_c = L_{lanyard} + L_{dec} + L_{worker} + L_{safety} \approx 6.1$ m | m | For 1.8 m anchor height |
| 9 | **MAD (Min. Approach Distance) – 11 kV** | $MAD = 2.5$ m | m | Per IS 5216 |
| 10 | **Noise Exposure Limit (8-hr TWA)** | $L_{ex} \leq 85$ dBA | dB | Per Factories Act Sec 41A |
| 11 | **Man-hour Frequency Rate (LTIFR)** | $LTIFR = \dfrac{LTI \times 10^6}{MH}$ | per million hours | LTI = Lost Time Injuries, MH = Man-hours |
| 12 | **Crane Outrigger Pad Area** | $A = \dfrac{0.75 \times W_{crane}}{GBC}$ | m² | GBC = Ground Bearing Capacity (kN/m²) |
| 13 | **Heat Stress (Wet Bulb Globe Temp)** | $WBGT \leq 30^\circ C$ continuous work | °C | Above 30°C, mandatory rest cycles |

### **The KTU 5×5 Risk Matrix (MUST MEMORIZE)**

| ↓ Severity (S) / Likelihood (L) → | **1 – Rare** | **2 – Unlikely** | **3 – Possible** | **4 – Likely** | **5 – Almost Certain** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 – Catastrophic (Death)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | **20** | **25** |
| **4 – Major (Permanent disability)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | **16** | **20** |
| **3 – Moderate (Lost-time injury)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **2 – Minor (First aid)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **1 – Negligible (No injury)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Risk Band Classification (KTU Standard):**
- **1–4:** Low (Acceptable – routine controls suffice).
- **5–9:** Medium (Caution – additional controls required).
- **10–15:** High (Corrective Action – senior management approval).
- **16–25:** Extreme (Stop Work – operation prohibited until risk is reduced).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in practice?**
> 1. **Insurance & Liability:** Every industrial insurance policy (Marine Cargo, Erection All-Risk – EAR) is **void** if installation is performed without a documented Method Statement and PTW. A single non-compliance can result in claim rejection worth **crores of rupees**.
> 2. **Statutory Inspections:** The **Inspector of Factories** has the legal authority to **shut down** any installation where he observes unsafe practice under **Section 40 of the Factories Act**.
> 3. **OSHA (International) Compliance:** Indian companies exporting to the US, EU, or GCC must align installation safety with **OSHA 29 CFR 1926** (Construction Safety) and **ISO 45001:2018**.
> 4. **Project Schedule Impact:** Industrial projects typically lose **15–25% of total installation man-hours** to accidents and rework. A proper safe sequence compresses the schedule by 10–12%.
> 5. **Bankability:** International lenders (World Bank, ADB) for Greenfield projects in Kerala mandate the **ESMS (Environmental and Social Management System)** framework – installation safety is a **mandatory pre-disbursement condition**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Risk Score from First Principles (L × S Model)

The KTU standard **Risk Score** is derived from the **Fine–Kinney Method** (1970, American Society of Safety Engineers), adapted for KTU 2024.

### **Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let the **probability of a hazardous event** in a given installation task be $P$, and the **cost of consequence** (in monetary, human, or environmental terms) be $C$. Risk is then the *expected value of loss*:

$$
\text{Risk} = P \times C
$$

Since the **Fine–Kinney method** quantifies these on **ordinal scales (1–5)** rather than continuous values, we apply the substitution:

- Likelihood level $L$ corresponds to probability $P$ via a **monotonic mapping**.
- Severity level $S$ corresponds to consequence cost $C$ via a **monotonic mapping**.

Therefore, in **discrete ordinal form**:

$$
R = L \times S
$$

where $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and $S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

**Step 1: Determine Likelihood (L) on a 1–5 scale.**

- $L = 1$: The event may only occur in exceptional circumstances.
- $L = 2$: The event could occur, but not expected.
- $L = 3$: The event is expected to occur once during the project.
- $L = 4$: The event will probably occur.
- $L = 5$: The event is expected to occur more than once.

**Step 2: Determine Severity (S) on a 1–5 scale.**

- $S = 1$: Negligible – scratch, near miss.
- $S = 2$: Minor – first-aid injury.
- $S = 3$: Moderate – lost-time injury.
- $S = 4$: Major – permanent disability, fatality avoided.
- $S = 5$: Catastrophic – fatality, large-scale environmental damage.

**Step 3: Multiply to get the Risk Score.**

$$
R = L \times S
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Risk Band.**

$$
\text{Band}(R) = \begin{cases} \text{Low}, & R \in [1, 4] \\ \text{Medium}, & R \in [5, 9] \\ \text{High}, & R \in [10, 15] \\ \text{Extreme}, & R \in [16, 25] \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Hierarchy of Controls.**

$$
R_{residual} = R_{inherent} \times (1 - E_{control})
$$

where $E_{control}$ is the **efficacy factor** of the applied control:
- Elimination: $E = 1.00$
- Substitution: $E = 0.90$
- Engineering Control: $E = 0.75$
- Administrative Control: $E = 0.50$
- PPE only: $E = 0.30$

If $R_{residual} > 15$, the task **must not proceed**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sling Tension for a Multi-Legged Lift

In a typical plant installation (e.g., lifting a transformer tank), a 4-legged sling is used. The vertical load is $W$ (in kg), and the sling makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.

**Step 1: Resolve the forces.**
The vertical component of the tension in each sling must support an equal share:

$$
T \cdot \sin\theta = \dfrac{W}{n}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $T$ (in terms of the angle measured from the horizontal):**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

If the angle is measured from the **vertical** (as per KTU standard notation), then:

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example.**

Lift a 4,000 kg transformer using a 2-legged sling at $60^\circ$ from vertical:

$$
T = \dfrac{4000}{2 \cdot \cos 60^\circ} = \dfrac{4000}{2 \times 0.5} = 4000 \ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4: Verify against sling rated capacity.**
If the sling is rated at 5,000 kg per leg, the safety factor is:

$$
SF = \dfrac{5000}{4000} = 1.25
$$

The minimum **Kerala Factories Rules / IS 14562** safety factor for a 2-legged lift is **SF = 2.0**. Therefore, this configuration is **UNSAFE** – a stronger sling or wider angle is required.

**Step 5: Re-evaluate at $30^\circ$ from vertical:**

$$
T = \dfrac{4000}{2 \cdot \cos 30^\circ} = \dfrac{4000}{2 \times 0.866} = 2309 \ \text{kg}
$$

Now the safety factor is:

$$
SF = \dfrac{5000}{2309} = 2.17
$$

This is **ACCEPTABLE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A *narrower* sling angle (closer to horizontal) **dramatically increases** the tension and **decreases** the safety factor. The standard minimum sling angle is **$60^\circ$ from horizontal** (i.e., $30^\circ$ from vertical).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fall Clearance Calculation

A rigger is anchored at height $H_a = 1.8$ m (e.g., working on a transformer tank top). The lanyard length is $L_{lanyard} = 1.8$ m. The deceleration distance of the shock absorber is $L_{dec} = 1.0$ m. The worker's height is $H_w = 1.8$ m. A safety margin of $L_{safety} = 0.5$ m is required.

**Total Fall Clearance Required:**

$$
h_c = L_{lanyard} + L_{dec} + H_w + L_{safety}
$$

**Substituting the values:**

$$
h_c = 1.8 + 1.0 + 1.8 + 0.5 = 5.1 \ \text{m}
$$

**Check against ground level:**
The anchor is at 1.8 m, so the worker can fall a maximum of:

$$
h_{fall} = h_c - H_a = 5.1 - 1.8 = 3.3 \ \text{m}
$$

The worker will stop **3.3 m** below the anchor, which is **above the ground** – the system is **SAFE**.

> [!WARNING]
> If $h_c \leq 0$ (i.e., the calculated clearance is less than the anchor height), it is mathematically impossible to deploy a lanyard of that length – the worker will **strike the ground**. This is called the **"swing fall"** or **"pendulum effect"** hazard.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Installation Hazards & Controls (KTU 2024 Mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> This table is **directly testable in the ESE** as a 7-mark sub-question (e.g., "List 5 installation hazards and their controls for a 11 kV switchyard"). Memorize the **hazard–control pairings**.

| # | Installation Task | Hazard | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | Risk (R = L×S) | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lifting 50-tonne transformer | Dropped load | 5 | 3 | 15 (High) | Dual-crane lift, certified slings, exclusion zone 1.5× boom radius |
| 2 | Working at height (>1.8 m) | Fall from height | 5 | 4 | 20 (Extreme) | Full-body harness + retractable lanyard, edge protection |
| 3 | 11 kV cable termination | Electric shock/arc flash | 5 | 4 | 20 (Extreme) | LOTO, V-V-E test, arc-rated clothing (8 cal/cm²), Class 2 gloves |
| 4 | Hot work (welding, grinding) | Fire / explosion | 5 | 3 | 15 (High) | Hot work permit, fire watcher, fire extinguisher within 5 m |
| 5 | Confined space entry | Asphyxiation | 5 | 3 | 15 (High) | Gas testing (LEL < 5%, O₂ 19.5–23.5%), rescue plan, attendant |
| 6 | Trench excavation (>1.2 m) | Collapse, struck-by | 4 | 3 | 12 (High) | Shoring/sloping, ladder access every 7.5 m, edge barricade |
| 7 | Hydraulic pressure testing | Hose whip / rupture | 4 | 2 | 8 (Medium) | Pneumatic-hydraulic safety relief, exclusion zone, calibrated gauge |
| 8 | Manual handling (>25 kg) | Musculoskeletal injury | 3 | 4 | 12 (High) | Mechanical aids, team lifting, training |
| 9 | Noise from pile driving | Hearing loss | 3 | 4 | 12 (High) | Ear-muffs, time limit, audiometric test |
| 10 | Working near live OH line | Electrocution | 5 | 3 | 15 (High) | Maintain MAD, line covers, shutdown if possible |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Safe Sequence of Installation". Why is it important in the installation of plant and equipment?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **safe sequence of installation** is the prescribed **chronological order** in which the various activities of plant erection (mechanical, electrical, instrumentation, civil) must be performed to **minimize the compounding of hazards** and ensure the **integrity of partially completed work**.

**Importance (2 marks):**
1. It ensures that **prerequisites are met** before a task begins (e.g., foundation cured → structural erection → equipment mounting → cable laying → energization).
2. It **avoids rework and backtracking**, which are major causes of installation accidents.
3. It enables **inter-stage QA/QC hold points** to verify safety before the next step.
4. It maintains **regulatory compliance** with the Factories Act, IS codes, and the National Electrical Code.

(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the importance — a standard KTU 3-mark answer.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a Permit-to-Work (PTW) system? List any four types of PTWs used during plant installation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Permit-to-Work (PTW) system** is a **formal written authorization procedure** that ensures work, particularly hazardous work, is performed only after all **specified safety conditions have been met**. It is a **legal document** that links the **isolating authority** (operations) to the **performing authority** (contractor).

**Four Types of PTWs (2 marks):**
1. **Hot Work Permit** – for welding, cutting, grinding, or any spark-producing activity.
2. **Cold Work Permit** – for non-spark, non-electrical work in normal areas.
3. **Electrical Work Permit / LOTO Permit** – for energization, de-energization, or testing of electrical equipment.
4. **Confined Space Entry Permit** – for entry into vessels, tanks, ducts, or trenches with restricted egress.
5. **Excavation Permit** – for digging beyond 1.2 m depth.
6. **Working at Height Permit** – for any work above 1.8 m elevation.

(1 mark for the PTW definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining 4 types.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question – CHOICE A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

### **Question A(a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain in detail the various risks encountered during the installation of plant and equipment. Describe the 5×5 Risk Score Matrix method to quantify risk, with a suitable example from an 11 kV switchyard installation.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Part 1 – Categories of Risk (3 Marks):**
The risks during plant installation can be classified into **five major categories**:

1. **Mechanical Risks:** Crane collapse, dropped loads, sling failure, crush injuries, fall of objects, entrapment.
2. **Electrical Risks:** Electrocution from live conductors, arc flash burn, short circuits, induced voltages, static discharge.
3. **Chemical/Process Risks:** Toxic gas release, fire, explosion, asphyxiation, chemical burns.
4. **Environmental Risks:** Heat stress, cold exposure, noise (>85 dBA), dust inhalation, poor visibility.
5. **Ergonomic/Human Risks:** Musculoskeletal injuries from manual handling, fatigue, human error due to language/communication barriers.

**Part 2 – The 5×5 Risk Score Matrix (3 Marks):**
The **Risk Score** is calculated as:
$$
R = L \times S
$$
where $L$ is the **Likelihood (1–5)** and $S$ is the **Severity (1–5)**.
The **Risk Band** is:
- 1–4 → Low (Acceptable with routine controls).
- 5–9 → Medium (Caution; additional controls).
- 10–15 → High (Senior management approval; corrective action).
- 16–25 → Extreme (Stop work; operation prohibited).

**Part 3 – Numerical Example (1 Mark):**
*Task:* Erecting an 11 kV VCB panel without LOTO.
- $L = 4$ (Likely, as live busbars are nearby).
- $S = 5$ (Catastrophic – arc flash can cause fatality).
- $R = 4 \times 5 = 20$ → **Extreme Risk** → **Stop Work**.

After applying LOTO and arc-rated PPE:
- $L = 1$ (Rare).
- $R_{residual} = 1 \times 5 = 5$ → **Medium** → **Acceptable**.

---

### **Question A(b) [7 Marks]**
**Discuss the safe sequence to be followed during the installation of a 50 MVA, 110/11 kV power transformer at a substation. Highlight at least 6 critical safety checkpoints.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Phase 1: Pre-Installation (2 Marks)**
1. **Method Statement & Lifting Plan** approved by the Project Manager.
2. **Civil plinth** cured for at least 28 days, dimensions checked against GA drawing.
3. **Earthing pit** earth resistance tested ($\leq 1 \ \Omega$).
4. **Crane selection** (typically 80–100 tonne capacity for 50 MVA), with a valid load-test certificate.
5. **Sling angle** calculated ($60^\circ$ from horizontal minimum), D-shackles and spreader beam certified.
6. **Toolbox talk** conducted with riggers, electricians, and supervisors; all PPE verified (hard hat with chin strap, Class 2 gloves, safety shoes, hi-vis vest).

**Phase 2: Installation (3 Marks)**
7. **Receiving inspection** of the transformer – check oil level, silica gel, pressure gauge, and **buchholz relay** for transit damage.
8. **Lifting** of the transformer tank – **all four jacks used simultaneously**, no shock loading, no dragging.
9. **Lowering** onto plinth using **jack-and-pack method**; final alignment within $\pm 5$ mm of the centerline.
10. **Bushing installation** using certified lifting tackle; **no cantilever loading**.
11. **Cable termination** – apply LOTO upstream, V-V-E test, terminate HT and LT cables, fit **cable glands** with proper torque.
12. **Earthing connections** – two distinct earth pits (body earth and neutral earth), each $\leq 1 \ \Omega$.

**Phase 3: Post-Installation / Pre-Commissioning (2 Marks)**
13. **Oil filtration** (if required) to BDV $\geq 60$ kV / 2.5 mm gap.
14. **Insulation Resistance (IR) test** – HV-LV-Earth: minimum 1,000 MΩ (or as per IS 2026).
15. **Ducter test** of windings to confirm no inter-turn faults.
16. **Tap changer operation** verified on all 17 positions.
17. **Protection relay coordination** – Buchholz, OTI, WTI, PRD, oil level, MOG.
18. **Pre-energization checklist** signed by the Site Engineer, Safety Officer, and Client.
19. **Controlled energization** – close the HV breaker; monitor no-load current, temperature, and sound.
20. **Handover** with **as-built drawings**, **test certificates**, and **warranty cards**.

(Each phase carries the marks proportional to its weightage. Use the numbered list to demonstrate the safe sequence and interleave the safety checkpoints.)

---

## 5.3 Part B Question – CHOICE B (14 Marks) [Alternative]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

### **Question B(a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the concept of Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) in the context of electrical installation safety. Describe the 6-step LOTO procedure with a suitable example.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
**Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** is a **mandatory energy-isolation procedure** that ensures that a machine or electrical circuit is **de-energized, locked, and verified** before any installation, maintenance, or servicing work is performed. It is mandated by:
- The **Factories Act, 1948 (Sec 6 & 36)**,
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** (international benchmark),
- The **Indian Electricity Rules, 1956 (Rule 45)**.

The **lock** prevents unauthorized re-energization; the **tag** identifies the person performing the work and warns others.

**6-Step LOTO Procedure (5 Marks):**

1. **Step 1 – Prepare for Shutdown:** Identify all energy sources (electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, chemical). Notify all affected employees.

2. **Step 2 – Notify Affected Personnel:** Inform operators, supervisors, and downstream workers that a shutdown is imminent.

3. **Step 3 – Shut Down the Equipment:** Operate the normal stop switch / push button / control. Allow the equipment to come to a complete stop.

4. **Step 4 – Isolate Energy Sources:** Open the upstream isolator / circuit breaker / valve. **Physically disconnect** any plug or coupling. Bleed pneumatic/hydraulic lines.

5. **Step 5 – Apply Lock and Tag:** Each authorized person attaches their **personal lock** and **tag** to the isolation point. **Group lockout box** is used if multiple workers are involved.

6. **Step 6 – Verify Zero-Energy State (ZES):** Use an **approved voltage tester** to verify the absence of voltage using the **V-V-E method (Verify-Verify-Earth)**. Try to start the equipment; it should not start. Now work can begin.

**Example:** Before terminating a 415 V motor feeder, the electrician:
- Switches OFF the upstream MCCB,
- Applies a **red padlock** to the MCCB handle,
- Tags it with name, date, and contact,
- Tests the motor terminals with a **non-contact voltage tester**, then a **contact-type multimeter**,
- Confirms dead → begins termination.

---

### **Question B(b) [7 Marks]**
**What are the major causes of accidents during the installation of heavy plant? Discuss the Hierarchy of Controls as a risk-reduction strategy, with at least 4 examples from a petrochemical plant installation.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Major Causes of Installation Accidents (3 Marks):**

1. **Inadequate Planning:** No Method Statement, no JSA, no PTW.
2. **Failure to Follow Procedures:** Skipping LOTO, lifting with uncertified slings.
3. **Human Error:** Fatigue, distraction, untrained personnel.
4. **Poor Equipment Condition:** Defective crane, leaking hose, faulty PPE.
5. **Inadequate Communication:** Language barrier, no toolbox talk, no supervisor presence.
6. **Environmental Factors:** Heat, rain, confined space, working at night.
7. **Management Failure:** Production pressure overriding safety ("schedule over life" syndrome).

**Hierarchy of Controls (4 Marks):**
The **Hierarchy of Controls** (most effective → least effective) is a **5-tier framework** for risk reduction:

$$
\text{Elimination} > \text{Substitution} > \text{Engineering Control} > \text{Administrative Control} > \text{PPE}
$$

**Examples from a Petrochemical Plant Installation:**

1. **Elimination (Most Effective):**
   - *Risk:* Hydrostatic testing with water in a naphtha pipeline.
   - *Control:* **Eliminate the test medium** – use an inert gas (N₂) pre-test, then a transfer line isolation. No hydro-test on hot lines.

2. **Substitution:**
   - *Risk:* Use of a 6.6 kV motor starter in a hazardous area.
   - *Control:* **Substitute** with a 415 V VFD-driven motor driven from a safe area, removing the explosion-proof enclosure requirement.

3. **Engineering Control:**
   - *Risk:* Gas leak during pipe jointing.
   - *Control:* **Local exhaust ventilation (LEV)** + **continuous LEL gas detector** + **automatic emergency shutdown (ESD)** valve.

4. **Administrative Control:**
   - *Risk:* Confined space entry into a distillation column.
   - *Control:* **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** with **gas test certificate**, **rescue plan**, **attendant stationed at the manhole**, **time-limited entry** (typically 4 hours).

5. **PPE (Last Resort):**
   - *Risk:* Exposure to benzene vapors.
   - *Control:* **Air-supplied respirators (ASR)**, **chemical-resistant suits (Level B)**, **chemical gloves**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't Confuse "Hazard" with "Risk":** Hazard = source of harm; Risk = probability × severity. Students often use them interchangeably — this **costs 1–2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **The Risk Score is Multiplicative, not Additive.** Writing $R = L + S$ is **wrong** and will fetch **zero marks** in the calculation.
> 3. **The Hierarchy of Controls order is strictly fixed:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. Reversing the order (e.g., "PPE is the most important") **loses full credit**.
> 4. **In the LOTO procedure, "Verification of Zero-Energy State" is the most critical step.** Many students skip Step 6 in the answer — the examiner **always** allocates 1 mark for this.
> 5. **For crane/lifting questions, always mention the sling angle (60° from horizontal minimum), the load-test certificate validity (12 months), and the exclusion zone (1.5× boom radius).** Skipping any of these three is a 1-mark loss each.
> 6. **Numerical answers must show all steps.** If the Risk Score calculation jumps from "$L = 4, S = 5$" directly to "$R = 20$" without showing the multiplication, **0.5 mark is deducted** for lack of working.
> 7. **Statutory references earn bonus marks.** Mentioning the Factories Act, IS 5216, or NEC 2023 in the answer improves the impression and may earn a discretionary 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 10 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ The **5 Phases of Plant Installation**: Pre-installation → Material receipt → Mechanical erection → Electrical installation → Pre-commissioning → Commissioning.
- ✅ **Risk Score formula** is **multiplicative**: $R = L \times S$ (NOT additive). Range: **1 to 25**.
- ✅ **Risk Bands**: 1–4 (Low), 5–9 (Medium), 10–15 (High), 16–25 (Extreme).
- ✅ **Stop Work Authority** applies if $R \geq 16$, or if the work is being performed without a valid **PTW**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls Order (Mnemonic: "Every Safe Person Always Prefers Elimination"):** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.
- ✅ **LOTO is a 6-step procedure** ending with **V-V-E (Verify-Verify-Earth) Zero-Energy Verification**.
- ✅ **Sling angle minimum** is **60° from horizontal** (or **30° from vertical**). Narrower angle → higher tension → unsafe.
- ✅ **Crane inspection certificate** must be renewed **annually** (every 12 months). Always check.
- ✅ **Earth resistance** at substations: $\leq 1 \ \Omega$; for general plant: $\leq 5 \ \Omega$.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic test pressure** = $1.5 \times P_{design}$, held for **30 minutes minimum**.
- ✅ **Cable minimum bend radius (HV)** = **12 × Cable OD**.
- ✅ **Minimum Approach Distance (MAD)** for 11 kV: **2.5 m** (per IS 5216). For 415 V: **1.0 m**.
- ✅ **Mandatory working-at-height protection** above **1.8 m** (per Kerala Factories Rules).
- ✅ **Toolbox Talk** is a **daily** 5–10 minute pre-shift safety briefing.
- ✅ **PTW validity** is typically **per shift (8 hours)**; reissue required for next shift.
- ✅ **BIS codes to remember**: **IS 5216** (Safety Procedures), **IS 14481** (Installation of Electrical Equipment), **IS 14562** (Lifting Tackles), **IS 2026** (Power Transformers), **IS 694** (PVC Cables).
- ✅ **Statutory Acts**: **Factories Act 1948**, **Indian Electricity Rules 1956**, **National Electrical Code 2023**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1926** (international).
- ✅ **Common exam keywords** that always require definitions: "Hazard", "Risk", "PPE", "PTW", "LOTO", "TBT", "JSA", "Method Statement", "Hierarchy of Controls", "HAZID", "MAT", "MAD".
- ✅ **Numerical Questions** are almost always based on the **5×5 Risk Matrix** or the **Sling Tension Formula**. Practice at least 5 of each.
- ✅ **Memorize the formula table in Section 2.3** — it covers 95% of all KTU numericals on this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Board Examiner's Perspective:**
> "The 14-mark question on installation safety is a **discriminator** between an average student and a topper. A topper will always: (1) state the statutory reference, (2) use the correct 5×5 matrix, (3) mention the Hierarchy of Controls, and (4) provide a worked numerical example. Missing any one of these four reduces the score to **10–11 out of 14**. Aiming for 14/14? Master the **Risk Matrix** and the **LOTO procedure** — these two alone fetch 7 marks."

**End of Module 4 – Safety During Installation of Plant and Equipment Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCSFT403).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety During Testing and Commissioning. Test on Relays, Protection and Interlock Systems for Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Safety During Testing and Commissioning. Test on Relays, Protection and Interlock Systems for Safety # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Safety During Testing and Commissioning — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Testing** is the systematic application of controlled electrical, mechanical, and thermal stimuli to verify that equipment performs within design specifications. **Commissioning** is the engineered process of verifying, validating, and placing newly installed (or modified) electrical systems into active service in a safe, documented, and traceable manner. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PCSFT403), safety during testing and commissioning is a structured engineering discipline encompassing **personnel protection, equipment integrity, procedural compliance, and verification documentation**. It integrates statutory regulations (Indian Electricity Rules, IS 5216, NFPA 70E equivalent, CEA Safety Regulations) with practical engineering practices. ### Key Terminology Anchors - **Lock-Out and Tag-Out (LOTO)**: A safety procedure ensuring that an electrical isolator is physically locked in the OFF/SAFE position and a uniquely identified tag is affixed to prevent re-energization during testing. - **Permit-to-Work (PTW)**: A formal written authorization issued by an authorized engineer permitting specified work to be carried out on de-energized or specially controlled live systems. - **Pre-Commissioning Checks**: A battery of non-energized and controlled-energized tests performed before handing over equipment for live operation. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pre-Flight Checklist" > [!NOTE] > Think of testing and commissioning like an **aircraft pre-flight checklist**. The pilot (commissioning engineer) does not start the engines (energize the system) until every instrument (relay, breaker, interlock) has been confirmed functional, every fuel line (cable, busbar) has been pressure-tested (insulation resistance test), and every safety mechanism (interlock) has been verified to engage if anything goes wrong. | Phase | Aircraft Analogy | Electrical Reality | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Pre-energization** | Walk-around inspection, control surface check | Visual inspection, IR test, continuity test | | **Energization** | Engine start, instrument calibration | No-load charging, relay calibration | | **Loaded operation** | Taxi and takeoff | Full-load operation with protective settings | | **Abnormal event** | Stall recovery system | Protection relay trip + interlock engagement | --- ## 1.3 Formal Definitions — Relays, Protection, and Interlocks ### Relay A **relay** is an electrically operated switch that detects abnormal conditions in a power system and commands a **circuit breaker (CB)** to open. Modern KTU-grade definitions classify relays into: - **Electromagnetic Relays** (attracted armature, induction disc, balanced beam) - **Static Relays** (analog electronic comparators) - **Numerical/Digital Relays** (microprocessor-based with **IEC 61850** communication) ### Protection System A coordinated network of current transformers (CTs), voltage transformers (VTs), **relay logic**, and **circuit breakers** designed to detect faults (overcurrent, earth fault, differential, distance) and isolate the faulted section in **<100 ms** for HV systems. > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Rule**: The protection system must operate **reliably**, **selectively**, **speedily**, and with **sensitivity**. This is the celebrated **"Reliability-Selectivity-Speed-Sensitivity" Quadrilateral** mandated by IS 3844 and IEC 60255. ### Interlock System An **interlock** is a hardware-and-logic arrangement that **forces** a specific operational sequence, preventing simultaneous access to mutually exclusive operations. In electrical systems, interlocks prevent: - Closing a breaker while the busbar is earthed - Withdrawing a breaker from a live panel - Closing a CB before downstream earthing switches are opened --- ## 1.4 Standard Test Voltages and Equipment Metrics | Equipment Class | Standard Test Voltage | Reference Standard | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **LV** (<1000 V) | **500 V DC** | IS 5216 / IEEE 43 | | **MV** (3.3 – 11 kV) | **5 kV DC** | IS 5216 | | **HV** (11 – 33 kV) | **5 – 10 kV DC** | IEC 60137 | | **EHV** (66 kV+) | **10 – 15 kV DC** | IEC 60840 | > The **500 V megohmmeter (Megger)** is the standard hand-held instrument for LV insulation resistance testing. The **5000 V Megger** is reserved for MV/HV applications. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposed Safety Protocol During Testing The safety protocol during testing and commissioning can be decomposed into the following sequence of structured engineering steps: 1. **Documentation Review** — Verify that single-line diagrams (SLD), wiring diagrams, and protection coordination studies match the installed equipment. 2. **Pre-energization Visual Inspection** — Confirm busbar clearances (minimum **75 mm** for 415 V systems, scaling up with voltage), earth bonding, and warning labels. 3. **Mechanical Checks** — Verify breaker spring charging, contact alignment, and SF₆ gas pressure (for GIS: **0.5 MPa** at 20°C nominal). 4. **Electrical Tests (De-energized)** — Insulation resistance, continuity, earth resistance, polarity, and phase sequence. 5. **Energization under Controlled Conditions** — Step-by-step busbar charging, transformer back-charge via DGA monitors. 6. **Secondary Injection Tests** — Verify relay logic without energizing the power circuit. 7. **Primary Injection Tests** — Verify complete CT-relay-breaker chain using high-current test sets. 8. **Commissioning Records** — Document all measured values, deviations, and sign-offs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule**: Every protective device **MUST** undergo secondary injection testing **AND** primary injection testing before being declared "commissioned." This is a non-negotiable requirement under IS 5216 (Part II) and CEA Safety Regulations 2010. --- ## 2.2 Critical Safety Equations — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $R_{earth} \leq \dfrac{V_{touch}}{I_{disc}}$ | Maximum permissible earth resistance (IEEE 80) | $\Omega$, $V_{touch}$ in V, $I_{disc}$ in A | | 2 | $\text{IR}_{\min} = k \cdot (V_{rated} + 1)$ | Minimum insulation resistance per kV | $M\Omega$, $k = 1\text{–}2$ typical | | 3 | $\text{Drop-off ratio} = \dfrac{I_{drop}}{I_{pick}}$ | Reset ratio of a relay | Dimensionless, typically **0.85–0.95** | | 4 | $\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{fault}}{I_{pick} \cdot CT_{ratio}}$ | Plug Setting Multiplier | Dimensionless | | 5 | $t = a \cdot \left(\dfrac{PSM^b - 1}{c}\right)$ | Standard inverse IDMT characteristic | IEC 60255-151 | | 6 | $I_{differential} = \vert I_{1} - I_{2} \vert$ | Differential protection operating quantity | A, based on CT polarities | | 7 | $Z_{reach} = \dfrac{V_{phase}}{I_{phase}}$ | Distance relay impedance measurement | $\Omega/\text{phase}$ | | 8 | $V_{step} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rated} \cdot k_{factor}$ | Step voltage at fault point | V, $k_{factor}$ from grounding | | 9 | $P_{arc} = V_{arc} \cdot I_{arc} \cdot t_{arc}$ | Arc-flash incident energy (Lee method) | Joules / cm² | > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners routinely award zero marks for missing units in numerical answers. Always carry the unit through the calculation. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The protocols covered in this module directly govern the following real-world deliverables: - **Substation Energization Audits** — Used by utilities (KSEB, NTPC, Power Grid) to certify new substations before charging. - **Industrial Plant Acceptance Tests** — Mandatory for ISO 9001 / ISO 45001 audit-trail compliance. - **Transformer Commissioning** — Determines whether a new transformer is fit for parallel operation. - **Relay Coordination Studies** — Ensures downstream relays trip **before** upstream relays (discrimination / selectivity). > The **Time-Current Characteristic (TCC)** curve overlay for coordinated overcurrent relays is the single most important graph a KTU examiner expects students to recognize. It plots operating time (y-axis, log scale) against fault current (x-axis, log scale) for every relay in the chain. --- ## 2.4 Numerical Instantiation — Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM) Consider a relay with a pickup current setting of **1.0 A** on a CT ratio of **400/5 A**. A three-phase fault produces a primary current of **6000 A**. The Plug Setting Multiplier is calculated as: $$I_{secondary} = \dfrac{I_{primary}}{CT_{ratio}} = \dfrac{6000}{400/5} = \dfrac{6000 \cdot 5}{400}$$ $$I_{secondary} = \dfrac{30000}{400} = 75 \text{ A}$$ $$\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{secondary}}{I_{pickup}} = \dfrac{75}{1.0} = 75$$ > A PSM of **75** is well into the instantaneous (high-set) zone of any inverse-time overcurrent relay — the breaker will open in **<30 ms**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Earth Electrode Resistance (Schwarz Equation) The earth resistance of a single driven rod electrode, using **Schwarz's formula**, is derived from the spherical-shell field distribution around the rod. Let: - $L$ = Length of the rod (m) - $d$ = Diameter of the rod (m) - $\rho$ = Soil resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$) The Schwarz expression for a single vertical rod in homogeneous soil is: $$R_{rod} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ ### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation **Step 1: Define the elementary resistance of a soil shell** Consider a hemispherical shell of soil at radius $r$ from the rod, with thickness $dr$. The elementary resistance offered by this shell is: $$dR = \rho \cdot \dfrac{dr}{2\pi r^{2}}$$ The hemispherical shell has surface area $2\pi r^{2}$ (half of a full sphere since current spreads downward into the soil). **Step 2: Integrate from the rod surface to the effective radius** The total resistance is the integral from the rod radius $r_{0} = d/2$ to the effective outer radius $L$ (since for a rod of length $L$, most current flows within a distance comparable to $L$): $$R_{rod} = \int_{d/2}^{L} \dfrac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r^{2}}$$ **Step 3: Evaluate the integral** $$R_{rod} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \int_{d/2}^{L} \dfrac{dr}{r^{2}}$$ $$R_{rod} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{r} \right]_{d/2}^{L}$$ $$R_{rod} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{L} + \dfrac{2}{d} \right]$$ **Step 4: Convert to logarithmic form** For a long thin rod where $L \gg d$, the term $2/d$ dominates. Rewriting: $$R_{rod} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2L}{d} - 1 \right]$$ Since $\ln(8L/d) = \ln(8) + \ln(L/d)$ and for practical ratios $L/d > 100$, the empirical correction reduces the bracket term to: $$R_{rod} \approx \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$ This is the **Schwarz Equation** — a derivation-rich formula often quoted in KTU Part A questions. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — IDMT Relay Operating Time (Standard Inverse) The **Standard Inverse Time-Current Characteristic** as defined by IEC 60255-151 is given by: $$t(s) = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot k}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}$$ For a typical **Very Inverse** curve: $$t(s) = \dfrac{13.5 \cdot k}{\text{PSM} - 1}$$ Where: - $t$ = operating time in seconds - $k$ = time multiplier setting (TMS), typically **0.05 to 1.0** - $PSM$ = Plug Setting Multiplier ### Worked Numerical Example > **Problem**: An IDMT overcurrent relay has a pickup of **100%** and a TMS of **0.3**. A fault of **3000 A** primary current occurs on a CT ratio of **400/5 A**. Determine the relay operating time using the Standard Inverse curve. **Solution Steps**: Step 1: Compute secondary fault current. $$I_{sec} = \dfrac{3000}{400/5} = \dfrac{3000 \cdot 5}{400} = \dfrac{15000}{400} = 37.5 \text{ A}$$ Step 2: Compute pickup current at 100% setting. $$I_{pickup} = \dfrac{5}{1} = 5 \text{ A (secondary)}$$ Step 3: Compute PSM. $$\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{sec}}{I_{pickup}} = \dfrac{37.5}{5} = 7.5$$ Step 4: Apply the Standard Inverse formula with $k = 0.3$. $$t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot 0.3}{7.5^{0.02} - 1}$$ Step 5: Compute the exponent. $$7.5^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \cdot \ln(7.5)} = e^{0.02 \cdot 2.0149} = e^{0.0403} = 1.0411$$ Step 6: Substitute and simplify. $$t = \dfrac{0.042}{1.0411 - 1} = \dfrac{0.042}{0.0411}$$ $$t \approx 1.022 \text{ seconds}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern**: In ESE questions, examiners frequently substitute the formula **directly with a known PSM** to test if students know the IDMT expression. Memorize the Standard Inverse form: $t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot k}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Relay Test Decision Tree | Relay Type | Primary Test | Acceptance Criteria | Common Failure Mode | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Overcurrent (IDMT)** | Secondary injection at 1.05 $I_{pick}$ | Must operate within 30 s | Stuck contact | | **Earth Fault** | Primary injection through CT primary | Pickup at ±5% of setting | CT polarity reversal | | **Differential (Transformer)** | Through-fault + internal-fault simulation | Through-fault restraint, internal-fault trip | CT ratio mismatch | | **Distance (Z1/Z2/Z3)** | Impedance sweep via OMICRON or Doble | Reach accuracy ±5% | Memory in polar loop | | **Under/Over Voltage** | Voltage ramp test | Pickup at 90% / 110% of $V_{rated}$ | PT fuse blown | | **Buchholz Relay** | Gas accumulation simulation | Trip on gas volume > 100 ml | Slow response | | **Numerical Relay (IED)** | IEC 61850 GOOSE simulation | Trip command within 30 ms | Network latency | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Commissioning Workflow — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Project Handover] --> A2[Documentation Review] A2 --> A3[Visual Inspection] A3 --> A4[Mechanical Checks] A4 --> A5[De-Energized Tests] A5 --> A51[Insulation Resistance Test] A5 --> A52[Earth Resistance Test] A5 --> A53[Continuity Test] A5 --> A54[Polarity and Phase Sequence] A51 --> A6[Controlled Energization] A52 --> A6 A53 --> A6 A54 --> A6 A6 --> A7[Secondary Injection Tests] A7 --> A8[Primary Injection Tests] A8 --> A9[Interlock Verification] A9 --> A10[Load Trial Run] A10 --> A11[Final Documentation] A11 --> A12[Hand Over] ``` > This block flow represents the **sequential gate-by-gate commissioning protocol** mandated in IS 5216. Each gate must be signed off before progressing to the next; any failure rolls back to remediation. --- ## 4.2 Protection and Interlock Schematic — Substation Bus Scheme ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INCOMING I1[Incoming 11 kV Line] --> I2[CB1 Main Incomer] end subgraph BUS I2 --> BUS1[Busbar 11 kV] BUS1 --> T1[Outgoing Feeder 1] BUS1 --> T2[Outgoing Feeder 2] BUS1 --> T3[Transformer CB] BUS1 --> E1[Earthing Switch] end subgraph PROTECT CT1[CT Set 1] --- R1[Relay IDMT] CT2[CT Set 2] --- R2[Relay Earth Fault] CT3[CT Set 3] --- R3[Relay Differential] end R1 --- I2 R2 --- I2 R3 --- T3 subgraph INTERLOCK IL1[Key Interlock A] IL2[Mechanical Interlock B] IL3[Logic Interlock C] end IL1 --- E1 IL2 --- I2 IL3 --- T1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interlock Logic Anchor**: In a standard double-busbar substation, the **earthing switch** and **bus coupler breaker** MUST be interlocked such that the earth switch can ONLY close when the bus is de-energized. This is achieved via **Kirk-key interlocks** or **logic interlocks** in modern numerical relays. --- ## 4.3 Time-Current Characteristic (TCC) Coordination Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TCC_PLOT AX[X-Axis: Fault Current in A] AY[Y-Axis: Operating Time in s] end curve1["Incomer Relay IDMT
TMS 0.3
PS 125%"] curve2["Feeder 1 Relay IDMT
TMS 0.2
PS 100%"] curve3["Feeder 2 Relay IDMT
TMS 0.15
PS 80%"] HSI["High Set Instantaneous
at 8 x In"] AX --> curve1 AX --> curve2 AX --> curve3 AX --> HSI ``` > The TCC demonstrates **graded protection coordination**: for any given fault current on Feeder 2, Feeder 2's relay operates **first**, followed by Feeder 1, followed by the Incomer. The **discrimination time** (typically **0.3 – 0.5 s**) separates adjacent curves. --- ## 4.4 Relay Test Sequence — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Test Commenced] --> pre1[Verify PTW and LOTO] pre1 --> pre2[Isolate Primary Circuit] pre2 --> pre3[Connect Test Kit] pre3 --> inj1[Inject 80% Pickup] inj1 --> check1{Operates?} check1 -- No --> pass1[No Trip Correct] check1 -- Yes --> fail1[Fail Adjust Settings] pass1 --> inj2[Inject 105% Pickup] inj2 --> check2{Operates?} check2 -- Yes --> pass2[Trip Correct] check2 -- No --> fail2[Fail Investigate] pass2 --> inj3[Inject at PSM 2x] inj3 --> time1[Measure Operating Time] time1 --> curve[Plot on TCC] curve --> signoff[Sign Off] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **"State the purpose of Lock-Out and Tag-Out (LOTO) procedures in electrical testing. Mention any three cardinal rules of LOTO."** **Model Answer:** LOTO ensures that **electrical equipment is reliably isolated from all energy sources** before testing or maintenance, preventing inadvertent re-energization by unauthorized personnel. It is a globally mandated safety protocol under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 and is reinforced in India under the CEA Safety Regulations 2010. **Three Cardinal Rules**: 1. **Isolation** — Every energy source (electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic) must be physically disconnected. 2. **Locking** — A unique padlock, keyed or combination-locked, must be applied by the authorized worker to the isolation point. 3. **Tagging** — A danger tag bearing the worker's name, date, and reason for isolation must be affixed to the lock. The tag **NEVER** substitutes for the lock — both are mandatory. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **"Differentiate between 'Plug Setting' and 'Time Multiplier Setting' in an IDMT overcurrent relay."** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Plug Setting (PS) | Time Multiplier Setting (TMS) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | The pickup current threshold at which the relay begins to operate, expressed as a percentage of CT secondary current | A scaling factor that shifts the relay's time-current curve vertically (faster or slower operation) | | **Typical Range** | 50%, 75%, 100%, 125%, 150%, 175%, 200% | 0.05 to 1.0 in steps of 0.05 | | **Effect on TCC** | Shifts curve horizontally | Shifts curve vertically | | **Determined By** | Fault current level to be detected | Required discrimination time | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently lose **2 to 3 marks** by: > 1. Skipping units in the final answer (always write **A, V, Ω, s** explicitly). > 2. Failing to state the **assumptions** at the start of derivations (e.g., "Assuming homogeneous soil resistivity"). > 3. Conflating **reach** and **operating time** in distance relay problems. > 4. Omitting the **standard reference** (IS/IEC clause) in safety-related answers. ### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the step-by-step procedure for safety testing and commissioning of a new 11 kV substation. Discuss the role of insulation resistance testing and earth resistance testing in ensuring personnel and equipment safety. **(7 marks)** **b)** A differential relay is used to protect a 5 MVA, 11 kV / 433 V Dyn11 transformer. The CT ratios on HV and LV sides are 300/5 A and 4000/5 A respectively. During an external fault, the relay must restrain. Calculate the currents flowing through the relay operating coil under normal full-load and through-fault conditions. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): The step-by-step safety testing and commissioning procedure for a new 11 kV substation is as follows: **Step 1 — Pre-Commissioning Documentation** Verify single-line diagram, equipment nameplates, protection coordination study, and IS/IEC compliance certificates. **Step 2 — Visual Inspection** - Busbar clearances (minimum **125 mm** for 11 kV) - Earthing conductor continuity - Warning boards and barrier ropes **Step 3 — De-Energized Electrical Tests** **Insulation Resistance Test**: Using a **5 kV Megger**, the IR value between phases and to earth must exceed the minimum benchmark: $$\text{IR}_{\min} = k \cdot (V_{rated} + 1) \text{ M}\Omega$$ For 11 kV with $k = 1$: $$\text{IR}_{\min} = 1 \cdot (11 + 1) = 12 \text{ M}\Omega$$ A measured value **>12 MΩ** confirms insulation integrity. Below this, the system MUST NOT be energized. **Earth Resistance Test**: Using a **fall-of-potential method**, the combined earth electrode resistance should be: $$R_{earth} \leq \dfrac{V_{touch}}{I_{disc}}$$ For 11 kV substations, $V_{touch} \leq 250 \text{ V}$ and $I_{disc} = 0.5 \text{ A}$, giving: $$R_{earth} \leq 0.5 \, \Omega$$ This is the **most critical** safety measurement — it directly determines touch and step voltages during a fault. **Step 4 — Controlled Energization and Load Trial** #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1 — Compute Rated Full-Load Currents** HV side (11 kV): $$I_{HV} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6}}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = \dfrac{5000000}{19052.56} \approx 262.4 \text{ A}$$ LV side (433 V): $$I_{LV} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6}}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = \dfrac{5000000}{750.07} \approx 6666.7 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Secondary Currents at Full Load** $$I_{sec,HV} = \dfrac{262.4}{300/5} = \dfrac{262.4 \cdot 5}{300} = 4.373 \text{ A}$$ $$I_{sec,LV} = \dfrac{6666.7}{4000/5} = \dfrac{6666.7 \cdot 5}{4000} = 8.333 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Through-Fault Currents** For an external fault producing **10 times rated current**: $$I_{sec,HV}^{fault} = 10 \cdot 4.373 = 43.73 \text{ A}$$ $$I_{sec,LV}^{fault} = 10 \cdot 8.333 = 83.33 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4 — Differential Operating Current** $$I_{diff} = \vert I_{sec,HV}^{fault} - I_{sec,LV}^{fault} \cdot k_{compensation} \vert$$ After applying the **magnetic balance compensation factor** for the Dyn11 vector group, the relay coil carries approximately the **spill current**, which should be **<5% of through-fault current** for proper restraint: $$I_{spill} \approx 0.05 \cdot 83.33 \approx 4.17 \text{ A}$$ > A properly biased differential relay will **restrain** under this spill current because the through-fault creates a flux in the restraint winding that opposes the operating winding. --- ### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the principle of operation of an IDMT overcurrent relay. Discuss the significance of PSM and TMS in relay coordination. **(7 marks)** **b)** An IDMT relay has a plug setting of **125%** and a TMS of **0.2** on a 400/5 A CT circuit. The fault current is **5000 A** (primary). Using the Standard Inverse characteristic, calculate the relay operating time. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): **Principle of IDMT Relay Operation**: The **Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT)** relay combines two characteristics: 1. **Inverse portion**: Operating time decreases as fault current increases. 2. **Definite Minimum Time portion**: Below a threshold, the operating time becomes constant (minimum). **Block Diagram Description**: The CT secondary feeds current to the **operating coil** of an induction disc type relay. The disc torque is proportional to $I^{2}$. A **permanent magnet** provides braking torque. The disc rotates until it closes the contacts. $$\text{Disc operating torque} \propto I^{2}$$ $$\text{Disc braking torque} = \text{constant from permanent magnet}$$ The **PSM (Plug Setting Multiplier)** shifts the pickup threshold, while **TMS (Time Multiplier Setting)** scales the time dial position. In coordination studies, **lower TMS** is set for downstream relays and **higher TMS** for upstream relays to maintain discrimination. #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1 — Compute Secondary Fault Current** $$I_{sec} = \dfrac{I_{primary}}{CT_{ratio}} = \dfrac{5000}{400/5} = \dfrac{5000 \cdot 5}{400} = 62.5 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Pickup Current at PS = 125%** $$I_{pick} = 1.25 \times 5 = 6.25 \text{ A (secondary)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute PSM** $$\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{sec}}{I_{pick}} = \dfrac{62.5}{6.25} = 10$$ **Step 4 — Apply Standard Inverse Formula** $$t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot k}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}$$ Substituting $k = 0.2$: $$t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot 0.2}{10^{0.02} - 1}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the Exponent** $$10^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \cdot \ln(10)} = e^{0.02 \cdot 2.3026} = e^{0.04605} = 1.04713$$ **Step 6 — Final Calculation** $$t = \dfrac{0.028}{1.04713 - 1} = \dfrac{0.028}{0.04713}$$ $$t \approx 0.594 \text{ s}$$ > **Answer**: The relay will operate in approximately **0.594 seconds** for this fault condition. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision Anchors** — Memorize the points below before any KTU board examination. - **LOTO** = Lock + Tag + Verify zero energy + Permit-to-Work. Both lock AND tag are mandatory; one does not substitute the other. - **Megger Voltage Levels**: **500 V DC** for LV, **5 kV DC** for MV/HV systems. - **Earth Resistance Benchmark**: **<0.5 Ω** for HV substations, **<1 Ω** for industrial plants, **<5 Ω** for domestic installations. - **Insulation Resistance Rule of Thumb**: **IR (MΩ) ≥ (kV rating + 1)**, typically with **$k = 1$**. - **IDMT Standard Inverse**: $t = \dfrac{0.14 \cdot TMS}{PSM^{0.02} - 1}$. - **IDMT Very Inverse**: $t = \dfrac{13.5 \cdot TMS}{PSM - 1}$. - **IDMT Extremely Inverse**: $t = \dfrac{80 \cdot TMS}{PSM^{2} - 1}$. - **Drop-off Ratio** of an IDMT relay is typically **0.85 – 0.95** (not 1.0). - **Differential Protection** requires CT polarity verification (dot convention) and **magnetic balance compensation** for vector-group displacement (e.g., Dyn11). - **Distance Relay Zones**: Z1 = 80–90% of line impedance (instantaneous), Z2 = 120–150% (graded), Z3 = next-line backup. - **Interlock Hierarchy**: **Mechanical > Key > Electrical > Logic**. Mechanical interlocks are the most fail-safe. - **Arc Flash Boundary** calculations use **IEEE 1584** empirical equations; in KTU numerical problems, simplified Lee method is acceptable. - **CT Polarity Test**: For every CT, the **dot (P1)** terminal faces the source; reversal inverts the relay's view of fault direction. - **Commissioning Documentation**: Every test must be countersigned by **at least two engineers** (testing + witness) per IS 5216. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Insight**: The 14-mark ESE questions on this topic almost always combine a **theory sub-part** (7 marks) with a **numerical sub-part** (7 marks). Practice sketching **TCC curves** by hand for any given PSM/TMS combination — this is a recurring 3-mark short-answer type question.

topics.title: Hazardous Zones, Classification of Hazardous Zones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT403 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hazardous Zones, Classification of Hazardous Zones

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Hazardous Area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (IS/IEC 60079-10-1 aligned):**
> A **Hazardous Area (or Hazardous Location)** is defined as a place (three-dimensional space) in which an explosive gas atmosphere, explosive dust atmosphere, or combustible dust is present, or may be expected to be present, in quantities such that special precautions for the construction, installation, and use of electrical apparatus become necessary.

In simpler engineering terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme expected answer style):
> An area is classified as **hazardous** when the **concentration of flammable gases, vapours, mists, or combustible dusts** in air falls within the **explosive (flammable) limits**, thereby creating a risk of fire or explosion if an ignition source (spark, hot surface, or arc) is introduced.

### 1.2 The Fire Triangle — Foundation of Hazard

> [!NOTE]
> For an explosion to occur in any hazardous zone, **THREE simultaneous conditions** must be satisfied (commonly known as the **Explosion Triangle**):
>
> 1. **Fuel** — Flammable substance (gas, vapour, dust)
> 2. **Oxidiser** — Usually atmospheric oxygen (≈21% in air)
> 3. **Ignition Source** — Spark, arc, or hot surface with sufficient energy (Minimum Ignition Energy, MIE)

Removing any one leg of this triangle prevents an explosion. Hazardous zone classification aims to **predict the probability of the fuel + oxidiser leg occurring**, so that ignition sources (electrical equipment) can be appropriately designed or eliminated.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!TIP]
> **Think of your kitchen while cooking on a gas stove:**
>
> - The **gas cylinder knob area** = always leaking some gas → extremely high risk → **Zone 0 / Division 1**
> - The **immediate vicinity of the burner (say 1 metre)** = gas occasionally escapes during ignition → moderate risk → **Zone 1 / Division 1**
> - The **rest of the kitchen, near the window** = only during a major leak would gas reach there → low risk → **Zone 2 / Division 2**
> - The **dining room next door** = gas will practically never accumulate → safe area (**Non-Hazardous / Unclassified**)
>
> **Now imagine replacing your kitchen with a petroleum refinery, paint shop, or LPG bottling plant.** The same logic applies, but the stakes (mass of fuel, frequency of release, public danger) are vastly higher — that is exactly why **standardised zone classification** exists.

---

## 1.4 Why is Classification Mandatory? (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Indian Standard **IS/IEC 60079-10-1:2015** & **OISD-GDN-117** (Oil Industry Safety Directorate).
>
> Classification is done to:
> 1. **Select correct Ex-proof equipment** (flameproof, increased safety, intrinsically safe).
> 2. **Reduce installation cost** by avoiding over-engineering in low-risk areas.
> 3. **Ensure worker safety** and statutory compliance under **Factories Act 1948** & **Petroleum Rules 2002**.
> 4. **Minimise insurance liability** and equipment downtime.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** | Substance dependent | Minimum fuel-air ratio for ignition |
| **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)** | Substance dependent | Maximum fuel-air ratio for ignition |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** | **0.2 mJ** (Hydrogen) | Lowest for any gas |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** | 200 °C – 600 °C | Determines **Temperature Class** |
| **Flash Point** | < 60 °C (Class A/B liquids) | Liquids below this are hazardous |
| **Atmospheric Oxygen** | ≈ **20.9 %** by volume | Constant oxidiser |
| **Reference Pressure** | **101.325 kPa** | Standard ambient |
| **Reference Temperature** | **20 °C (293.15 K)** | Classification baseline |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parameters Determining Zone Classification

According to **IS/IEC 60079-10-1**, hazardous area classification is determined by evaluating **three primary variables**:

### (a) Source of Release — The Most Important Factor

A **source of release** is a point or location from which a flammable gas, vapour, or liquid can be released into the atmosphere, forming an explosive atmosphere.

> [!NOTE]
> **Grade of Release (KTU High-Yield):**
>
> | Grade | Definition | Typical Example |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Continuous Grade** | Release persists for **> 1000 hours/year** | Fixed roof tank vent, open dip tank |
> | **Primary Grade** | Release likely during **normal operation (10–1000 h/year)** | Pump seal, valve gland, sampling point |
> | **Secondary Grade** | Release unlikely in normal operation, but possible (**< 10 h/year)** | Flanged joint, threaded pipe fitting |

### (b) Release Rate & Ventilation

The **rate of release** depends on:
- Liquid/gas pressure
- Hole size (assumed leak diameter typically **2 mm to 25 mm**)
- Viscosity of fluid
- **Ventilation degree** (High, Medium, Low)

### (c) Type of Flammable Substance

Determined by its **gas group** (IIA, IIB, IIC) and **temperature class** (T1–T6).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Hazardous Zone Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarises every formula, ratio, and boundary condition the KTU Board expects a student to remember for Module 4.**

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Explosive Limits Range** | $\Delta\phi \;=\; \phi_{UEL} \;-\; \phi_{LEL}$ | % by volume in air | $\phi$ = volume fraction of fuel |
| 2 | **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)** | $\dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} \;=\; \dfrac{4.76}{x} \cdot \left( n_C + \dfrac{n_H}{4} - \dfrac{n_O}{2} \right)$ | kg/kg | For hydrocarbon C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$ |
| 3 | **Volume of Hazardous Cloud (idealised hemisphere)** | $V_{cloud} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3}\,\pi\,r^{3}$ | m³ | Used in dispersion modelling |
| 4 | **Dilution Ventilation Required** | $Q_{vent} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{release}}{LEL \,\cdot\, \text{Safety Factor}}$ | m³/min | Safety factor usually **5× to 10×** |
| 5 | **Auto-Ignition vs Surface Temp rule** | $T_{surface,max} \;<\; \left(AIT \;-\; 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}\right)$ | °C | Equipment surface must stay cooler than AIT |
| 6 | **Minimum Ignition Energy (Hydrogen)** | $MIE_{H_2} \;=\; \mathbf{0.2\,mJ}$ | millijoules | Lowest of all gases |
